title: From Focusing to Con-Focusing: Optimal Power Transfer in Line-of-Sight Near-Field MIMO
summary: Beamfocusing is the established near-field strategy for a large array serving a single-antenna user. We consider the single-user line-of-sight MIMO link, free of multipath, in which the user, too, carries an extended aperture, and show that the focusing prescription inverts: beyond a modest Fresnel number, focusing on the user is outperformed by far-field steering. Under fully analog, unit-modulus beamforming, we derive closed-form power gains for focusing (each aperture phase-matched to the other's center) and for steering (a planar phase ramp) in the Fresnel regime, and prove that their comparison is governed by two dimensionless quantities: the link Fresnel number, the product of the two aperture lengths normalized by wavelength and link distance, and the aperture ratio, irrespective of how many elements discretize the apertures. For equal apertures the two gains cross exactly once, at the universal value 1.947; beyond it, focusing loses ten dB per decade of Fresnel number, and the advantage celebrated in the MISO literature survives only as the receive aperture vanishes. We then derive the strategy that is order-optimal at every Fresnel number, con-focusing: both apertures aim at the common point from which they subtend equal angles. It attains the rank-one eigenbound in leading constant, needs no channel knowledge, degenerates to plain steering for equal apertures, and is acquirable within one beam-refinement round with no geometry exchange between the terminals.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes near-field beamfocusing vs. far-field steering in single-user line-of-sight MIMO links, showing that steering outperforms focusing beyond a Fresnel number of ~1.947 for equal apertures, and proposes "con-focusing" as an order-optimal strategy that requires no channel knowledge.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to challenge the prevailing wisdom that near-field beamfocusing is optimal for large arrays serving single-antenna users, by considering the case where the user also has an extended aperture (MIMO link), and showing conditions under which simple far-field steering performs better.
AI.method: Under fully analog, unit-modulus beamforming, the authors derive closed-form power gains for focusing (phase-matched to center) and steering (planar phase ramp) in the Fresnel regime. They prove the comparison is governed by two dimensionless quantities: link Fresnel number and aperture ratio. They also derive the con-focusing strategy that is order-optimal at every Fresnel number.
AI.result: For equal apertures, focusing and steering gains cross exactly once at Fresnel number ~1.947; beyond that, focusing loses 10 dB per decade of Fresnel number. The con-focusing strategy attains the rank-one eigenbound in leading constant, needs no channel knowledge, and degenerates to steering for equal apertures.
AI.conclusion: The advantage of beamfocusing celebrated in MISO literature only survives as the receive aperture vanishes. The proposed con-focusing is order-optimal and acquirable within one beam-refinement round with no geometry exchange between terminals.

title: Bayesian-Optimized Multi-Source Domain Adaptation for Post-Earthquake Damage Assessment
summary: Efficient and intelligent post-earthquake structural damage assessment is critical for rapid disaster response. Although data-driven approaches have shown promise in this domain, traditional supervised learning relies on large labeled datasets that are impractical to obtain for earthquake-damaged structures. To overcome this limitation, we propose a Bayesian-optimized multisource domain adaptation framework for predicting post-earthquake structural damage on a target building without the need for any damage labels. The framework comprises three key steps. First, it extracts features from multiple source domains and the target domain and feeds them into a classifier and a domain discriminator. The classifier ensures the features remain damage-sensitive, while the discriminator promotes their invariance across domains. Second, the framework assigns a weighing factor to each source domain to balance their contributions during training. Finally, Bayesian optimization is employed to optimize these source domain weights, aiming to maximize prediction accuracy on the target domain. This framework offers a robust solution for structural damage assessment when labeled data are scarce, significantly enhancing post-earthquake damage assessment capabilities.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Bayesian-optimized multisource domain adaptation framework that predicts post-earthquake structural damage on target buildings without requiring any damage labels, using feature extraction, domain discrimination, weighted source domains, and Bayesian optimization of weights.
AI.motivation: Traditional supervised learning for earthquake damage assessment requires large labeled datasets that are impractical to obtain for damaged structures, creating a critical bottleneck for rapid disaster response.
AI.method: Three-step framework: (1) feature extraction with a classifier (damage-sensitive) and domain discriminator (domain-invariant), (2) assigning weighing factors to each source domain, (3) Bayesian optimization to optimize source domain weights for maximum target domain prediction accuracy.
AI.result: Enables accurate structural damage prediction on a target building without any damage labels by leveraging multiple source domains with optimized weighting.
AI.conclusion: Offers a robust, label-free solution for post-earthquake structural damage assessment that significantly enhances rapid disaster response capabilities when labeled data are scarce.

title: Hybrid AI-Physics Framework for Post-Earthquake Structural Damage Diagnosis with Sparse Sensing
summary: Rapid and reliable post-earthquake damage assessment is critical for public safety, re-occupancy decisions, and effective emergency response. This paper presents a physics-informed, unsupervised learning framework that enables structural damage diagnosis in sparsely instrumented buildings following seismic events. The approach fuses real sensor data with physics-based simulations to create a hybrid spatiotemporal input grid, extending observability to regions without sensors. A Spatiotemporal Composite Autoencoder Network (SCAN) processes this hybrid input, learning the structure's undamaged behavior from pre-event or ambient-condition data alone. SCAN integrates convolutional layers for extracting localized spatial features and LSTM layers for modeling temporal dynamics, enabling it to recognize deviations from normal behavior caused by damage. Post-event sensor data are analyzed through the trained model, and anomalies are flagged based on elevated reconstruction and prediction errors. These error patterns are spatially mapped to localize potential damage, even in uninstrumented areas. By embedding low-fidelity physical estimates directly into the input representation, the framework enhances detection sensitivity while requiring no labeled damage examples. This hybrid AI-physics approach offers a scalable, interpretable, and data-efficient solution for real-time post-earthquake structural diagnostics, providing critical decision support for emergency managers and accelerating safe and targeted recovery efforts.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed unsupervised learning framework (SCAN) that fuses real sensor data with simulations for post-earthquake damage diagnosis in sparsely instrumented buildings.
AI.motivation: Rapid and reliable post-earthquake damage assessment is critical yet challenging in sparsely instrumented buildings; existing methods often require labeled damage examples or dense sensor networks.
AI.method: Proposes a Spatiotemporal Composite Autoencoder Network (SCAN) that fuses real sensor data with physics-based simulations into a hybrid spatiotemporal input grid, uses convolutional and LSTM layers to learn undamaged behavior from pre-event data, and detects anomalies via reconstruction/prediction errors post-event.
AI.result: The framework enables damage detection and spatial localization even in uninstrumented areas, with enhanced sensitivity from embedding low-fidelity physical estimates into the input representation, requiring no labeled damage examples.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid AI-physics approach offers a scalable, interpretable, and data-efficient solution for real-time post-earthquake structural diagnostics, supporting emergency response and recovery efforts.

title: Cross-channel Specific Emitter Identification and Verification via Signal Envelope
summary: Specific emitter identification (SEI) determines which known emitter a received signal originates from, while specific emitter verification (SEV) determines whether the received signal genuinely comes from its claimed emitter. In this paper, we consider the effect of wireless fading channels on SEI and SEV. When the Rician $K$-factor varies, the resulting distribution shift induced by the channel degrades both identification and verification performance. To address this issue, we first theoretically prove that the coefficient of variation of the signal envelope is strictly monotonic with respect to the Rician $K$-factor. Motivated by this property, we propose an envelope-guided adaptive feature modulation (EAFM) identifier for SEI and an EAFM with Mahalanobis distance metric learning (EAFM-MD) verifier for SEV. Specifically, the proposed EAFM identifier adopts a dual-branch neural network to extract device-oriented features from the IQ-domain input and channel-conditioning features from the normalized signal envelope, and adaptively modulates the former via feature-wise linear modulation. Then, we extend the EAFM identifier to an EAFM-MD verifier. The device-fingerprint library is constructed by storing the feature centroid and covariance for each enrolled device, along with the within-device Mahalanobis distances of training signals. For verification, the Mahalanobis distance between the extracted test features and each stored centroid is computed using the stored covariance matrix, and the minimum distance is compared to the corresponding device threshold to make a decision. Finally, numerical results show that the proposed EAFM identifier improves cross-channel identification performance, while the proposed EAFM-MD verifier achieves superior detection performance against unknown spoofing attacks.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the degradation of Specific Emitter Identification (SEI) and Specific Emitter Verification (SEV) under varying Rician fading channels by proposing an envelope-guided adaptive feature modulation (EAFM) approach. It theoretically proves monotonicity between signal envelope's coefficient of variation and Rician K-factor, then develops an EAFM identifier using dual-branch neural networks and an EAFM-MD verifier using Mahalanobis distance metric learning. Results show improved cross-channel identification and detection against spoofing attacks.
AI.motivation: Wireless fading channels, especially varying Rician K-factor, cause distribution shifts that degrade both SEI (identifying which emitter) and SEV (verifying claimed emitter identity) performance. Existing methods lack robustness against such channel-induced variations.
AI.method: 1) Theoretically proves coefficient of variation of signal envelope is strictly monotonic w.r.t. Rician K-factor. 2) Proposes EAFM identifier: dual-branch neural network extracting device-oriented features (IQ-domain) and channel-conditioning features (normalized signal envelope), then adaptively modulates via feature-wise linear modulation. 3) Extends to EAFM-MD verifier: stores feature centroid and covariance per device, uses Mahalanobis distance for verification against spoofing attacks.
AI.result: The EAFM identifier improves cross-channel identification performance under varying Rician fading; the EAFM-MD verifier achieves superior detection performance against unknown spoofing attacks.
AI.conclusion: Envelope-guided adaptive feature modulation effectively mitigates channel-induced distribution shifts, enhancing both identification and verification tasks in real-world fading environments. The theoretical monotonicity property enables reliable channel state estimation for adaptive modulation.

title: Auditing LLM-Governed Social Robots with Culture-Specific Moral Gradients
comment: Accepted for publication in Proceedings of the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '26)
summary: LLM-governed social robots increasingly decide who receives real-world assistance first. As prioritization norms vary across cultures by age, status, and group size, failure to calibrate pluralistically can scale into unequal access. Yet LLM moral audits remain English-centered, rarely test embodied contexts, leaving pluralistic calibration as an urgent diagnostic gap amid intensifying LLM-robot deployment. We introduce a gradient-based audit framework for multilingual evaluation of LLM moral trade-off behavior against cultural preference gradients. Grounded in nine cross-domain social robotics reviews (>8,000 papers), we derive symmetry-controlled scenarios across care, education, and services, translating the Moral Machine Experiment's "whom to spare" into "whom to assist first" dilemmas with preserved identity trade-offs (many vs. few; young vs. old; higher vs. lower status). We audit four LLMs across four country-language pairs in four prompting regimes (57,600 decisions), benchmarked against country-specific MME preference gradients. Ordinal concordance tests whether models differentiate cultural contexts; a governance typology maps vulnerabilities in gradient differentiation, directional tendency, and deliberation. We find persistent, culturally asymmetric gradient tracking failures that prompting alone cannot reliably correct: quality calibration is nearly twice as strong for Western-language decisions as for Chinese and Japanese; high determinism in majority-first trade-offs often erases cross-cultural gradients; partial sensitivity to age- and status-based norms risks sidelining minorities. Prompting effects are uneven; only contrastive exemplars yield consistent gains, while reasoning-only prompts can worsen tracking. Our results motivate multilingual, pluralistic audits as an LLM-robot pre-deployment gate and suggest model factors are a more robust lever than prompting alone.

AI.tldr: LLM-governed social robots show culturally asymmetric failures in prioritizing who gets assistance first, with Western-language decisions calibrated nearly twice as well as East Asian ones, and prompting alone cannot reliably fix these biases.
AI.motivation: As LLM-governed social robots are deployed to prioritize assistance in real-world settings, cultural norms about who gets helped first vary significantly. Existing moral audits are English-centered and rarely test embodied contexts, leaving a gap in understanding whether these systems handle multicultural moral trade-offs equitably.
AI.method: The authors developed a gradient-based audit framework for multilingual evaluation, deriving symmetry-controlled scenarios from nine social robotics reviews (>8,000 papers). They translated Moral Machine trade-offs (many vs. few, young vs. old, higher vs. lower status) into assistance dilemmas, audited 4 LLMs across 4 country-language pairs with 4 prompting regimes (57,600 decisions), and benchmarked against MME preference gradients using ordinal concordance tests and a governance typology.
AI.result: Culturally asymmetric gradient tracking failures were found: quality calibration nearly twice as strong for Western-language decisions (English, German) vs. Chinese/Japanese; high determinism in majority-first trade-offs erases cross-cultural gradients; partial sensitivity to age/status risks sidelining minorities; prompting effects are uneven—only contrastive exemplars yield consistent gains while reasoning-only prompts can worsen tracking.
AI.conclusion: Multilingual, pluralistic audits should be a pre-deployment gate for LLM-robot systems, and model architecture factors are a more robust lever than prompting alone to fix cross-cultural calibration failures.

title: Can AI Draw Science? A Benchmark for Evaluating Scientific Figure Generation by Text-to-Image and Multimodal Models
summary: Text-to-image and multimodal generative models are increasingly used to produce scientific figures such as mechanism diagrams, experimental-design schematics, conceptual frameworks, and graphical abstracts. Yet existing image-generation benchmarks (e.g., GenEval, T2I-CompBench, DPG-Bench) evaluate natural images and measure compositionality, object counting, or photorealism. None of them measure what makes a generated scientific figure usable: correct and legible text labels, faithful depiction of entities and their relations, coherent diagrammatic structure, and adherence to disciplinary drawing conventions. We introduce SciDraw-Bench, a benchmark of 32 structured scientific-figure generation tasks spanning eight figure types and ten disciplines, where each task pairs a natural-language prompt with a machine-checkable specification of required labels, relations, components, conventions, and negative constraints. We propose a four-dimensional evaluation protocol: Text Fidelity (OCR-based label recall and character error rate), Semantic Correctness (vision-language-model judging against the specification), Structural Quality, and Convention Adherence, together with a meta-evaluation protocol and a preliminary inter-judge reliability analysis (human-rating validation is ongoing). We evaluate a domain-specific system, SciDraw AI, against representative general-purpose text-to-image models, and outline a code-to-figure baseline as a planned extension. In a pilot over all eight figure types, the domain-specific system substantially outperforms the general-purpose baselines on every dimension and figure type, with the largest gaps on semantic correctness and convention adherence; text fidelity remains the hardest dimension for all systems.

AI.tldr: SciDraw-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating text-to-image generation of scientific figures across 8 figure types and 10 disciplines, using a four-dimensional evaluation protocol (Text Fidelity, Semantic Correctness, Structural Quality, Convention Adherence). A domain-specific system (SciDraw AI) substantially outperforms general-purpose models, especially on semantic correctness and convention adherence, though text fidelity remains challenging for all systems.
AI.motivation: Existing image-generation benchmarks evaluate natural images but fail to assess what makes scientific figures usable: correct text labels, faithful entity relations, coherent diagrammatic structure, and disciplinary drawing conventions. This gap motivates the need for a dedicated scientific figure generation benchmark.
AI.method: The paper introduces SciDraw-Bench with 32 structured tasks across 8 figure types and 10 disciplines, pairing natural-language prompts with machine-checkable specifications. A four-dimensional evaluation protocol is proposed: Text Fidelity (OCR-based), Semantic Correctness (VLM-based), Structural Quality, and Convention Adherence, plus meta-evaluation and inter-judge reliability analysis.
AI.result: In a pilot over all eight figure types, the domain-specific system (SciDraw AI) substantially outperforms general-purpose baselines on every dimension and figure type, with the largest gaps on semantic correctness and convention adherence. Text fidelity remains the hardest dimension for all systems.
AI.conclusion: Current general-purpose text-to-image models struggle to produce usable scientific figures. Domain-specific systems show clear advantages, but text fidelity remains a critical bottleneck. A code-to-figure baseline is planned as an extension.

title: GeoISF: Instance Semantic Forest Inspired Large-Scale Cross-View Geo-Localization via Ground LiDAR-to-Satellite Image
summary: The problem of localization on a large-scale satellite image given a frame of query ground view point clouds remains challenging. Existing LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization methods struggle in large-scale scenarios due to limited semantic alignment and the modality gap between point clouds and satellite images. This paper introduces the large-scale LiDAR-to-image geo-localization pipeline called GeoISF. GeoISF introduces an instance semantic forest constructed using WordNet, which enhances temporal semantic representation and discriminative power by integrating semantic trees from multiple frames. By leveraging environmental semantic representation as a shared medium, GeoISF effectively bridges the modality gap and improves semantic matching accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of GeoISF in large-scale cross-view localization, 13.22 times better than the parallel LiDAR-to-image method in the R@10 metric on the KITTI dataset. The proposed method addresses the existing gap in large-scale LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization, offering a robust solution to the computational and accuracy challenges inherent in such scenarios. We will release the code as an open-source resource available online for the broader research community.

AI.tldr: GeoISF is a LiDAR-to-satellite-image geo-localization pipeline that uses an instance semantic forest built from WordNet to bridge the modality gap between point clouds and satellite images, achieving 13.22x improvement in R@10 on KITTI dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization methods struggle in large-scale scenarios due to limited semantic alignment and the modality gap between point clouds and satellite images.
AI.method: GeoISF introduces an instance semantic forest constructed using WordNet, which enhances temporal semantic representation and discriminative power by integrating semantic trees from multiple frames. It uses environmental semantic representation as a shared medium to bridge the modality gap.
AI.result: Achieves 13.22 times better performance than the parallel LiDAR-to-image method in the R@10 metric on the KITTI dataset.
AI.conclusion: GeoISF addresses the existing gap in large-scale LiDAR-to-image cross-view localization, offering a robust solution to computational and accuracy challenges. Code will be released as open-source.

title: Recursive Self-Evolving Agents via Held-Out Selection
summary:
LLM agents are increasingly improved without weight updates by evolving a natural-language artifact, such as reflections, workflows, playbooks, cheatsheets, or optimized prompts, that conditions a frozen policy. Such methods are typically reported as wins on the single benchmark where they help. We study them apples-to-apples and surface a sharper picture. We introduce RSEA, a Recursive Self-Evolving Agent that carries a compact three-layer natural-language state: an imperative strategy, reusable skills, and a procedural playbook. Across generations, RSEA rewrites all three layers from its own trajectories and commits a candidate only if it does not regress on a disjoint held-out split, using a strict keep-better gate.
  Across four diverse benchmarks, ALFWorld, GAIA, (τ)-bench, and WebShop, and six faithful baselines, ReAct, Reflexion, GEPA, AWM, ACE, and Dynamic Cheatsheet, all evaluated on one shared local backbone, we find three main results. First, no artifact universally wins. RSEA is the strongest single-pass method on ALFWorld, reaching 69.3% compared with 64.6% for ReAct (McNemar (p=0.015)), and reaches 79.4% with retry, the best overall result. However, concrete-workflow induction, represented by AWM, is best on the strong-backbone tool-use tasks. Second, unguarded context evolution is high-variance and unsafe. Dynamic Cheatsheet, which curates context online without a held-out gate, is near-best on ALFWorld at 70.7%, yet collapses on WebShop, with a score of 0.14 compared with 0.43 for ReAct. Third, RSEA's strict held-out selection is what makes recursive self-evolution monotone-safe: it never significantly underperforms the base agent on any benchmark and falls back to vanilla ReAct when evolved context would hurt.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RSEA, a recursive self-evolving agent that improves LLM performance across benchmarks through a three-layer natural-language state with strict keep-better gating. Results show no universal best method, unguarded evolution can collapse, and RSEA's held-out selection enables safe monotonic improvement.
AI.motivation: Current methods for evolving LLM agents via natural-language artifacts (reflections, workflows, etc.) are typically reported as wins on isolated benchmarks without fair cross-method comparison, leading to fragmented understanding of what truly works.
AI.method: RSEA carries a three-layer natural-language state (strategy, skills, playbook), recursively rewrites all three layers from its own trajectories across generations, and commits a candidate only if it does not regress on a held-out split using a strict keep-better gate.
AI.result: RSEA achieves 69.3% on ALFWorld (vs 64.6% ReAct, p=0.015) and 79.4% with retry (best overall). Unguarded evolution (Dynamic Cheatsheet) collapses on WebShop (0.14 vs 0.43 ReAct). No artifact universally wins across all four benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: RSEA's strict held-out selection makes recursive self-evolution monotone-safe, never significantly underperforming the base agent, while unguarded context evolution is high-variance and can be unsafe.

title: A Zero-Shot Deep Image Prior Framework for Denoising and Deconvolution in Fluorescence Microscopy
summary: Fluorescence microscopy images are degraded by noise and diffraction-induced blur, which compromise structural fidelity and limit quantitative analysis. Supervised deep learning methods achieve impressive restoration performance but require large-scale paired datasets that are difficult to obtain in practice. To address this issue, we propose SDIP, a zero-shot deep image prior (DIP) framework that sequentially performs denoising and deconvolution without external training data. An aSeqDIP-based module first suppresses noise while preserving fine structures through sequential autoencoding regularization. In the deconvolution stage, a wavelet-based background correction step is incorporated before the proposed RLG-DIP module performs artifact-reduced deconvolution. RLG-DIP uses the Richardson-Lucy deconvolution result as a physically consistent guidance prior, integrating the imaging model with the implicit prior of DIP to stabilize the ill-posed deconvolution process. Experiments on the BioSR dataset across multiple cellular structures demonstrate that SDIP improves both signal-to-noise ratio and resolution, achieving superior visual quality and improved quantitative performance on most evaluated structures. The proposed framework may also provide useful insights for designing physically guided DIP methods for other inverse problems.

AI.tldr: SDIP is a zero-shot deep image prior framework that performs sequential denoising and deconvolution on fluorescence microscopy images without paired training data, using an aSeqDIP module for noise suppression and an RLG-DIP module guided by Richardson-Lucy deconvolution for artifact-reduced restoration.
AI.motivation: Fluorescence microscopy images suffer from noise and blur, and supervised deep learning methods require large paired datasets that are impractical to obtain. SDIP aims to achieve high-quality restoration without external training data.
AI.method: SDIP consists of two stages: (1) an aSeqDIP module with sequential autoencoding regularization for noise suppression, and (2) an RLG-DIP module that incorporates wavelet-based background correction and uses Richardson-Lucy deconvolution as a physically consistent guidance prior for artifact-reduced deconvolution.
AI.result: Experiments on the BioSR dataset across multiple cellular structures show SDIP improves both signal-to-noise ratio and resolution, achieving superior visual quality and better quantitative performance on most evaluated structures.
AI.conclusion: SDIP provides an effective zero-shot restoration framework for fluorescence microscopy and offers insights for designing physically guided DIP methods for other inverse problems.

title: Convertible Codes: MSR-to-MSR Conversion with Optimal Access and Bandwidth
summary:
In this paper, we study convertible codes in the merge regime and focus on the minimum storage regenerating (MSR) setting, where both the initial codes and the final code admit optimal single-node repair. We propose explicit MSR-to-MSR conversion schemes and analyze their performance in terms of access cost and conversion bandwidth.
  We first construct convertible MSR codes in the irregular setting, where the $m$ initial codes may have different parameters, achieving optimal access cost. We further consider the practically important same-code setting, where all initial codewords are drawn from the same MSR code. By introducing a row-matching technique, we obtain constructions simultaneously achieving optimal access cost and conversion bandwidth in most parameter regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes explicit MSR-to-MSR conversion schemes for convertible codes in the merge regime, achieving optimal access cost and conversion bandwidth in both irregular and same-code settings.
AI.motivation: To study convertible codes in the merge regime within the MSR setting, where both initial and final codes must support optimal single-node repair, aiming to minimize access cost and conversion bandwidth.
AI.method: Proposes explicit MSR-to-MSR conversion schemes. In the irregular setting, constructs codes with optimal access cost. In the same-code setting, introduces a row-matching technique to achieve optimal access cost and conversion bandwidth simultaneously.
AI.result: Achieves optimal access cost in the irregular setting, and simultaneously optimal access cost and conversion bandwidth in most parameter regimes of the same-code setting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed constructions demonstrate efficient convertible MSR codes for the merge regime, with strong optimality guarantees in both irregular and same-code settings.

title: A Survey of Physical-layer Authentication Enhanced by Emerging Spatial Domain Technologies
summary: This article surveys spatial-domain-enhanced Physical-layer Authentication (PLA), with Dual-polarized Antennas (DPA), Massive Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO), and Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) as the primary focus. With the rapid growth of wireless deployments, authentication mechanisms face stringent requirements for high security, low overhead, and low latency. PLA offers lightweight identity verification by exploiting physical-layer characteristics. However, the effectiveness of PLA critically depends on how physical observations are constructed and validated under wireless channels. Unlike existing surveys that mainly organize PLA by authentication modality, feature source, and evaluation metrics, this work emphasizes the connection between spatial-domain enhancement mechanisms, the resulting feature representation, and the authentication procedure. We review how DPA, Massive MIMO, and RIS reshape PLA feature representation, and we summarize newly introduced security threats along with representative defense strategies. Case studies further illustrate the practical impact, such as representative detection-probability trends across Signal-to-Noise Ratio regimes and quantitative comparisons among representative schemes. Finally, we outline promising future opportunities enabled by Dynamic Metasurface Antennas, Extra-large MIMO, and spatial configuration with artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: This survey reviews spatial-domain-enhanced Physical-layer Authentication using DPA, Massive MIMO, and RIS, focusing on how these technologies reshape feature representation and authentication procedures, while also discussing new security threats, case studies, and future opportunities.
AI.motivation: Existing surveys mainly organize PLA by authentication modality, feature source, and evaluation metrics, lacking a focus on the connection between spatial-domain enhancement mechanisms, feature representation, and authentication procedures. This work fills that gap.
AI.method: The paper surveys how DPA, Massive MIMO, and RIS reshape PLA feature representation, summarizes newly introduced security threats and defense strategies, and provides case studies with detection-probability trends and quantitative comparisons.
AI.result: Case studies show representative detection-probability trends across SNR regimes and quantitative comparisons among representative PLA schemes using spatial-domain enhancements.
AI.conclusion: Promising future directions include Dynamic Metasurface Antennas, Extra-large MIMO, and spatial configuration integrated with AI.

title: A Query-Driven Communication-Efficient Digital Twins Design for Autonomous Driving
summary: Digital twins (DTs) have become a potential technology to perform risk-free simulation of physical entities for deterministic and high-reliability services in diverse scenarios such as autonomous driving and low-altitude economy. In the autonomous driving scenario, traditional DT methods that rely solely on vehicle's real-time state synchronization, however, might lead to unacceptable computing and communication consumption for construction of high-fidelity DT with redundant data. To address this issue, we first propose a query-driven DT architecture to enable the DT to actively request the desired environment data from vehicles based on its simulation result. Then, we formulate an optimization problem whose goal is to minimize autonomous driving position error while accounting for DT fidelity and communication constraints. We also design a cross-time-step progressive query mechanism to further improve communication efficiency. The simulation results show that our proposed method achieves a 24% reduction in planning position error compared to traditional methods, while reducing communication overhead by 40%.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a query-driven digital twin architecture that actively requests environment data from vehicles based on simulation needs, achieving 24% lower position error and 40% less communication overhead compared to traditional methods in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Traditional DT methods rely on continuous real-time state synchronization, causing excessive computing and communication costs due to redundant data transmission for high-fidelity DT construction in autonomous driving.
AI.method: 1) Proposes a query-driven DT architecture where the DT actively requests environment data from vehicles based on simulation needs. 2) Formulates an optimization problem minimizing position error under DT fidelity and communication constraints. 3) Designs a cross-time-step progressive query mechanism to improve communication efficiency.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a 24% reduction in planning position error and a 40% reduction in communication overhead compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The query-driven approach significantly improves both accuracy and communication efficiency for DT-based autonomous driving systems.

title: On the Necessity of a Liquid Substrate for Mesh Intelligence
summary: A mesh of sovereign agents has no center: no shared clock, no shared model, and no coordinator to gather data or retrain. Its competence rests on each agent folding the projections its peers emit into a single internal state, online, from observations that arrive at irregular, unscheduled times, on a substrate whose weights it cannot retrain. Any one of these constraints is tractable on its own; folding optimally under all three at once is not. We ask what such a substrate must be, and prove two necessary conditions from one model of a self-evolving latent observed at irregular, exogenous times. Because the latent changes, its optimal estimator is time-varying: an adaptive timescale is necessary, and every fixed-gain filter is strictly suboptimal. And because arrivals are clock-free, the optimal estimate depends on the elapsed gap between them, which no gap-blind network recovers at any width or depth. This second condition is capacity-independent: scale cannot substitute for the missing dependence. The two conditions intersect in the continuous-time liquid class. An LSTM satisfies the first, a fixed continuous-time filter the second, and a multi-timescale liquid network both. Synthetic experiments confirm each: the network attains the timescale, and the separation is computed exactly. The characterization is necessary, not sufficient, and binds fixed-weight substrates: a network free to retrain reaches the class by other means. Proved per agent, the necessity binds every agent of a mesh, a structural condition on mesh intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper proves two necessary conditions for optimal estimation in a mesh of sovereign agents with no shared clock, model, or coordinator: agents must have (1) an adaptive timescale (fixed-gain filters are suboptimal) and (2) dependence on elapsed inter-arrival gaps (gap-blind networks fail at any scale). These conditions converge in continuous-time liquid networks, which satisfy both.
AI.motivation: A mesh of sovereign agents operates without a central coordinator, shared clock, or shared model—each agent must fold asynchronous, irregular observations from peers into a single internal state on a fixed-weight substrate. While each constraint is individually tractable, optimal inference under all three simultaneously is an unsolved problem.
AI.method: The authors model a self-evolving latent process observed at irregular, exogenous times. They formally derive necessary conditions for optimal estimation under these constraints, then test them with synthetic experiments comparing LSTMs, fixed continuous-time filters, and multi-timescale liquid networks.
AI.result: Fixed-gain filters are strictly suboptimal; adaptive timescales are necessary. Gap-blind networks (e.g., LSTMs) cannot recover the optimal estimate regardless of width or depth—this is a capacity-independent limitation. Multi-timescale liquid networks satisfy both conditions and achieve the correct timescale separation.
AI.conclusion: Continuous-time liquid networks are a necessary architectural class for optimal inference in decentralized, clock-free agent meshes. The conditions are necessary (not sufficient) and bind fixed-weight substrates specifically. These results impose a structural condition on mesh intelligence.

title: Memory-Augmented LSTM Autoencoder for Unsupervised Activity Recognition with IMU Sensor Fusion
summary: HAR using Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) sensors is vital for healthcare monitoring and rehabilitation. Despite deep learning advancements, major challenges remain: reliance on labeled data, multi-sensor fusion complexity, and the limited ability of unsupervised methods to capture spatiotemporal dependencies. These issues are pronounced in real-world scenarios with noisy data, overlapping activities, and missing labels. We propose a fully unsupervised spatiotemporal feature fusion framework using a memory-augmented autoencoder. It enhances activity representations via short temporal windows of multi-sensor IMU data, enabling real-time applications. Our framework extracts hierarchical static features via a Stacked Autoencoder, fusing them within and across sensors. A sequence-to-sequence LSTM Autoencoder then temporally refines these features, incorporating historical motion patterns without labels. We analyze key hyperparameters to identify configurations that maximize feature separability under short-window constraints. Evaluated on DaLiAc and PAMAP2 using realistic inter-class window segmentation, our method achieves 96.6% and 98.4% accuracy, respectively, surpassing supervised baselines and unsupervised approaches. Our method improves feature separability by up to 9% despite shorter temporal windows. While our realistic inter-class segmentation reduces accuracy by ~7%, it was intentionally adopted to better reflect real-world activity transitions and practical relevance.

AI.tldr: A fully unsupervised spatiotemporal feature fusion framework using a memory-augmented autoencoder achieves 96.6% and 98.4% accuracy on DaLiAc and PAMAP2 for HAR, surpassing supervised baselines by combining stacked autoencoders, LSTM autoencoders, and inter-class window segmentation.
AI.motivation: To address three key challenges in HAR: reliance on labeled data, multi-sensor fusion complexity, and limited ability of unsupervised methods to capture spatiotemporal dependencies in real-world noisy environments.
AI.method: A memory-augmented autoencoder framework that: (1) extracts hierarchical static features via Stacked Autoencoder, (2) fuses features within/across sensors, (3) employs sequence-to-sequence LSTM Autoencoder for temporal refinement, all without labels.
AI.result: 96.6% accuracy on DaLiAc, 98.4% on PAMAP2; up to 9% improvement in feature separability; realistic inter-class segmentation reduces accuracy ~7% but better reflects real-world transitions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unsupervised framework not only outperforms supervised baselines but also demonstrates practical viability for real-world HAR applications with noisy data and missing labels through realistic segmentation.

title: Data and Evaluation Closed-Loop for Model Capability Enhancement
summary: Model capability is the central variable in LLM pre-training, yet is never observed directly: data shapes it prospectively, while evaluation reveals it only retrospectively, compressing samples, prompts, decoding, and scoring rules into one noisy score. Practical optimization runs this backward: a failure is observed first, and the engineer must infer the corpus fix. The two sides speak incompatible vocabularies -- benchmark names and per-sample correctness versus data sources, domains, and quality labels -- so this inference is usually intuition, not method. We close this gap with the \emph{capability slice}: a group of evaluation samples sharing background condition, task type, solving operation, and output constraint -- precise enough to localize a single weakness yet stable enough to survive aggregation, unlike a benchmark name, too coarse, or a single sample, too noisy. Built around this unit, an evaluation taxonomy, a non-instruction data taxonomy, and mapping rules form a closed loop turning a benchmark-level failure into a targeted, testable data intervention. We test this loop on two case studies pulling in opposite directions. First, the loop rules the data out: continued pre-training drives BBH down by $-46.82\%$, but diagnosis traces this to a single masked \texttt{\textless EOS\textgreater} loss rather than weakened reasoning; restoring it recovers BBH to $66.44$, above the original checkpoint, without changing the data. Second, the loop rules the data in: a persistent math-reasoning weakness is decomposed by solving operation into specific failing combinations, and a weakness-targeted sampling procedure built from it lifts AIME2025/AIME2026 Pass@128 from $6.67$/$0.00$ to $26.67$ each. The same unmodified loop reaches opposite, correct verdicts in both cases, showing the evaluation-to-data inference can be routine, auditable, and experimentally validated rather than intuitive.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the "capability slice"—a fine-grained evaluation unit that bridges the gap between observed LLM failures and data interventions—enabling routine, auditable diagnosis and targeted data fixes without relying on intuition.
AI.motivation: LLM pre-training suffers from a fundamental mismatch: evaluation reveals failures as benchmark scores or per-sample errors, but data interventions must be specified in terms of sources, domains, and quality labels. Engineers rely on intuition to bridge this gap; the paper aims to replace that intuition with a systematic, closed-loop methodology.
AI.method: The paper proposes the "capability slice"—an evaluation sample group defined by four dimensions (background condition, task type, solving operation, output constraint) that is precise enough to isolate a weakness yet stable enough for aggregation. Around this unit, an evaluation taxonomy, a non-instruction data taxonomy, and mapping rules form a closed loop from benchmark failure to targeted data intervention.
AI.result: Two case studies: (1) A BBH performance drop (-46.82%) was traced not to weakened reasoning but to a masked <EOS> loss; restoring it recovered BBH to 66.44, above the original checkpoint. (2) A persistent math-reasoning weakness was decomposed by solving operation, and a targeted sampling procedure lifted AIME2025/AIME2026 Pass@128 from 6.67/0.00 to 26.67 each.
AI.conclusion: The same unmodified loop correctly reached opposite verdicts in both cases, demonstrating that evaluation-to-data inference can be routine, auditable, and experimentally validated rather than intuitive.

title: Measured-Subspace Consistency: A Plug-and-Play Operator for Diffusion Posterior Sampling in Accelerated MRI Reconstruction
summary: Diffusion posterior samplers for accelerated MRI can reconstruct accurately yet still disagree on the acquired k-space across samples, placing posterior variability on coefficients the scanner has already measured. We identify this measured-subspace leakage as a physical-admissibility failure. Under a hard-constraint model it violates the measurement constraint and inflates the reported uncertainty with disagreement about coefficients the scanner has already determined. To quantify this leakage, we introduce complementary measured- and unmeasured-subspace k-space dispersion metrics (MSD/USD). We then present Measured-Subspace Consistency (MSC), a training-free terminal correction that wraps any compatible image-space posterior sampler with a standard multi-coil consistency lock. The ideal lock follows classical range/null-space data consistency. Our contribution is to repurpose it as a black-box posterior audit and correction rather than a new reconstructor or learned sampler. Theoretically, we prove that the ideal transform confines pairwise sample differences to the MRI null space and bound the residual cross-subspace coupling left by practical sensitivity-weighted implementations. Across six base samplers and two MRI anatomies, including out-of-distribution transfer where a knee prior reconstructs brain, MSC substantially reduces measured-subspace dispersion for Soft samplers (a median 16.5x reduction for DPS across five brain contrasts, up to ~29x), while preserving unmeasured-subspace diversity and acting as a near-identity map for Consistent ones. Furthermore, MSC maintains or modestly improves PSNR/SSIM, with no retraining, retuning, or significant computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies "measured-subspace leakage" in diffusion posterior samplers for accelerated MRI, where posterior variability incorrectly affects already-measured k-space coefficients. It introduces MSD/USD metrics to quantify this and proposes MSC—a training-free terminal correction that enforces measurement consistency, achieving substantial leakage reduction while preserving diversity and image quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion posterior samplers for accelerated MRI produce reconstructions that disagree on acquired k-space data, which violates physical admissibility and inflates uncertainty. The authors aim to quantify and correct this "measured-subspace leakage."
AI.method: The authors introduce complementary measured- and unmeasured-subspace k-space dispersion metrics (MSD/USD) to quantify leakage, and propose Measured-Subspace Consistency (MSC)—a training-free terminal correction that applies a classical range/null-space data consistency lock to any compatible image-space posterior sampler.
AI.result: Across six base samplers and two MRI anatomies (including out-of-distribution transfer), MSC substantially reduces measured-subspace dispersion (e.g., 16.5x median reduction for DPS, up to ~29x), preserves unmeasured-subspace diversity, and maintains or modestly improves PSNR/SSIM without retraining or significant overhead.
AI.conclusion: MSC effectively audits and corrects measured-subspace leakage in diffusion posterior samplers, improving physical admissibility and uncertainty quantification without requiring new training or tuning.

title: Brownian Bridge Diffusion-Based Joint Channel Estimation and Data Detection for Jamming-Resilient Receivers
summary: In next-generation wireless networks, the growing density of devices and limited spectrum resources pose severe jamming challenges to fragile legitimate communication links in the wireless electromagnetic environment. Crucially, when jamming overlaps with pilot and data symbols in both time and frequency domains, it inflicts a severe bottleneck on receiver-side joint estimation and detection. Existing schemes often lack an effective framework to combat such jamming contamination, thereby failing to guarantee reliable transmission. To address this issue, we propose a Brownian bridge diffusion-based joint channel estimation and data detection framework (BBD-JCED) for jamming-resilient receivers. Specifically, the proposed framework comprises two core modules: the first extracts jamming features in the short-time Fourier transform (STFT) domain and suppresses jamming samples, thereby improving the signal-to-jamming-plus-noise ratio (SJNR) of the received signal; the second introduces a Brownian bridge diffusion (BBD) process to model the evolution of the suppressed signal and the encoded bits in the presence of channel estimation errors, thereby enabling enhanced joint channel estimation and data detection. To alleviate the computational burden of the BBD process in the second module, we further derive a fast ordinary differential equation (ODE) solver that enables its low-complexity iterative evolution. Finally, we design a multi-module training algorithm to improve the data recovery capability of the proposed framework. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior bit recovery performance compared with baseline schemes while maintaining a lower number of model parameters and competitive computational complexity.

AI.tldr: Proposes BBD-JCED, a Brownian bridge diffusion-based framework for joint channel estimation and data detection that is resilient to jamming in wireless networks.
AI.motivation: Existing schemes lack an effective framework to combat jamming contamination that overlaps with pilot and data symbols, failing to guarantee reliable transmission in dense wireless environments with limited spectrum.
AI.method: Two-module framework: (1) STFT-based jamming feature extraction and suppression to improve SJNR; (2) Brownian bridge diffusion process modeling suppressed signal and encoded bits under channel estimation errors, with a fast ODE solver for low-complexity iterative evolution and a multi-module training algorithm.
AI.result: Superior bit recovery performance compared to baseline schemes, with fewer model parameters and competitive computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: The BBD-JCED framework effectively addresses jamming contamination for joint estimation and detection, achieving reliable transmission with improved efficiency.

title: Physics Equivariance for Robust Generalization in Wireless Foundation Model
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Wireless foundation models (WFMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for learning multiple channel state information (CSI) acquisition tasks. However, unlike natural language tokens governed by statistical co-occurrence, wireless channels are generated by electromagnetic propagation laws, and current WFM training is constrained by limited data scale, narrow distribution coverage dominated by simulations, and a pronounced sim-to-real gap. As a result, simply scaling model parameters and CSI samples does not necessarily yield robust and generalizable models. In this paper, we advocate enabling physics equivariance as a principled and explainable inductive bias for WFMs. Specifically, we focus on a universal propagation property for electromagnetic waves, termed wave equivariance: when the input CSI is modulated along time-frequency-space dimensions, the output channel response should exhibit the corresponding transformation. Empirical studies show that the vanilla-WFM fails to reliably acquire such equivariance even with a large number of model parameters and training samples. To address this, we design the physics-intrinsic WFM (phys-WFM) with wave equivariance, which explicitly aligns model behaviors with an interpretable wave propagation structure. Results demonstrate that the proposed design effectively captures wave equivariance and substantially improves robustness and generalization to unseen environments under distribution shift, offering a physics-grounded and testable route toward explainable wireless foundation models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes physics-intrinsic wireless foundation models (phys-WFM) that incorporate wave equivariance—a universal electromagnetic propagation property—as an inductive bias. This approach improves robustness and generalization over vanilla WFMs, especially under distribution shifts and sim-to-real gaps.
AI.motivation: Current wireless foundation models (WFMs) trained on limited, simulated CSI data suffer from distribution coverage issues and a sim-to-real gap. Simply scaling model size and data does not guarantee robust or generalizable performance.
AI.method: The authors design phys-WFM by embedding wave equivariance—a universal electromagnetic wave propagation property—as a physics-grounded inductive bias. When input CSI is modulated along time-frequency-space dimensions, the output channel response is forced to transform accordingly, aligning model behavior with wave propagation laws.
AI.result: Empirical studies show vanilla WFMs fail to acquire wave equivariance even with large model and data scale. phys-WFM captures wave equivariance effectively, significantly improving robustness and generalization in unseen environments under distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing physics equivariance offers a principled, explainable, and testable path toward more robust and generalizable wireless foundation models, bridging the sim-to-real gap.

title: RoboGaze: Evaluating Robot World Models via Structured Vision-Language Analysis
comment: First version 29 pages, 7 figures. Project webpage: https://robogaze-eval.github.io/
summary: Recent advances in robot world models enable synthetic video generation for embodied prediction and planning. However, evaluating these videos is challenging: visually realistic outputs often violate physical laws, temporal consistency, or task logic, while conventional metrics and monolithic Vision-Language Model (VLM) judges fail to generalize or provide precise diagnostic value. We present RoboGaze, a training-free, multi-agent VLM framework that provides structured, interpretable evaluation for generated robot-manipulation videos. Given a task instruction and video, RoboGaze operates via a three-stage pipeline: task-scene grounding, dimension-specific specialist routing, and critic-based verification. It outputs temporally localized glitch reports categorized under a novel 6-dimension, 30-type robotics-specific taxonomy. To benchmark RoboGaze, we introduce a human-validated dataset of 382 clips spanning simulated and real-world multi-view manipulation. Evaluating eight open-source and proprietary VLM backbones, RoboGaze dramatically outperforms zero-shot baselines, improving description-F1 by up to +43 points and temporal alignment (F1 x IoU) by up to +37 points, closing approximately 85% of the gap to the human ceiling. Furthermore, its critic verifier mitigates the "cry-wolf" false-positive flaw of standard VLMs, lifting clean-clip accuracy from under 25% to over 80%. RoboGaze offers a scalable, highly interpretable diagnostic tool for the rigorous evaluation of robot world models.

AI.tldr: RoboGaze is a training-free multi-agent VLM framework that evaluates robot manipulation videos using a structured 3-stage pipeline (task-scene grounding, dimension-specific specialist routing, critic-based verification), outputting glitch reports under a 6-dimension, 30-type taxonomy. It outperforms zero-shot baselines by up to +43 points F1 and +37 points temporal alignment, reducing false positives and closing ~85% of the gap to human performance.
AI.motivation: Evaluating generated robot manipulation videos is difficult because visually realistic outputs often violate physical laws, temporal consistency, or task logic, while conventional metrics and monolithic VLM judges fail to generalize or provide precise diagnostic value.
AI.method: RoboGaze uses a training-free multi-agent VLM framework with a three-stage pipeline: (1) task-scene grounding, (2) dimension-specific specialist routing, (3) critic-based verification. It outputs temporally localized glitch reports under a novel 6-dimension, 30-type robotics-specific taxonomy.
AI.result: On a human-validated dataset of 382 clips spanning simulated and real-world multi-view manipulation, RoboGaze improves description-F1 by up to +43 points and temporal alignment (F1 x IoU) by up to +37 points, closing ~85% of the gap to the human ceiling. Its critic verifier lifts clean-clip accuracy from under 25% to over 80%.
AI.conclusion: RoboGaze offers a scalable, highly interpretable diagnostic tool for the rigorous evaluation of robot world models, effectively addressing false-positive issues and providing structured feedback.

title: Position: RL Researchers Need to Distinguish Between Solving Simulators and Using Simulators as a Proxy
comment: This work has been accepted at the ICML 2026 position paper track. The peer reviewed reference is provided in the public OpenReview page at https://openreview.net/forum?id=KIobEIpCkp Additionally the publication can be seen at this link : https://icml.cc/virtual/2026/poster/67184
summary: One goal in reinforcement learning (RL) research is to understand general-purpose sequential decision-making, using benchmark simulators as a proxy for learning in deployment settings. When running experiments, however, the goal of achieving high performance in the simulator can mutate into focusing exclusively on solving the simulator. To achieve high scores, researchers may adopt solutions exclusively meant for solving simulators, rather than learning while the agent is deployed outside a simulator. Solving simulators is also worthy of investigation, but it is a fundamentally different RL research question. In this paper, we argue that RL researchers need to distinguish between two use cases of simulators: solving simulators and using simulators as a proxy for learning in deployment. We first discuss how these two use-cases are importantly different, in terms of constraints on how the agent can use the simulator, which algorithms are appropriate, and which evaluation metrics are appropriate. We then highlight several issues and misleading conclusions that can occur by not making the distinction between these two settings clear, supported with examples and simple experiments. This work is a call to the community to begin clearly distinguishing how they are using simulators in their work, hopefully sparking further discussion on which empirical practices work best in each setting.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that RL researchers should clearly distinguish between two use cases of simulators: solving the simulator (optimizing for high scores in that specific environment) vs. using simulators as a proxy for real-world deployment (learning generalizable sequential decision-making). The authors discuss how these settings differ in constraints, algorithms, and evaluation, and show how conflating them leads to misleading conclusions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address a fundamental confusion in RL research: the goal of achieving high performance in benchmark simulators can unintentionally shift from understanding general-purpose decision-making to merely "solving" the simulator itself. The authors want to clarify that these are two distinct research questions requiring different empirical practices.
AI.method: The authors provide a conceptual discussion distinguishing the two use cases (solving simulators vs. proxy for deployment), analyze the differences in constraints, appropriate algorithms, and evaluation metrics, and support their arguments with examples and simple experiments demonstrating issues that arise from conflating the two settings.
AI.result: The paper identifies several misleading conclusions that can occur when the distinction is not made clear, supported by empirical examples. The key result is a call to action for the community to explicitly clarify which simulator use case they are addressing in their work.
AI.conclusion: RL researchers should begin clearly distinguishing how they use simulators in their work. Both "solving simulators" and "simulators as proxies for deployment" are valid research directions, but they are fundamentally different problems requiring different constraints, algorithms, and evaluation metrics. The community should adopt clearer empirical practices for each setting.

title: Zero-Label Driving Scenario Complexity Detection via Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
summary: Identifying complex and safety-critical driving scenarios in large unlabelled datasets is an important but expensive problem. Existing approaches rely on human annotators, supervised classifiers, or carefully engineered rule sets, all of which require substantial prior knowledge about what constitutes a difficult scenario. We ask whether a model can discover scenario complexity on its own, with no labels at any stage. We train a minimal Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) on structured agent state data from the nuPlan mini dataset and use the temporal prediction error as a zero-shot complexity score. Without access to any ground-truth labels during training or evaluation setup, the model assigns significantly higher scores to scenarios involving unprotected turns, crosswalk interactions, and pedestrian proximity, and significantly lower scores to lane-following and stationary-traffic scenarios. We validate this finding through four ablation experiments that isolate the source of the signal, and through a downstream anomaly detection evaluation that achieves Average Precision of 0.512 against a 0.436 chance baseline. The results show that temporal prediction error in a self-supervised latent world model is a practical proxy for driving scenario complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using a self-supervised Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) trained on unlabeled driving data, where temporal prediction error serves as a zero-shot complexity score to identify safety-critical driving scenarios without any labels.
AI.motivation: Identifying complex and safety-critical driving scenarios in large unlabelled datasets is expensive and requires substantial prior knowledge (human annotators, supervised classifiers, or rule sets).
AI.method: Train a minimal JEPA on structured agent state data from nuPlan mini dataset; use temporal prediction error as a zero-shot complexity score without any labels.
AI.result: Higher scores for unprotected turns, crosswalk interactions, pedestrian proximity; lower scores for lane-following and stationary traffic. Anomaly detection achieves AP of 0.512 vs 0.436 baseline.
AI.conclusion: Temporal prediction error in a self-supervised latent world model is a practical proxy for driving scenario complexity.

title: GPTNT: Benchmarking Real-Time Collaboration Between Multimodal Agents on Keep Talking And Nobody Explodes
comment: Project website and code at https://gptnt.github.io
summary: Multimodal models are increasingly deployed to solve tasks collaboratively with humans or other artificial agents. Existing benchmarks show that these models possess many of the required component capabilities, but the conditions that coincide in collaboration, including time pressure, information asymmetry, and imperfect communication, are usually studied in isolation. We introduce GPTNT, a benchmark built on the cooperative video game Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes, in which two agents must coordinate to defuse procedurally generated bomb puzzles against a live countdown. One agent can see and manipulate the bomb but does not have the defusal instructions; the other has the instructions but cannot see or manipulate the bomb. Neither agent can succeed alone: success requires effective and efficient communication. Unlike turn-based proxies, GPTNT requires agents to act asynchronously and communicate in real time. GPTNT is designed to separate collaboration from reliance on memorized solutions: the instruction manual, the partner, or both can be withheld to isolate what a model derives in the moment from what it already knows. We show that GPTNT poses a substantial challenge for state-of-the-art systems: none of the closed- or open-source models we test defuses a single bomb in real time, a bar that human players clear. Through controlled experiments, we identify critical weaknesses in state tracking, efficient action under time pressure, ambiguity handling, and error recovery. We release GPTNT as a benchmark for collaborative performance that current evaluations leave unmeasured. Because it runs on the real game, GPTNT benefits from procedural generation and inherits a living modding community, allowing the benchmark to evolve as models improve rather than being solved once and retired.

AI.tldr: GPTNT is a new benchmark built on the cooperative game Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes that tests multimodal models' ability to collaborate in real-time under time pressure, information asymmetry, and imperfect communication. Current state-of-the-art models fail to defuse a single bomb, while humans succeed, revealing critical weaknesses in state tracking, time-pressured action, ambiguity handling, and error recovery.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks study collaboration components (time pressure, information asymmetry, imperfect communication) in isolation. Real-world collaboration requires all these conditions simultaneously, but no benchmark currently captures this combined challenge for multimodal models.
AI.method: GPTNT uses the video game Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes, where two agents must coordinate: one sees and manipulates the bomb but lacks defusal instructions; the other has instructions but cannot see the bomb. The benchmark requires real-time, asynchronous communication under a live countdown, and can isolate collaboration from memorized solutions by withholding the manual, the partner, or both.
AI.result: No closed- or open-source model tested can defuse a single bomb in real time, a bar that human players clear. Controlled experiments identify critical weaknesses in state tracking, efficient action under time pressure, ambiguity handling, and error recovery.
AI.conclusion: GPTNT reveals a significant gap between current multimodal models and human collaborative performance. The benchmark is designed to evolve with the modding community, preventing it from being solved once and retired.

title: DeVAR: Low-Dose CT Denoising via Visual Autoregressive Modeling
summary: Computed tomography (CT) plays a crucial role in medical diagnosis, but minimizing radiation exposure while maintaining image quality remains a critical challenge. Low-dose CT (LDCT) protocols reduce radiation risks but inevitably suffer from severe noise and artifacts that compromise diagnostic accuracy. While existing deep learning methods have achieved promising results, there remains a continuous quest for generative paradigms that intrinsically capture global-to-local structural dependencies to better preserve fine anatomical details. To this end, we propose DeVAR, a novel generative framework that applies visual autoregressive modeling (VAR) to LDCT denoising for the first time. Conditioned on global context provided by LDCT prefix tokens, DeVAR progressively generates discrete token maps of the target normal-dose CT (NDCT) via next-scale prediction. Because quantization inherently discards high-frequency information, we introduce a residual refiner to capture subtle anatomical structures beyond the capacity of a discrete codebook. Finally, empowered by a dual-representation hybrid training strategy, our hybrid NDCT decoder seamlessly integrates continuous and discrete latents to reconstruct high-fidelity, detail-preserved images. Extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate that DeVAR consistently achieves superior qualitative and quantitative performance compared to state-of-the-art LDCT denoising methods.

AI.tldr: DeVAR is a novel generative framework applying visual autoregressive modeling (VAR) to low-dose CT denoising, using next-scale prediction with discrete token maps, a residual refiner for fine details, and a dual-representation hybrid training strategy to outperform state-of-the-art methods on public datasets.
AI.motivation: Minimizing radiation exposure in CT while maintaining image quality is a critical challenge, as low-dose CT suffers from noise and artifacts. Existing deep learning methods still lack generative paradigms that capture global-to-local structural dependencies to preserve fine anatomical details.
AI.method: DeVAR applies visual autoregressive modeling (VAR) to LDCT denoising via next-scale prediction of discrete token maps conditioned on LDCT prefix tokens, adds a residual refiner to recover subtle anatomical structures lost in quantization, and employs a dual-representation hybrid training strategy that integrates continuous and discrete latents for reconstruction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate DeVAR consistently achieves superior qualitative and quantitative performance compared to state-of-the-art LDCT denoising methods.
AI.conclusion: DeVAR effectively addresses LDCT denoising by capturing global-to-local dependencies through visual autoregressive modeling and hybrid representations, setting a new state-of-the-art benchmark.

title: Salami Slicing Trellis for Synchronization Errors in DNA Coding
summary: On top of substitution errors, DNA storage channels suffer from both insertions and deletions at the same time. It is therefore important to develop error-correcting codes with efficient encoders and decoders that can combat all three types of noise. This paper introduces the salami-slicing trellis, a decision-feedback trellis that computes bitwise posterior probabilities along each strand and is coupled with polar codes across strands. The decoder alternates between advancing the trellises by one position and polar-decoding the resulting cross-strand slice, feeding the decoded bits back to the trellises for the next position. Simulations suggest that the resulting coding scheme approaches the conjectured capacity of the substitution-insertion-deletion channel.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a salami-slicing trellis and polar code-based scheme for DNA storage channels that handles substitutions, insertions, and deletions, approaching the channel's conjectured capacity.
AI.motivation: DNA storage channels suffer from substitution, insertion, and deletion errors simultaneously, requiring error-correcting codes that can efficiently handle all three noise types.
AI.method: The salami-slicing trellis (a decision-feedback trellis) computes bitwise posterior probabilities per strand, coupled with polar codes across strands. Decoding alternates between advancing trellises by one position and polar-decoding cross-strand slices, feeding decoded bits back to trellises.
AI.result: Simulations show the coding scheme approaches the conjectured capacity of the substitution-insertion-deletion channel.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively combats all three types of noise (substitutions, insertions, deletions) and approaches channel capacity in DNA storage systems.

title: Unified Generalization for Frequency-Domain Channel Extrapolation Across Near-Field and Far-Field Scenarios
summary: As antenna arrays grow, near-field effects become non-negligible in large-scale MIMO, making accurate low-overhead channel acquisition crucial in both far-field and near-field regimes. Deep-learning-based frequency-domain channel extrapolation can reduce pilot overhead, but existing extrapolators generalize poorly to unseen distances and environments, especially across near-field and far-field channels. We propose a physically interpretable framework to unify generalization across both regimes. Our key insight is that angular profiles are regime-dependent, while delay profiles share a sparsity structure that can be aligned. Based on this, we develop a physics-guided disentanglement and alignment pipeline with multi-cluster decoupling, angle-delay feature disentanglement, and delay-domain alignment, enabling the model to learn distribution-stable delay features while reusing heterogeneous angular features. We further design a unified near/far-field DL extrapolator (UNiFi-DLE) and detail its dataset preparation, training, and inference. Simulations and sim-to-real experiments show that UNiFi-DLE generalizes robustly to unseen near-field and far-field scenarios and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes UNiFi-DLE, a deep-learning-based channel extrapolation framework that unifies near-field and far-field MIMO channel acquisition by exploiting the shared delay-domain sparsity while disentangling regime-dependent angular features, achieving robust generalization to unseen scenarios.
AI.motivation: Large-scale MIMO systems face non-negligible near-field effects, and existing deep-learning frequency-domain channel extrapolators generalize poorly across near-field and far-field regimes, especially to unseen distances and environments.
AI.method: A physics-guided disentanglement and alignment pipeline is proposed, including multi-cluster decoupling, angle-delay feature disentanglement, and delay-domain alignment. This enables learning distribution-stable delay features while reusing heterogeneous angular features, forming the UNiFi-DLE framework with specific dataset preparation, training, and inference designs.
AI.result: Simulations and sim-to-real experiments demonstrate that UNiFi-DLE generalizes robustly to unseen near-field and far-field scenarios and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: By exploiting the insight that delay profiles share a sparsity structure across regimes while angular profiles are regime-dependent, the proposed framework effectively unifies near-field and far-field channel extrapolation with strong generalization.

title: Event-Conditioned Diagnostics of Kinematic, Contact, and Object-Permanence Fields in Passive Object-State World Models
summary: World models can predict future physical states, but prediction accuracy alone does not explain how physical information is organized and used inside their latent dynamics. We introduce a controlled diagnostic protocol for studying event-conditioned latent physical structure in passive object-state world models. The protocol tests whether hidden representations encode event-regime information, whether event contexts reweight non-exclusive physical field readouts, and whether field-aligned representational components have functional consequences for prediction. Using a balanced controlled-generator dataset with free-motion, collision, and occlusion events, we evaluate recurrent, attention-based, and latent state-space transition models under a fixed-horizon forecasting setup. The models learn useful predictive dynamics and their hidden states support reliable event-regime readout. Event contexts systematically reweight kinematic, contact, and object-permanence field readouts: free motion is kinematic-dominant, collision combines kinematic and contact structure, and occlusion combines motion-related and object-permanence structure. Time-aligned and directional-consistency analyses further show phase-related shifts in field emphasis. Finally, fixed-horizon projection causal field effect (CFE) shows that suppressing field-aligned directions can degrade event-relevant prediction, with strongest evidence for contact-aligned structure in collision-contact windows and more qualified evidence for object-permanence-aligned structure in hard-occlusion hidden windows. These results support event-conditioned organization and fixed-horizon functional sensitivity of latent physical fields, while not implying explicit physical modules, isolated causal circuits, or context-invariant sliding-window generalization.

AI.tldr: A diagnostic protocol reveals that world models' latent representations encode event-regime information (free motion, collision, occlusion) and that event contexts reweight kinematic, contact, and object-permanence physical field readouts, with functional consequences for fixed-horizon prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Prediction accuracy in world models does not explain how physical information is organized and used in latent dynamics, motivating a controlled diagnostic to study event-conditioned latent physical structure.
AI.method: A controlled diagnostic protocol tests event-regime encoding, context-dependent reweighting of physical field readouts, and functional consequences via fixed-horizon causal field effect (CFE) analysis on recurrent, attention-based, and state-space models using a balanced dataset of free-motion, collision, and occlusion events.
AI.result: Models encode event-regime information; free motion emphasizes kinematics, collision mixes kinematic+contact, occlusion mixes motion+object-permanence; suppressing field-aligned directions degrades prediction, especially for contact (collision) and object-permanence (occlusion).
AI.conclusion: Physical fields are event-conditioned and functionally relevant for fixed-horizon prediction, but the organization is not modular, causally isolated, or context-invariant.

title: Learning to Distributedly Estimate under Partially Known Dynamics: A Covariance-Agnostic Neural Kalman Consensus Filter
comment: Under review in IEEE journal, 13 pages, 9 figures
summary: Online latent state estimation constitutes a fundamental challenge within the artificial intelligence field, serving as a foundational tool for diverse applications, including sequential decision making, anomaly and change-point detection. In this paper, a novel online distributed sensing framework, where agents collaborate and exchange information to perform latent state estimation, is presented. The proposed estimator combines available partial domain knowledge with the representation capabilities of deep neural networks. In particular, the designed sensing framework incorporates prior estimates, optimized consensus weights, and Kalman-like recursive updates to perform decentralized inference, without relying on knowledge of noise statistics.
