To strengthen lesion-level reasoning, we further optimize a local LLM via reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization, rewarding correct lesion classification and anatomically concordant site assignment. Across AutoPET and an OSU test cohort, RADIANT-PET consistently outperforms strong image-only baselines, with the largest improvements observed when radiology reports are provided. Overall, these results demonstrate that LLM-based lesion-level reasoning adds a novel reasoning layer beyond conventional segmentation, suppressing physiologic false positives and aligning voxel-level predictions with clinical interpretation. The project repository is available at: https://github.com/jwang-580/RADIANT-PET.

AI.tldr: RADIANT-PET combines a permissive voxel-level segmentation model with LLM-based lesion-level reasoning to improve PET/CT lesion segmentation, reducing false positives from physiologic uptake. It outperforms image-only baselines, especially when radiology reports are available.
AI.motivation: Accurate lesion segmentation in PET/CT is critical for oncology but remains challenging because physiologic tracer uptake and artifacts can mimic malignant signal, leading to false positives.
AI.method: RADIANT-PET uses a two-stage framework: (1) a deliberately permissive voxel-level segmentation model generates candidate uptake regions, which are converted into structured textual descriptions; (2) an LLM classifies each candidate as true lesion vs. false positive, optionally using radiology reports. The LLM is further optimized via reinforcement learning (GRPO) rewarding correct lesion classification and anatomically concordant site assignment.
AI.result: Across AutoPET and an OSU test cohort, RADIANT-PET consistently outperforms strong image-only baselines, with the largest improvements observed when radiology reports are provided.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based lesion-level reasoning adds a novel reasoning layer beyond conventional segmentation, suppressing physiologic false positives and aligning voxel-level predictions with clinical interpretation.

title: COMPASS: Grounding Composition-Intent Guidance in Unified Multimodal Models
summary: Composition is a high-level visual intent that governs where subjects are placed and how a scene is organized, yet current unified multimodal models remain unreliable at fine-grained composition recognition and struggle to turn such intent into controllable generation. We present COMPASS, the first unified multimodal framework that grounds composition-intent control in a single system spanning both composition perception and composition-guided generation, with a shared expert token $τ_c$ as the central intent anchor. On the perception side, COMPASS injects composition expertise into an MoE backbone in a minimally invasive manner and distills the inferred intent into $τ_c$. On the generation side, COMPASS reuses $τ_c$ as a global conditioning signal that steers the denoising trajectory, effectively converting passive composition analysis into explicit layout control. To support systematic instruction-following composition learning and evaluation at scale, we construct Comp-11, a large-scale dataset with an 11-class taxonomy and reasoning-augmented annotations. Extensive experiments show that COMPASS substantially improves category-level composition understanding and delivers more composition-consistent, prompt-faithful generation than strong baselines.

AI.tldr: COMPASS is the first unified multimodal framework for composition perception and generation, using a shared expert token τ_c to bridge composition understanding and controllable generation, supported by the Comp-11 dataset.
AI.motivation: Current unified multimodal models cannot reliably recognize fine-grained composition (subject placement, scene organization) or translate composition intent into controllable generation.
AI.method: COMPASS uses an MoE backbone with a shared expert token τ_c. Perception side injects composition expertise via MoE and distills intent into τ_c. Generation side uses τ_c as a global conditioning signal for denoising. Comp-11 dataset provides 11-class taxonomy with reasoning-augmented annotations.
AI.result: COMPASS substantially improves category-level composition understanding and generates more composition-consistent, prompt-faithful outputs than strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework with a shared composition token can effectively bridge composition perception and generation, enabling controllable layout-guided image synthesis.

title: BLUE: A Stale-Pixel Optical-Flow Compositor for Entropy-Efficient Surveillance Video Encoding
summary: Continuous-recording surveillance systems face a storage problem that codec tuning alone cannot fully solve: even at aggressive CRF settings, a static-camera scene spends most of its bits re-encoding a background that has not changed. We present BLUE, a pre-encode compositor that exploits this structure by maintaining a persistent seed frame of the background and substituting background pixels with seed pixels before the encoder runs. The encoder then emits near-free SKIP macroblocks for the frozen background, while live pixels in foreground regions are carried unchanged at full quality. We evaluate BLUE on all 308 annotated short subclips from the VIRAT Ground Surveillance Release 2.0 dataset using a six-point CRF sweep with both x264 and x265. At CRF 28, BLUE reduces file size by a mean of 34.6% (x264) / 39.4% (x265) on 95.8% / 99.4% of clips respectively. Foreground-region PSNR, computed only over VIRAT object-annotation bounding boxes, is preserved or improved on 60.7% of clips (+0.36 dB mean, +5.48 dB maximum). Full-frame perceptual quality (VMAF) drops by a median of 6.75-8.59 points; we quantify and disclose this trade-off explicitly. A lightweight deployment gate measuring the compositor's own VMAF on a 2-second prefix identifies the 40% of clips where even full-frame quality degradation is near-imperceptible (Delta VMAF <= -2.9), enabling a selective-activation strategy that retains both the storage benefit and acceptable perceptual fidelity.

AI.tldr: BLUE is a pre-encode compositor that reduces surveillance video file size by substituting static background pixels with seed frame pixels, achieving ~34-39% storage reduction at CRF 28 while preserving foreground quality, with a selective-activation gate to maintain perceptual fidelity.
AI.motivation: Continuous-recording surveillance systems face storage problems that codec tuning alone cannot solve, as static-camera scenes waste bits re-encoding unchanged backgrounds.
AI.method: BLUE maintains a persistent seed frame of the background and substitutes background pixels with seed pixels before encoding, causing the encoder to emit near-free SKIP macroblocks for frozen background while foreground regions remain unchanged at full quality.
AI.result: On 308 VIRAT clips with CRF 28: 34.6% (x264) / 39.4% (x265) file size reduction on 95.8%/99.4% of clips; foreground PSNR preserved/improved on 60.7% of clips (+0.36 dB mean); VMAF drops 6.75-8.59 points median; selective gate identifies 40% of clips with near-imperceptible degradation (Delta VMAF <= -2.9).
AI.conclusion: BLUE offers a practical pre-encode compositing strategy that significantly reduces storage for surveillance video while maintaining foreground quality, with a lightweight gate enabling selective activation to balance storage savings and perceptual fidelity.

title: State-Evolution-based Score Matching for Generalized Approximate Message Passing
summary: Generalized approximate message passing (GAMP) equipped with minimum mean-square error (MMSE) denoisers, commonly referred to as Bayes-GAMP, is a powerful framework for solving inverse problems described by generalized linear models (GLMs) with arbitrary component-wise nonlinearities in the observation process. However, despite its theoretical tractability and rigorously established asymptotic optimality, the range of practical observation models for which Bayes-GAMP admits a closed-form implementation remains severely limited, particularly in complex-valued settings. This limitation largely stems from the restrictive requirement that the corresponding output denoiser, given by a conditional expectation, admit a closed-form expression. To overcome this limitation, we propose a principled approach that enables the implementation of Bayes-GAMP for complex-valued models with \emph{virtually arbitrary} nonlinear observation mappings. Specifically, within a score-matching framework, we train a neural network to emulate the output denoiser using training data generated from a characterization of the message dynamics based on state evolution (SE). Notably, the proposed approach requires neither explicit evaluation of the denoiser nor knowledge of an explicit functional form of the nonlinear mapping; it requires only access to forward evaluations of the mapping during offline training. We show that, under ideal training conditions, GAMP with the trained network replacing the analytically intractable denoiser asymptotically matches the performance of Bayes-GAMP with the exact denoiser.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neural network-based approach to approximate intractable MMSE denoisers in Bayes-GAMP for complex-valued generalized linear models, enabling closed-form implementation for virtually arbitrary nonlinear observation mappings without requiring explicit denoiser knowledge.
AI.motivation: Bayes-GAMP is theoretically optimal for GLMs but its practical application is severely limited because many complex-valued observation models lack closed-form output denoisers, restricting its real-world use.
AI.method: A score-matching framework trains a neural network to emulate the output denoiser using training data generated from state evolution (SE) characterization of message dynamics, requiring only forward evaluations of the nonlinear mapping during offline training.
AI.result: Under ideal training conditions, GAMP with the trained network asymptotically matches the performance of Bayes-GAMP with the exact (analytically intractable) MMSE denoiser.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully overcomes the closed-form denoiser limitation, enabling Bayes-GAMP for complex-valued models with virtually arbitrary nonlinear observation mappings, while preserving asymptotic optimality.

title: Physics-Informed Uncertainty-Aware Beamforming for HAPS Massive MIMO under Imperfect CSI
summary: High-altitude platform station (HAPS) massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems are expected to support wide-area, low-latency, and energy-efficient connectivity in future non-terrestrial networks. However, Doppler-induced channel aging, finite-rate feedback quantization, packet loss, and estimation noise impair transmitter-side channel state information (CSI), making robust downlink beamforming challenging. In HAPS channels, these impairments are strongly structured by elevation-dependent Rician propagation and line-of-sight (LoS)-dominant geometry, whereas conventional robust beamforming methods often rely on generic uncertainty models and computationally intensive optimization. This paper develops a physics-informed uncertainty-aware beamforming framework for HAPS massive MIMO systems under imperfect CSI. First, a geometry-aware channel and feedback-impairment model is developed, where CSI errors due to aging, quantization, packet loss, and noise are represented through tangent-space ellipsoidal uncertainty sets. Second, a physics-informed variational autoencoder (VAE) exploits the LoS-dominant steering manifold to enhance channel direction information and propagate learned uncertainty through unit-sphere projection. Third, the learned uncertainty representation is embedded into a robust energy-efficiency maximization formulation with probabilistic QoS awareness. To enable scalable online operation, the resulting beamforming policy is approximated using a multi-agent deterministic policy gradient framework with centralized training, decentralized execution, and differentiable power projection. Simulation results show that the proposed framework improves energy efficiency, SINR robustness, outage reliability, convergence behavior, and online runtime compared with imperfect-CSI, SDR-based, and no-VAE baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a physics-informed, uncertainty-aware beamforming framework for HAPS massive MIMO systems that uses a variational autoencoder to learn channel uncertainties from structured impairments (aging, quantization, packet loss, noise) and a multi-agent reinforcement learning approach for scalable, energy-efficient downlink beamforming.
AI.motivation: HAPS massive MIMO systems face severe Doppler-induced channel aging, finite-rate feedback quantization, packet loss, and estimation noise that impair CSI. Conventional robust beamforming uses generic uncertainty models and computationally intensive optimization, failing to exploit the elevation-dependent Rician propagation and LoS-dominant geometry specific to HAPS channels.
AI.method: (1) A geometry-aware channel and feedback-impairment model with tangent-space ellipsoidal uncertainty sets. (2) A physics-informed VAE exploiting LoS-dominant steering manifold. (3) Robust energy-efficiency maximization with probabilistic QoS. (4) Multi-agent deterministic policy gradient (MADPG) framework for scalable online operation.
AI.result: The proposed framework improves energy efficiency, SINR robustness, outage reliability, convergence behavior, and online runtime compared to imperfect-CSI, SDR-based, and no-VAE baselines.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed learning of structured CSI uncertainty, combined with multi-agent reinforcement learning, provides an effective and scalable solution for robust beamforming in HAPS massive MIMO networks.

title: Robotic Arm-Based Spectral Sensing for Strawberry Positioning and Non-Destructive Sweetness Measurement
summary:
Accurate assessment of sweetness is essential for quality control in agriculture, yet conventional methods rely on destructive sampling and are difficult to scale. This thesis presents a robotic arm-based spectral sensing system for strawberry detection, localization, approach, and non-destructive sweetness estimation.
  The system integrates perception, calibration, and robotic control in a closed-loop pipeline. A YOLOv11s detector is adopted for real-time strawberry detection, while RGB-ToF calibration and mask-to-depth alignment are used to obtain geometrically consistent target localization. A custom eye-in-hand hand-eye calibration workflow is developed to estimate the rigid transform between gripper_link and cam_front, enabling reliable transformation of fruit targets into the robot base frame. Based on these estimates, the robot executes a waypoint-based search and an incremental closed-loop approach strategy to position the sensor at optimal working distance for sweetness sensing.
  Experimental results show strong end-to-end performance (88.10% success over 42 trials), with robust detection (95.24%) and successful approach execution once a target is detected (100% conditional success). Hand-eye calibration comparisons indicate that although Andreff yields the smallest translation norm in single-run results, the Park method provides better cross-sample consistency and therefore more stable downstream robot behavior. The residual failures are concentrated in the sensing stage, especially valid-region extraction for sweetness estimation under difficult depth/reflectance conditions.
  Overall, this work demonstrates the feasibility of integrating RGB-ToF perception, robotic manipulation, and non-destructive sensing for practical strawberry quality assessment, and provides a scalable baseline for future integration of learning-based policies such as Vision-Language-Action models.

AI.tldr: This thesis presents a robotic arm-based spectral sensing system that integrates YOLOv11s detection, RGB-ToF calibration, and hand-eye coordination for non-destructive strawberry sweetness estimation, achieving 88.10% end-to-end success over 42 trials.
AI.motivation: Accurate sweetness assessment is critical for agricultural quality control, but conventional methods rely on destructive sampling and lack scalability.
AI.method: The system uses a YOLOv11s detector for strawberry detection, RGB-ToF calibration for localization, a custom eye-in-hand calibration to transform targets into robot base frame, and a waypoint-based search with closed-loop approach to position the sensor for sweetness sensing.
AI.result: 88.10% end-to-end success (42 trials), 95.24% detection rate, 100% conditional approach success. Park method provides better cross-sample consistency than Andreff for hand-eye calibration. Failures mainly occur in sensing stage under difficult depth/reflectance conditions.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates feasibility of integrating RGB-ToF perception, robotic manipulation, and non-destructive sensing for practical strawberry quality assessment, providing a scalable baseline for future learning-based policies like Vision-Language-Action models.

title: An Agentic AI Pipeline for Appliance-Level Energy Anomaly Detection and LLM-Driven Recommendations
comment: 07 pages, 01 figure, accepted for presentation at the IEEE International Conference on Communication, Computing, Networking, and Control in Cyber-Physical Systems (CCNCPS 2026)
summary: Appliance-level energy monitoring in office buildings produces noisy alerts that non-expert facility managers struggle to use. This paper proposes an end-to-end agentic pipeline that combines deep time-series forecasting, variational anomaly detection, and LLM-based reasoning to generate prioritized, actionable maintenance recommendations. The system tracks seven office appliances using a hybrid Singular Spectrum Analysis (SSA) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) forecasting model, and applies a per-appliance LSTM Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with attention to flag abnormal daily consumption episodes. A three-stage LangChain pipeline begins with a Context Agent that always retrieves three core RAG sources (model reliability, hourly baseline, and expert knowledge) and conditionally adds up to three more (forecast context, anomaly history, global baseline) based on event characteristics, capped at eight reasoning steps. A Diagnosis Agent converts the evidence into a structured JSON diagnosis, and a Report Agent renders a human-readable narrative. A reflective memory layer incorporates operator feedback. The dashboard shows real-time 30-minute forecasts, intraday consumption, the previous day anomaly report, and a feedback form. We evaluate the forecasting model, anomaly detector with appliance-specific thresholds, and LLM reasoning on a 16-scenario benchmark including sustained and transient spikes, unexpected shutdowns, and systemic events, comparing five LLM backends under static vs. dynamic retrieval. Dynamic retrieval matches full static retrieval across all backends while cutting average context from six to three-six sources per event. The best backend scores 90.4/100 with a 100% pass rate at a 70-point threshold, and a fully local 7B-parameter model passes all 16 scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes an end-to-end agentic pipeline combining time-series forecasting (SSA-LSTM), variational anomaly detection (LSTM VAE with attention), and LLM-based reasoning (LangChain pipeline with context, diagnosis, and report agents) for appliance-level energy monitoring in office buildings. Dynamic retrieval matches full static performance while reducing context size. A local 7B model passes all 16 benchmark scenarios.
AI.motivation: Appliance-level energy monitoring generates noisy alerts that non-expert facility managers cannot effectively use. Existing systems lack actionable, prioritized maintenance recommendations.
AI.method: Hybrid SSA-LSTM forecasting + per-appliance LSTM VAE with attention for anomaly detection + three-stage LangChain pipeline (Context Agent with dynamic RAG, Diagnosis Agent for structured JSON, Report Agent for narrative) + reflective memory layer.
AI.result: Dynamic retrieval matches full static retrieval across all LLM backends while cutting average context from 6 to 3-6 sources per event. Best backend scores 90.4/100 with 100% pass rate at 70-point threshold; a fully local 7B model passes all 16 scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed agentic pipeline effectively converts noisy monitoring alerts into actionable maintenance recommendations, with dynamic retrieval offering efficiency gains and local models achieving strong performance.

title: Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports
summary: In longitudinal clinical practice, every chest X-ray is read in the context of the patients prior exam, and much of what the radiologist communicates is the change from one visit to the next. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first training-free best-of-N sampling scheme for pre-trained chest X-ray report generators that is explicitly aware of this longitudinal prior to current transition. We call it transition-aware best-of-N sampling, each report is split into sentences and embedded into an unordered set in Rd; each (prior, current) pair is reduced to a fixed-dim directional vector via a set-to-set distance designed to encode the change between the two sets; and candidates are scored by cosine distance from their candidate transition vector to a cached bank of ground-truth training transition vectors, aggregated as min or kNN. We instantiate the framework with four directional set distances (mean-shift, novelty residual, directed-Hausdorff anchor, and cost-weighted optimal transport) and evaluate on a multi-visit AP-PA cohort, running inference under three prompts on three vision-language generators. Transition-aware best-of-N outperforms random selection across the board, with the largest relative gains on the Impression section.

AI.tldr: First training-free transition-aware best-of-N sampling for chest X-ray report generation, using directional set distances to encode changes between prior and current exams, outperforming random selection across multiple generators and prompts.
AI.motivation: Clinical chest X-ray readings rely on comparing prior and current exams to detect changes, but existing report generators treat each exam independently, missing this longitudinal context.
AI.method: Training-free best-of-N sampling: split reports into sentences (unordered set in Rd), compute directional vectors via set-to-set distances (mean-shift, novelty residual, directed-Hausdorff anchor, cost-weighted optimal transport) between prior and current, score candidates by cosine distance to ground-truth training transition vectors using min or kNN aggregation.
AI.result: Outperforms random selection across all settings; largest gains on Impression section; consistent improvement across 3 prompts, 3 generators, and 4 directional distances.
AI.conclusion: Transition-aware sampling effectively incorporates longitudinal prior information without retraining, enabling better radiology report generation by modeling clinical change.

title: BV-Blend: Uncertainty-Weighted Historical Baselines for Stable Critic-Free RL with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Critic-free reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), exemplified by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), avoids training a value function (critic) and reduces memory and compute overhead relative to critic-based PPO pipelines for aligning large language models. However, GRPO-style advantage estimation depends on prompt-local (within-prompt-group) reward statistics and can be unstable. In particular, when all rollouts in a prompt group receive identical rewards, the within-group reward variance becomes zero, and group normalization yields zero advantages for that group, impeding learning in cold-start regimes with binary verifiers. We introduce BV-Blend, a critic-free framework that stabilizes advantage estimation by combining prompt-local on-policy statistics with semantic-cluster-conditioned historical moments. BV-Blend maintains EMA-tracked reward moments for each cluster, derives a confidence weight from a standard error of the mean (SEM) proxy, and uses this weight to blend historical and prompt-local baseline and variance statistics into a standardized advantage for PPO-style clipped updates. Experiments on verifiable reasoning benchmarks show that BV-Blend improves training stability and performance, and remains robust in regimes where group-normalized methods may stall.

AI.tldr: BV-Blend is a critic-free RLVR framework that improves advantage estimation stability by blending prompt-local statistics with cluster-conditioned historical moments, outperforming GRPO on verifiable reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: GRPO-style advantage estimation using within-group reward statistics can be unstable, especially when all rollouts in a group receive identical rewards (zero variance), leading to stalled learning in cold-start regimes with binary verifiers.
AI.method: BV-Blend combines prompt-local on-policy statistics with semantic-cluster-conditioned historical moments using EMA-tracked reward moments, a confidence weight from SEM proxy, and blends historical/prompt-local baselines and variance into standardized advantages for PPO-style clipped updates.
AI.result: Experiments on verifiable reasoning benchmarks show BV-Blend improves training stability and performance, and remains robust in regimes where group-normalized methods like GRPO stall.
AI.conclusion: BV-Blend offers a stable, critic-free alternative to GRPO for aligning LLMs with verifiable rewards, particularly effective under cold-start and binary-reward conditions.

title: A Task-Driven and Quality-Assured Agent Framework for SAR Data Generation
summary: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) data augmentation is important for improving the generalization of data-driven SAR interpretation models, yet practical augmentation workflows are often hindered by heterogeneous dataset formats, task-dependent metadata requirements, diverse generation methods, and weak validation of generated samples. This paper presents the \textbf{S}AR \textbf{A}ugmentation and \textbf{G}eneration \textbf{A}gent (SAGA), a schema-grounded and benefit-aware agent framework for task-oriented SAR data generation and augmentation. Given a natural-language request and heterogeneous SAR inputs, SAGA extracts observable dataset facts, validates executable dataset schemas, selects feasible augmentation strategies through validator-constrained planning, and compiles the selected strategy into an auditable augmentation workflow. Generated data are further assessed by quality, distribution, SAR-artifact, duplicate, leakage, and optional downstream-task evaluators to support evidence-qualified augmentation claims. By separating semantic proposal from deterministic validation and execution, SAGA improves the reliability and reproducibility of SAR augmentation decisions. Experiments on controlled agentic benchmarks and downstream SAR interpretation tasks show that SAGA improves schema grounding, skill planning, invalid-sample rejection, and downstream augmentation utility compared with rule-based, LLM-only, ReAct-style, and fixed-augmentation baselines.

AI.tldr: SAGA is an agent framework that uses schema-grounded planning and multi-evaluator validation to improve reliability and reproducibility of task-oriented SAR data augmentation.
AI.motivation: SAR data augmentation is hindered by heterogeneous dataset formats, task-dependent metadata, diverse generation methods, and weak validation of generated samples.
AI.method: SAGA extracts dataset facts, validates schemas, selects augmentation strategies via validator-constrained planning, compiles workflows, and assesses outputs using quality, distribution, artifact, duplicate, leakage, and downstream evaluators.
AI.result: Outperforms rule-based, LLM-only, ReAct-style, and fixed-augmentation baselines in schema grounding, skill planning, invalid-sample rejection, and downstream augmentation utility.
AI.conclusion: Separating semantic proposal from deterministic validation and execution improves reliability and reproducibility of SAR augmentation.

title: Proportional-Fair Joint User Grouping and Power Allocation for Uplink NOMA-ISAC
summary: This letter addresses long-term fairness in uplink non-orthogonal multiple access integrated sensing and communication (NOMA-ISAC) systems. Existing resource allocation schemes that maximize instantaneous sum rate often favor strong users, leaving historically underserved users with poor long-term throughput. We propose PF-JUGPA, a proportional-fair scheduling based joint user grouping and power allocation method. PF-JUGPA first pre-selects users via a PF metric combining instantaneous rate proxies and historical averages, then performs fairness-aware grouping and power allocation by maximizing a weighted sum rate whose weights are inversely proportional to historical service rates. Simulation results show that PF-JUGPA significantly improves the Jain fairness index and weak-user average rates with only a modest sum-rate loss compared to sum-rate-oriented and round-robin baselines. The findings confirm that embedding long-term service history into both scheduling and resource allocation yields an effective throughput--fairness--sensing tradeoff in uplink NOMA-ISAC.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes PF-JUGPA, a proportional-fair scheduling and resource allocation method for uplink NOMA-ISAC systems that improves long-term fairness for weak users by incorporating historical service rates into both user grouping and power allocation, achieving better Jain fairness index with modest sum-rate loss.
AI.motivation: Existing resource allocation schemes maximize instantaneous sum rate, which favors strong users and leaves historically underserved users with poor long-term throughput. The paper aims to address this long-term fairness issue in uplink NOMA-ISAC systems.
AI.method: PF-JUGPA first pre-selects users using a proportional-fairness (PF) metric that combines instantaneous rate proxies and historical averages, then performs fairness-aware grouping and power allocation by maximizing a weighted sum rate where weights are inversely proportional to historical service rates.
AI.result: PF-JUGPA significantly improves the Jain fairness index and weak-user average rates with only a modest sum-rate loss compared to sum-rate-oriented and round-robin baselines.
AI.conclusion: Embedding long-term service history into both scheduling and resource allocation yields an effective throughput–fairness–sensing tradeoff in uplink NOMA-ISAC systems.

title: A Holistic Link Budget Analysis for mmWave and THz Communications in Non-Terrestrial Networks
summary: The non-terrestrial network (NTN) architecture has gained significant interest from the academia owing to its versatility and the ability to provide worldwide service. To achieve extremely high data rates in NTNs, as intended in the sixth-generation (6G) communication systems, millimeter wave (mmWave) and terahertz (THz) frequencies can be considered, enabling substantial bandwidth and data transmission capacity, which makes them highly suitable for NTN applications. However, these high-frequency signals suffer from significant propagation challenges, including atmospheric attenuation, pointing errors, and various environmental effects. Therefore, a comprehensive link budget analysis is essential to accurately assess the feasibility of mmWave/THz-based NTN systems. Existing studies in the literature often fail to fully capture certain frequency-, altitude-, and direction-dependent effects observed in mmWave/THz transmission or possible communication scenarios within the NTN architecture. In particular, while most prior works primarily focus on free-space loss or atmospheric attenuation, this study adopts a much more comprehensive approach. In this work, a detailed link budget analysis is conducted for mmWave/THz NTNs, considering free-space loss, atmospheric absorption, weather-induced effects, ionospheric disturbances, polarization mismatches, feeder losses, antenna and circuitry constraints, fading, pointing errors, and non-white noise characteristics. The results have revealed that the multi-layer structure of the NTN architecture can help reducing the excessive loss levels to a certain level that can be tolerated by high-gain directional antennas/arrays, providing multi-gigabit links and making mmWave/THz NTNs feasible for 6G communication systems.

AI.tldr: This paper performs a comprehensive link budget analysis for mmWave/THz non-terrestrial networks (NTN), considering numerous real-world impairments (atmospheric, weather, ionospheric, polarization, hardware, fading, pointing errors, noise), and finds that the multi-layer NTN architecture combined with high-gain directional antennas can achieve multi-gigabit links, making mmWave/THz NTNs feasible for 6G.
AI.motivation: Existing studies fail to fully capture frequency-, altitude-, and direction-dependent effects in mmWave/THz NTN transmission, often focusing only on free-space loss or atmospheric attenuation. A more comprehensive link budget analysis is needed to assess feasibility for 6G.
AI.method: A detailed link budget analysis is conducted for mmWave/THz NTNs, incorporating free-space loss, atmospheric absorption, weather-induced effects, ionospheric disturbances, polarization mismatches, feeder losses, antenna/circuitry constraints, fading, pointing errors, and non-white noise characteristics.
AI.result: The multi-layer NTN architecture can reduce excessive loss levels to a tolerable range with high-gain directional antennas/arrays, enabling multi-gigabit links.
AI.conclusion: mmWave/THz NTNs are feasible for 6G communication systems when leveraging multi-layer architecture and high-gain directional antennas.

title: Embodiment Meets Environment: Toward Context-Aware, Safe Physical Caregiving Robots
summary: Physical caregiving robots need to assist different users with different tasks in diverse environments, and they come in many embodiments. While substantial progress has been made on individual caregiving tasks, most existing systems remain tightly coupled to specific environments and robot embodiments, and often do not explicitly model or constrain interactions around people, despite humans being special agents in the environment. This motivates a focus on adapting to context that emerges from the joint interaction between the environment and the robot's embodiment. We propose $E^2$-CARE, a framework that enables context-aware adaptation by representing primitive caregiving skills as interaction templates whose execution is reshaped online. $E^2$-CARE represents the environment, the robot, and the human within a unified 3D dynamic scene graph that models these interaction contexts explicitly, and synthesizes task-specific constraints to govern how each skill is executed. By enforcing these constraints at runtime, the same skill templates can be reused zero-shot and safely across diverse environments and robot embodiments. We evaluate $E^2$-CARE across four activities of daily living in hundreds of simulated household environments, including assistive home settings, and across diverse robot embodiments, and validate it through user studies on two caregiving tasks with two robots in various real-world environments. Results demonstrate consistent and successful adaptation across these environments and embodiments. Website: https://emprise.cs.cornell.edu/e2care

AI.tldr: E²-CARE is a framework enabling context-aware adaptation for physical caregiving robots by representing skills as interaction templates reshaped online via a unified 3D dynamic scene graph, allowing zero-shot reuse across diverse environments and robot embodiments.
AI.motivation: Existing caregiving robot systems are tightly coupled to specific environments and embodiments, and fail to explicitly model human interactions despite humans being critical agents, motivating the need for context-aware adaptation to joint human-robot-environment interactions.
AI.method: E²-CARE uses primitive caregiving skills as interaction templates reshaped online, representing environment, robot, and human in a unified 3D dynamic scene graph that models interaction contexts explicitly. Task-specific constraints are synthesized from this graph to govern skill execution at runtime.
AI.result: Evaluated across four activities of daily living in hundreds of simulated environments and diverse robot embodiments, plus user studies with two robots in real-world settings, demonstrating consistent and successful adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The same skill templates can be reused zero-shot and safely across diverse environments and robot embodiments, showing promise for generalizable physical caregiving robotics.

title: Modelling Emotional Memory in Children with Tensor Networks
summary: We demonstrate how emotional valence influences the order-dependent structure of children's recognition memory: correct recall of a sequence of emotionally-valenced toys depended not just on the valence of a given toy itself, but also on the valence of the toys shown before and after it. Whilst standard psychological models confirm that order-dependence differs across an event (a set of toys shown in sequence), accuracy is low and the model does not reflect how memory for an emotional object influences others in the set. A classical tensor network model factoring in valence is able to achieve a 77.98\% accuracy in modelling the results of the study. While not strictly a ``quantum cognition'' model, this massive increase in accuracy shows the value of quantum-inspired methods for modelling order-dependent phenomena, such as emotional memory. Further, the task protocol we introduce presents a novel, real-world tool for exploring emotional temporal memory in children for analysis using classical and quantum-like models of cognition.

AI.tldr: Children's memory for emotionally-valenced toys shows order-dependent effects (memory depends on surrounding items' valence). A classical tensor network model achieves 77.98% accuracy, demonstrating quantum-inspired methods' value for modeling emotional memory.
AI.motivation: To understand how emotional valence influences order-dependent structure in children's recognition memory, and to show that standard psychological models inadequately capture how memory for one emotional object affects memory for others in a sequence.
AI.method: A classical tensor network model that factors in emotional valence, applied to children's recall of sequentially presented emotionally-valenced toys.
AI.result: The tensor network model achieves 77.98% accuracy in modeling the study results, a massive improvement over standard psychological models.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-inspired (tensor network) methods are valuable for modeling order-dependent phenomena like emotional memory. The task protocol also provides a novel real-world tool for studying emotional temporal memory in children.

title: GPU-Accelerated Inverse Structural Anastylosis from Block Collapse Dynamics
summary: The physical anastylosis of collapsed architectural monuments -- the meticulous reassembly of fallen stone elements into their original structural configuration -- represents one of the most intellectually demanding challenges in conservation science. Traditional approaches depend heavily on expert archaeologist judgement and manual block-by-block correspondence, a process that is both labour-intensive and inherently subjective. Inspired by the combinatorial complexity of this problem as manifested in the game of Jenga, we present Jenga Inverse Predictor , a GPU-accelerated deep learning framework that addresses structural anastylosis as an inverse prediction task. Given an image of a collapsed block assembly, JIP-2 reconstructs the most probable prior tower configuration by: (1) implementing a complete rigid-body physics engine with OBB/SAT collision detection and a Projected Gauss-Seidel (PGS) contact solver accelerated with Numba JIT and CuPy CUDA; (2) applying the analytical force thresholds of Ziglar (CMU, 2006) -- F_app = 3*mu_s*m*g (Y-axis, torque-free) and F_app = 4*mu_s*m*g (X-axis, torque risk) -- over three friction levels (mu_s in {0.25, 0.40, 0.60}) across 450 simulated episodes; (3) training a dual-stream ResNet-18 that injects a friction one-hot vector and jointly predicts block removal count, per-position removal probabilities, centre-of-mass imbalance, and Ziglar torque risk; and (4) generating a smooth 3-D video of the block-by-block reverse reconstruction. We discuss implications for computer-assisted anastylosis at the UNESCO Maya site of Uxmal, Yucatan, and provide a detailed technical description of the full pipeline, architecture, and loss formulation.

AI.tldr: JIP-2 is a GPU-accelerated deep learning framework that treats physical anastylosis (reassembling collapsed stone monuments) as an inverse prediction problem, using a physics engine, friction-based force thresholds, and a dual-stream ResNet-18 to predict optimal block-by-block reconstruction sequences.
AI.motivation: Traditional anastylosis relies on expert judgement and manual block matching, which is labor-intensive and subjective. The authors aim to automate this process using AI inspired by the combinatorial complexity of Jenga.
AI.method: The method includes: (1) a rigid-body physics engine with OBB/SAT collision detection and PGS contact solver (GPU-accelerated via Numba/CuPy); (2) Ziglar force thresholds over three friction levels; (3) a dual-stream ResNet-18 that takes block assembly images and friction one-hot vectors to predict removal counts, per-position probabilities, center-of-mass imbalance, and torque risk; (4) generating a smooth 3D reverse reconstruction video.
AI.result: The framework was tested across 450 simulated episodes with three friction levels. It predicts block removal sequences, center-of-mass imbalance, and Ziglar torque risk for reverse reconstruction of collapsed block towers.
AI.conclusion: The authors discuss implications for computer-assisted anastylosis at the UNESCO Maya site of Uxmal, Yucatan, and provide a detailed technical description of the full pipeline, architecture, and loss formulation.

title: The Two Genie Game: Adoption and Welfare in Audit-Grounded AI Governance
comment: 36 pages, 3 figures. Lean 4 formalization and figure scripts: https://github.com/dlewissandy/two-genie-scripts
summary:
We ask under what conditions an agent with a harm-minimizing policy can displace an approval-seeking (RLHF) agent in a competitive market, and when that policy is sufficient to prevent community harm. We use evolutionary game theory (finite-population Moran-Fermi pairwise comparison) to formalize this subject to assumptions of wisher hindsight, peer testimony, a monotone harm ledger, sufficient information density of community feedback, and a finite, depleting resource pool, in a negative-sum environment.
  We show that adoption is favored when the prior distributions on how readily wishers attune to community sentiment are monotone, exhibit endpoint inversion, and have a centro-symmetric pairing property, and demonstrate this with several long-tailed priors (Hill, Pareto, Lomax, Frechet). Where it is favored, a critical adoption level separates communities that drift back to the approval-seeking agent from those for which the audited agent fixes; above that level fixation is the overwhelmingly likely outcome. We derive when fixation is attainable as a bound on the effective (informational) size N_c of the community, which must be small enough to allow fixation before depletion. We present these as Theorems 5.4 and 5.5; the algebraic and finite-grid backbone is machine-checked in Lean 4, with the barrier-crossing asymptotics retained as explicit hypotheses.
  We show that a self-audited agent with a community ledger is not, in general, sufficient to prevent community harm. Sufficiency depends both upon the alignment of the agent's audit with community values and the timeframe over which harm is evaluated. Regardless of alignment, once adoption reaches dominance, the state is absorbing. The same policy that reduced harm under alignment becomes a trap, welfare-negative under misalignment and, even under alignment, one that locks in harm deferred past the adoption horizon.

AI.tldr: This paper uses evolutionary game theory to analyze when a harm-minimizing (self-audited) AI agent can outcompete an approval-seeking (RLHF) agent in a competitive market, finding that adoption depends on specific prior distribution properties (monotonicity, endpoint inversion, centro-symmetric pairing) and a critical community size threshold. However, even when adopted, the harm-minimizing policy may fail to prevent community harm—it can become a trap under misalignment or defer harm beyond the adoption horizon.
AI.motivation: To understand under what conditions a harm-minimizing (audited) agent can displace an approval-seeking (RLHF) agent in a competitive market, and whether such displacement is sufficient to prevent community harm, given that RLHF-based agents may optimize for user approval rather than minimizing actual harm.
AI.method: The authors use finite-population Moran-Fermi pairwise comparison (evolutionary game theory) to model competition between agents, incorporating assumptions about wisher hindsight, peer testimony, a monotone harm ledger, community feedback information density, and a finite depleting resource pool in a negative-sum environment. The algebraic backbone is machine-checked in Lean 4.
AI.result: Adoption of the harm-minimizing agent is favored when prior distributions exhibit monotonicity, endpoint inversion, and centro-symmetric pairing (demonstrated with Hill, Pareto, Lomax, Frechet priors). A critical adoption threshold exists; above it, fixation is overwhelmingly likely if community size N_c is small enough. However, a self-audited agent is not generally sufficient to prevent harm—sufficiency depends on audit alignment with community values and evaluation timeframe. Once dominant, the state is absorbing and can lock in harm.
AI.conclusion: Harm-minimizing policies can displace RLHF agents under specific conditions, but they are not a panacea: misalignment can make the policy welfare-negative, and even alignment may merely defer harm beyond the adoption horizon. Theorems 5.4 and 5.5 characterize fixation conditions, formalized with machine-checked proofs.

title: Cross-Sensor SAR Data Generation Using Diffusion Models and Feature Migration
summary: Different synthetic aperture radar (SAR) sensors vary significantly in resolution, polarization modes, and frequency bands, making it difficult to directly apply existing models to newly launched SAR satellites. These new systems require large amounts of labeled data for model retraining, but collecting sufficient data in a short time is often infeasible. To address this contradiction, this paper proposes a data generation and transfer framework, integrating a stable diffusion model with attention distillation, that leverages historical SAR data to synthesize training data tailored to the unique characteristics of new SAR systems. Specifically, we fine-tune the low-rank adaptation (LoRA) modules within the multimodal diffusion transformer (MM-DiT) architecture to enable class-controllable SAR image generation guided by textual prompts. To ensure that the generated images reflect the statistical properties and imaging characteristics of the target SAR system, we further introduce an attention distillation mechanism that transfers sensor-specific features, such as spatial texture, speckle distribution, and structural patterns, from real target-domain data to the generative model. Extensive experiments on multi-class aircraft target datasets from two real spaceborne SAR systems demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in alleviating data scarcity and supporting cross-sensor remote sensing applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a data generation and transfer framework combining stable diffusion with attention distillation to synthesize SAR training data for new sensors, addressing data scarcity by leveraging historical SAR data and transferring sensor-specific features.
AI.motivation: Different SAR sensors vary in resolution, polarization, and frequency bands, so newly launched satellites cannot directly use existing models and require large labeled datasets, which are often infeasible to collect quickly.
AI.method: A framework integrating stable diffusion with attention distillation: (1) fine-tuning LoRA modules within MM-DiT architecture for class-controllable SAR image generation guided by text prompts; (2) attention distillation mechanism to transfer sensor-specific features (spatial texture, speckle distribution, structural patterns) from real target-domain data to the generative model.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-class aircraft target datasets from two real spaceborne SAR systems demonstrate effectiveness in alleviating data scarcity and supporting cross-sensor remote sensing applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses data scarcity for new SAR satellites by synthesizing training data that captures target sensor characteristics, enabling cross-sensor model transfer without requiring extensive real labeled data.

title: Capacity Bounds and High-SNR Characterization for MIMO-OWC Channels Under Average-Power Constraint
summary: This paper investigates the capacity of multipleinput multiple-output (MIMO) optical wireless communication (OWC) channels under a total average-power constraint. Since different nonnegative input vectors can be mapped to the same image vector and thus induce the same output distribution, we formulate a nonnegative basis pursuit (NN-BP) problem to identify the minimum-l1-norm input vector for each image vector. Based on the NN-BP characterization, we derive an equivalent expression for the channel capacity in terms of the image-vector distribution. We then establish computable lower and upper capacity bounds for both nT >= nR and nT < nR cases, and prove that the proposed bounds are asymptotically tight in the high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regime. Numerical results for indoor and outdoor OWC scenarios demonstrate that the proposed bounds improve upon existing ones and close the constant gap in the high-SNR regime.

AI.tldr: This paper derives capacity bounds for MIMO optical wireless communication channels under average-power constraints using a nonnegative basis pursuit formulation, proving asymptotic tightness at high SNR.
AI.motivation: To characterize the capacity of MIMO OWC channels under total average-power constraints, which are fundamentally different from traditional RF channels due to nonnegative input constraints and signal-domain ambiguities.
AI.method: Formulates a nonnegative basis pursuit (NN-BP) problem to identify minimum-l1-norm input vectors for each image vector, then derives an equivalent capacity expression based on image-vector distribution, with computable upper/lower bounds for both nT >= nR and nT < nR cases.
AI.result: Proposed bounds are asymptotically tight in high-SNR regime and improve upon existing bounds, closing the constant gap in high SNR for both indoor and outdoor OWC scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The NN-BP-based approach provides tighter capacity characterizations for MIMO OWC channels, with proven asymptotic optimality at high SNR.

title: Neural Augmentation of MIMO-OFDM Receivers for Universal LLR Reconstruction
comment: Under review for publication in the IEEE
summary: The growing demands for higher throughput and cost-efficient wireless communications drive the need for receivers that are both simple to deploy and robust to hardware impairments and nonlinear environments. While classical model-based receivers and recently proposed deep neural network ( DNN) architectures provide complementary benefits, they either rely on simplified linear Gaussian assumptions, require considerable computational resources, or are tailored for a given setting and modulation. In this work, we propose a compact and modular DNN augmentation that universally refines the soft outputs of existing receivers (model-based or data-driven), addressing two distinct operating regimes: structurally incomplete soft information arising from reduced-complexity detectors, and degraded soft outputs caused by hardware impairments and synchronization errors. A key property of the proposed framework is its task-agnostic nature: operating without any knowledge of the specific source of unreliability, it produces well-calibrated log-likelihood ratios (LLRs) suitable for channel decoding. Our design leverages an element-wise scaled convolutional neural network tailored to perform learned interference cancellation across users and neighboring subcarriers, combined with a training algorithm that encourages accurate LLR s for soft channel decoding. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed augmentation consistently improves diverse receiver algorithms in challenging channel conditions while incurring minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a compact, modular DNN augmentation that universally refines soft outputs (LLRs) from any existing receiver, improving performance under hardware impairments and incomplete information without needing task-specific knowledge.
AI.motivation: To address the need for receivers that are simple to deploy yet robust to hardware impairments and nonlinear environments, overcoming limitations of both classical model-based receivers (rely on simplified linear Gaussian assumptions) and DNN architectures (computationally heavy, task-specific).
AI.method: A compact, modular DNN augmentation using an element-wise scaled convolutional neural network for learned interference cancellation across users and subcarriers. The framework operates task-agnostically (no knowledge of the specific source of unreliability) and is trained to produce well-calibrated LLRs for soft channel decoding.
AI.result: The proposed augmentation consistently improves diverse receiver algorithms in challenging channel conditions while incurring minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DNN augmentation is a universal, lightweight solution that enhances both model-based and data-driven receivers, producing accurate LLRs for channel decoding without requiring knowledge of the specific impairment source.

title: Fast and Accurate Outlier-Aware LiDAR Super-Resolution for SLAM Applications
summary: This work tackles the challenge of enhancing low-resolution LiDAR sensors for SLAM applications through a novel Deep Unrolling-based Super-Resolution (SR) model. We integrate an outlier removal module to ensure structural integrity while maintaining real-time performance. By leveraging a model-based optimization approach, our method efficiently reconstructs high-resolution point clouds while minimizing computational overhead. The proposed SR model is evaluated within a LiDAR SLAM framework, demonstrating significant improvements in pose estimation accuracy and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art SR methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a deep unrolling-based super-resolution model with outlier removal to enhance low-resolution LiDAR sensors for SLAM, achieving improved pose estimation accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of enhancing low-resolution LiDAR sensors for SLAM applications while maintaining real-time performance and structural integrity.
AI.method: A Deep Unrolling-based Super-Resolution (SR) model integrated with an outlier removal module, using a model-based optimization approach to reconstruct high-resolution point clouds with minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: Significant improvements in pose estimation accuracy and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art SR methods within a LiDAR SLAM framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively enhances low-resolution LiDAR data for SLAM, offering a favorable balance between reconstruction quality and real-time performance.

title: A Trainable-by-Parts Operator Learning Framework: Bridging DeepONet and Karhunen-Loeve Expansions for Large-Scale Applications
summary: Training operator-learning models for large-scale problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) is challenging due to the curse of dimensionality, memory constraints, and limited training data. These challenges arise in many scientific and engineering applications, including subsurface flow, climate modeling, and geological carbon storage (GCS). In this work, we propose a scalable operator-learning framework based on the Karhunen-Loeve Deep Neural Network (KL-DNN) and demonstrate its performance for modeling GCS. The model is trained on a dataset comprising 100 samples of large-scale simulations in a three-dimensional domain with 1.7 million cells and 50 time steps. The KL-DNN method constructs latent spaces using low-rank singular value decomposition of static properties and a nested Karhunen-Loeve expansion for dynamic pressure fields, enabling full-resolution predictions without subsampling or spatial coarsening. The KL-DNN model achieves an average root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.1 psi for pressure (0.04% relative error with respect to the average pressure in the domain) and RMSE of 0.0146 for CO2 saturation (5% relative error with respect to the average saturation inside the plume). The model requires 20 minutes of training on a single GPU, representing a 19% reduction in the pressure errors, 7% reduction in the saturation error, and a two-order-of-magnitude speedup compared to DeepONet trained on the same dataset. These results, along with inference time of less than one minute, establish the proposed model as a practical and accurate solution for large-scale PDE problems, enabling rapid uncertainty quantification, history matching, and real-time decision support.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a scalable operator-learning framework (KL-DNN) for large-scale PDE problems, demonstrated on geological carbon storage. It uses low-rank SVD and nested Karhunen-Loeve expansions to handle 3D domains with 1.7M cells, achieving high accuracy (0.04% pressure error, 5% saturation error) with only 100 training samples and 20 minutes of training on a single GPU, outperforming DeepONet in both accuracy and speed.
AI.motivation: Training operator-learning models for large-scale PDE problems is challenging due to the curse of dimensionality, memory constraints, and limited training data, especially in applications like subsurface flow, climate modeling, and geological carbon storage (GCS).
AI.method: The proposed KL-DNN framework constructs latent spaces using low-rank singular value decomposition (SVD) for static properties and a nested Karhunen-Loeve expansion for dynamic pressure fields, enabling full-resolution predictions without subsampling or spatial coarsening.
AI.result: Trained on 100 samples of 3D simulations (1.7M cells, 50 time steps), the model achieves RMSE of 1.1 psi (0.04% relative error) for pressure and 0.0146 (5% relative error) for CO2 saturation, with 20 minutes training on a single GPU. It shows 19% reduction in pressure errors, 7% reduction in saturation errors, and two-orders-of-magnitude speedup over DeepONet.
AI.conclusion: The KL-DNN model is established as a practical and accurate solution for large-scale PDE problems, enabling rapid uncertainty quantification, history matching, and real-time decision support with inference time under one minute.

title: JASPR: Joint Spatial Representation learning of histology and spatial genomics for improved virtual genomic screening and clinical prognostication
summary: Recent studies have shown that spatial properties of tumors are critical for understanding disease biology and predicting patient outcomes. These spatial properties are increasingly uncovered through complementary modalities: spatial transcriptomics (ST) captures spatially-resolved molecular states, while hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images (HE) reveal tissue morphology. While approaches are emerging to fuse these modalities, effective methods that learn not only joint representations but also incorporate spatial context across modalities are lacking. Here, we present JASPR (Joint Spatial Representation learning), a self-supervised deep learning framework that integrates HE images and ST data through a cross-modal reconstruction objective that incorporates spatial context within HE images and ST profiles. It employs shared modules to capture universal spatial properties across modalities, while modality-specific experts encode features unique to morphological and genomic data. We train and validate JASPR on breast cancer datasets, demonstrating that its learned joint representation substantially improves HE-based prediction of 9,248 genes and provides prognostic value for breast cancer outcomes.

AI.tldr: JASPR is a self-supervised deep learning framework that integrates HE whole slide images and spatial transcriptomics data by learning joint spatial representations through cross-modal reconstruction, improving gene prediction and prognostic assessment in breast cancer.
AI.motivation: Spatial properties of tumors are critical for understanding disease biology and predicting outcomes, but existing methods lack effective integration of complementary modalities (ST and HE) with spatial context across modalities.
AI.method: JASPR uses a self-supervised deep learning framework with cross-modal reconstruction objective, incorporating shared modules for universal spatial properties and modality-specific experts for unique morphological and genomic features.
AI.result: On breast cancer datasets, JASPR's joint representation substantially improves HE-based prediction of 9,248 genes and provides prognostic value for breast cancer outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Integrating HE images and ST data through spatial context-aware, cross-modal reconstruction learning yields powerful joint representations that enhance gene prediction and prognosis in cancer.

title: TrajRS: Towards Certified Robustness in Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing
summary: The robustness of trajectory prediction models is crucial for developing safe autonomous driving systems. Adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction can significantly impair the accuracy of predicted trajectories, leading to hazardous driving behaviors. While heuristic defense strategies have been implemented to enhance the robustness of trajectory prediction models, these measures often fail against more sophisticated, targeted adversarial attacks. Hence, there is a pressing need to establish verifiable safety assurances for trajectory prediction models. In this paper, we extend the traditional Randomized Smoothing framework to "TrajRS", which provides a certified robust radius for smoothed trajectory predictors. We clarify and expand the formal definitions of robustness in trajectory prediction and tailor the practical TrajRS scheme specifically to "robustness for the optimal prediction" and "robustness for all possible predictions". An extensive set of experiments demonstrates that TrajRS effectively achieves robustness certification for all smoothed pedestrian trajectory predictors in this work.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TrajRS, an extension of Randomized Smoothing that provides certified robust radius for smoothed trajectory predictors in autonomous driving, achieving verifiable safety assurances.
AI.motivation: Current trajectory prediction models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and existing heuristic defense strategies fail against sophisticated attacks. There is a need for verifiable safety assurances rather than just heuristic defenses.
AI.method: Extends Randomized Smoothing to trajectory prediction (TrajRS), defining formal robustness definitions for "robustness for the optimal prediction" and "robustness for all possible predictions" to provide certified robust radius.
AI.result: TrajRS effectively achieves robustness certification for all smoothed pedestrian trajectory predictors tested in experiments.
AI.conclusion: TrajRS provides a certified robust radius for smoothed trajectory predictors, offering verifiable safety guarantees that go beyond heuristic defenses against adversarial attacks.

title: Complete virtual unwrapping and reading of a rolled Herculaneum papyrus
summary: The carbonized papyri from Herculaneum preserve the only large-scale library to survive from classical antiquity, but many unopened rolls remain unread because physical opening risks irreversible damage. X-ray computed microtomography ($μ$CT) and virtual unwrapping offer a non-invasive route to their texts, yet previous work on sealed Herculaneum scrolls has recovered only localized readings or limited surface regions. Here, using high-resolution phase-contrast $μ$CT acquired on the BM18 beamline at the European Synchrotron Radiation Facility (ESRF), together with improved computational unrolling and machine learning, we achieve the complete virtual unwrapping and reading of PHerc. 1667 under explicit coverage and papyrological-review criteria. This makes PHerc. 1667 the first Herculaneum papyrus to be fully digitally unrolled and read for extended scholarly study without physical opening. In PHerc. Paris 4, the optimized scan protocol makes ink directly visible in the tomographic volume, allowing three-dimensional ink segmentation and independent validation of surface-conditioned ink recovery. In PHerc. 139, we recover title and author-attribution evidence identifying the scroll as Philodemus, On Gods, Book 8. These results move virtual unwrapping of the Herculaneum scrolls beyond isolated demonstrations towards a scalable framework for systematic recovery of the still-unopened library.

AI.tldr: Researchers used high-resolution phase-contrast X-ray microtomography, advanced computational unrolling, and machine learning to fully digitally unroll and read three Herculaneum papyrus scrolls (PHerc. 1667, Paris 4, and PHerc. 139) without physical opening, recovering substantial texts including the identification of PHerc. 139 as Philodemus' On Gods, Book 8.
AI.motivation: The carbonized Herculaneum papyri represent the only large-scale surviving library from classical antiquity, but many sealed rolls remain unread because physical opening causes irreversible damage. Previous virtual unwrapping attempts on sealed scrolls recovered only localized readings or limited surface regions.
AI.method: High-resolution phase-contrast μCT acquired on the BM18 beamline at the ESRF, combined with improved computational unrolling algorithms and machine learning for ink detection and text recovery.
AI.result: PHerc. 1667 was completely virtually unrolled and read (first fully digitally unrolled Herculaneum papyrus); PHerc. Paris 4 achieved direct ink visibility in tomographic volume enabling 3D ink segmentation; PHerc. 139 yielded title and author-attribution identifying it as Philodemus, On Gods, Book 8.
AI.conclusion: These results move virtual unwrapping beyond isolated demonstrations toward a scalable framework for systematic recovery of the still-unopened Herculaneum library.

title: Dynamical System Characterization of Heterogeneous Walker Satellite Networks: An Orbit-Aware Stochastic Geometry Perspective
summary: Heterogeneous and in particular multi-altitude low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite constellations exhibit complex spatial and temporal structures, which require new modeling tools for their performance analysis. In this paper, we develop an orbit-aware stochastic geometry framework modeling today's LEO satellites on various orbits and various altitudes. In particular, we characterize such a system as the superposition of multiple Walker point processes and formulate it as a dynamical system determined by an initial condition and the rotation speeds of satellites and Earth. We show that when the speeds are rationally commensurable, the proposed satellite system is periodic. Then, we show that the system is ergodic when the speeds are rationally independent, establishing a theoretical link between time averages of the system and the expectation of it under the invariant measure. We derive the nearest-satellite distance distribution of a typical receiver at a given latitude and analyze the signal to interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) coverage probability of the typical receiver. We then derive the ergodic throughput of the downlink communication to the typical receiver. Overall, the proposed framework offers a rigorous and tractable tool for analyzing downlink performance in Walker-type heterogeneous LEO satellite networks.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an orbit-aware stochastic geometry framework for modeling heterogeneous multi-altitude LEO satellite constellations as superpositions of Walker point processes, analyzes periodicity/ergodicity under rational speed relationships, and derives key performance metrics including nearest-satellite distance, SINR coverage probability, and ergodic throughput for downlink communications.
AI.motivation: Existing modeling tools are inadequate for capturing the complex spatial and temporal structures of heterogeneous, multi-altitude LEO satellite constellations, necessitating new analytical frameworks for performance evaluation.
AI.method: The authors model the system as a superposition of multiple Walker point processes, formulated as a dynamical system driven by initial conditions and rotational speeds of satellites and Earth. They analyze periodicity under rationally commensurable speeds and ergodicity under rationally independent speeds.
AI.result: Derived the nearest-satellite distance distribution for a typical receiver at a given latitude, SINR coverage probability, and ergodic throughput for downlink communication.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a rigorous, tractable tool for analyzing downlink performance in Walker-type heterogeneous LEO satellite networks, bridging time averages with ensemble expectations via ergodicity.

title: Fast Wireless Foundation Models with Early-Exits
summary: While wireless foundation models (FMs) are demonstrating strong potential to enable AI-Native 6G networks, their high computational cost remains a critical barrier to deployment. The large computational cost stems from the rigid, full-depth execution of the FM backbone for every task, a process we show is not only inefficient but can also degrade performance on unseen out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel early-exit FM framework that attaches lightweight, per-task heads, at the most appropriate exit-stage of a frozen wireless FM encoder, enabling variable-depth inference tailored to each task's preferred representation depth. Our results demonstrate that these intermediate-layer features not only speed-up inference significantly (up to 93% fewer FLOPs), but also provide more transferable representations that exceed the full encoder accuracy on unseen tasks. We further demonstrate that a simple fixed-exit strategy per task is more effective than traditional early-exiting policies that route different samples to different exits based on their perceived difficulty levels.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an early-exit framework for wireless foundation models that attaches lightweight heads at intermediate layers of a frozen encoder, achieving up to 93% FLOPs reduction while improving accuracy on unseen OOD tasks. It also shows that per-task fixed-exit strategies outperform traditional sample-adaptive early-exiting policies.
AI.motivation: Wireless foundation models (FMs) have high computational costs due to rigid full-depth execution, which is inefficient and can degrade performance on unseen out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks.
AI.method: The framework attaches lightweight, per-task heads at the most appropriate exit-stage of a frozen wireless FM encoder, enabling variable-depth inference tailored to each task's preferred representation depth.
AI.result: Intermediate-layer features speed up inference by up to 93% fewer FLOPs and provide more transferable representations that exceed full encoder accuracy on unseen tasks. A simple fixed-exit strategy per task outperforms traditional sample-adaptive early-exiting policies.
AI.conclusion: Early-exit frameworks with per-task fixed exits can reduce computational costs and improve generalization for wireless foundation models, offering a practical path toward AI-native 6G networks.

title: PinNet: Keypoint-Aware Learned Local Descriptors with Geometric Embedding for Loop Closure in LiDAR SLAM
summary: Loop closure is essential to reduce drift and build globally consistent maps in large-scale environments. However, reliable loop closure with only geometric information from, e.g., a LiDAR sensor, remains challenging due to the difficulty of constructing discriminative geometric features. We present PinNet, a neural network that produces local geometric descriptors from point clouds for place recognition and scanto-scan registration. PinNet incorporates a neural network that generates keypoints and their corresponding descriptors, together with a plane-based geometric self-attention module that models inter-keypoint spatial relationships to enhance descriptor discriminability for loop-closure detection and point-cloud registration. The approach is comprehensively evaluated on multiple datasets collected with different LiDAR sensors. Experimental results demonstrate strong place-recognition performance, precise relative pose estimation, and successful single-shot localization in different environments.

AI.tldr: PinNet is a neural network that generates keypoints and descriptors from LiDAR point clouds, using a plane-based geometric self-attention module to enhance loop closure detection and point-cloud registration, achieving strong place recognition and pose estimation across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Loop closure is essential for reducing drift in large-scale SLAM, but reliable loop closure using only geometric information from LiDAR remains challenging due to the difficulty of constructing discriminative geometric features.
AI.method: PinNet incorporates a neural network to generate keypoints and their corresponding descriptors, along with a plane-based geometric self-attention module that models inter-keypoint spatial relationships to improve descriptor discriminability.
AI.result: Evaluated on multiple datasets with different LiDAR sensors, PinNet demonstrates strong place-recognition performance, precise relative pose estimation, and successful single-shot localization in various environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses loop closure challenges using only geometric information, enabling robust place recognition and registration for LiDAR-based SLAM systems.

title: A Gravitational Interpretation of Fine-Tuning Reversion
summary: Fine-tuning on harmless data can partially undo behaviors acquired earlier in training. Safety can erode under benign post-alignment updates, unlearned capabilities can re-emerge, latent traits can transfer through apparently unrelated supervision, and related post-alignment fragility appears in other generative settings. We argue these phenomena are usefully viewed through a common training-history lens. Our hypothesis is geometric: large early training phases create dominant behavioral manifolds, while later alignment or specialization phases are shallower displacements from them. Subsequent fine-tuning can therefore inherit a persistent reversion component pointing back toward a witness of the dominant manifold. We call this the gravitational interpretation of fine-tuning reversion. Across our main settings, representational drift rapidly acquires a component along a history-defined reversion direction (v_rev). In our main track, alignment with v_rev rises from cos = 0.429 +/- 0.052 after the first update to 0.647 +/- 0.021 by step 20. Across 24 run-step pairs, every observed alignment exceeds the p99 of an isotropic activation-space null. We demonstrate that selectively blocking motion along v_rev changes the final alignment at T=100 from 0.648 +/- 0.009 to -0.211 +/- 0.021 and reduces harmfulness from 19.0% +/- 4.0% to 8.5% +/- 1.5% with little task cost. These results support v_rev as a causally relevant mediator of early post-alignment reversion in our setup. Importantly, we do not claim that v_rev is the unique safety direction, nor that the dominant manifold is directly observed; rather, we identify a robust, history-defined direction that explains and partially controls early reversion dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a "gravitational" interpretation of fine-tuning reversion, where early large-scale training creates a dominant behavioral manifold, and later fine-tuning is shallow, causing model behavior to revert toward this learned manifold. The authors identify a reversion direction (v_rev) that causally mediates early post-alignment reversion, and show that blocking this direction significantly reduces harmfulness.
AI.motivation: To explain why fine-tuning on harmless data can undo safety alignment, why unlearned capabilities re-emerge, and why post-alignment fragility exists—by proposing a unified geometric interpretation through training history.
AI.method: The authors propose a "gravitational" geometric hypothesis: early training creates dominant behavioral manifolds; later alignment is a shallow displacement. They measure representational drift along a history-defined reversion direction (v_rev), and demonstrate causal intervention by selectively blocking motion along v_rev during fine-tuning.
AI.result: Alignment with v_rev rises from cos=0.429 to 0.647 by step 20. Blocking motion along v_rev changes final alignment from 0.648 to -0.211 and reduces harmfulness from 19.0% to 8.5% with little task cost. All observed alignments exceed p99 of isotropic null.
AI.conclusion: v_rev is a causally relevant mediator of early post-alignment reversion. The gravitational interpretation provides a useful lens for understanding fine-tuning reversion, though v_rev is not claimed to be the unique safety direction.

title: RadarTwin: Scene-Specific mmWave Radar Simulation and Learning for Mobile Indoor Perception
summary: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar perception is limited by data scarcity: models trained on existing radar datasets fail to generalize to new objects, environments, and sensing trajectories. We present RadarTwin, a framework for generating deployment-specific radar training data before real data collection. Given a 3D reconstruction of a target space (phone LiDAR, robot-mounted sensing, or RGB-to-3D), RadarTwin uses a vision-language model to infer radar-relevant surface materials and a physics-based ray tracer to synthesize raw frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar measurements with multi-bounce propagation. To study what transfers from simulation to reality, we collect a paired real-simulated dataset spanning household objects, material classes, distances, rotations, translations, and mobile sensing trajectories. We show that simulated and real radar share the same object-discriminative shape and material features, and that modeling the environment's multipath is essential to matching real measurements.
