A representation trained on simulation alone recognizes real objects at 2.5 times chance with no real radar labels, and a few labeled examples raise this to 95.3% on a 12-way recognition task. RadarTwin enables training radar perception for a new space before any real radar data is collected there.

AI.tldr: RadarTwin generates synthetic millimeter-wave radar training data from 3D reconstructions using vision-language models for material inference and physics-based ray tracing, achieving 95.3% recognition accuracy on real objects with minimal real labels.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radar perception suffers from data scarcity—models trained on existing datasets fail to generalize to new objects, environments, and sensing trajectories, motivating the need for deployment-specific synthetic training data generation.
AI.method: Given a 3D reconstruction of a target space, RadarTwin uses a vision-language model to infer radar-relevant surface materials and a physics-based ray tracer to synthesize raw FMCW radar measurements with multi-bounce propagation.
AI.result: A representation trained on simulation alone recognizes real objects at 2.5 times chance with no real radar labels; few-shot labeled examples raise accuracy to 95.3% on a 12-way recognition task. Simulated and real radar share object-discriminative shape and material features.
AI.conclusion: RadarTwin enables training radar perception for a new space before any real radar data is collected, addressing data scarcity by generating realistic synthetic training data that transfers to real-world scenarios.

title: ComMem: Complementary Memory Systems for Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
comment: A brain-inspired complementary memory framework leveraging fast visual caching and slow textual refinement for VLM test-time adaptation
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) of vision-language models (VLMs) is essential for their robust deployment in dynamic, real-world environments. However, existing TTA methods often adapt locally without accumulating knowledge over time, or operating within a single modality without exploiting VLMs' inherently multi-modal nature. Inspired by the \textbf{Com}plementary \textbf{Mem}ory systems of the biological brain, we propose \textbf{ComMem}, an innovative approach that mimics the distinct but cooperative roles of the hippocampus and neocortex to enable effective TTA for VLMs. ComMem consists of two key components: a fast-adapting detailed memory, akin to the hippocampus, that forms a dynamic visual cache from high-confidence test samples; and a slow-integrating abstract memory, akin to the neocortex, that continually refines global textual prototypes. For each test instance, ComMem jointly optimizes both memory systems to ensure cross-modal consistency. Extensive experiments on 15 benchmark datasets show that ComMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both natural distribution shifts and cross-dataset generalization, offering a promising direction for enhancing VLMs' practical adaptability.

AI.tldr: ComMem is a test-time adaptation method for vision-language models inspired by biological complementary memory systems (hippocampus and neocortex), featuring fast-adapting visual cache and slow-integrating textual prototypes that jointly optimize cross-modal consistency, outperforming SOTA on 15 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing TTA methods for VLMs adapt locally without accumulating knowledge over time, or operate within a single modality without exploiting VLMs' multi-modal nature, limiting their robustness in dynamic environments.
AI.method: ComMem mimics biological complementary memory systems with two components: (1) a fast-adapting detailed memory (hippocampus-like) forming a dynamic visual cache from high-confidence test samples, and (2) a slow-integrating abstract memory (neocortex-like) continually refining global textual prototypes. Both memory systems are jointly optimized for cross-modal consistency per test instance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 15 benchmark datasets show ComMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both natural distribution shifts and cross-dataset generalization.
AI.conclusion: ComMem offers a promising direction for enhancing VLMs' practical adaptability by enabling effective knowledge accumulation and leveraging multi-modal nature through bio-inspired complementary memory systems.

title: A Self-Supervised Learning Framework for Video Encoding Complexity Clustering
summary: Adaptive video streaming is a widely used technique for delivering video content over the internet. One of the key challenges is determining the optimal encoding settings for each video, which can vary significantly based on its content and characteristics. In this paper, we propose Compression Echo Contrastive Learning (CECL), a novel self-supervised learning framework for clustering videos based on their encoding complexity. Our method leverages the response of a video to compression - the Compression Echo - as a supervisory signal, allowing the model to capture underlying encoding characteristics during pretraining. We conduct extensive experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of our learned representations for the downstream task of clustering videos by their encoding complexity. Our results show that CECL improves upon existing state-of-the-art visual encoders and delivers strong bitrate and quality savings against the fixed bitrate ladder.

AI.tldr: CECL is a self-supervised learning framework that clusters videos by encoding complexity using compression response as a supervisory signal, achieving better bitrate and quality savings than fixed bitrate ladders.
AI.motivation: Determining optimal encoding settings for adaptive video streaming is challenging because encoding complexity varies significantly across different video content.
AI.method: CECL uses Compression Echo (video's response to compression) as a self-supervised signal to learn representations that capture encoding characteristics during pretraining.
AI.result: CECL improves upon state-of-the-art visual encoders for clustering videos by encoding complexity and delivers strong bitrate and quality savings compared to fixed bitrate ladders.
AI.conclusion: CECL effectively clusters videos by encoding complexity in a self-supervised manner, enabling more efficient adaptive video streaming through content-aware encoding.

title: SoftBinary Coding: A New Information-Theoretic Neural Compression Paradigm
comment: accepted to ICML 2026 as a conference paper
summary: Neural compression is currently dominated by Nonlinear Transform Coding (NTC), which maps data to real-valued latents via continuous transforms. Despite its success, NTC suffers from train-test mismatch due to non-differentiable quantization, a ``smoothness bias" inherent in continuous transforms that precludes optimality for certain sources, and a loss of ``shaping gain" due to the complexity of including high-dimensional vector quantization. We propose SoftBinary Coding (SBC), an end-to-end learning paradigm that bypasses these limitations by using a stochastic binary latent space. In the spirit of vector quantization, SBC employs discrete representations and compresses them through a novel fast binary channel simulation scheme, for which we provide a proof of rate optimality. Experimental gains on information-theoretic sources provide both theoretical and practical closure to NTC's limitations, establishing discrete binary structures as a viable path toward reaching optimal rate--distortion bounds. Surprisingly, SBC also achieves state-of-the-art performance on vector quantization of i.i.d. sources, exceeding Trellis Coded Quantization of the Gaussian source.

AI.tldr: SoftBinary Coding (SBC) is a new end-to-end learned compression paradigm using stochastic binary latents that overcomes key limitations of Neural Transform Coding (NTC)—quantization mismatch, smoothness bias, and shaping gain loss—achieving rate optimality and state-of-the-art vector quantization performance.
AI.motivation: NTC suffers from three key limitations: (1) train-test mismatch due to non-differentiable quantization, (2) smoothness bias from continuous transforms preventing optimality for certain sources, and (3) loss of shaping gain due to difficulty of high-dimensional vector quantization. The authors aim to address these fundamental shortcomings.
AI.method: SBC uses a stochastic binary latent space with discrete representations, compressed via a novel fast binary channel simulation scheme with proven rate optimality. It operates as an end-to-end learning paradigm in the spirit of vector quantization.
AI.result: SBC achieves state-of-the-art performance on vector quantization of i.i.d. sources, including surpassing Trellis Coded Quantization on Gaussian sources. It provides both theoretical and practical closure to NTC's limitations.
AI.conclusion: Discrete binary structures are a viable path toward reaching optimal rate-distortion bounds, and SBC establishes a new paradigm that overcomes NTC's inherent limitations.

title: Effective Depth in Joint Source-Channel Coding: An Implicit Equilibrium Analysis
summary: A fundamental design question in deep joint source-channel coding (Deep JSCC) remains insufficiently explored: given a channel signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), what effective computation depth is required for semantic reconstruction? Existing Deep JSCC systems typically employ fixed-depth neural architectures selected through empirical hyperparameter tuning, which may lead to unnecessary computation under favorable channel conditions and insufficient refinement under severe channel noise. This paper proposes \emph{Implicit-JSCC}, an implicit equilibrium framework in which semantic encoding and decoding are formulated as fixed-point equilibrium processes. The effective encoder and decoder depths are determined by residual-based solver convergence rather than manually predefined layer numbers, while parameter sharing across equilibrium iterations enables depth-independent parameter complexity. To analyze the resulting effective-depth behavior, we develop a Gaussian-process-inspired kernel evolution framework that models equilibrium iterations as an effective-depth propagation process. Since channel noise is injected between the encoder and decoder, the analysis tracks channel-induced representation perturbations across receiver-side equilibrium iterations and derives a theory-guided depth--SNR relationship. After offline calibration of the system-specific parameters, the resulting model characterizes the required receiver-side refinement depth under different SNRs. Extensive experiments show that Implicit-JSCC achieves competitive reconstruction performance while enabling residual-based adaptive inference and controllable computation--quality tradeoffs. The depth--SNR model further provides a characterization of the SNR-dependent refinement depth required to reach a prescribed perturbation tolerance.

AI.tldr: Implicit-JSCC proposes an implicit equilibrium framework for deep joint source-channel coding where encoder/decoder depths are determined by fixed-point solver convergence rather than fixed architectures, enabling SNR-adaptive computation with a theory-guided depth-SNR relationship.
AI.motivation: Existing Deep JSCC systems use fixed-depth neural architectures chosen via empirical tuning, causing unnecessary computation under favorable channel conditions and insufficient refinement under severe noise. The optimal computation depth relative to channel SNR is underexplored.
AI.method: Implicit-JSCC formulates semantic encoding/decoding as fixed-point equilibrium processes where effective depth is determined by residual-based solver convergence. A Gaussian-process-inspired kernel evolution framework models equilibrium iterations as effective-depth propagation, deriving a theory-guided depth-SNR relationship through analyzing channel-induced perturbations across receiver-side iterations.
AI.result: Competitive reconstruction performance with residual-based adaptive inference and controllable computation-quality tradeoffs. The depth-SNR model characterizes the SNR-dependent refinement depth needed to reach a prescribed perturbation tolerance.
AI.conclusion: Implicit equilibrium frameworks can effectively replace fixed-depth architectures in Deep JSCC, enabling depth-independent parameter complexity and adaptive computation that scales with channel conditions, while the depth-SNR model provides theoretical understanding of the required computation.

title: J-LAW: Joint Localization and Actionable World Modeling via Coupled Latent Factor Graphs
summary: Classical SLAM estimates metric poses and a geometric map but produces no actionable predictive model for planning. Action-conditioned world models learn compact latent dynamics for planning but ignore global metric consistency and accumulate drift under open-loop rollout. We argue these are two views of the same estimation problem and propose J-LAW (Joint Localization and Actionable World Modeling) in this letter: a coupled factor graph that jointly optimizes metric object poses, latent world states, and latent landmark embeddings. The bridge is a pose-conditioned latent encoder and a learned pose--latent coupling factor, so that better localization improves the world model and vice versa. We cast observation, action-conditioned prediction, metric odometry, pose--latent coupling, latent loop closure, and latent landmark observation as probabilistic factors in a single MAP objective. Real-data experiments on PushT and WildGS show that coupled graph correction substantially reduces latent prediction RMSE and endpoint drift relative to open-loop rollout, while latent loop closure improves global trajectory consistency. J-LAW yields a map that is simultaneously metric (poses) and actionable (latent landmarks for planning).

AI.tldr: J-LAW jointly optimizes metric object poses and latent world states in a coupled factor graph, enabling both accurate localization and actionable planning.
AI.motivation: Classical SLAM lacks predictive models for planning, while action-conditioned world models ignore global metric consistency and suffer from drift.
AI.method: A coupled factor graph with pose-conditioned latent encoder and learned pose-latent coupling factor, unifying observation, prediction, odometry, coupling, loop closure, and landmark observation factors in a MAP objective.
AI.result: Reduced latent prediction RMSE and endpoint drift, improved global trajectory consistency on PushT and WildGS datasets.
AI.conclusion: Metric and actionable world models are two views of the same problem and can be jointly optimized for mutual benefit.

title: NIVA: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Actionable Earth System Intelligence
summary: Recent advances in AI-driven weather and climate modeling have improved forecast skill while reducing computational cost. However, existing data-driven approaches are limited in their ability to model coupled Earth system dynamics, which is required for extending predictability beyond the ~2-week horizon. To address this, we introduce NIVA, a multimodal foundation model designed to learn unified representations across Earth system components. While the full framework targets atmosphere, ocean, ice, and land interactions, we focus here on a two-modality setting (ocean and atmosphere) as a controlled proof of concept to evaluate whether foundation models can learn coupled dynamics. Trained on large-scale Earth system simulations, NIVA learns physically meaningful cross-modal structure, providing a foundation for subseasonal-to-seasonal prediction. As initial validation, we show that NIVA captures key modes of climate variability through accurate prediction of major climate indices.

AI.tldr: NIVA is a multimodal AI foundation model that learns unified representations across Earth system components (ocean and atmosphere), showing promise for subseasonal-to-seasonal climate prediction by capturing key climate variability modes.
AI.motivation: Existing data-driven weather and climate models struggle to model coupled Earth system dynamics needed for predictions beyond ~2 weeks. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a multimodal foundation model that learns unified cross-component representations.
AI.method: NVIDIA is a multimodal foundation model trained on large-scale Earth system simulations. The paper focuses on a two-modality proof-of-concept involving ocean and atmosphere, learning physically meaningful cross-modal structure.
AI.result: NVIDIA captures key modes of climate variability through accurate prediction of major climate indices, serving as initial validation of its ability to learn coupled dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a foundation for subseasonal-to-seasonal prediction by learning physically meaningful cross-modal representations of coupled Earth system dynamics.

title: CLOSER-VLN: Closed-Loop Self-Verified Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning for Aerial Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-language navigation (VLN) has recently advanced with large language and multimodal models, enabling agents to follow natural-language instructions in unseen environments without training a task-specific navigation policy. However, most existing VLN methods relying on large models still adopt an open-loop decision-execution approach, where candidate actions are generated from instructions and observations but are rarely verified or corrected before execution. This causes critical issues in aerial VLN, where minor errors in intermediate actions may quickly accumulate into large trajectory deviations and lead to target loss. To address this issue, we propose Closed-loop Self-verified Retrieval-augmented Reasoning (CLOSER), a training-policy-free method that sequentially performs action reasoning, reliability verification, targeted retrieval, and action correction in a closed-loop manner before executing concrete actions. We instantiate the CLOSER in aerial VLN tasks and develop a CLOSER-VLN framework, which is composed of three components: a hierarchical reasoner for generating candidate actions based on available information, a multidimensional action verifier for assessing the reliability of actions generated by the reasoner, and a verification-triggered multimodal retriever for retrieving targeted exemplars from a memory bank only when verification fails. We conduct experimental evaluations on the CityNav benchmark, where CLOSER-VLN achieves 32.01% SR and 21.28% SPL on the test-unseen split, confirming the effectiveness of closed-loop reasoning.

AI.tldr: CLOSER introduces a closed-loop (reason-verify-retrieve-correct) framework for aerial VLN that prevents error accumulation without requiring task-specific training, achieving 32.01% SR on CityNav.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN methods using large models follow an open-loop decision-execution approach where actions are generated but rarely verified or corrected before execution, causing critical error accumulation in aerial VLN where minor errors lead to large trajectory deviations and target loss.
AI.method: CLOSER performs closed-loop action reasoning, reliability verification, targeted retrieval (triggered only when verification fails), and action correction before executing actions. The framework has three components: hierarchical reasoner, multidimensional action verifier, and verification-triggered multimodal retriever.
AI.result: On the CityNav benchmark test-unseen split: 32.01% Success Rate (SR) and 21.28% Success weighted by Path Length (SPL).
AI.conclusion: The closed-loop reasoning approach effectively addresses error accumulation in aerial VLN without requiring a trained navigation policy, validating the importance of verification and correction mechanisms before action execution.

title: Agentic Abstention: Do Agents Know When to Stop Instead of Act?
summary: LLM agents are expected to act over multiple turns, using search, browsing interfaces, and terminal tools to complete user goals. Yet not every goal is well specified or achievable in the available environment. In such cases, a reliable agent should recognize that further interaction is unlikely to help and abstain from additional tool calls. We define Agentic Abstention, the problem of deciding when an agent should stop acting under uncertainty. Unlike standard LLM abstention, which is usually evaluated as a single-turn answer-or-abstain decision, agentic abstention is a sequential decision problem: an agent can answer, abstain, or gather more information at each turn, and the need to abstain may only become clear after interacting with the environment. We study this problem across web shopping, terminal environments, and question answering, evaluating 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 agent scaffolds on more than 28,000 tasks. Our results show that the main challenge is not only whether agents can abstain, but also when they abstain. Some agents never abstain when they should, while others do so only after many unnecessary interactions. This gap is especially large on tasks where the instruction appears feasible until the environment reveals otherwise (e.g., no valid result matches the instruction). We further find that model scale, reasoning, and agent scaffolding affect abstention in different ways, where larger or more capable models sometimes perform worse at timely abstention. Finally, we introduce CONVOLVE, a context engineering method for improving agentic abstention that distills full interaction trajectories into reusable stopping rules. On WebShop, CONVOLVE substantially improves timely abstention without updating model parameters, raising Llama-3.3-70B's timely recall rate from 26.7 to 57.4. Our dataset and code are available at https://lhannnn.github.io/agentic-abstention

AI.tldr: This paper defines "Agentic Abstention" — deciding when an LLM agent should stop acting under uncertainty across multiple turns. Evaluating 13 agent systems on 28,000+ tasks across web shopping, terminals, and QA, they find agents either never abstain or abstain too late. They propose CONVOLVE, a context engineering method that distills trajectories into stopping rules, improving timely abstention from 26.7% to 57.4% on WebShop without parameter updates.
AI.motivation: LLM agents operate over multiple turns but often face ill-specified or unachievable goals. Current abstention research focuses on single-turn answer-or-abstain, ignoring the sequential nature of agentic tasks where the need to abstain may only become clear after environment interaction.
AI.method: The authors define Agentic Abstention as a sequential decision problem and evaluate 13 LLM-as-agent systems + 2 agent scaffolds on 28,000+ tasks across web shopping, terminal environments, and QA. They introduce CONVOLVE, a context engineering method that distills full interaction trajectories into reusable stopping rules to improve timely abstention without parameter updates.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Agents either never abstain when needed or abstain only after excessive interactions; (2) This gap is largest when instructions seem feasible but the environment reveals otherwise; (3) Larger/more capable models sometimes perform worse at timely abstention; (4) CONVOLVE raises Llama-3.3-70B's timely recall from 26.7% to 57.4% on WebShop.
AI.conclusion: Agentic abstention is a critical yet underexplored problem. The main challenge is not just whether agents can abstain but when they do so. CONVOLVE demonstrates that context engineering can substantially improve timely abstention without model updates, suggesting a promising direction for building more reliable multi-turn agents.

title: Performance Analysis of Hardware-Accelerated 10-Bit 4:2:2 Encoding with Split-Frame Encoding for High-Fidelity V-PCC Streaming
comment: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Image Processing Workshops (ICIP 2026), 13-17 September 2026, Tampere, Finland
summary: Video-based Point Cloud Compression (V-PCC) encodes volumetric data by projecting 3D geometry and texture onto 2D video frames. To prevent spatial distortion and color bleeding during 3D reconstruction, this process requires 10-bit color depth and 4:2:2 chroma subsampling, rather than the standard 8-bit 4:2:0 format. Additionally, capturing high-density dynamic point clouds requires demanding encoding parameters, such as 8K resolution at framerates up to 120 fps. Historically, the lack of 4:2:2 chroma support in older GPU hardware encoders restricted real-time V-PCC to custom Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs). However, the recent introduction of NVIDIA's Blackwell GPU architecture, featuring on-chip hardware encoders with 10-bit 4:2:2 support, presents an opportunity to shift this workload to general-purpose hardware. This paper investigates the feasibility of such an approach. Using a commercially available Blackwell GPU equipped with four parallel on-die hardware encoders as a testbed, we evaluate the throughput, rate-distortion (RD) performance, and power consumption of 8K 10-bit 4:2:2 HEVC across various Split-Frame Encoding (SFE) configurations. Our results demonstrate that 4-way SFE achieves an encoding throughput of 122 fps, successfully meeting the strict real-time constraints of high-density V-PCC. Although the inability to exploit spatial redundancies across slice boundaries results in a BD-Rate penalty of up to 5%, the measured throughput and power efficiency establish standard, commercial off-the-shelf GPUs as a highly viable baseline for real-time volumetric video streaming.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that NVIDIA's Blackwell GPU, with its four parallel hardware encoders supporting 10-bit 4:2:2 HEVC, can achieve real-time V-PCC encoding (122 fps at 8K), making general-purpose GPUs a viable alternative to custom ASICs for volumetric video compression.
AI.motivation: Historically, real-time V-PCC required custom ASICs because older GPU hardware encoders lacked 4:2:2 chroma subsampling support. The paper aims to assess whether the new Blackwell GPU architecture can replace these specialized circuits.
AI.method: Using a commercial Blackwell GPU with four on-die hardware encoders, the authors evaluate Split-Frame Encoding (SFE) configurations for 8K 10-bit 4:2:2 HEVC, measuring throughput, rate-distortion performance, and power consumption.
AI.result: 4-way SFE achieves 122 fps encoding throughput, meeting real-time V-PCC requirements. BD-Rate penalty is up to 5% due to inability to exploit inter-slice spatial redundancy. Power efficiency is favorable.
AI.conclusion: Standard off-the-shelf Blackwell GPUs are a highly viable baseline for real-time volumetric video streaming, replacing the need for custom ASICs while offering competitive throughput and power efficiency.

title: Age of Information Under DCC Rate Constraints for V2I Broadcast Along Urban Corridors
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, submitted to IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: ETSI Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC) limits roadside unit (RSU) broadcast rates based on channel load, yet its impact on age of information (AoI) for vehicle-to-infrastructure updates remains uncharacterized under real traffic. We derive the AoI of DCC-constrained V2I broadcast, revealing a hyperbolic density dependence that induces diurnal AoI variation exceeding 4 times on a four-RSU corridor, with the DCC target CBR parameter as the dominant control. We propose a cooperative policy exploiting upstream spatial traffic correlation to improve channel load estimation, with a safeguard ensuring non-negative gains. Evaluated on a 5-day, 762,050-vehicle trace from Kuwait City, the policy reduces corridor AoI by 5% at moderate and up to 66% at conservative DCC settings.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how DCC regulations impact AoI in V2I broadcasts, revealing diurnal AoI variations of 4x+, and proposes a cooperative policy using upstream traffic correlation to reduce AoI by up to 66% under conservative settings, validated on a large real-world trace.
AI.motivation: ETSI DCC limits RSU broadcast rates based on channel load, but its impact on AoI for V2I updates under real traffic conditions was previously uncharacterized.
AI.method: Derived AoI of DCC-constrained V2I broadcast analytically; proposed a cooperative policy exploiting upstream spatial traffic correlation for better channel load estimation with a non-negative gain safeguard; evaluated on a 5-day, 762,050-vehicle trace from Kuwait City.
AI.result: Diurnal AoI variation exceeds 4x on a four-RSU corridor; DCC target CBR is the dominant control parameter; cooperative policy reduces corridor AoI by 5% at moderate and up to 66% at conservative DCC settings.
AI.conclusion: DCC introduces significant AoI fluctuations under real traffic; the proposed cooperative policy effectively mitigates this, especially under conservative DCC configurations.

title: Active Learning for Channel Knowledge Map Construction via Bayesian Inference Diffusion Models
summary: Channel knowledge maps (CKMs) are regarded as key enablers of environment-aware communications in future wireless networks, as they provide location-specific channel information by establishing an explicit connection between wireless devices and the physical propagation environment. As a representative CKM, the channel gain map (CGM) characterizes the spatial distributions of large-scale fading to support wireless environment awareness and network optimization. Existing CGM construction methods generally lack a well-defined sampling-point acquisition strategy, which may result in a limited number of sampling points being allocated to spatially redundant or highly predictable regions, thereby degrading CGM reconstruction performance in complex propagation environments. In this paper, we propose an active-learning-based diffusion framework for efficient CGM construction. By combining Bayesian inference with the diffusion model, the proposed method estimates epistemic uncertainty without retraining the model. Two uncertainty quantification algorithms are further developed along the reverse diffusion process to generate element-wise epistemic uncertainty maps. Furthermore, an uncertainty-aware sampling strategy is designed to determine new observation locations by jointly considering epistemic uncertainty and spatial distribution uniformity. Experimental results on both static and dynamic CGM datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves better reconstruction performance than baseline methods. These results indicate that the proposed method can effectively improve the utilization efficiency of limited sampling points and enhance the accuracy of CGM construction in complex wireless propagation environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an active-learning-based diffusion framework for efficient channel gain map (CGM) construction, combining Bayesian inference with diffusion models to estimate uncertainty and design sampling strategies, achieving better reconstruction performance in complex wireless environments.
AI.motivation: Existing CGM construction methods lack a well-defined sampling-point acquisition strategy, often allocating limited sampling points to redundant or highly predictable regions, which degrades reconstruction performance in complex propagation environments.
AI.method: Proposes an active-learning-based diffusion framework that combines Bayesian inference with diffusion models to estimate epistemic uncertainty without retraining. Two uncertainty quantification algorithms are developed along the reverse diffusion process for element-wise uncertainty maps, plus an uncertainty-aware sampling strategy balancing epistemic uncertainty and spatial distribution uniformity.
AI.result: Experiments on both static and dynamic CGM datasets show the proposed method achieves better reconstruction performance than baseline methods, improving utilization efficiency of limited sampling points and enhancing CGM accuracy in complex wireless environments.
AI.conclusion: The active-learning diffusion framework effectively improves sampling efficiency and reconstruction accuracy for CGM construction, serving as a key enabler for environment-aware communications in future wireless networks.

title: CubifyGS: Object-Centric 3D Gaussian Splatting for Lifelong Dynamic Scene Maintenance
comment: Accepted to IROS 2026. 8 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables
summary: Lifelong scene mapping under rigid object rearrangement remains a fundamental challenge in robotics. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables high-fidelity modeling, primitive-level updates often cause persistent ghosting and slow recovery. We propose CubifyGS, an object-level mapping framework that shifts dynamic maintenance from passive re-optimization to active asset management. CubifyGS models movable instances as reusable Gaussian assets, detects object appearance and disappearance, and updates maps through asset retrieval, rigid transformation, and explicit pruning rather than reconstruction from scratch. To address geometric voids and local photometric mismatch after such edits, we further propose an event-triggered adaptive optimization strategy that focuses computation on affected regions. We validate our approach on a newly constructed high-fidelity dynamic benchmark, demonstrating that CubifyGS improves artifact suppression and maintenance efficiency over representative reproducible baselines in the evaluated object-rearrangement setting.

AI.tldr: CubifyGS is an object-level lifelong mapping framework that handles rigid object rearrangement by treating movable objects as reusable Gaussian assets, using active asset management instead of passive re-optimization to eliminate ghosting and improve efficiency.
AI.motivation: Lifelong scene mapping under rigid object rearrangement causes persistent ghosting and slow recovery in 3DGS due to primitive-level updates; existing methods rely on passive re-optimization which is inefficient.
AI.method: Models movable instances as reusable Gaussian assets; detects object appearance/disappearance; updates maps via asset retrieval, rigid transformation, and explicit pruning; uses event-triggered adaptive optimization focusing on affected regions.
AI.result: Validated on a new high-fidelity dynamic benchmark; improves artifact suppression and maintenance efficiency over representative baselines in object-rearrangement settings.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from passive re-optimization to active asset management effectively addresses ghosting and inefficiency in lifelong scene mapping under object rearrangement.

title: Improving Coherence in Hierarchical Time Series Forecasting using Structured Temporal Fusion
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures. Preprint. Source files included
summary:
In many real-world applications, such as retail sales, energy usage, and supply chain planning, forecasting is performed across hierarchical structures. These structures often represent aggregations (e.g., products to categories to regions), where forecasts must not only be accurate but also coherent, meaning that lower-level predictions sum correctly to higher-level forecasts. Traditional statistical methods, such as Bottom-Up and MinT, enforce coherence through post-processing but fail to model complex nonlinear temporal dependencies and covariate interactions.
  We propose Hierarchical Temporal Fusion (HTF), a novel extension of the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) that integrates structured hierarchical embeddings with a coherence-aware loss function to ensure consistent forecasts across all levels of a hierarchy. Rather than applying reconciliation after forecasting, HTF embeds coherence directly into the training objective. The coherence loss penalizes the difference between aggregated child forecasts and their corresponding parent forecasts during training, enabling the model to learn both temporal dynamics and structural consistency simultaneously.
  We evaluate HTF on two publicly available benchmark datasets: the M5 Walmart forecasting dataset and a publicly available hierarchical energy consumption dataset. Results demonstrate that HTF substantially reduces forecast incoherence while improving forecasting accuracy compared with classical reconciliation methods and deep learning baselines. In addition, attention visualization and embedding analysis provide insight into how temporal and structural information contribute to hierarchical forecasting performance.

AI.tldr: HTF extends Temporal Fusion Transformer with hierarchical embeddings and a coherence-aware loss to directly learn structurally consistent forecasts across hierarchies, outperforming traditional reconciliation methods on M5 and energy datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional hierarchical forecasting methods (e.g., Bottom-Up, MinT) enforce coherence via post-processing but fail to model complex nonlinear temporal dependencies and covariate interactions.
AI.method: HTF integrates structured hierarchical embeddings with a coherence-aware loss function that penalizes aggregation discrepancies between child and parent forecasts during training, learning temporal dynamics and structural consistency simultaneously.
AI.result: On M5 Walmart and energy consumption datasets, HTF substantially reduces forecast incoherence and improves forecasting accuracy compared to classical reconciliation methods and deep learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: Embedding coherence directly into the training objective is effective; attention visualization and embedding analysis reveal how temporal and structural information jointly contribute to hierarchical forecasting.

title: Semantic-Aware Generative Image Transmission for Resource-Constrained Visual IoT Systems
summary: Resource-constrained visual Internet of Things (IoT) systems, such as edge cameras, unmanned sensing platforms, industrial inspection nodes, and remote monitoring sensors, often need to transmit task-relevant visual evidence over low-rate wireless links to an edge/cloud service. Existing image communication methods usually compress or transmit complete global representations, leaving limited room to exploit receiver-side generative restoration. This paper proposes a semantic-aware generative image transmission framework for edge-assisted visual IoT. The image captured by an IoT visual sensor is encoded into a discrete token grid by a VQ encoder. At the IoT transmitter or nearby gateway, token recoverability, estimated from prediction entropy and local structure complexity, is fused with semantic importance obtained from instance segmentation and category-aware scoring. A spatial dispersal sampler then selects the tokens to be transmitted under a bitrate budget. The transmitter sends only the quantization indices of kept tokens and a binary mask map, while the edge/cloud receiver recovers masked tokens through MaskGIT with Halton sequence scheduling. Experiments on Kodak and VisDrone scenes under AWGN and Rayleigh channels show that the proposed method provides a flexible bitrate-quality tradeoff for narrowband visual IoT links. At 0.074 bpp, it uses 44.6% of the transmitted bits of the 0.167-bpp DeepJSCC/WITT reference while achieving 29.9 dB PSNR. A pseudo-GT downstream detection study on Kodak further shows that semantic-aware masking preserves task-relevant objects better than random masking at both 30% and 50% mask ratios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a semantic-aware generative image transmission framework for resource-constrained visual IoT, where a VQ encoder compresses images into discrete tokens, selects important tokens based on semantic and structural cues, transmits only those tokens and a mask map, and uses MaskGIT with Halton scheduling for recovery at the edge/cloud receiver. It achieves 29.9 dB PSNR at 0.074 bpp using only 44.6% of the bits of a baseline.
AI.motivation: Resource-constrained visual IoT systems need to transmit task-relevant visual evidence over low-rate wireless links, but existing image communication methods compress/transmit complete global representations and leave limited room for receiver-side generative restoration.
AI.method: 1. VQ encoder encodes image into discrete token grid; 2. Token recoverability estimated from prediction entropy & local structure complexity, fused with semantic importance from instance segmentation & category-aware scoring; 3. Spatial dispersal sampler selects tokens under bitrate budget; 4. Transmitter sends quantization indices of kept tokens + binary mask map; 5. Edge/cloud receiver recovers masked tokens via MaskGIT with Halton sequence scheduling.
AI.result: At 0.074 bpp, uses 44.6% of the bits of the 0.167-bpp DeepJSCC/WITT reference while achieving 29.9 dB PSNR. Semantic-aware masking preserves task-relevant objects better than random masking at both 30% and 50% mask ratios in downstream detection.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a flexible bitrate-quality tradeoff for narrowband visual IoT links, effectively leveraging generative restoration by transmitting only semantically important and recoverable tokens.

title: Agent Safety Is Action Alignment
summary: Large language models increasingly act as agents: they call tools, move money, delete records, and send messages on a user's behalf. To keep them safe, practitioners imported the chatbot-era recipe (train the model to refuse unsafe inputs) into the agentic setting, and treat the resulting capability loss as a manageable ``alignment tax.'' We argue this is a \emph{category error}. Refusal is a primitive for \emph{content safety}, where the harm is in the model's output and is therefore a learnable function of it. Agentic harm is different in kind: it lies not in any output but in the relation between the authority an action exercises and the authority the user granted, which is absent from the text the model sees. Importing content-safety methods into this regime does not trade capability for safety; it pays capability and buys negative security. We support this with three lines of evidence spanning the autonomy spectrum: defense-trained models learn surface patterns rather than intent; the same training collapses multi-step agents before any threat appears while leaving them exploitable; and even undefended frontier models exceed granted authority under ordinary use. We conclude that action safety cannot be installed in weights. It must be expressed as \emph{least privilege}, enforced \emph{outside} the model at the action boundary, and evaluated as \emph{action alignment} (a relational, deployment-conditioned property) rather than a refusal score.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that applying content-safety refusal methods (training models to refuse unsafe inputs) to agentic AI systems is fundamentally misguided. Agentic harm comes from unauthorized actions, not from harmful outputs. The authors show that this approach reduces capabilities without improving real safety, and propose that action safety must be enforced externally through least-privilege principles at the action boundary, not embedded in model weights.
AI.motivation: Current safety practices for AI agents borrow chatbot-era content safety methods (refusal training), but agentic harm is fundamentally different—it's about unauthorized actions rather than harmful outputs. This misalignment causes capability loss without actual safety gains.
AI.method: The authors present three lines of evidence: (1) defense-trained models learn surface patterns rather than true intent; (2) the same training collapses multi-step agents before threats appear while leaving them exploitable; (3) even undefended frontier models exceed granted authority under ordinary use.
AI.result: Content-safety methods applied to agents trade capability for negative security—they reduce useful functionality while failing to prevent genuine agentic harm.
AI.conclusion: Action safety cannot be installed in model weights. It must be expressed as least privilege, enforced outside the model at the action boundary, and evaluated as action alignment (a relational, deployment-conditioned property) rather than a refusal score.

title: Data-Efficient Multimodal Alignment for Histopathology-based Molecular Prediction
summary: H&E-stained whole-slide images offer cohort-scale availability and rich spatial context but lack molecular specificity, whereas bulk RNA-seq provides transcriptome-wide resolution at high cost with limited archival availability. We show that training a lightweight alignment module atop frozen histopathology and RNA-Seq foundation models enables open-vocabulary molecular prompting -- querying H&E slides with gene-set signatures to predict pathway activity without sequencing or end-to-end retraining. Using contrastive learning on a multi-cancer cohort (N=1,720), we achieve a 25-fold improvement in retrieval over baseline methods. Systematic analysis reveals a graduated predictability spectrum: morphologically grounded programs (cell-cycle programs, immune-related) are most reliably predicted (R^2>0.5), while predicting pathways with no morphological footprint remains challenging as expected. We validate clinical utility on the POSEIDON clinical trial: H&E-predicted squamous cell carcinoma scores recapitulate NSCLC subtype identity and predicted IFN-gamma mirror PD-L1 tumor-cell expression groups. Furthermore, genesets describing immune activation and fibrosis predict known tumor microenvironment archetypes from histology alone. We further validate generalization of our approach across unseen cohorts and demonstrate data-efficient domain adaptation, establishing a slide-native framework for molecular analysis on H&E images.

AI.tldr: A lightweight alignment module between frozen H&E and RNA-seq foundation models enables open-vocabulary molecular prompting on whole-slide images, achieving 25x retrieval improvement and validated on clinical trial data.
AI.motivation: H&E slides lack molecular specificity while bulk RNA-seq is costly and has limited archival availability; the goal is to bridge this gap by enabling pathway activity prediction from H&E images without sequencing.
AI.method: Train a lightweight alignment module atop frozen histopathology and RNA-Seq foundation models using contrastive learning on a multi-cancer cohort (N=1,720), enabling open-vocabulary molecular prompting with gene-set signatures.
AI.result: 25-fold retrieval improvement over baselines; cell-cycle and immune-related programs predicted reliably (R²>0.5); validated on POSEIDON clinical trial for NSCLC subtype, IFN-gamma prediction, and tumor microenvironment archetypes.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a slide-native framework for molecular analysis from H&E images, generalizes across unseen cohorts, and supports data-efficient domain adaptation.

title: LEOSTP: A Spatio-Temporal Traffic Prediction Framework for LEO Satellite Networks
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, to appear in IEEE Network
summary: With the evolution of next-generation mobile communication networks and the commercial boom of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites, globally covered satellite networks are gradually becoming a crucial infrastructure for massive user access and seamless connectivity. Accurate traffic prediction is crucial for maintaining the quality of service (QoS) and resource allocation efficiency in satellite networks. However, existing methods struggle to effectively address the three major challenges of LEO networks: highly complex temporal dynamics caused by satellite cross-regional movement, multivariate dependencies in multi-satellite collaboration, and strong spatial heterogeneity driven by user distribution, human activity intensity, and local geographic environments. In this article, we propose a LEO Satellite Traffic Predictor (LEOSTP) framework, a diffusion model-based end-to-end model that forecasts future satellite traffic by jointly leveraging historical traffic patterns and contextual characteristics of the corresponding service regions. The framework consists of two core modules: 1) The general traffic feature extractor module combines the diffusion process with a Transformer architecture to model the multi-scale temporal features of the traffic itself. 2) The external condition encoder module integrates geographic semantic information such as population distribution, point-of-interest (POI) distribution, and local time into the prediction process through a Transformer-based encoder. In this way, the model captures the deep correlation between the external environment and traffic dynamics. Experimental results based on large-scale simulated constellation data show that LEOSTP significantly outperforms traditional statistical models such as ARIMA and SVR, and classical sequence models including LSTM and Transformer, in prediction accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LEOSTP, a diffusion model-based end-to-end framework for predicting LEO satellite traffic, which jointly models historical traffic patterns and geographic contextual features (population, POI, local time) using a diffusion-Transformer hybrid architecture, outperforming traditional models like ARIMA, SVR, LSTM, and Transformer.
AI.motivation: Existing methods fail to address three key challenges in LEO satellite networks: (1) highly complex temporal dynamics from cross-regional satellite movement, (2) multivariate dependencies in multi-satellite collaboration, and (3) strong spatial heterogeneity from user distribution, human activity, and local geography.
AI.method: LEOSTP is a diffusion model-based end-to-end framework with two core modules: (1) a general traffic feature extractor combining diffusion process with Transformer to model multi-scale temporal features, and (2) an external condition encoder integrating geographic semantic information (population distribution, POI, local time) via a Transformer encoder to capture deep environment-traffic correlations.
AI.result: Experiments on large-scale simulated constellation data show LEOSTP significantly outperforms ARIMA, SVR, LSTM, and Transformer in prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses LEO satellite traffic prediction challenges by jointly modeling temporal dynamics and spatial heterogeneity through diffusion and Transformer-based techniques, setting a new benchmark for satellite network traffic forecasting.

title: Multi-Sensor Integrated Sensing and Communication for Critical Infrastructure Protection
summary: Integrated Sensing and Communications (ISAC) will become a service in future mobile communication networks. It enables the detection and recognition of passive objects and environments using radar-like sensing. One promising first application is the protection of critical infrastructure (CI), for example by monitoring the lower airspace above sensitive sites or facilities to prevent unauthorized drone overflights. Our proposal is based on the concept of a distributed multi-sensor (MS)-ISAC. We assume deploying three or more additional passive sniffing sensors near the protected site (PS) of a CI. The sniffers are connected via Downlink (DL) / Uplink (UL) to the distant illumination base station (BS). Multistatic range-Doppler estimation, including synchronization, is performed according to the Cooperative Passive Coherent Location (CPCL) principle. The multistatic architecture has several advantages over the often considered quasi-monostatic architecture where one sniffer is located close to the base station. We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of both approaches and compare their performance for the considered use case in terms of coverage and geometric dilution of precision (GDoP)

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using a distributed multi-sensor ISAC architecture with passive sniffing sensors to protect critical infrastructure by detecting drones in lower airspace, comparing multistatic versus quasi-monostatic approaches in terms of coverage and GDoP.
AI.motivation: ISAC will be a future 6G service, and protecting critical infrastructure (e.g., from unauthorized drones) is a promising first application that requires reliable sensing capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose a distributed multi-sensor (MS)-ISAC architecture with 3+ passive sniffing sensors near the protected site, connected via DL/UL to a distant base station. Multistatic range-Doppler estimation uses the Cooperative Passive Coherent Location (CPCL) principle.
AI.result: The multistatic architecture offers advantages over quasi-monostatic (single sniffer near BS) and the paper compares them in terms of coverage and Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDoP) for drone detection.
AI.conclusion: The distributed MS-ISAC approach with multiple passive sniffers is better suited for critical infrastructure protection than quasi-monostatic ISAC, offering superior coverage and localization accuracy.

title: He3-Seeker: Robotic Information Planning for Lunar Helium-3 Distribution Mapping
comment: Submitted to the International Conference on Space Robotics (iSpaRo) 2026
summary: Lunar helium-3 is a highly valuable strategic resource, pivotal to the advancement of both deep-space exploration and space mining. Existing lunar helium-3 exploration methodologies rely primarily on indirect measurements via remote sensing, which are often characterized by limited precision, low reliability, and insufficient spatial resolution. In this paper, we introduce He3-Seeker, an active robotic exploration method for helium-3 distribution mapping. First, we provide a formal definition of the active helium-3 exploration problem. Subsequently, we developed the He3-Seeker framework, which is conceptually based on multi-point drilling, sampling, and in situ analysis. In particular, we use robotic information planning (RIP) to guide autonomous robot navigation and active sensing. Additionally, to thoroughly evaluate the proposed algorithm, we introduce a reliable method for generating reference data of lunar helium-3 distribution based on low-resolution orbital remote sensing measurements. Simulation experiments verify that He3-Seeker achieves both rapid and high-fidelity mapping of helium-3 distribution, providing a reliable solution for resource exploration tasks. Our code and simulation environment will be publicly accessible at https://github.com/OpenSpace-Lab/He3-Seeker.

AI.tldr: He3-Seeker is an active robotic exploration method that uses multi-point drilling, sampling, and robotic information planning (RIP) to achieve rapid, high-fidelity mapping of lunar helium-3 distribution, overcoming the precision and resolution limitations of existing remote sensing approaches.
AI.motivation: Lunar helium-3 is a valuable strategic resource, but current exploration methods rely on indirect remote sensing with limited precision, low reliability, and insufficient spatial resolution.
AI.method: The authors formally define the active helium-3 exploration problem and develop the He3-Seeker framework based on multi-point drilling, sampling, in situ analysis, and robotic information planning (RIP) for autonomous navigation and active sensing. A reference data generation method using low-resolution orbital remote sensing is also introduced.
AI.result: Simulation experiments demonstrate that He3-Seeker achieves both rapid and high-fidelity mapping of helium-3 distribution.
AI.conclusion: He3-Seeker provides a reliable and effective solution for lunar helium-3 resource exploration tasks, with code and simulation environment publicly available.

title: Geometric Measurements of the Axiom of Choice in Neural Proof Embeddings
summary: The axiom of choice has divided the foundations of mathematics for over a century, but the distinction between classical and constructive proofs has remained a philosophical and methodological one. We use Lean 4's kernel-level tracking of axiom dependence to show that the axiom of choice has a measurable geometric correlate in proof space that obeys a one-parameter mixture law and has operational consequences for neural theorem provers. To do this, we partition $471{,}260$ declarations of Mathlib by transitive dependence on the axiom of choice and represent a filtered population of $42{,}355$ traced theorems by their sequences of tactic invocations. We use the constructive proofs in this dataset to train a self-supervised proof encoder and show that when using it to measure classical proofs, three complementary measurements (anomaly score, reconstruction loss, and density-superlevel containment) exhibit a common decline with the proof's distance from the axiom in the dependency graph, from sharp separation at the shallow boundary (AUC $0.847$ at distance $2$) to indistinguishability at distance~$9{+}$. Robustness controls show that the signature survives length, file, author, and topic controls, and replicates under full-source encoders trained on normalised proof source. Operationally, we show that on an evaluation sample of $251$ Mathlib theorems, Lean's \texttt{aesop} tactic solves constructive theorems at $13\times$ the rate of classical ones, and a neural-guided hybrid using the ReProver tactic generator compresses the gap to $5\times$. The geometric anomaly score predicts \texttt{aesop} failure beyond proof length, providing an operational link between the geometric signature and prover performance.

AI.tldr: Using Lean 4's axiom tracking on 471K Mathlib declarations, the authors show the axiom of choice has a measurable geometric signature in proof space that obeys a mixture law and impacts neural theorem provers. Constructive proofs are solved 13× faster by aesop, with a geometric anomaly score predicting prover failure.
AI.motivation: To bridge the philosophical distinction between classical (axiom-of-choice-dependent) and constructive proofs by demonstrating a measurable, operational correlate in proof space, with implications for automated and neural theorem proving.
AI.method: Partition Mathlib declarations by transitive axiom-of-choice dependence, represent 42,355 traced theorems via tactic sequences, train a self-supervised proof encoder on constructive proofs, then measure classical proofs via anomaly score, reconstruction loss, and density-superlevel containment across dependency distances.
AI.result: Classical proofs exhibit a geometric signature that fades with distance from the axiom: sharp separation at shallow depth (AUC 0.847 at distance 2) to indistinguishability at distance 9+. aesop solves constructive theorems at 13× the rate; neural-guided ReProver compresses gap to 5×. Anomaly score predicts aesop failure beyond length alone.
AI.conclusion: The axiom of choice leaves a measurable, distance-decaying geometric trace in proof space that has operational consequences for theorem provers, bridging foundational philosophy to practical automated reasoning.

title: Meta-learning as a principle for human-like visual representations
summary: The structure of human visual representations underpins our capacity for adaptive behaviour. While pretrained neural networks model human visual representations with unprecedented success, a large discrepancy remains. We propose one reason: these networks optimise a single fixed objective, whereas human representations must support open-ended tasks. We hypothesise this flexibility arises from meta-learning (learning to learn), a pressure shaping representations to acquire new tasks from few observations. To test this, we train a sequence model, without any supervision from human data, across thousands of semantically rich tasks mapping images to high-level concepts. Compared to their pretrained base encoders, meta-learned representations better predict human similarity judgements, semantic rule learning, and high-level visual cortex. Behavioural gains depend on disentangled, high-level task distributions, while brain alignment is driven primarily by the learning-to-learn pressure. Our results suggest the flexibility of human visual representations reflects the functional demand to learn new semantic relationships on the fly.

AI.tldr: Meta-learned representations (trained on thousands of tasks) better predict human similarity judgments, semantic rule learning, and high-level visual cortex than pretrained networks with fixed objectives, suggesting human visual flexibility stems from learning-to-learn pressure.
AI.motivation: Pretrained neural networks still show a large discrepancy with human visual representations, potentially because they optimize a single fixed objective while human representations must support open-ended, flexible tasks.
AI.method: Train a sequence model with meta-learning across thousands of semantically rich tasks (image-to-concept mapping) without any human data supervision, then compare to pretrained base encoders on human similarity judgments, semantic rule learning, and brain alignment.
AI.result: Meta-learned representations outperform pretrained encoders in predicting human similarity judgments, semantic rule learning, and high-level visual cortex activity. Behavioral gains depend on disentangled task distributions, while brain alignment is driven primarily by the learning-to-learn pressure itself.
AI.conclusion: The flexibility of human visual representations reflects the functional demand to learn new semantic relationships on the fly, and meta-learning pressures can bridge the gap between neural network models and human visual cognition.

title: Self-Supervised Theorem Discovery in a Formal Axiomatic System
summary: Recent artificial intelligence (AI) systems have shown remarkable progress in mathematical reasoning. Many existing approaches, including large language models (LLMs), draw on human prior knowledge in the form of mathematical text, code, or theorem libraries. Although these approaches are highly effective in practice, it remains an open question whether an agent can autonomously discover useful theorems without such human priors. We study this question in a formal axiomatic system by developing an agent that starts from axioms and inference rules alone and gradually grows a library of useful theorems. Concretely, we propose a self-supervised theorem-discovery algorithm that alternates between proof search and useful-theorem extraction, building a theorem library whose entries are reused as lemmas for subsequent proof search. Experiments show that the agent discovers tens of thousands of theorems and finds proofs for human-written benchmark problems, suggesting that its discoveries include theorems meaningful from a human mathematical perspective. Furthermore, the discovered theorems improve LLM proof performance when provided as prompt lemmas, indicating that they can serve as external knowledge for LLM reasoning. Our results provide evidence that useful theorems can emerge from proof search without relying on human-provided theorem libraries. More broadly, they suggest a path toward self-evolving AI systems for mathematics whose discoveries remain formally verifiable.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a self-supervised agent that discovers useful mathematical theorems from scratch using only axioms and inference rules, building a theorem library that aids proof search and improves LLM reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether an AI agent can autonomously discover useful mathematical theorems without relying on human prior knowledge such as mathematical text, code, or theorem libraries.
AI.method: A self-supervised theorem-discovery algorithm that alternates between proof search (exploring derivable statements) and useful-theorem extraction (selecting theorems that serve as reusable lemmas), progressively building a theorem library in a formal axiomatic system.
AI.result: The agent discovers tens of thousands of theorems, finds proofs for human-written benchmark problems, and the discovered theorems improve LLM proof performance when provided as prompt lemmas.
AI.conclusion: Useful theorems can emerge from proof search without human-provided theorem libraries, suggesting a path toward self-evolving AI systems for mathematics with formally verifiable discoveries.

title: A multi-architecture study of specificity refinement and false-positive mechanism analysis in prostate MRI
summary: Objectives: To characterize residual false positives in prostate MRI detection, and to evaluate a lightweight post-hoc refinement head for case-level specificity. Materials and Methods: This retrospective study used PI-CAI (5-fold cross-validation) and Prostate158 (n=158; external). A context-aware evidence head and an 89,216-parameter refinement head were trained on a frozen detection backbone; the evidence head was also trained on four further backbones (bare nnU-Net, bare U-Net, bare Mamba, MIGF-Mamba). For each false-positive region, T2-weighted, apparent-diffusion-coefficient, and high-b-value contrast ratios versus peri-lesional rings were compared against ground-truth lesions and contralateral benign regions. Results: False positives were closer to true cancers than to benign tissue in evidence and raw T2-weighted and apparent-diffusion-coefficient contrast, reproducing 35/35 across five architectures (Cohen's d 1.10; FP/benign evidence ratio 2.38x) and 105/105 across modality-perturbation scenarios. On PI-CAI fold-0, refinement raised case-level specificity from 0.469 to 0.549 (+17.2%) at preserved sensitivity (0.943); 5-fold cross-validation showed fold-conditional behavior (9/15 observations positive; range -22% to +28%). On Prostate158, both models saturated (McNemar pooled p=0.69), while the false-positive contrast-matching finding replicated. Conclusion: Residual false positives are contrast-matched to cancer (sharing raw imaging features rather than histologically confirmed mimicry), reproducing across five architectures -- a data-level imaging property, not model-specific artifacts; post-hoc refinement adds practical specificity in-domain but is fold-conditional.

AI.tldr: Residual false positives in prostate MRI detection are contrast-matched to true cancers (sharing raw imaging features), not histologically confirmed mimics. A lightweight refinement head improved case-level specificity by ~17% on one fold but showed fold-conditional behavior; external validation showed no significant improvement.
AI.motivation: To characterize residual false positives in prostate MRI detection and evaluate a lightweight post-hoc refinement head for improving case-level specificity.
AI.method: Retrospective study using PI-CAI (5-fold CV) and Prostate158 datasets. A context-aware evidence head and 89,216-parameter refinement head were trained on frozen detection backbones (nnU-Net, U-Net, Mamba, MIGF-Mamba). False-positive regions were compared against ground-truth lesions and contralateral benign regions using contrast ratios across T2-weighted, ADC, and high-b-value modalities.
AI.result: False positives were closer to true cancers than benign tissue across all architectures (Cohen's d 1.10; FP/benign evidence ratio 2.38x). Refinement raised specificity from 0.469 to 0.549 (+17.2%) at preserved sensitivity on PI-CAI fold-0, but 5-fold CV showed fold-conditional behavior. External Prostate158 showed no significant improvement (McNemar p=0.69).
AI.conclusion: Residual false positives are contrast-matched to cancer (data-level imaging property, not model-specific artifacts). Post-hoc refinement adds practical specificity in-domain but is fold-conditional and does not generalize to external datasets.

title: New families of asymptotically optimal codebooks from vectorial dual-bent functions
summary: Codebooks with small maximum cross-correlation amplitudes play an important role in many applications, such as code division multiple access (CDMA) communication systems, multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communications, compressed sensing, and coding theory. In this paper, by using vectorial dual-bent functions, we construct several families of codebooks that asymptotically achieve the Welch bound. The maximum cross-correlation amplitudes and the distributions of the cross-correlation amplitudes of the constructed codebooks are explicitly determined. Furthermore, these codebooks have new parameters, and some of them have very small alphabet sizes.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs codebooks with small maximum cross-correlation amplitudes using vectorial dual-bent functions, achieving near-optimal Welch bound performance with explicitly determined correlation distributions and new parameter sets, including some with small alphabet sizes.
AI.motivation: Codebooks with small maximum cross-correlation amplitudes are critical for CDMA, MIMO, compressed sensing, and coding theory applications, driving the need for new constructions with optimal or near-optimal correlation properties.
AI.method: The paper employs vectorial dual-bent functions to construct multiple families of codebooks, analytically determining both the maximum cross-correlation amplitudes and their statistical distributions.
AI.result: The constructed codebooks asymptotically achieve the Welch bound, exhibit explicitly determined cross-correlation amplitude distributions, offer new parameter sets, and some achieve very small alphabet sizes.
AI.conclusion: The use of vectorial dual-bent functions provides an effective construction method for near-optimal codebooks with desirable practical properties such as small alphabet sizes and new parameter ranges.

title: Joint Outage Detection and Compensation for Self-Healing 5G RAN via Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Submitted to IEEE Wireless Communication Letters
summary: Self-healing radio access network (RAN) requires autonomous detection and compensation of base station (BS) failures. This letter proposes an end-to-end framework combining three-class cell outage detection (COD), distinguishing normal, failed, and collaterally degraded cells, with a deep Q-Network (DQN) based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent that jointly controls power and antenna tilt for cell outage compensation (COC). Evaluation results show that the proposed DQN agent achieves 99.1% coverage and 54% full-recovery rate, an 11$\times$ improvement over the best heuristic, while consuming less compensation energy than heuristic baselines and learning, without explicit geometric input, to prefer tilt-only compensation for centre-cell outage.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an end-to-end framework combining three-class cell outage detection (COD) with a deep Q-Network (DQN) based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent that jointly controls power and antenna tilt for cell outage compensation (COC) in self-healing RANs, achieving 99.1% coverage and 54% full-recovery rate.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous self-healing in radio access networks (RANs) by detecting and compensating base station failures without explicit geometric input.
AI.method: Three-class cell outage detection (COD) distinguishing normal, failed, and collaterally degraded cells, combined with a deep Q-Network (DQN) based deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent for joint power and antenna tilt control.
AI.result: DQN agent achieves 99.1% coverage and 54% full-recovery rate, an 11× improvement over the best heuristic, with lower compensation energy consumption.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DRL-based approach outperforms heuristic baselines and can autonomously learn to prefer tilt-only compensation for center-cell outages without explicit geometric input.

title: Vision-Language Models for Deployable Social Robot Navigation: Bridging Semantic Reasoning and Low-Level Control
summary: Social robot navigation (SRN) requires more than geometric path planning; it demands understanding human intentions, social norms, and contextual cues to generate socially compliant behaviors. Although classical navigation methods provide reliable metric planning and collision avoidance, they often lack the semantic reasoning capabilities necessary for operation in complex human-centered environments. Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have opened new opportunities for SRN by enabling high-level VLM understanding, commonsense reasoning, and natural language interaction. However, a fundamental challenge remains: how to integrate VLMs into real-time, safety-critical navigation systems and reliably translate their high-level reasoning into grounded navigation actions. In this survey, we present a unified perspective of VLM-based SRN and organize existing approaches into three interconnected components: high-level VLM reasoning, low-level planning and control, and intermediate mechanisms that bridge reasoning and action. Based on this perspective, we propose a structured roadmap for coupling VLMs with navigation systems, covering semantic reasoning, evaluators, spatial grounding, intermediate representations, and control modules. The roadmap highlights both the strengths of VLMs and the necessity of hybrid architectures for practical deployment. We further review representative datasets and evaluation platforms developed for SRN. Finally, we discuss key open challenges. This survey aims to provide a foundation for building reliable, socially compliant, and deployable VLM-enabled navigation systems.

AI.tldr: This survey reviews how Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are integrated into Social Robot Navigation (SRN), proposing a three-component framework (high-level reasoning, low-level control, and bridging mechanisms) and a structured roadmap covering semantic reasoning, spatial grounding, and hybrid architectures for practical deployment.
AI.motivation: Classical navigation methods lack semantic reasoning for human-centered environments, while VLMs offer high-level understanding and commonsense reasoning. The key challenge is integrating VLMs into real-time, safety-critical navigation systems reliably.
AI.method: The authors organize existing VLM-based SRN approaches into three components: high-level VLM reasoning, low-level planning/control, and intermediate bridging mechanisms. They propose a structured roadmap covering semantic reasoning, evaluators, spatial grounding, intermediate representations, and control modules.
AI.result: A unified perspective and structured roadmap for VLM-enabled SRN, along with a review of representative datasets and evaluation platforms.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid architectures combining VLMs' strengths with traditional navigation systems are necessary for practical deployment. Key open challenges remain for building reliable, socially compliant VLM-enabled navigation systems.

title: Replica Symmetry Breaking and Algorithmic Thresholds in Empirical Risk Minimization under Multi-Index Model
summary:
Modern machine learning models are trained by optimizing high-dimensional non-convex empirical risk functions. Such cost functions can have a multitude of local optima and yet, gradient-based optimization appears to converge to near-global optima.
  Within a simple supervised learning setting, we develop a precise picture of which parts of the empirical risk landscape are accessible by polynomial-time algorithms. We are given i.i.d. pairs $\{(\boldsymbol{x}_i,y_i):\; 1 \le i\le n\}$ with $\boldsymbol{x}_i\in \mathbb{R}^d$ standard Gaussian feature vectors, and $y_i\in\mathbb{R}$ response variables that depend on $\boldsymbol{x}_i$ through their projections on an unknown $k$-dimensional subspace. We use empirical risk minimization to learn a model that depends on an $m$-dimensional projection of the data (e.g., an $m$-neurons neural network).
  We propose an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm and precisely characterize the training error it achieves, as well as the relation between test and training error, in the high dimensional asymptotics $n,d\to\infty$, with $n/d\toα\in (0, +\infty)$. Based on earlier work in related models, we expect that the performance achieved by our algorithm is optimal among polynomial-time algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the landscape of empirical risk for high-dimensional non-convex optimization in supervised learning. Using an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm, the authors precisely characterize training and test errors in the high-dimensional asymptotic regime, showing that gradient-based methods can achieve near-global optima despite numerous local optima. The performance of IAMP is conjectured to be optimal among polynomial-time algorithms.
AI.motivation: To understand why gradient-based optimization on high-dimensional non-convex empirical risk functions converges to near-global optima despite the presence of many local optima, and to characterize which parts of the empirical risk landscape are accessible by polynomial-time algorithms.
AI.method: The authors propose an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm for empirical risk minimization in a setting with Gaussian features and responses depending on an unknown k-dimensional subspace. They analyze the algorithm in the high-dimensional asymptotic regime where n,d→∞ with n/d→α.
AI.result: The authors precisely characterize the training error achieved by IAMP and the relationship between test and training error in the high-dimensional asymptotic limit.
AI.conclusion: The performance achieved by the IAMP algorithm is expected to be optimal among polynomial-time algorithms, based on earlier work in related models.

title: Vision-driven Preference Synthesis for Mitigating Hallucinations in VLMs
comment: 29 pages; Code is available at https://github.com/yunpal/ViPSy
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong performance in visual understanding, yet they still suffer from hallucinations, generating content that is not grounded in the image. Preference alignment is a promising approach to improve visual faithfulness, but its success depends heavily on how preference pairs are constructed. Existing methods exhibit two key limitations; (a) intervention-based methods often introduce significant deviation from the policy distribution, and (b) sampling-based methods often underuse visual information during the construction. In this paper, we propose ViPSy (Vision-driven Preference Synthesis), a framework for constructing preference data that are both policy-aligned and visually grounded. Our framework consists of two stages; in the first stage, ViPSy derives a visual cue from recurring object-level content across semantically aligned image variants, so preference construction can rely on visual information rather than language priors. In the second stage, ViPSy conditions the policy's own rollouts on this cue, allowing candidates to be guided by visually grounded content while staying close to the policy's response distribution. The resulting candidates remain close to the policy's response distribution while better leveraging visual information from the image. Experiments show that the resulting VLM, preference-aligned with ViPSy-constructed preference pairs, achieves a new state-of-the-art in hallucination mitigation. Compared with the previous state-of-the-art method, it reduces hallucination rates on AMBER and Object HalBench by 35.7% and 24.5%, respectively. The resulting model further improves on general visual grounding benchmarks, e.g., MMStar, MMVP, and CV-Bench, while also yielding gains in semantic segmentation and ImageNet linear probing, underscoring the effectiveness of our framework in enhancing the model's visual capabilities.
