Although this upper bound is differentiable, its gradient was not derived. Therefore, we analytically derive the gradient of the WS upper bound to characterize directions leading to flat minima. Based on this, we propose Hessian Spectral Range (HSR) Regularization, which updates parameters along the steepest descent direction of the WS bound. Experiments demonstrate that HSR Regularization narrows the Hessian eigenvalue spectrum, avoids sharp minima and saddle points, and promotes convergence to flat minima. Although the applicability of this method is currently limited to cross-entropy loss and three-layer architectures, to the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study to report a closed-form gradient that promotes convergence to flat minima without numerical approximations. Therefore, the theoretical analysis of this gradient is expected to contribute to the further development of NNs.

AI.tldr: The paper derives the gradient of the Wolkowicz-Styan upper bound on the Hessian's maximum eigenvalue for three-layer NNs with cross-entropy loss, and proposes HSR Regularization that updates parameters along this gradient to promote convergence to flat minima.
AI.motivation: To understand how data distributions and NN parameters structurally determine directions toward flat minima, as existing algorithms reduce Hessian eigenvalues but lack analytical characterization of these directions.
AI.method: Analytically derives the gradient of the Wolkowicz-Styan (WS) upper bound on the maximum eigenvalue of the cross-entropy loss Hessian in three-layer NNs, then proposes Hessian Spectral Range (HSR) Regularization that updates parameters along the steepest descent direction of the WS bound.
AI.result: HSR Regularization narrows the Hessian eigenvalue spectrum, avoids sharp minima and saddle points, and promotes convergence to flat minima in experiments.
AI.conclusion: This is the first study to report a closed-form gradient that promotes convergence to flat minima without numerical approximations, though currently limited to cross-entropy loss and three-layer architectures, and is expected to contribute to further NN development.

title: DiffRGD: An Inference-Time Diffusion Guidance Through Riemannian Gradient Descent
summary: Recently, diffusion models have been widely adopted in generative modeling and have served as foundational models for many image generation tasks. To control the generation without costly re-training or fine-tuning, many works seek inference-time guidance methods to steer the latent via a differentiable objective at inference time. However, these methods cannot effectively preserve the original Gaussian distribution because they introduce distributional drift, thereby degrading the sample quality. To address this gap, we propose DiffRGD, a distribution-aware guidance framework that explicitly preserves the latent Gaussian structure. DiffRGD formulates each sampling step as a constrained optimization problem on a spherical manifold induced by the latent Gaussian distribution, and solves it efficiently via Riemannian Gradient Descent (RGD). DiffRGD is a plug-and-play method that can be seamlessly integrated into any pre-trained diffusion model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiffRGD outperforms previous methods in most image restoration and conditional generation tasks. Our codebase is available at https://github.com/jwliao1209/DiffRGD.

AI.tldr: DiffRGD is a plug-and-play, distribution-aware guidance framework for diffusion models that preserves the latent Gaussian structure via Riemannian Gradient Descent on a spherical manifold, outperforming prior inference-time guidance methods in image restoration and conditional generation.
AI.motivation: Existing inference-time guidance methods for diffusion models introduce distributional drift that degrades sample quality because they cannot effectively preserve the original Gaussian distribution of the latent variables.
AI.method: DiffRGD formulates each sampling step as a constrained optimization problem on a spherical manifold induced by the latent Gaussian distribution, solved efficiently via Riemannian Gradient Descent (RGD), explicitly preserving the Gaussian structure without re-training.
AI.result: Outperforms previous methods in most image restoration and conditional generation tasks across extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: DiffRGD provides an effective plug-and-play solution for guided diffusion model inference that maintains distributional integrity while achieving superior generation quality.

title: Customized Generative AI Agent for Transportation Engineering Practice: A Development and Continued Pre-training Guideline
summary: Recent advancements in generative artificial intelligence (AI) and large language models (LLMs) have shown significant promise in automating complex reasoning, summarization, and question-answering tasks. However, the effectiveness of general-purpose LLMs in specialized engineering domains remains limited due to insufficient exposure to technical standards, engineering terminology, and domain-specific semantics. This study proposes a systematic approach to developing a customized generative AI agent for transportation engineering applications. A curated corpus of U.S. transportation manuals, design guidelines, and regulatory documents is used to conduct continued pretraining of six state-of-the-art LLMs through a unified low-rank adaptation (LoRA) framework. The training process is monitored to ensure convergence and model stability. Performance is evaluated using standard natural language processing metrics, including BLEU-4 and ROUGE, with Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B demonstrating the highest domain alignment and response quality. Results validate the effectiveness of LoRA-based adaptation in improving LLM performance on technical content interpretation and context-specific reasoning. This work contributes a reproducible development framework for constructing domain-specialized generative AI agents, supporting broader deployment in transportation research, design, planning, and policy analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a systematic approach to developing domain-specialized generative AI agents for transportation engineering by performing continued pretraining of six LLMs (including Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B) on a curated corpus of U.S. transportation manuals and regulatory documents using LoRA, with results showing improved performance on technical content interpretation and context-specific reasoning.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs struggle in specialized engineering domains due to insufficient exposure to technical standards, engineering terminology, and domain-specific semantics, limiting their effectiveness in transportation engineering tasks.
AI.method: The authors curated a corpus of U.S. transportation manuals and regulatory documents, performed continued pretraining of six state-of-the-art LLMs using a unified LoRA framework, monitored training for convergence/stability, and evaluated performance with BLEU-4 and ROUGE metrics.
AI.result: Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B demonstrated the highest domain alignment and response quality among the six models tested, validating LoRA-based adaptation effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-based domain adaptation effectively improves LLM performance on technical content interpretation, and the work provides a reproducible framework for developing domain-specialized generative AI agents for transportation research, design, planning, and policy analysis.

title: PS-MOT: Cultivating Instance Awareness from Point Seeds for Multi-Object Tracking
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. The source code is available at https://github.com/xifen523/PS-MOT
summary: We introduce Point-supervised Multi-Object Tracking (PS-MOT) as a cost-effective alternative to traditional bounding box supervision, shifting the focus from spatial fitting to topological center-driven representation. However, PS-MOT faces challenges, e.g., spatial ambiguity and identity drift due to the lack of explicit geometric structure and scale constraints. To address these, we propose PS-Track, a hierarchical pipeline transitioning from points to instances across data, model, and loss levels. At the data level, we introduce Temporal-Feedback Prompting (TFP) to evolve points into temporally consistent pseudo-labels using negative spatial cues and motion priors. At the model level, we design the Point-Excited Wavelet Attention (PEWA) module, which leverages semantic correlations to activate high-frequency components, ``hallucinating'' object boundaries. At the loss level, Uncertainty-Guided Gaussian Learning (UGL) models pseudo-labels as probabilistic distributions, dynamically calibrating supervision intensity. Experiments on DanceTrack, EmboTrack, SportsMOT, and JRDB demonstrate that PS-Track provides a feasible and effective point-supervised alternative across diverse tracking scenarios, establishing a new state-of-the-art for point-supervised tracking. The source code is available at https://github.com/xifen523/PS-MOT.

AI.tldr: PS-Track is a point-supervised multi-object tracking method that uses a hierarchical pipeline (data, model, loss levels) to overcome spatial ambiguity and identity drift, achieving SOTA results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To reduce annotation cost, the authors propose PS-MOT using point supervision instead of bounding boxes, but face challenges of spatial ambiguity and identity drift. PS-Track addresses these issues.
AI.method: Three-level hierarchical pipeline: (1) Data-level: Temporal-Feedback Prompting (TFP) generates temporally consistent pseudo-labels; (2) Model-level: Point-Excited Wavelet Attention (PEWA) hallucinates object boundaries via high-frequency components; (3) Loss-level: Uncertainty-Guided Gaussian Learning (UGL) models pseudo-labels as probabilistic distributions for dynamic calibration.
AI.result: Experiments on DanceTrack, EmboTrack, SportsMOT, and JRDB show PS-Track achieves SOTA for point-supervised tracking.
AI.conclusion: PS-Track provides a feasible and effective point-supervised alternative across diverse tracking scenarios, establishing a new SOTA.

title: Lossy Compression for Sparse Aggregation
summary: We consider the problem of transmitting sparse local updates to the server in a distributed learning system. Specifically, the system consists of $n$ clients, each possessing a $k$-sparse $d$-dimensional local model, and a central server responsible for aggregating the clients' models into a global model. The goal is to characterize the tradeoff between the communication cost in the transmission from the clients to the server and the accuracy in aggregating the global model. We propose a compression scheme for sparse local models by concatenating a covering method and a sketching method. We also present a converse based on f-divergence, which strengthens the conventional Fano-type lower bounds. The proposed lower bound is tight for the frequency estimation case, that is, each coordinate takes values in a binary alphabet. For general alphabets, the proposed achievable schemes remain suboptimal relative to the converse bounds, indicating that a complete characterization of the communication-accuracy tradeoff requires further investigation.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the communication-accuracy tradeoff in distributed learning when transmitting sparse local updates from clients to a server. It proposes a compression scheme combining covering and sketching methods, and derives a converse bound using f-divergence that is tighter than Fano-type bounds. The lower bound is tight for binary alphabets, but gaps remain for general alphabets.
AI.motivation: To characterize the fundamental tradeoff between communication cost and aggregation accuracy when transmitting sparse local model updates from clients to a central server in distributed learning systems.
AI.method: Proposes a compression scheme that concatenates a covering method and a sketching method for sparse local models. For the converse, uses f-divergence to derive lower bounds that strengthen conventional Fano-type bounds.
AI.result: The f-divergence-based lower bound is tight for the binary alphabet (frequency estimation) case. For general alphabets, the achievable scheme remains suboptimal compared to the converse bound.
AI.conclusion: A complete characterization of the communication-accuracy tradeoff remains open for general alphabets, requiring further investigation.

title: HJ-SafeDMP: Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability-Guided Dynamic Movement Primitives for Provably Safe Robot Motion
summary: Robots deployed in safety-critical environments must execute motions that are simultaneously robust to disturbances and provably safe from collisions. Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) offer inherent stability, temporal flexibility, and efficient trajectory generalization from single demonstrations, but they lack formal safety certificates. Conversely, Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) Reachability analysis provides a principled framework for computing worst-case safety margins and forward-invariant safe sets, but classical grid-based methods suffer from the curse of dimensionality and are impractical for real-time control. This paper introduces HJ-SafeDMP, a framework that integrates DMPs with learned HJ Reachability-based safety value functions to achieve provably safe, robust, and computationally efficient robot motion. We learn a Control Barrier Value Function (CBVF) from offline demonstration data using a model-free, finite-difference HJ recursion and deploy it as a real-time safety filter via a closed-form control law that modulates the DMP output. Unlike optimization-based CBF-QP approaches, our method achieves safety filtering without online quadratic program solves, preserving the computational efficiency of DMPs. We further incorporate an expectile-based offline learning objective that avoids querying out-of-distribution actions, and a conformal prediction calibration step that provides finite-sample probabilistic safety coverage. Experimental evaluation on a 7-DOF robot manipulator demonstrates that HJ-SafeDMP achieves formal safety guarantees with orders-of-magnitude faster execution than optimization-based baselines, while maintaining the robustness and adaptability of DMPs for human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: HJ-SafeDMP integrates Dynamic Movement Primitives with learned Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability safety value functions to achieve provably safe, robust, and computationally efficient robot motion, demonstrated on a 7-DOF manipulator.
AI.motivation: Robots in safety-critical environments need motions that are both robust to disturbances and provably safe from collisions. DMPs offer stability and efficiency but lack formal safety guarantees, while HJ Reachability provides formal safety but is computationally intractable for real-time control. The gap motivates combining both approaches.
AI.method: Learns a Control Barrier Value Function (CBVF) from offline demonstration data using model-free, finite-difference HJ recursion, deployed as a real-time safety filter via closed-form control law modulating DMP output. Includes expectile-based offline learning avoiding out-of-distribution actions and conformal prediction for probabilistic safety coverage.
AI.result: On a 7-DOF robot manipulator, HJ-SafeDMP achieves formal safety guarantees with orders-of-magnitude faster execution than optimization-based baselines, while maintaining DMP robustness and adaptability.
AI.conclusion: HJ-SafeDMP successfully bridges DMPs and HJ Reachability, providing provably safe, robust, and computationally efficient motion that is suitable for real-time human-robot interaction in safety-critical environments.

title: Entropy Regularized Reinforcement Learning for Zero-Sum Stochastic Differential Games in a Regime-Switching Jump-Diffusion Process
summary: To address parameter misspecification and sudden structural environmental changes in conventional stochastic differential game (SDG) frameworks, this paper introduces a distributional control approach that characterizes optimal strategies as probability distributions over actions, conditioned on the continuous state, the discrete regime state, and parameters. This forms a reinforcement learning framework for entropy-regularized zero-sum stochastic differential games (ERRL-ZSSDGs) in a regime-switching jump-diffusion process. Using the dynamic programming principle (DPP), we derive the associated coupled systems of Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs (HJBI) equations, from which equilibrium strategies are expressed via gradients of the value function. For linear-quadratic problems, semi-analytical solutions for both value function and equilibrium strategies are obtained by solving a system of coupled ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In more general settings, an Actor-Critic policy improvement algorithm is developed to approximate the value functions and equilibrium policies across different regimes. The method is applied to an investment game, and numerical examples illustrate the effect of the temperature parameter and regime transitions on optimal policies and values.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a distributional control approach for stochastic differential games, forming an entropy-regularized reinforcement learning framework for zero-sum games in regime-switching jump-diffusion processes. It derives HJBI equations, provides semi-analytical solutions for linear-quadratic problems, and develops an Actor-Critic algorithm for general settings, with application to investment games.
AI.motivation: To address parameter misspecification and sudden structural environmental changes in conventional stochastic differential game (SDG) frameworks.
AI.method: Distributional control approach characterizing optimal strategies as probability distributions over actions conditioned on continuous state, discrete regime state, and parameters. Uses dynamic programming principle to derive coupled systems of HJBI equations. Provides semi-analytical solutions for linear-quadratic problems via ODEs, and develops an Actor-Critic policy improvement algorithm for general settings.
AI.result: Equilibrium strategies expressed via gradients of value function; semi-analytical solutions for linear-quadratic problems; numerical examples illustrating temperature parameter and regime transition effects on optimal policies and values.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ERRL-ZSSDG framework effectively handles parameter misspecification and structural changes in SDGs, with tractable solutions for linear-quadratic cases and a practical Actor-Critic algorithm for general problems.

title: MedDiffuseMix: Preserving Diagnostic Evidence with Saliency-Aware Diffusion Medical Image Data Augmentatio
comment: Under review Signal Image and Video Processing
summary: Limited data availability, class imbalance, and domain variability remain major barriers to reliable medical image classification. Conventional augmentation can improve training diversity but may distort diagnostically informative structures, whereas unconstrained generative augmentation may introduce label-inconsistent content. This paper proposes MedDiffuseMix, a saliency-guided diffusion mixing framework for controlled medical image augmentation. The method uses classifier-derived saliency maps to separate high-saliency diagnostic regions from low-saliency background areas and applies diffusion-guided mixing mainly to regions with lower diagnostic importance. Adaptive mixing, Gaussian boundary blending, and a saliency-preservation constraint reduce semantic distortion and reject or attenuate samples that shift model attention away from clinically relevant evidence. The framework is evaluated on four public benchmarks: the Radiological Society of North America pneumonia chest radiography dataset, Musculoskeletal Radiographs, PatchCamelyon, and the Breast Cancer Histopathological Image Classification dataset. Experiments with convolutional and transformer-based classifiers show that MedDiffuseMix improves accuracy, F1-score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve compared with standard augmentation, Mixup, GenMix, SaliencyMix, and diffusion-based augmentation baselines. Ablation studies confirm the importance of saliency guidance, adaptive region mixing, and smooth boundary blending. Visual attribution analysis further indicates that MedDiffuseMix better preserves diagnostically salient regions. These results suggest that saliency-guided diffusion mixing is an effective augmentation strategy for limited-data medical image classification.

AI.tldr: MedDiffuseMix uses saliency-guided diffusion mixing to augment limited medical image data, improving classification accuracy while preserving diagnostically important regions.
AI.motivation: Limited data, class imbalance, and domain variability hinder reliable medical image classification. Conventional augmentation may distort diagnostic structures, while generative augmentation can introduce label-inconsistent content.
AI.method: The framework uses classifier-derived saliency maps to separate high-importance diagnostic regions from background, then applies diffusion-guided mixing mainly to low-saliency areas. It incorporates adaptive mixing, Gaussian boundary blending, and a saliency-preservation constraint.
AI.result: Evaluated on four public benchmarks (RSNA pneumonia, MURA, PatchCamelyon, BreakHis) with CNN and transformer classifiers. Outperforms standard augmentation, Mixup, GenMix, SaliencyMix, and diffusion baselines in accuracy, F1, and AUC. Ablation studies validate key components.
AI.conclusion: Saliency-guided diffusion mixing is an effective augmentation strategy for limited-data medical image classification, better preserving diagnostically salient regions than existing augmentation methods.

title: Preventing Error Propagation in Multi-Agent AI through Runtime Monitoring
summary: Multi-agent AI systems can improve answer selection by allowing different language models to exchange reasoning traces, revise initial predictions, and support a final decision. However, such communication may also introduce reliability risks: reasoning from one agent can correct another agent's mistake, but it can also mislead an agent that was initially correct. This paper studies reliable multi-agent AI communication through reasoning exchange and runtime answer revision. We develop a framework in which agents first answer multiple-choice questions independently, then share reasoning traces and revise their decisions. We conduct numerical experiments where we evaluate whether this process improves accuracy, produces more positive than negative answer transitions, and remains effective across domains such as cybersecurity, networking, and general knowledge. The results help identify when multi-agent reasoning improves reliability and when it may propagate errors.

AI.tldr: This paper studies whether multi-agent AI systems improve answer reliability through reasoning exchange, finding that while communication can correct mistakes, it can also propagate errors depending on the domain.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent AI systems allow language models to exchange reasoning and revise answers, but this communication introduces reliability risks where correct agents may be misled by incorrect reasoning from others.
AI.method: A framework where agents independently answer multiple-choice questions, then share reasoning traces and revise their decisions. Experiments evaluate accuracy changes, positive vs. negative answer transitions across cybersecurity, networking, and general knowledge domains.
AI.result: The multi-agent reasoning exchange improves accuracy in some cases but can also lead to error propagation; the effect varies across domains.
AI.conclusion: The results help identify when multi-agent reasoning improves reliability and when it may propagate errors.

title: Towards World Model-Empowered Integrated Sensing, Communication, and Decision for Complex Unmanned Systems
summary: Complex unmanned systems comprising satellites, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs), and quadruped robots are increasingly deployed to perform large-scale sensing and autonomous operations. We propose a world model-empowered sensing, communication, decision (SCD) integration framework for complex unmanned communication networks. The proposed architecture establishes a closed-loop system where a unified world model jointly optimizes time-sensitive sensing, wireless communication, and intelligent decision-making. To regulate sensing freshness and reduce redundant data generation, we propose a time-sensitive age of information (AoI)-driven sensing mechanism that dynamically schedules sensing updates based on task urgency and predictive uncertainty. Furthermore, a predictive world model is developed to jointly represent environmental dynamics, wireless channel evolution, and agent mobility within a hybrid deterministic-stochastic latent space. This enables proactive communication scheduling and decision evaluation via latent rollout. To support large-scale heterogeneous coordination, a multi-granularity knowledge graph is further designed to organize cross-population relationships among satellites, UAVs, UGVs, and ground agents. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed SCD framework outperforms conventional systems, highlighting the significant potential of world models for supporting unmanned systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a world model-empowered sensing, communication, decision (SCD) integration framework for complex unmanned systems (satellites, UAVs, UGVs, quadruped robots). It introduces an AoI-driven sensing mechanism, a predictive world model for joint environment-channel-mobility representation, and a multi-granularity knowledge graph for heterogeneous coordination, showing performance gains over conventional systems.
AI.motivation: Complex unmanned systems involving diverse platforms (satellites, UAVs, UGVs, quadruped robots) require tightly integrated sensing, communication, and decision-making to perform large-scale autonomous operations, but existing approaches lack a unified framework to jointly optimize these aspects under time-sensitive constraints.
AI.method: (1) A closed-loop SCD integration architecture with a unified world model. (2) An AoI-driven sensing mechanism that dynamically schedules updates based on task urgency and predictive uncertainty. (3) A predictive world model with hybrid deterministic-stochastic latent space for jointly representing environment, wireless channels, and agent mobility. (4) A multi-granularity knowledge graph for heterogeneous cross-population coordination.
AI.result: Numerical results show the proposed SCD framework outperforms conventional systems, demonstrating the potential of world models for supporting unmanned systems.
AI.conclusion: The world model-empowered integration framework effectively fuses sensing, communication, and decision-making for heterogeneous unmanned systems, with AoI-driven sensing and predictive latent modeling providing key advantages over baseline approaches.

title: Keypose Exploration: Efficient Automatic Trajectory Labelling and Cross-Embodiment Policy Transfer
comment: Accepted by IROS2026. Code available at: https://github.com/YupuLu/keypose_labelling
summary: Keypose-based manipulation decomposes tasks into critical waypoints to simplify policy learning for long-horizon tasks, but existing approaches rely on task-specific heuristics or manual annotation to extract keyposes from demonstrations. We present an automatic trajectory labelling pipeline for grasp-related tasks. This pipeline combines vision-language models (VLMs) for semantic event detection with classical trajectory analysis for precise temporal alignment, requiring VLM inference only on one single demo among repeating ones per task. Using the labelled data, we train a keypose-guided Diffusion Policy (DP) that exploits keypose conditioning to intervene demonstration distributions. We explore the possibility to apply this property for cross-embodiment transfer: candidate keyposes are sampled and filtered via a reachability map, steering the policy toward kinematically feasible keyposes for the target robot. As a preliminary feasibility study, experiments on two robomimic tasks show that the labelled data produces policies matching a standard DP baseline, and that reachability-filtered keypose conditioning may benefit zero-shot transfer on the multimodal insertion task when feasible candidates are available.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an automatic pipeline using VLMs and trajectory analysis to label keyposes from demonstrations, enabling keypose-guided Diffusion Policy learning and preliminary exploration of cross-embodiment transfer via reachability-filtered keypose conditioning.
AI.motivation: Existing keypose-based manipulation methods rely on task-specific heuristics or manual annotation to extract keyposes, which limits scalability and automation.
AI.method: A pipeline combining VLMs for semantic event detection and classical trajectory analysis for temporal alignment, requiring only one VLM inference per task. Keypose-guided Diffusion Policy (DP) is trained, and cross-embodiment transfer is explored via sampling/filtering keyposes using a reachability map.
AI.result: Labelled data produces policies matching standard DP baseline on two robomimic tasks; reachability-filtered keypose conditioning shows potential for zero-shot transfer on multimodal insertion task when feasible candidates exist.
AI.conclusion: The automatic labelling pipeline is effective, and keypose-conditioned policies may benefit cross-embodiment transfer, though results are preliminary and limited to certain conditions.

title: Entropy-Regularized Reinforcement Learning for Linear-Quadratic Stackelberg Differential Games in Regime-Switching Diffusion Models
summary: Stackelberg differential games (SDGs) provide a powerful framework for hierarchical decision-making in stochastic and continuous-time environments, yet their solution remains computationally challenging due to the complexity of traditional dynamic programming and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs (HJBI) methods, especially in high-dimensional systems. This paper proposes an entropy-regularized reinforcement learning (ERRL) approach for linear-quadratic SDGs (LQ-SDGs) within a continuous-time diffusion framework governed by Markovian regime switching. The key innovation lies in deriving exploratory weakly-coupled HJBI equations with entropy regularization, which promotes stochastic policies that actively avoid suboptimal equilibria -- a limitation of classical SDG methods. Neural networks are integrated to approximate regime-dependent value functions and solve high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) efficiently, while a novel sampling technique enhances computational tractability. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework compared to conventional approaches, particularly in escaping suboptimal traps through exploratory policies. The study highlights the critical role of entropy regularization and neural network approximations in achieving robust solutions for hierarchical decision-making problems under abrupt environmental shifts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an entropy-regularized reinforcement learning approach for solving linear-quadratic Stackelberg differential games in continuous-time diffusion frameworks with regime switching, using neural networks for high-dimensional PDE approximation and a novel sampling technique.
AI.motivation: Traditional dynamic programming and HJBI methods for Stackelberg differential games are computationally challenging, especially in high-dimensional systems, and classical SDG methods tend to converge to suboptimal equilibria.
AI.method: The authors derive exploratory weakly-coupled HJBI equations with entropy regularization to promote stochastic policies, integrate neural networks for regime-dependent value function approximation, and develop a novel sampling technique for computational tractability.
AI.result: The framework effectively escapes suboptimal traps through exploratory policies and outperforms conventional approaches in numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Entropy regularization and neural network approximations are critical for achieving robust solutions in hierarchical decision-making problems under abrupt environmental shifts.

title: JuZhou 1.0 Technical Report: The First Edge-Native Text-to-Image Foundation Model Trained Entirely on China-Developed AI Accelerators
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models typically require substantial computational resources and cloud infrastructure, posing significant challenges for edge deployment in terms of latency, cost, and user privacy. We present JuZhou 1.0, an ultra-lightweight T2I foundation model designed for fully offline, on-device execution. JuZhou 1.0 achieves its efficiency through four key designs: (1) a compact image-generation backbone consisting of a 0.385B-parameter denoising U-Net and a 1.90M-parameter distilled decoder, totaling approximately 0.387B parameters; (2) Rectified Flow training combined with DMD2 distillation, reducing inference to 4 sampling steps; (3) Chinese semantic alignment trained on 9M curated image-text pairs, enabling direct Chinese prompting without external translation at inference time; and (4) a training and distillation pipeline completed on domestically developed Sugon K100 AI accelerators without relying on NVIDIA GPUs for training or distillation. Despite its compact scale, the 28-step base model of JuZhou 1.0 achieves an overall GenEval score of 0.69, outperforming published baselines including SDXL (2.6B, 0.55), SD3-Medium (2B, 0.62), and IF-XL (4.3B, 0.61). We further validate the full poetry-to-image pipeline on Android and the core CLIP-U-Net-VAE generation branch on iOS. On a smartphone powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform, the 4-step U-Net denoising branch runs in approximately 1.6 seconds, while the full Android poetry-to-image pipeline takes 4.5 seconds with on-device prompt refinement on Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. These results position JuZhou 1.0 as a practical approach to mobile text-to-image generation and provide a concrete reference for Chinese-native generation, domestic-compute training, and fully offline on-device deployment after one-time installation.

AI.tldr: JuZhou 1.0 is a 0.387B-parameter ultra-lightweight text-to-image diffusion model designed for fully offline on-device execution. It uses Rectified Flow, DMD2 distillation (4 steps), and Chinese semantic alignment trained on domestic K100 accelerators. It achieves GenEval 0.69 (outperforming SDXL, SD3-Medium, IF-XL) and runs in ~1.6s (denoising) or ~4.5s (full pipeline) on flagship smartphones.
AI.motivation: Existing T2I diffusion models require cloud infrastructure, causing latency, cost, and privacy issues for edge deployment. The authors aim to enable fully offline, on-device text-to-image generation with a lightweight model that also supports native Chinese prompting and domestically developed hardware training.
AI.method: (1) Compact backbone: 0.385B-param U-Net + 1.90M-param distilled decoder (~0.387B total). (2) Rectified Flow training + DMD2 distillation, reducing inference to 4 steps. (3) Chinese semantic alignment trained on 9M curated image-text pairs for direct Chinese prompting. (4) Entire training/distillation pipeline on domestic Sugon K100 AI accelerators (no NVIDIA GPUs).
AI.result: 28-step base model achieves GenEval 0.69, outperforming SDXL (0.55), SD3-Medium (0.62), IF-XL (0.61). On Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5, 4-step denoising runs in ~1.6s; full Android pipeline takes ~4.5s on Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. Validated on Android and iOS.
AI.conclusion: JuZhou 1.0 demonstrates that practical, high-quality text-to-image generation is feasible fully offline on mobile devices, with competitive generation quality, native Chinese support, and complete independence from NVIDIA hardware for training.

title: Memory as an Attack Surface in LLM Agents: A Study on Multiple-Choice Question Answering
summary: AI agents extend conventional large language model (LLM) applications by integrating language understanding with task execution, external tool use, and memory mechanisms. While memory allows agents to retain prior interactions and provide more personalized and context-aware responses, it also introduces a new vulnerability: information stored in memory can influence future outputs even when the current query is clean. In this paper, we investigate memory manipulation in LLM-based agents for multiple-choice question answering. We first design and implement an LLM-based AI agent with an external memory component that stores and retrieves task-relevant information. We then introduce basic memory manipulation scenarios in which misleading or corrupted memories are inserted into the agent before it answers multiple-choice questions. Using a controlled experimental setup, we compare the agent's performance before and after memory manipulation and measure changes in answer accuracy, attack success rate, and selection of manipulated options. Our results show that even simple memory manipulations can noticeably affect the agent's final answers, causing it to select incorrect options despite receiving clean and well-formed questions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how memory manipulation in LLM-based AI agents can compromise their performance in multiple-choice question answering, showing that inserting misleading information into external memory causes agents to select incorrect answers even when the current query is clean.
AI.motivation: While memory enhances personalization and context-awareness in AI agents, it introduces a new vulnerability where stored information can influence future outputs. The authors aim to study this security risk specifically in LLM-based agents for multiple-choice QA tasks.
AI.method: They design an LLM-based agent with external memory for storing/retrieving task-relevant info, then insert misleading/corrupted memories before QA tasks. Performance is compared before/after manipulation using metrics like answer accuracy, attack success rate, and selection of manipulated options.
AI.result: Simple memory manipulations noticeably affect the agent's final answers, causing it to select incorrect options despite receiving clean, well-formed questions.
AI.conclusion: Memory manipulation is a real and effective attack vector against LLM-based agents, highlighting the need for security measures in memory components.

title: Semantic Noise Aided Secure Image Transmission over MIMO Fading Channels
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
summary: Existing semantic communications have exhibited satisfactory performance in many tasks, but secure image transmission remains insufficiently explored. We propose a novel secure image semantic communication (SISC) framework over multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) fading channels. To ensure high-quality image reconstruction for the legitimate semantic user (SU) and simultaneously interfere with the eavesdropper (Eve), we design a semantic noise generation (SNG) network. This network generates a beneficial semantic noise map based on both the source features and the SU channel state information (CSI). An efficient channel estimation enhanced network is incorporated to obtain the accurate CSI and enhance the system performance. Furthermore, to improve the secure image reconstruction quality, we develop an efficient transceiver beamformer optimization algorithm, where the formulated problem is solved using the constrained stochastic successive convex approximation method. In the proposed SISC framework, semantic noise generation and beamforming optimization work together to ensure secure and high-quality image transmission. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed semantic noise aided transmission scheme effectively protects image information from leakage to Eve while maintaining high-fidelity image reconstruction at SU.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a secure image semantic communication (SISC) framework over MIMO channels, using a semantic noise generation network and beamforming optimization to protect against eavesdroppers while maintaining high-quality image reconstruction for legitimate users.
AI.motivation: Existing semantic communications perform well in many tasks, but secure image transmission remains insufficiently explored.
AI.method: The paper designs (1) a semantic noise generation (SNG) network that creates beneficial noise based on source features and legitimate user CSI to interfere with eavesdroppers, (2) a channel estimation enhanced network for accurate CSI, and (3) a transceiver beamformer optimization algorithm using constrained stochastic successive convex approximation.
AI.result: The proposed semantic noise aided transmission scheme effectively protects image information from leakage to Eve while maintaining high-fidelity image reconstruction at the legitimate user.
AI.conclusion: Semantic noise generation and beamforming optimization work together to ensure secure and high-quality image transmission in the SISC framework.

title: A Unified Framework for Multi-Contact Path Planning in the Rolling Robot Systems
summary: Rolling motion planning is challenging because rolling contact imposes nonholonomic constraints and the configuration evolves on a curved manifold. The problem becomes substantially harder in multi-contact settings, where multiple bodies roll without slip and the contact states are coupled. This paper presents a new framework for multi-contact path planning in spherical rolling robotics under no-slip constraints. We first derive a compact kinematic model for multi-sphere rolling using Montana's contact-coordinate formulation, where each contact is represented by a stacked five-state vector. Building on this model, we construct a Voronoi-based roadmap directly on the spherical contact manifold, incorporating spherical-cap obstacles and mutual-exclusion regions via on-manifold collision checking, and refine discrete graph paths using manifold-consistent log-exp smoothing. The resulting smoothed surface paths are then lifted to admissible multi-contact rolling motions through the derived Montana kinematics and validated via forward simulation. We further evaluate feasibility and path quality versus trajectory smoothness, Voronoi seed density, and computation time. The proposed framework provides a foundation for extending the method to non-spherical geometries, time-varying obstacle environments, and experimental validation on physical rolling robotic platforms.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework for multi-contact path planning in spherical rolling robotics under no-slip constraints, using Montana's contact-coordinate formulation, Voronoi-based roadmaps on spherical manifolds, and log-exp smoothing to generate admissible rolling motions.
AI.motivation: Rolling motion planning is inherently difficult due to nonholonomic constraints and curved manifolds, and becomes substantially harder in multi-contact settings where multiple bodies roll without slip and contact states are coupled — current methods lack a unified framework for such problems.
AI.method: Derives a compact kinematic model using Montana's contact-coordinate formulation with stacked five-state vectors; builds Voronoi-based roadmaps on spherical contact manifolds with on-manifold collision checking (spherical-cap obstacles, mutual-exclusion regions); refines paths via manifold-consistent log-exp smoothing; lifts surface paths to admissible rolling motions via Montana kinematics and forward simulation.
AI.result: The framework enables feasible multi-contact rolling motion planning with validated path quality, evaluated against trajectory smoothness, Voronoi seed density, and computation time.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a foundation for extending to non-spherical geometries, time-varying obstacles, and physical rolling robot platforms.

title: Constrained Tabular Diffusion for Finance
comment: Published at ACM International Conference on AI in Finance (ICAIF) 2025
summary: Generative models in finance face the dual challenge of producing realistic data while satisfying strict regulatory and economic objectives, a requirement that standard tabular diffusion models cannot provide. To address this difficulty, we introduce Constrained Tabular Diffusion for Finance (CTDF), a novel integration of sampling-time feasibility operations with mixed-type tabular diffusion in financial applications. By incorporating a training-free feasibility operator into the reverse-diffusion sampling loop, CTDF enforces hard constraints for applications such as simulation, legal compliance, and extrapolation. Extensive experiments on large-scale financial datasets demonstrate zero constraint violations and improvement in scarce data utility. CTDF establishes a robust method for generating trustworthy and compliant synthetic data, opening new avenues for rigorous generative modeling and analysis in the financial domain.

AI.tldr: CTDF integrates training-free feasibility operators into tabular diffusion models to enforce hard constraints, achieving zero violations and better data utility on financial datasets.
AI.motivation: Standard tabular diffusion models cannot satisfy strict regulatory and economic constraints required in financial applications.
AI.method: Introduces a training-free feasibility operator into the reverse-diffusion sampling loop to enforce hard constraints on mixed-type tabular data.
AI.result: Zero constraint violations and improved scarce data utility on large-scale financial datasets.
AI.conclusion: CTDF provides a robust method for generating trustworthy, compliant synthetic financial data, opening new avenues for rigorous generative modeling in finance.

title: CLEAR-MoE: Shared-Basis Expert Extraction from Frozen Vision Transformers via Calibration-Driven Layer Selection
summary: We present CLEAR-MoE, a four-phase post-training pipeline that converts a frozen pretrained Vision Transformer (ViT) into a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model without updating backbone weights. The pipeline (i) scores feed-forward network (FFN) layers by sparsity, clusterability, and output sensitivity; (ii) decomposes selected layers into a shared low-rank SVD basis and per-cluster residual experts using k-means clustering; (iii) trains lightweight routers supervised by cluster labels; and (iv) dispatches tokens through pluggable CUDA backends. On Imagenette with DeiT-Small, CLEAR-MoE retains 99.9% of the dense model's accuracy (86.70 +/- 0.02% versus 86.73%). Extensive ablation studies reveal a consistent empirical finding: the shared SVD basis is the primary factor responsible for preserving accuracy. Random routing, learned routing, and three different router architectures produce nearly identical performance, with accuracy varying by at most 0.06 percentage points (86.62%-86.68%). Accuracy also remains stable across different SVD ranks, expert counts (2-8), calibration set sizes (50-500), and random seeds. This behavior generalizes across five ViT backbones (DeiT-Tiny, DeiT-Small, DeiT-Base, ViT-Small, and ViT-Base), covering models from 5.7M to 86.6M parameters, with accuracy differences <= 0.10 percentage points from their dense counterparts. On a GTX 960 GPU, routing and scatter-gather overhead make the CLEAR-MoE FFN 1.3-1.7x slower than the dense implementation. A dispatch microbenchmark further shows that routing is an order of magnitude more memory-bound than expert matrix multiplications, identifying fused dispatch kernels as a promising direction for future optimization.

AI.tldr: CLEAR-MoE converts a frozen pretrained ViT into a sparse MoE model via a four-phase post-training pipeline, retaining ~99.9% dense accuracy (86.70% vs 86.73% on Imagenette/DeiT-Small). Key finding: accuracy preservation is driven by the shared SVD basis, not routing mechanisms. However, current CUDA implementation is 1.3-1.7x slower due to routing overhead.
AI.motivation: To convert frozen pretrained Vision Transformers into sparse Mixture-of-Experts models without updating backbone weights, enabling efficient inference while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Four-phase post-training pipeline: (i) score FFN layers by sparsity, clusterability, and output sensitivity; (ii) decompose selected layers into shared low-rank SVD basis + per-cluster residual experts via k-means; (iii) train lightweight routers supervised by cluster labels; (iv) dispatch tokens through pluggable CUDA backends.
AI.result: On Imagenette with DeiT-Small, CLEAR-MoE retains 99.9% dense accuracy (86.70% vs 86.73%). The shared SVD basis is the primary accuracy-preserving factor; routing mechanisms have negligible impact (≤0.06 ppt variation). Generalizes across five ViT backbones (5.7M-86.6M params) with ≤0.10 ppt accuracy drop. Currently 1.3-1.7x slower than dense due to routing/scatter-gather overhead.
AI.conclusion: CLEAR-MoE effectively converts frozen ViTs into MoEs with minimal accuracy loss, but current speed penalty points to fused dispatch kernels as a key optimization direction for future work.

title: Low-cost concept-based localized explanations: How far can we get with training-free approaches?
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI), 8-10 May 2026, Granada, Spain. Code: https://github.com/darianfgUgr/CoNa
summary: Concept-based Explainable AI (C-XAI) seeks human-understandable explanations grounded in semantic concepts, yet validation is limited by the scarcity of fine-grained concept annotations. We evaluate whether mid-scale Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can perform localized concept naming under strict zero-shot conditions by assigning labels to bounding-box regions at both object and part levels. We propose a reproducible zero-shot evaluation protocol for Concept Naming (CoNa) with (i) closed-set, category-constrained prompting for moderate vocabularies and (ii) Open-CoNa, an embedding-similarity-based strategy for large label spaces. Experiments with four MLLMs (7B-32B) show consistent performance trends across datasets, reaching 62%-88% object-level exact-match accuracy, highlighting the potential of training-free concept annotation from localized regions. We discuss limitations and failure modes and release a reproducible framework to support future low-cost C-XAI research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes two zero-shot protocols (CoNa and Open-CoNa) for using MLLMs to name concepts in bounding-box regions without training, achieving 62-88% accuracy at object level, aiming to reduce the need for expensive fine-grained annotations in C-XAI.
AI.motivation: Current C-XAI validation is limited by the scarcity of fine-grained concept annotations; the authors aim to explore whether MLLMs can perform localized concept naming under zero-shot conditions to address this bottleneck.
AI.method: Two protocols: (1) CoNa - closed-set, category-constrained prompting with moderate vocabularies; (2) Open-CoNa - embedding-similarity-based strategy for large label spaces. Tested on four MLLMs (7B-32B) with bounding-box regions at object and part levels.
AI.result: Consistent performance trends across datasets, reaching 62%-88% object-level exact-match accuracy, demonstrating the potential of training-free concept annotation from localized regions.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot MLLM-based concept naming is feasible for cost-effective C-XAI, but limitations and failure modes remain. The authors release a reproducible framework to support future research.

title: When and Which Sensor to Observe? Timely Tracking of a Joint Markov Source
summary: We investigate the problem of remote estimation (at a monitor) of a discrete-time joint Markov process with individual components which can be observed with dedicated sensors. At a given time slot, the monitor has the option of staying idle or sending a pull request to one of the sensors to obtain a partial state value, while the sensors are assumed to have heterogeneous sampling costs. Our goal is to develop a monitor pull policy, i.e., determining when and towards which sensor to send a pull request, in order to minimize a weighted sum of average age of incorrect information (AoII), or in short age, and sampling costs. As the communication model, we assume an erasure channel with a fixed one-slot delay from each sensor to the monitor. In this setting, the monitor does not perfectly know either the state of the process or the age, at any given time. We first obtain a sufficient statistic, namely belief, representing the joint distribution of the age and the current state of the observed process, by using the history of all pull requests and observations. Then, we formulate the optimization problem as a continuous state-space Markov decision process (MDP), namely belief-MDP, for the solution of which we propose two model predictive control (MPC) methods, namely MPC without terminal costs (MPC-WTC), and reinforcement learning MPC (RL-MPC). The effectiveness of the proposed methods is validated by numerical examples.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses remote estimation of a joint Markov process where a monitor can pull partial state updates from sensors with heterogeneous costs over erasure channels with delay. It formulates the problem as a belief-MDP and proposes two MPC-based methods (MPC-WTC and RL-MPC) to minimize weighted age of incorrect information (AoII) plus sampling costs.
AI.motivation: To minimize a weighted sum of average Age of Incorrect Information (AoII) and heterogeneous sampling costs in a remote estimation setting where the monitor lacks perfect knowledge of both the process state and the age, due to erasure channels and delays.
AI.method: The authors first derive a sufficient statistic (belief) representing the joint distribution of age and state. They formulate the problem as a continuous state-space belief-MDP and solve it using two model predictive control approaches: MPC without terminal costs (MPC-WTC) and reinforcement learning MPC (RL-MPC).
AI.result: The effectiveness of both proposed MPC methods (MPC-WTC and RL-MPC) is validated through numerical examples, demonstrating their ability to balance AoII minimization with sampling costs under imperfect information and communication constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed belief-MDP framework with MPC-based solution methods effectively addresses the remote estimation problem with heterogeneous sensor costs, erasure channels, and imperfect knowledge, providing practical policies for minimizing AoII and sampling costs.

title: Hybrid Active-Online Learning Framework for Label-Efficient Concept Drift Adaptation in Optical Network Failure Detection
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at the European Conference on Optical Communication (ECOC 2026)
summary: We propose a hybrid active-online learning framework for label-efficient concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection. Using margin-based selective labeling, our method achieves nearceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples, with negligible latency overhead compared to static inference.

AI.tldr: A hybrid active-online learning framework using margin-based selective labeling achieves near-ceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples for optical network failure detection.
AI.motivation: To achieve label-efficient concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection, reducing the labeling burden while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.method: Hybrid active-online learning with margin-based selective labeling, querying only 3.4% of streaming samples.
AI.result: Near-ceiling accuracy and AUC scores with negligible latency overhead compared to static inference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables efficient drift adaptation with minimal labeling cost and latency in optical network failure detection.

title: TAP-VLA: Tactile Annotation Prompting for Vision Language Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate impressive reasoning over visual, semantic, and spatial task variations by leveraging large-scale vision and language pre-training. They remain, however, largely blind to contact forces, which seldom manifest clearly in visual feedback but are central to contact-rich manipulation. Tactile sensing measures these forces directly, but integrating it into VLAs is difficult: tactile data is absent from the large-scale corpora used to pre-train VLAs, so adding it as a new input modality induces a distribution shift that erodes the very pre-training that makes VLAs effective. We propose Tactile Annotation Prompting for Vision-Language-Action models (TAP-VLA), a simple framework that supplies tactile feedback through visual augmentation rather than architectural change. TAP-VLA extracts shear fields from visuo-tactile sensors and overlays them as spatially-grounded vectors onto the multi-view RGB images the policy already consumes, yielding a clear, interpretable tactile cue in the VLA's native observation space. Because the architecture is untouched, the approach requires no tactile pre-training, adds negligible compute, and stays close to the pre-training distribution. Across four contact-rich tasks, TAP-VLA succeeds on 78% of trials, compared to under 50% for vision-only fine-tuning and alternative tactile-fusion baselines -- including tasks where the baselines perform no better than chance.

AI.tldr: TAP-VLA integrates tactile feedback into Vision-Language-Action models via visual augmentation (overlaying shear fields onto RGB images) rather than architectural changes, achieving 78% success on contact-rich tasks vs. under 50% for baselines.
AI.motivation: VLAs lack tactile understanding because contact forces are not visible in standard visual data, yet tactile data cannot be easily added since it's absent from pre-training corpora and would cause distribution shift.
AI.method: Extract shear fields from visuo-tactile sensors and overlay them as spatially-grounded vectors onto multi-view RGB images, keeping the VLA architecture unchanged.
AI.result: 78% success rate across four contact-rich tasks, compared to under 50% for vision-only fine-tuning and alternative tactile-fusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: Tactile feedback can be effectively supplied through visual augmentation without architectural modification, preserving pre-training benefits and requiring no tactile pre-training.

title: A Path-Space Formulation of Prediction in World Models: From a Single Action to Prediction, Planning, and Irreversibility
summary: We propose a path-space formulation of prediction in AI world models. Rather than sequences of one-step conditional distributions, we argue that a world model implicitly defines a probability measure over future trajectories. In the local regime where latent dynamics admit an effective Markovian description, this path measure takes the Onsager-Machlup form. Within this framework, prediction (most probable trajectory), planning (constrained optimization), and uncertainty (fluctuations) emerge as operations on a single action functional. We decompose the latent dynamics into reversible and irreversible components and introduce operational measures of entropy production from model rollouts. In controlled small-scale attention-based models, we find that attention asymmetry is acquired during training in proportion to the irreversibility of the data. Symmetrizing the learned attention suppresses entropy production and selectively degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics while preserving relaxational prediction. These results suggest that irreversibility may serve as a computational resource for predictive world models. More generally, the fundamental predictive object is a distribution over future paths rather than states.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a path-space formulation for AI world models using the Onsager-Machlup action functional, showing that prediction, planning, and uncertainty emerge from a single framework. It reveals that attention asymmetry encodes data irreversibility and serves as a computational resource for long-horizon prediction.
AI.motivation: Current world models treat prediction as sequences of one-step conditional distributions, lacking a unified framework for trajectory-level prediction, planning, and uncertainty quantification. The authors argue for reformulating prediction as a distribution over future paths rather than states.
AI.method: The authors develop a path-space formulation where world models define probability measures over future trajectories, taking the Onsager-Machlup form under Markovian latent dynamics. They decompose dynamics into reversible/irreversible components and derive entropy production measures from model rollouts, then test on small-scale attention-based models.
AI.result: Attention asymmetry is acquired during training in proportion to data irreversibility. Symmetrizing attention suppresses entropy production and degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics while preserving relaxational prediction. Entropy production serves as a measurable operational quantity.
AI.conclusion: Irreversibility may serve as a computational resource for predictive world models. The fundamental predictive object should be a distribution over future paths, not states.

title: Detecting Clinical Hallucinations in LVLMs via Counterfactual Visual Grounding Uncertainty
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) are increasingly used for clinical image understanding, yet they remain vulnerable to \emph{hallucinations}--producing textual findings or attributes not supported by the image. We present a vision-traceable hallucination detection framework that audits arbitrary LVLM responses via visual evidence grounding, requiring neither modification nor internal access to the hidden states of LVLMs. Given an LVLM response, we extract visually verifiable entities and use a medical-domain-adapted Qwen-VL grounding verifier to localize each entity on the input image. To enhance the robustness of our detection method, we introduce a counterfactual entity perturbation method and estimate visual evidence uncertainty by contrasting factual and counterfactual grounding results. Specifically, we compute an entity-level uncertainty score from the positive confidence, counterfactual confidence, and their grounding overlap for binary hallucination decision-making. Experiments on multiple medical imaging modalities and LVLM backbones demonstrate that our method consistently improves hallucination detection performance over recent baselines, while providing interpretable localization evidence and strong cross-model transferability. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Agentic-CliniAI/CounterVHD.

AI.tldr: A vision-traceable hallucination detection framework for LVLMs that uses counterfactual entity perturbation and grounding verification to detect hallucinations without modifying LVLM internals, demonstrating improved performance across medical imaging modalities.
AI.motivation: LVLMs used in clinical image understanding suffer from hallucinations (generating textual findings not supported by images), and existing detection methods require internal model access or modifications, which is impractical for auditing arbitrary LVLM systems.
AI.method: 1) Extract visually verifiable entities from LVLM responses. 2) Use a medical-domain-adapted Qwen-VL grounding verifier to localize entities. 3) Introduce counterfactual entity perturbation to estimate visual evidence uncertainty. 4) Compute entity-level uncertainty scores from positive confidence, counterfactual confidence, and grounding overlap for binary hallucination decisions.
AI.result: The method consistently outperforms recent baselines in hallucination detection across multiple medical imaging modalities and LVLM backbones, with strong cross-model transferability and interpretable localization evidence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an effective, model-agnostic approach to detecting LVLM hallucinations in medical imaging, offering both improved performance and interpretable grounding evidence without requiring internal model access.

title: Managing the Human Fallback: Skill Investment Under Improving AI and Worker Mobility
summary: When firms deploy autonomous AI, they must decide how much work to leave to the system and how much to keep workers engaged. This decision affects current output and future human capital. We develop a parsimonious two-period model in which AI may outperform the worker when it functions, but may fail with positive probability. A firm chooses worker engagement; engagement lowers current output for below-benchmark workers, but changes future skill through learning and erosion. We distinguish two dimensions of AI progress: capability, the system's output when it works, and reliability, the probability that it works. In a single-firm benchmark, engagement is valuable only as fallback investment. The firm engages the least-skilled workers most, because they have the largest skill gaps and are least costly to bring toward a useful fallback level. With worker mobility, engagement also affects labor-market sorting: workers prefer jobs that build more valuable skill trajectories. This sorting motive targets higher-skill workers near the AI frontier, where skill gains are more valuable and engagement is less costly. Mobility can therefore reverse the engagement pattern, shifting investment from the least-skilled toward the most-skilled workers below the AI benchmark. Mobility also reshapes how AI progress affects engagement: greater capability raises engagement by increasing the value of the skill trajectory a firm offers, whereas greater reliability can raise or lower it because it reduces fallback need while also changing learning opportunities. Under worker mobility, human-AI work design becomes a problem of human-capital investment, in which allocating work today shapes future skill.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a two-period model examining how firms should allocate work between autonomous AI and human workers, considering both current output and future human capital development. The key finding is that worker mobility fundamentally changes engagement patterns—shifting investment from least-skilled to most-skilled workers near the AI frontier—and reshapes how AI progress (capability vs. reliability) affects human engagement decisions.
AI.motivation: To understand how firms should balance current AI-driven output against future human capital development when deploying autonomous AI systems, particularly given that AI can outperform humans but may fail with positive probability.
AI.method: A parsimonious two-period economic model where AI may outperform workers when functioning but may fail with positive probability. The model distinguishes two dimensions of AI progress: capability (output when working) and reliability (probability of working).
AI.result: Without worker mobility, engagement is a fallback investment targeting least-skilled workers. With mobility, engagement shifts toward higher-skill workers near the AI frontier. Greater AI capability increases engagement, while reliability's effect is ambiguous—reducing fallback need while changing learning opportunities.
AI.conclusion: Under worker mobility, human-AI work design becomes a problem of human-capital investment where allocating work today shapes future skill. This reverses the engagement pattern from least-skilled to most-skilled workers and reshapes how different dimensions of AI progress affect engagement decisions.

title: When Stopping Fails: Rethinking Minimal Risk Conditions through Human-Interactive Autonomous Driving for Safe Transportation Systems
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure, Accepted to IEEE ITSC 2026
summary: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) are increasingly deployed in urban environments, yet their safety frameworks remain primarily designed around collision avoidance and minimal risk condition (MRC) behaviors such as slowing or stopping when uncertainty arises. Although effective in reducing immediate crash risk, real-world deployments indicate that stopping alone does not guarantee safe integration into human-governed roadway systems. Incidents reported by municipalities and public records show that AV fallback behaviors can obstruct traffic, interfere with emergency response operations, and create accessibility challenges for passengers and pedestrians. This paper presents an analysis of publicly documented incidents involving AV stopping behavior and human-AV interaction failures. We categorize these incidents according to limitations in perception, planning, and control within current AV architectures. Using this taxonomy, we identify key gaps in existing safety paradigms, particularly the lack of mechanisms for interpreting human authority, responding to multimodal instructions, and adapting to dynamic, socially regulated traffic conditions. We then review emerging research directions that support human-interactive perception, language-grounded and accessibility-aware planning, and assisted control through remote guidance and teleoperation. The analysis highlights the need to augment current AV safety frameworks with capabilities that enable cooperative interaction with human agents and infrastructure. These findings suggest that reliable urban deployment of AVs requires moving beyond passive fallback strategies toward human-interactive autonomy.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes publicly documented AV incidents involving stopping behaviors and human-AV interaction failures, categorizing them by perception, planning, and control limitations, and argues that AV safety frameworks must move beyond passive fallback strategies toward human-interactive autonomy for reliable urban deployment.
AI.motivation: Current AV safety frameworks primarily rely on collision avoidance and minimal risk condition behaviors like stopping, but real-world incidents show that stopping alone is insufficient—it can obstruct traffic, interfere with emergency response, and create accessibility issues, motivating the need to identify gaps and new directions.
AI.method: The authors analyze publicly documented incidents involving AV stopping behavior and human-AV interaction failures, categorize them according to limitations in perception, planning, and control within current AV architectures, and review emerging research directions.
AI.result: Key gaps identified include lack of mechanisms for interpreting human authority, responding to multimodal instructions, and adapting to dynamic socially regulated traffic conditions. Emerging directions include human-interactive perception, language-grounded/accessibility-aware planning, and assisted control via remote guidance and teleoperation.
AI.conclusion: Reliable urban AV deployment requires augmenting current safety frameworks with cooperative interaction capabilities, moving beyond passive fallback strategies toward human-interactive autonomy.

title: Hierarchical Decision Making with Structured Policies: A Principled Design via Inverse Optimization
summary: Hierarchical decision-making frameworks are pivotal for addressing complex control tasks, enabling agents to decompose intricate problems into manageable subgoals. Despite their promise, existing hierarchical policies face critical limitations: (i) reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods struggle to guarantee strict constraint satisfaction, and (ii) optimal control (OC)-based approaches often rely on myopic and computationally prohibitive formulations. To reconcile these trade-offs, hierarchical RL-OC architectures have emerged as a promising paradigm. However, the formulation of the lower-level optimization within these frameworks remains underexplored, often relying on heuristic or myopic objectives. In this work, we propose a principled framework that systematically integrates upper-level goal abstraction with structured lower-level decision making. We adopt an inverse optimization approach to inform the structure of the lower-level problem from expert demonstrations, ensuring that the objective of the lower-level policy remains aligned with the overall long-term task goal. To validate the approach, our framework is evaluated on distinct decision making tasks: network-based resource allocation and continuous collision avoidance. Empirical results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines based on end-to-end RL, learning-augmented optimal control, and existing hierarchical RL approaches in both efficiency and decision quality.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical RL-OC framework that uses inverse optimization to align lower-level objectives with long-term goals, outperforming baselines in resource allocation and collision avoidance tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing hierarchical policies face critical limitations: RL-based methods struggle with constraint satisfaction, while OC-based approaches are myopic and computationally prohibitive. Hierarchical RL-OC architectures exist but their lower-level optimization formulation remains underexplored, often relying on heuristic or myopic objectives.
AI.method: The framework systematically integrates upper-level goal abstraction with structured lower-level decision making. It adopts an inverse optimization approach to inform the lower-level problem structure from expert demonstrations, ensuring alignment between lower-level objectives and long-term task goals.
AI.result: Evaluated on network-based resource allocation and continuous collision avoidance tasks, the method consistently outperforms end-to-end RL, learning-augmented optimal control, and existing hierarchical RL approaches in both efficiency and decision quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed principled framework successfully addresses the underexplored lower-level optimization problem in hierarchical RL-OC architectures, demonstrating superior performance across distinct decision-making domains.

title: MammoFlow: Multiview Mammogram Synthesis with Anatomically Consistent Flow Matching
summary: Multiview mammography relies on paired craniocaudal (CC) and mediolateral oblique (MLO) views to provide complementary projections of a 3D breast volume, enabling precise anomaly localization. However, acquiring high-quality, balanced datasets remains challenging for deep learning applications. We propose a novel method to synthesize multiview mammograms by leveraging the inherent geometric relationship between CC and MLO views. To enforce an implicit 3D consistency prior during generation, we develop an alignment module that searches a 2D affine transformation subspace to establish optimal anatomical correspondence. Leveraging this alignment, we introduce a pixel-space self-consistency loss based on the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between the 1D anteroposterior (AP) axis tissue distributions of the generated images. Integrated into a pretrained flow matching model, MammoFlow forces synthesized pairs to share physically plausible tissue distributions from the chest wall to the nipple. To our knowledge, this is the first work to guide multiview mammogram generation using implicit geometric tissue correspondence. Our method demonstrates superior image quality, passes expert radiologist evaluation, and generates physically consistent pairs that improve downstream classification AUC by 5%. Code is available at https://github.com/XYPB/MammoFlow

AI.tldr: MammoFlow synthesizes multiview mammogram pairs (CC and MLO views) by enforcing implicit 3D consistency via an alignment module and Earth Mover's Distance-based self-consistency loss, improving image quality and downstream classification by 5% AUC.
AI.motivation: Multiview mammography requires paired CC and MLO views for anomaly localization, but acquiring high-quality balanced datasets for deep learning is challenging.
AI.method: An alignment module searches a 2D affine transformation subspace for anatomical correspondence, combined with a pixel-space self-consistency loss using Earth Mover's Distance on 1D AP axis tissue distributions, integrated into a pretrained flow matching model.
AI.result: Superior image quality, passes expert radiologist evaluation, and improves downstream classification AUC by 5%.
AI.conclusion: First work to guide multiview mammogram generation using implicit geometric tissue correspondence, with publicly available code.

title: Characterizing Large Language Model Agentic Workflows: A Study on N8n Ecosystem
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted in low-code and no-code automation platforms, where non-expert users design workflows that combine natural language understanding with external services and APIs. LLM agents are LLM systems that use LLMs as a core "brain" to reason, plan, and autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks. In this paper, we present the first large-scale empirical study of LLM agentic workflows in low-code automation platforms. We analyze more than 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows and examine four aspects of their design: task distribution, structural and tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels. Our analysis shows that LLM workflows are not merely prompt response pipelines. Instead, LLMs are commonly embedded within broader automation structures involving control logic, external tools, communication services, storage systems, and human review points. We further find that while many workflows include lightweight post-processing or routing logic after LLM execution, explicit reliability mechanisms such as structured fallback paths, repair loops, failure-specific alerts, and human approval gates remain relatively uncommon. These results reveal a gap between the increasing deployment of LLM agents in practical automation ecosystems and the limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. Overall, our study provides ten empirical findings and five research takeaways for researchers, platform developers, and practitioners seeking to understand and improve real-world LLM agentic workflows.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study of 6,000+ n8n LLM agentic workflows in low-code platforms, finding that LLMs are embedded in broader automation structures with control logic and external tools, but explicit reliability mechanisms remain rare.
AI.motivation: LLMs are rapidly adopted in low-code/no-code automation platforms, yet there is limited empirical understanding of how LLM agents are actually designed and deployed in real-world, practical automation ecosystems.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical analysis of over 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows, examining task distribution, structural/tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels.
AI.result: LLM workflows are embedded in broader automation structures (control logic, external tools, communication services, storage, human review), not just prompt-response pipelines. However, explicit reliability mechanisms (fallback paths, repair loops, failure alerts, human approval gates) remain uncommon.
