AI.conclusion: There is a significant gap between increasing LLM agent deployment and limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. The study provides 10 empirical findings and 5 research takeaways for stakeholders.

title: Bayesian Best-Arm Identification with Abstention: A Polynomial-to-Exponential Phase Transition
summary: We study the Bayesian fixed-budget best-arm identification problem in which a learner can abstain from making a terminal recommendation. Subject to an abstention budget $α$, we analyze the probability of undetected error--the risk of recommending a suboptimal arm without abstaining. Our central finding is that abstention induces a phase transition: without abstention, the error probability decays polynomially in the sampling budget $T$; in contrast, introducing any small positive abstention budget shifts this to an exponential decay. For Gaussian priors and rewards, in the regime $T\to\infty$ followed by $α\downarrow0$, we establish exact matching information-theoretic lower bounds and algorithmic upper bounds on the optimal error exponent, which takes the form $\exp(-\frac{α^{2}T}{8κ_ν^{2}})$. The hardness parameter $κ_ν$ represents the prior density of the top-two gap at zero, highlighting that nearly tied instances drive the fundamental error. We introduce an adaptive algorithm, PGWS, that successfully achieves this optimal exponent by expending its abstention budget on statistically ambiguous instances. We further demonstrate that this polynomial-to-exponential improvement is exclusively a Bayesian phenomenon--in the frequentist setting, abstention only affects lower-order exponent terms. We also extend our results beyond the Gaussian model.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Bayesian fixed-budget best-arm identification with abstention, showing that even a small abstention budget shifts error decay from polynomial to exponential, with optimal exponent exp(-α²T/(8κν²)).
AI.motivation: To understand how the option to abstain from recommending (subject to a budget) affects the fundamental error probability in best-arm identification, and to characterize the optimal error exponent under Bayesian settings.
AI.method: The authors derive information-theoretic lower bounds and propose an adaptive algorithm (PGWS) that achieves the optimal error exponent by spending abstention budget on ambiguous instances. They analyze both Bayesian and frequentist settings, and extend beyond Gaussian models.
AI.result: Abstention induces a phase transition: polynomial decay without abstention vs. exponential decay with any positive abstention budget. The optimal error exponent is exp(-α²T/(8κν²)), where κν captures prior density of the top-two gap at zero.
AI.conclusion: Abstention fundamentally changes the error rate in Bayesian best-arm identification, achieving exponential improvement. This improvement is exclusively a Bayesian phenomenon and does not extend to frequentist settings.

title: On the Identifiability of Aided Inertial Navigation Under Measurement Delays: A Geometric Approach
comment: Technical Report STARS-2026-001, University of Toronto Institute for Aerospace Studies (24 pages)
summary: In aided inertial navigation, measurements from different sensors are often subject to unknown relative time delays. Consider a single aiding sensor whose measurements have an unknown but constant delay relative to the inertial-measurement data stream. We study the identifiability of the delay and the initial navigation state that parameterizes the trajectory. Identifiability depends on both the temporal structure of the aiding measurements and the form of the trajectory itself. Our geometric analysis shows that, for a larger class of uninformative (i.e., degenerate) trajectories than has previously been reported, the delayed measurement model admits a continuous symmetry that prevents unique delay-and-state recovery.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the identifiability of unknown constant time delays in aided inertial navigation systems, showing that certain degenerate trajectories create continuous symmetries preventing unique delay and state recovery.
AI.motivation: In aided inertial navigation, sensor measurements often suffer from unknown relative time delays. Prior work had not fully characterized when these delays and navigation states are uniquely identifiable.
AI.method: The authors employ geometric analysis to study the identifiability of delay and initial navigation state parameters under a delayed measurement model. They examine how temporal structure of aiding measurements and trajectory form affect identifiability.
AI.result: A larger class of degenerate (uninformative) trajectories than previously reported exhibits continuous symmetry, preventing unique delay-and-state recovery.
AI.conclusion: The identifiability of time delays in aided inertial navigation depends critically on trajectory structure, with certain degenerate trajectories fundamentally limiting unique recovery.

title: Generative Learning as a Tool to Improve Perception of Emotional Body Motion Expressions
summary: Emotional body motion expressions are an essential element of non-verbal communication. Effectively conveying these expressions through technology is of utmost importance, for example, with virtual reality avatars and in social robotics. Recent advances in generative models have opened new opportunities for advancing research on emotional body motion learning. However, generating accurate emotional expression representations is challenging, given the subtlety of emotional cues, individual variability, and cultural differences. We investigate whether a generative model can implicitly learn emotional body motions directly from culturally grounded motion-capture data, without explicit emotion-motion guidance. Using a dataset of emotional performances by 49 Japanese actors, we trained a Transformer-based generative model to generate expressive motions conditioned on 13 discrete emotion labels. We evaluate the generated motions from two perspectives: (1) an LSTM-based classifier to assess recognizability by machine observers, achieving a recognition accuracy of 22.80%, and (2) a human perception study with Japanese raters to assess alignment with human affective interpretations, yielding a recognition accuracy of 24.91%. Beyond these, we evaluate the utility of generative modeling for three practical tasks: augmenting emotion recognition models, extracting representative emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing smooth transitions between emotion intensities. Our findings highlight the potential of implicit, data-driven generative modeling to enhance affective computing applications and our understanding of emotion expressions.

AI.tldr: This study explores whether Transformer-based generative models can implicitly learn emotional body motions from motion-capture data of Japanese actors, without explicit emotion-motion guidance. The model generates motions conditioned on 13 discrete emotion labels, achieving recognition accuracies of 22.80% (machine) and 24.91% (human raters), and demonstrates utility in augmenting emotion recognition, extracting emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing emotion intensity transitions.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether generative models can implicitly learn emotional body motions from culturally grounded motion-capture data without explicit emotion-motion guidance, addressing challenges in generating accurate emotional expression representations due to subtle emotional cues, individual variability, and cultural differences.
AI.method: Used a Transformer-based generative model trained on emotional performance data from 49 Japanese actors (13 discrete emotion labels). Evaluated generated motions via LSTM-based classifier (machine recognition) and human perception study with Japanese raters. Also assessed utility for three practical tasks: augmenting emotion recognition models, extracting emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing smooth emotion intensity transitions.
AI.result: Machine recognition accuracy of 22.80% and human recognition accuracy of 24.91% for generated emotional body motions. The model showed practical utility for augmenting emotion recognition, extracting representative emotion-specific patterns, and synthesizing smooth transitions between emotion intensities.
AI.conclusion: Implicit, data-driven generative modeling has potential to enhance affective computing applications and our understanding of emotion expressions, despite relatively modest recognition accuracies.

title: DataComp-VLM: Improved Open Datasets for Vision-Language Models
summary: Building performant Vision-Language Models (VLMs) requires carefully curating large-scale training datasets, yet the community lacks systematic benchmarks for evaluating such curation strategies. We introduce DataComp for VLMs (DCVLM), a benchmark for controlled data-centric experiments to improve VLM training. As part of DCVLM, we collect 160 datasets spanning four data types -- image-caption pairs, multimodal interleaved documents, text-only, and instruction-tuning data -- into a corpus of 6T multimodal tokens. DCVLM allows participants to test curation strategies (filtering, mixing, formatting, sampling) across 1B-8B models and 6.25B-200B token budgets. Models are then evaluated on a carefully selected suite of up to 52 downstream benchmarks across 9 domains. We conduct extensive experiments on DCVLM and find that data mixing, not filtering, is key to a high-quality training dataset: instruction-heavy mixtures scale better than caption-heavy ones, with gains widening at larger scales. The resulting dataset, DCVLM-Baseline, enables training an 8B VLM to 63.6% accuracy on our 33-task core suite with 200B training tokens. Compared to FineVision, the state-of-the-art open VLM training dataset, this represents an improvement of +5.4pp. DCVLM and all accompanying artifacts will be made publicly available at https://www.datacomp.ai/dcvlm/.

AI.tldr: DCVLM is a benchmark for systematically evaluating data curation strategies for VLMs, revealing that data mixing (especially instruction-heavy mixtures) is more important than filtering. Their baseline dataset achieves 63.6% accuracy on 33 tasks, outperforming FineVision by +5.4pp.
AI.motivation: The community lacks systematic benchmarks for evaluating data curation strategies for training Vision-Language Models.
AI.method: Curated a corpus of 160 datasets (6T multimodal tokens) spanning four data types, and designed a controlled benchmark testing curation strategies (filtering, mixing, formatting, sampling) across 1B-8B models and 6.25B-200B token budgets, evaluated on up to 52 downstream benchmarks.
AI.result: Data mixing (not filtering) is key; instruction-heavy mixtures scale better than caption-heavy ones. DCVLM-Baseline enables 63.6% accuracy on 33 tasks with 200B tokens, outperforming FineVision by +5.4pp.
AI.conclusion: Data mixing strategy, particularly emphasizing instruction data, is critical for high-quality VLM training. DCVLM provides a standardized benchmark for future data-centric research.

title: HiComm: Hierarchical Communication for Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) often relies on communication to mitigate partial observability, yet most existing protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors detached from the structure of the observations they summarize. This design overlooks an important source of inductive bias in many cooperative environments, where observations naturally follow a hierarchy such as groups and entities. We propose \textsc{HiComm}, a plug-in communication module that grounds messages in the sender's hierarchical observation. \textsc{HiComm} is receiver-driven: the receiver issues a query, and the hierarchy is resolved through a three-stage decoding process that first selects a group, then a sender, and then an entity within that group, returning the corresponding feature slice as the message. This converts communication from unstructured vector transmission into structured information retrieval over the sender's observation hierarchy. We instantiate this mechanism with Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and a lightweight shared projection design that attaches to standard MARL pipelines. Experiments across cooperative MARL tasks with different observation structures and coordination demands show that \textsc{HiComm} matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines while reducing communication volume by up to $23\times$ per receiver per episode.

AI.tldr: HiComm is a plug-in communication module for cooperative MARL that grounds messages in the sender's hierarchical observation structure, using receiver-driven three-stage decoding (group → sender → entity) to achieve efficient, structured information retrieval, reducing communication volume by up to 23× while matching or outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing MARL communication protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors, ignoring the hierarchical structure (groups, entities) present in many cooperative environments, which is a valuable source of inductive bias.
AI.method: HiComm is a receiver-driven, three-stage hierarchical decoding process: first selects a group, then a sender, then an entity within that group, returning the corresponding feature slice. It uses Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and a lightweight shared projection design.
AI.result: HiComm matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines across cooperative MARL tasks while reducing communication volume by up to 23× per receiver per episode.
AI.conclusion: Structured, hierarchy-aware communication grounded in observation structure improves efficiency and performance in cooperative MARL compared to unstructured vector transmission.

title: Multi-Contact Force Estimation for Continuum Robots via Gaussian-Parameterized Factor Graphs
summary: Continuum robots offer key advantages in navigating unstructured environments, but their safe operation requires accurate estimation of the external contact forces acting anywhere along the robot body. Estimating these forces at unknown locations is an ill-conditioned problem, particularly for multiple contacts. We propose a unified shape and force estimation framework formulated on a factor graph. By incorporating a Gaussian mixture force parameterization into a discretized probabilistic Cosserat rod model, we reduce the dimensionality of the unknown external forces and mitigate the ill-conditioning of node-wise force estimation. The framework fuses strain, tendon tension, and pose measurements to simultaneously estimate the robot's shape and external forces while accounting for modeling and sensor uncertainties. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing methods in terms of force location and magnitude estimation for both single and multi-contact scenarios. We further present a progressive variant that introduces basis functions on demand to estimate contact forces sequentially during a simulated confined-navigation task.

AI.tldr: A factor graph-based framework using Gaussian mixture force parameterization and a probabilistic Cosserat rod model for simultaneous shape and multi-contact force estimation of continuum robots, with a progressive variant for sequential force estimation during navigation.
AI.motivation: Continuum robots need accurate external contact force estimation for safe operation, but estimating forces at unknown locations is ill-conditioned, especially for multiple contacts.
AI.method: Formulates shape and force estimation as a factor graph problem; incorporates Gaussian mixture force parameterization into a discretized probabilistic Cosserat rod model; fuses strain, tendon tension, and pose measurements.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods in force location and magnitude estimation for both single and multi-contact scenarios in numerical simulations. A progressive variant enables sequential contact force estimation during confined navigation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the ill-conditioned multi-contact force estimation problem for continuum robots, with demonstrated accuracy and practical applicability through a progressive estimation variant.

title: On design-unbiased algorithmic Machine Learning
summary: Machine Learning (ML) algorithms, such as k-Nearest Neighbours (kNN) or random forest, eschew the ideal of true data models in favour of predictive performance. However, minimising the MSE or F-score cannot lead to unbiasedness directly, which is important in many situations such as official statistics. We study the conditions of algorithmic ML, other than the existence and knowledge of true data models, which lead to unbiased prediction or classification for a given finite population, including how the training data may be sampled from the population, how a trained prediction algorithm can be tuned to achieve unbiased prediction or classification for that population, and how the performance of out-of-sample prediction or classification can be assessed unbiasedly. The inference is based on the known probability design of samples and training sets, rather than any assumed distributions or models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how Machine Learning algorithms can achieve unbiased prediction and classification for finite populations by leveraging known probability sampling designs, rather than relying on true data models or distributional assumptions.
AI.motivation: ML algorithms prioritize predictive performance (e.g., MSE, F-score) but do not directly guarantee unbiasedness, which is critical in fields like official statistics. The authors aim to identify conditions under which algorithmic ML can yield unbiased results.
AI.method: The study analyzes conditions for unbiasedness based on known probability sampling designs of training data, rather than assumed distributions. It examines how training data sampling, algorithm tuning, and out-of-sample performance assessment can be designed to achieve unbiasedness.
AI.result: The paper identifies specific conditions related to probability-based sampling and tuning strategies that enable unbiased prediction/classification without requiring true data models.
AI.conclusion: Unbiasedness in ML can be achieved through careful probability-based design of training samples and tuning procedures, offering a model-free inference framework suitable for official statistics and similar applications.

title: KM-Speaker: Keypoint-Based Style Control for High-Quality Speech-Driven 3D Facial Animation and Dialogue Localization
summary: Speech-driven 3D facial animation methods face significant challenges in simultaneously achieving high-fidelity motion and precise artistic control at production quality. Existing controllable models typically learn global style control by relying on large-scale, low-quality \emph{in-the-wild} datasets that compromise overall animation realism. Furthermore, these frameworks often lack the fine-grained temporal precision required for demanding tasks such as dialogue localization (e.g., dubbing), where matching specific facial expressions is as critical as lip synchronization. We present KM-Speaker (Keypoint-Matching Speaker), a novel keypoint-conditioned flow-based generative framework that provides both global style guidance and frame-level temporal control from reference performances. We propose a disentanglement strategy that separates audio-driven lip motion from keypoint-driven upper-face dynamics, together with a global style context preservation mechanism to ensure coherent full-face expressiveness. KM-Speaker advances example-based 3D facial animation by achieving high-fidelity motion and flexible controllability in a data-constrained setting, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods in lip-sync accuracy, style adherence, and expressive temporal control.

AI.tldr: KM-Speaker is a keypoint-conditioned flow-based generative framework for speech-driven 3D facial animation that achieves high-fidelity motion and precise artistic control by disentangling audio-driven lip motion from keypoint-driven upper-face dynamics, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in lip-sync accuracy, style adherence, and temporal control.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D facial animation methods struggle to balance high-fidelity motion with fine-grained artistic control, especially for demanding tasks like dubbing. Current controllable models rely on large-scale low-quality datasets compromising realism, and lack frame-level temporal precision for matching specific expressions.
AI.method: KM-Speaker is a keypoint-conditioned flow-based generative framework featuring: (1) a disentanglement strategy separating audio-driven lip motion from keypoint-driven upper-face dynamics, (2) a global style context preservation mechanism for coherent full-face expressiveness, and (3) both global style guidance and frame-level temporal control from reference performances.
AI.result: KM-Speaker consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in lip-sync accuracy, style adherence, and expressive temporal control, achieving high-fidelity motion and flexible controllability in a data-constrained setting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed keypoint-conditioned framework with motion disentanglement enables production-quality 3D facial animation with both global style control and precise frame-level temporal control, advancing example-based facial animation beyond current limitations.

title: Flow Reasoning Models: Scaling Reasoning Through Iterative Self-Refinement
summary: Discrete flow models have recently shown promising performance on few-step text generation; however, when naively applied to structured reasoning tasks such as Sudoku and Zebra puzzles, they converge confidently to incorrect answers (solving only $\sim$36% of Sudoku puzzles). We introduce Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs), a training and test-time-scaling framework for structured reasoning with flow models. We make the observation that, despite their poor solve rate, flow models can act as their own verifiers. A correct answer is a stable fixed point of the denoising dynamics, returning to itself when re-noised and re-solved. This enables a test-time-scaling paradigm: propose many candidate solutions and keep those that are dynamically stable, which alone reaches high solve rates on Sudoku-Shah (~$100\%$) and Zebra ($95.9\%$). This even generalizes to harder out-of-distribution puzzles like Sudoku-Extreme ($96.1\%$), without ever training on that distribution. This pure search, however, wastes a great deal of computation generating incorrect candidate solutions. We therefore design a training recipe to improve the base model's efficiency. First, we train flow models with a self-conditioning channel and close it at inference, letting them refine their own past predictions. Second, we train models to avoid their own failed generations using direct preference optimization. These changes substantially improve the base model's efficiency, letting it reach $99.2\%$ on Sudoku in just $7$ forward passes, over $8\times$ fewer than the strongest matched masked-diffusion baseline we compare needs for the same accuracy. When combined with test-time scaling, this lets flow models solve hard out-of-distribution puzzles (e.g. Sudoku-Extreme) far more efficiently.

AI.tldr: Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs) improve discrete flow models for structured reasoning by using self-verification (stable fixed points of denoising dynamics) for test-time scaling and preference-based training for efficiency, achieving ~100% on Sudoku and 95.9% on Zebra puzzles with 8x fewer forward passes than baselines.
AI.motivation: Discrete flow models perform poorly on structured reasoning tasks (e.g., only ~36% solve rate on Sudoku) because they confidently converge to incorrect answers.
AI.method: (1) Test-time scaling: propose many candidates and select those that are dynamically stable (i.e., return to themselves after re-noising and re-solving). (2) Self-conditioning training: train models with a self-conditioning channel and close it at inference. (3) Direct preference optimization: train models to avoid their own failed generations.
AI.result: Sudoku-Shah ~100%, Zebra 95.9%, generalizes to harder out-of-distribution Sudoku-Extreme 96.1%. With training improvements, 99.2% on Sudoku in just 7 forward passes, 8x fewer than baselines.
AI.conclusion: Flow models can serve as their own verifiers through dynamic stability, enabling test-time scaling; combined with efficient training, they solve structured reasoning tasks far more efficiently than prior methods.

title: TacGen: Touch Is a Necessary Dimension of Physical-World Representation -- Addressing Tactile Data Scarcity with Scalable Vision-to-Touch Alignment and Generation
summary: Touch resolves the physical-property ambiguity left by vision: exploratory contact recovers shape, texture, compliance, and material, and visuo-haptic object representations converge in ventral visual cortex. We ask whether representation learning can reproduce this grounding. TacGen mitigates the tactile-data scarcity bottleneck by combining pre-specified V+T contrastive alignment with a latent-space residual-MLP V->T generator that synthesizes tactile latents from RGB for tactile-data scaling. With matched DINOv2 backbones, splits, and probes, V+T improves matched V-only on mass (Delta R^2=+0.570), density (Delta acc=+0.067), hardness (+0.117), and uncertainty-banded force labels (Delta R^2=+0.281); all CIs exclude zero. The same representation lifts matched-capacity TACTO manipulation 0.246->0.979 while V-only capacity scaling accounts for only 4.5% of the gap, preserving 95.5%. The generator reaches cross-seed +0.589, with real tactile +0.585 inside the seed interval; the architecture comparison shows a 13pp downstream gap between reconstruction quality and representation utility. Across five-seed SSVTP/TVL reproductions, YCB-Sight transfer, three-backbone checks, permutation/random-feature controls, hash-verified manifests, and measured-force validation checks, the evidence supports the claim that touch supplies a necessary physical evidence channel for representations of contact-dependent properties.

AI.tldr: TacGen uses a V+T contrastive alignment and residual-MLP generator to synthesize tactile latents from RGB, improving physical property prediction and closing the visuo-haptic representation gap, showing touch provides necessary evidence for contact-dependent properties.
AI.motivation: To resolve the tactile-data scarcity bottleneck and investigate whether representation learning can reproduce the grounding where touch disambiguates physical properties left ambiguous by vision.
AI.method: TacGen combines pre-specified V+T contrastive alignment with a latent-space residual-MLP V→T generator that synthesizes tactile latents from RGB, using matched DINOv2 backbones for tactile data scaling.
AI.result: V+T improves over V-only on mass (ΔR²=+0.570), density (Δacc=+0.067), hardness (+0.117), and force labels (ΔR²=+0.281); generator achieves +0.589 cross-seed, real tactile +0.585; closes TACTO manipulation gap from 0.246→0.979.
AI.conclusion: The evidence across multiple validations supports that touch supplies a necessary physical evidence channel for representations of contact-dependent properties.

title: HARD-KV: Head-Adaptive Regularization for Decoding-time KV Compression
summary: Long-context LLM inference faces a fundamental conflict: head-adaptive compression algorithms (e.g., Top-$p$ nucleus sampling) offer superior accuracy by dynamically fluctuating memory budgets, yet modern inference engines (e.g., vLLM) demand rigid, static memory patterns to leverage CUDA Graphs and PagedAttention. We resolve this ``Static-Dynamic'' mismatch with HARD-KV, a unified framework that that bridges dynamic selection with rigid system constraints. HARD-KV introduces a Cascade Cache hierarchy, managing the token lifecycle across dense, sparse, and condensed tiers. Crucially, we propose a Logits Calibration mechanism that normalizes diverse importance metrics into a unified probability space, enabling consistent Top-$p$ budgeting across heterogeneous heads. To bridge the efficiency gap, we offer a system-level solution, which rewrites fragmented, dynamic indices into contiguous physical layouts compatible with high-performance inference engine. Extensive experiments on math-reasoning benchmarks (AIME, U-Math) verify that HARD-KV achieves up to 2$\times$ throughput improvement over static baselines while maintaining high-fidelity generation in 10k+ token scenarios. Code is available at https://github.com/SuDIS-ZJU/HARDInfer.

AI.tldr: HARD-KV resolves the conflict between head-adaptive dynamic compression and rigid static memory systems in LLM inference, using a Cascade Cache hierarchy and Logits Calibration to achieve 2x throughput improvement on long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental conflict between head-adaptive compression algorithms (dynamic, accurate) and modern inference engines (static, rigid), causing a "Static-Dynamic" mismatch that limits efficiency.
AI.method: HARD-KV proposes: (1) Cascade Cache hierarchy managing token lifecycle across dense, sparse, and condensed tiers; (2) Logits Calibration mechanism normalizing importance metrics into unified probability space for consistent Top-p budgeting; (3) System-level solution rewriting fragmented dynamic indices into contiguous physical layouts.
AI.result: Up to 2x throughput improvement over static baselines while maintaining high-fidelity generation in 10k+ token scenarios, verified on math-reasoning benchmarks (AIME, U-Math).
AI.conclusion: HARD-KV effectively bridges dynamic selection with rigid system constraints, offering a practical unified framework for efficient long-context LLM inference.

title: Digitizing Coaching Intelligence: An Agentic Framework for Holistic Athlete Profiling using VLM and RAG
summary: Athlete assessment is a critical process for tracking physical progress and identifying elite talent. However, during mass recruitment drives, traditional methods rely on manual observation, which is inherently subjective and unscalable, or basic computer vision (CV) systems limited to quantitative repetition counting. These standard approaches lack the "coaching intelligence" required to evaluate qualitative physiological markers such as form degradation, spinal articulation, and fatigue. This paper presents a novel, LLM-based hybrid agentic framework for automated, holistic athlete profiling that strictly aligns with the Sports Authority of India (SAI) assessment protocols. Orchestrated via LangGraph, our dual-pipeline architecture synthesizes the geometric precision of CV (MediaPipe) for kinematic tracking with the semantic reasoning of Vision-Language Models (Llama-4-scout). To overcome the latency and token constraints associated with multimodal video processing, we introduce a 3 X 3 "Smart Grid" temporal chunking strategy, reducing computational overhead by over 88% while preserving critical temporal continuity. To ensure data integrity and mitigate hallucination, the framework pioneers an autonomous "LLM-as-a-Judge" self-correction loop that cross-references quantitative and qualitative metrics before persistence. Finally, we implement a dual-persistence Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline utilizing a vector search engine (ChromaDB). This enables coaches to bypass rigid SQL databases and perform complex semantic queries (e.g., "Identify athletes with high endurance but poor core rigidity") using natural language. Experimental results demonstrate that this multi-agent approach significantly bridges the gap between raw biometric tracking and actionable coaching insights, offering a scalable, objective solution for national talent identification.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-based hybrid agentic framework for automated athlete assessment that combines computer vision (MediaPipe) for kinematic tracking with Vision-Language Models (Llama-4-scout) for semantic reasoning. Key innovations include a 3×3 "Smart Grid" temporal chunking strategy (88% latency reduction), an "LLM-as-a-Judge" self-correction loop to reduce hallucinations, and a dual-persistence RAG pipeline (ChromaDB) enabling natural language queries. The system aligns with Sports Authority of India protocols.
AI.motivation: Traditional athlete assessment methods are subjective (manual observation), unscalable, or limited to basic repetition counting. They lack the "coaching intelligence" to evaluate qualitative markers like form degradation, spinal articulation, and fatigue, which are critical for mass recruitment drives and national talent identification.
AI.method: A dual-pipeline hybrid agentic framework orchestrated via LangGraph: (1) CV pipeline using MediaPipe for geometric kinematic tracking, (2) VLM pipeline using Llama-4-scout for semantic reasoning. A 3×3 Smart Grid temporal chunking reduces video processing overhead by 88%. An LLM-as-a-Judge self-correction loop cross-references quantitative and qualitative metrics. A dual-persistence RAG pipeline (ChromaDB) enables natural language semantic queries.
AI.result: The Smart Grid chunking strategy reduces computational overhead by over 88% while preserving temporal continuity. The multi-agent approach bridges the gap between raw biometric tracking and actionable coaching insights, offering a scalable, objective solution for national talent identification aligned with SAI protocols.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully combines geometric precision and semantic reasoning for holistic athlete profiling, overcoming latency and hallucination challenges. It enables coaches to perform complex semantic queries in natural language, representing a significant advancement over traditional and basic CV-based assessment methods.

title: Pooled Leaderboards Hide System-Specific Winners: A Reporting-Protocol Audit of Offline Root-Cause Analysis Benchmarks
summary: Offline root-cause-analysis (RCA) benchmarks commonly rank methods by a single pooled top-1 accuracy across multiple subsystems, and engineers often read the pooled winner as a recommendation for their own subsystem. We audit that reading on three public RCA benchmark families -- OpenRCA, RCAEval, and PetShop -- covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. To keep pairwise comparisons on identical cases, the main analysis retains four methods or comparators with complete coverage: BARO, a CD-1min adapter, max-$|Z|$, and per-service alert-count. All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs, every random-effects 95\% prediction interval crosses zero, and case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 held-out subsystems, with regret reaching 24.8 pp on RCAEval / Sock-Shop. We release a 320-line audit module; given a matched RCA benchmark score table, it recomputes the same per-subsystem stability checks alongside pooled scores.

AI.tldr: Offline RCA benchmarks often report pooled top-1 accuracy across subsystems, but this audit shows that rankings are unstable across subsystems: pairwise comparisons show both-sign effects, prediction intervals cross zero, and leave-one-system-out selection can pick the worse method. The authors release an audit module for per-subsystem stability checks.
AI.motivation: Pooled top-1 accuracy in RCA benchmarks is commonly interpreted as a universal recommendation, but this practice may be misleading because different subsystems have different failure characteristics. The authors aim to audit whether such pooled rankings are stable across subsystems.
AI.method: The authors audit three benchmark families (OpenRCA, RCAEval, PetShop) covering 11 subsystems and 778 units. They compare four methods (BARO, CD-1min, max-|Z|, per-service alert-count) using pairwise comparisons, random-effects prediction intervals, case-level interaction tests, and leave-one-system-out selection regret.
AI.result: All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs; every 95% prediction interval crosses zero; 5 out of 6 case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability; leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 subsystems, with regret up to 24.8 pp.
AI.conclusion: Pooled RCA rankings are not stable across subsystems; engineers should not treat a single pooled winner as a universal recommendation. The authors provide an audit module to help check per-subsystem stability.

title: Empowering a Single-Frequency GNSS Receiver to Achieve High-Precision Positioning with Relative Observations
summary: Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) navigation is widely used to provide absolute, outdoor positioning in field robotics. Advances in Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) technology can achieve centimeter-level accuracy, facilitating autonomous navigation tasks. However, the cost and extra infrastructure used for RTK still hinder the application and more cost-effective solutions are desired. In this letter, we present a novel tightly-coupled state estimation framework that achieves high-precision localization by using low-cost, mass-market single-frequency GNSS receivers with any relative motion sensors (e.g., wheel encoder, camera, LiDAR). We propose a sliding-window factor graph that integrates generic relative motion with global epoch-to-anchor constraints derived from continuous carrier phase tracking. To eliminate the reliance on physical base stations, we introduce a virtual anchor mechanism: upon the initial observation of a satellite, its state is locked as a virtual reference to establish global epoch-to-anchor constraints. By substituting multi-frequency hardware redundancy with single-frequency multi-modal kinematic priors and a robust cycle-slip recovery technique, our approach ensures carrier-phase integrity on cheap receivers. Extensive real-world experiments on heterogeneous low-cost sensor suites validate that our method improves the accuracy of a single-frequency receiver from several meters to decimeter-level precision across diverse environments, providing an accurate, cost-effective and reliable alternative for autonomous navigation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a tightly-coupled state estimation framework that uses low-cost single-frequency GNSS receivers with relative motion sensors, replacing expensive RTK infrastructure with a virtual anchor mechanism and sliding-window factor graph to achieve decimeter-level precision for autonomous navigation.
AI.motivation: RTK GNSS provides centimeter-level accuracy but requires expensive multi-frequency receivers and additional base station infrastructure, limiting cost-effective deployment in field robotics.
AI.method: A sliding-window factor graph integrating relative motion sensors with global epoch-to-anchor constraints from continuous carrier phase tracking, using a virtual anchor mechanism that locks initial satellite observations as references, and robust cycle-slip recovery for single-frequency receivers.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show accuracy improvement from several meters to decimeter-level precision across diverse environments using heterogeneous low-cost sensor suites.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers an accurate, cost-effective, and reliable alternative to expensive RTK systems, making high-precision GNSS localization accessible for autonomous navigation with low-cost hardware.

title: Active Quantum Kernel Acquisition for Gaussian Process Regression
summary: Quantum kernel estimation on near-term hardware is shot-budgeted: every entry of the kernel Gram matrix is a Bernoulli expectation that must be sampled with a finite number of circuit executions. Recent work on quantum kernel classification has shown that allocating shots non-uniformly across kernel entries, weighted by their downstream task sensitivity, can reduce the shot budget required to reach a target accuracy. We extend this idea to Gaussian process (GP) regression, a setting whose downstream quantities (full-spectrum posterior variance, log-determinant, marginal likelihood) couple to kernel error more tightly than the sign-only outputs of classification. We derive three closed-form pair-level sensitivities predictive coupling $|α_iα_j|$, leave-one-out residual, and marginal-likelihood gradient and plug them into a Neyman-style minimum-variance allocation rule. To prevent catastrophic over-concentration when the warm-up sensitivity estimate is itself noisy, we add a high uniform coverage floor justified by a Frobenius lower bound on the missing-entry perturbation. On four UCI benchmarks and two synthetic RBF + Bernoulli controlled studies, the resulting allocator delivers $10$--$21\%$ test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation across the moderate-budget regime. The gain transfers (i) to genuine ZZ and Pauli-Z quantum kernels on quantum-natural data ($-13$--$15\%$ at low budget, $p<0.05$ paired) and (ii) to four downstream tasks (Bayesian quadrature, heteroscedastic regression, hyperparameter learning, multi-output Cokriging). On UCI features embedded into a ZZ kernel the gain disappears, consistent with the exponential-concentration regime where shot allocation has nothing to exploit.

AI.tldr: This paper extends non-uniform shot allocation (previously for classification) to Gaussian process regression, deriving three sensitivity metrics to guide shot budgets. The method achieves 10-21% RMSE improvement over uniform allocation on benchmarks, transfers to quantum kernels and multiple downstream tasks, but fails on UCI+ZZ kernel due to exponential concentration.
AI.motivation: Quantum kernel estimation on near-term hardware is shot-budgeted; existing non-uniform allocation work targets classification (sign-only outputs). GP regression has tighter coupling between kernel error and downstream quantities (variance, log-det, marginal likelihood), motivating a tailored allocation strategy.
AI.method: Derive three closed-form pair-level sensitivities: |α_iα_j|, leave-one-out residual, and marginal-likelihood gradient. Plug into Neyman-style minimum-variance allocation rule with a uniform coverage floor (Frobenius lower bound) to prevent over-concentration when warm-up estimates are noisy.
AI.result: 10-21% test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation on 4 UCI + 2 synthetic benchmarks; transfers to ZZ/Pauli-Z quantum kernels (-13-15% at low budget, p<0.05) and 4 downstream tasks; gain disappears on UCI+ZZ kernel due to exponential concentration.
AI.conclusion: Non-uniform shot allocation is effective for quantum GP regression given favorable kernel structure, but fails in the exponential concentration regime where signal is absent.

title: SatSplat: Geometrically-Accurate Gaussian Splatting for Satellite Imagery
summary: High-resolution satellite imagery demands 3D reconstruction methods that deliver both speed and geometric accuracy. Recent adaptations of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to satellite imagery demonstrate strong efficiency, but reconstruction quality often degrades under diverse illumination across multi-date, high-altitude acquisitions (with small intersection angles), limiting applicability to remote sensing and vision tasks. We present SatSplat, the first framework to adapt 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) to satellite photogrammetry, with online camera adjustment. We approximate satellite cameras with an affine model and learn a minimal delta parameterization for in-splat camera refinement from dense observations. The formulation is implemented with a 2DGS scene representation. To handle time-varying shadows and illumination changes, we integrate geometric shadow mapping and per-camera color correction during training. Across the evaluated DFC2019 and IARPA2016 benchmark sites, SatSplat achieves strong geometric accuracy while significantly outperforming prior 3DGS-based baselines. On our processed DFC2019 benchmark, SatSplat reduces mean absolute error by 11.93% and peak video memory by 31% relative to the previous state of the art. Our approach enables large-scale digital surface modeling with practical computational efficiency. The project page is available at https://gdaosu.github.io/satsplat/.

AI.tldr: SatSplat is a 2D Gaussian Splatting framework adapted for satellite photogrammetry with online camera adjustment, achieving higher geometric accuracy and 31% less memory than prior 3DGS methods on satellite benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-resolution satellite imagery requires fast and accurate 3D reconstruction, but existing 3DGS adaptations degrade under diverse illumination and small intersection angles from multi-date, high-altitude satellite acquisitions.
AI.method: SatSplat adapts 2D Gaussian Splatting to satellite imagery using an affine camera model with minimal delta parameterization for online camera refinement, plus geometric shadow mapping and per-camera color correction to handle illumination variations.
AI.result: On DFC2019 and IARPA2016 benchmarks, SatSplat achieves strong geometric accuracy, reducing mean absolute error by 11.93% and peak video memory by 31% over prior 3DGS-based methods.
AI.conclusion: SatSplat enables large-scale digital surface modeling with practical computational efficiency, outperforming prior 3DGS baselines in both accuracy and memory usage for satellite photogrammetry.

title: Direct Causation in International Humanitarian Law and the Challenge of AI-Mediated Civilian Cyber Operations
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, Workshop on Technical AI Governance Research ICML 2026
summary: International humanitarian law protects civilians from direct attack unless and for such time as they take direct part in hostilities, with the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance operationalising this rule through a three-criterion cumulative test. This paper argues that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the direct causation element of this test in a structurally specific way: when a civilian deploys an autonomous multi-agent cyber system of the kind recently demonstrated in offensive AI research, the "one causal step" standard fails because harm is produced by system-generated decisions made after human disengagement, and the integral-part requirement does not extend because it presupposes downstream human contributors whose conduct can be independently classified. The framework therefore defaults to treating such deployments as indirect participation, in tension with its purpose of capturing civilians who personally take part in hostilities. Beyond the doctrinal analysis, this paper identifies goal-specification granularity as the property on which the integral-part test's concreteness component implicitly turns, classifies AI-mediated operations along a five-level spectrum, and argues that existing technical AI governance instruments do not log or report this property.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the ICRC's "direct causation" test for direct participation in hostilities, because autonomous multi-agent systems produce harm through decisions made after human disengagement, causing the framework to default to treating such deployments as indirect participation.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that existing international humanitarian law frameworks for determining civilian direct participation in hostilities are structurally inadequate when applied to AI-mediated cyber operations.
AI.method: Doctrinal legal analysis of the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance, focusing on the direct causation element of the three-criterion cumulative test, applied to the case of autonomous multi-agent cyber systems.
AI.result: The "one causal step" standard fails because harm is produced by system-generated decisions after human disengagement; the integral-part requirement cannot extend because it presupposes downstream human contributors whose conduct can be independently classified.
AI.conclusion: The framework defaults to treating such deployments as indirect participation, which tensions with its purpose; goal-specification granularity is identified as the key property the integral-part test implicitly turns on, and existing AI governance tools fail to log or report this property.

title: Behavior Uncloning: Distilling Mode Redirection into Policy Weights without Inference-Time Steering
summary: Behavior-cloned policies often learn multiple behavior modes from demonstration datasets, including modes that are unsafe or otherwise undesired at deployment. For example, a policy trained on diverse handover demonstrations may learn to pass a knife blade-first. Standard remedies such as data curation and inference-time steering either require access to the original demonstrations for full retraining or add substantial inference-time overhead. To address this gap, we propose MoRE(Mode Redirection), which redirects policy rollouts toward desired behavior modes through a short "uncloning" step. Specifically, MoRE distills the redirection signal from a temporary mode classifier into the policy weights to steer behavior. A retain loss balances this edit by preserving desired-mode competence, allowing the standalone policy to suppress unwanted modes with zero inference-time overhead. Across eight simulated and real-world tasks, MoRE improves the average deployment success rate (SR) by 44 percentage points over the original mixed-mode policy. Among all compared adaptation and steering baselines, MoRE achieves the strongest SR and approaches the filtered-data retraining reference, while preserving task competence and inference speed. MoRE also generalizes across robot policy backbones, including Diffusion Policy and the Pi0.5 VLA, diverse task categories, and real-world deployments.

AI.tldr: MoRE (Mode Redirection) is a method that redirects behavior-cloned policies away from unsafe/unwanted behavior modes by distilling a redirection signal from a temporary mode classifier into policy weights via a short "uncloning" step, achieving 44% improvement in deployment success rate without inference-time overhead.
AI.motivation: Behavior-cloned policies often learn multiple behavior modes from demonstration datasets, including unsafe or undesired ones (e.g., a knife blade-first handover). Standard fixes like data curation or inference-time steering are costly—requiring full retraining or adding substantial overhead—motivating a more efficient solution.
AI.method: MoRE performs a short "uncloning" step that distills a redirection signal from a temporary mode classifier into the policy weights, while a retain loss preserves desired-mode competence, enabling the standalone policy to suppress unwanted modes with zero inference-time overhead.
AI.result: Across eight simulated and real-world tasks, MoRE improves average deployment success rate by 44 percentage points over the original mixed-mode policy, achieving the strongest SR among baselines and approaching filtered-data retraining performance, while preserving task competence and inference speed.
AI.conclusion: MoRE effectively redirects policies toward desired behavior modes efficiently, generalizes across robot policy backbones (Diffusion Policy, Pi0.5 VLA), diverse task categories, and real-world deployments, offering a practical solution for safe behavior cloning.

title: Fisher-Routed Mixture of Experts for Federated Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL) emerged as a promising distributed machine learning paradigm. However, extending FL to the class incremental learning scenarios introduces unique challenges: 1) Capacity conflict and catastrophic forgetting from the shared model overloading, 2) Heterogeneity from Non-Independent and Identically Distributed (Non-IID) data, and 3) Synchronized class misalignment. In this paper, we propose \textbf{F}isher-Routed \textbf{M}i\textbf{X}ture of Experts for \textbf{Fed}erated Class-Incremental Learning (\textsc{FedFMX}), a novel framework to address these challenges via adaptive expert specialization across clients. The crucial insight is to route each sample to an expert subset that jointly optimizes knowledge acquisition and retention. Specifically, we introduce a Fisher-Routed Expert Scoring (FRES) module to estimate expert importance via Fisher-based stability cost and gradient-based plasticity gain. Then, we design an Adaptive Expert Selection (AES) module by quantifying marginal contributions for adaptive expert subset determination. Finally, by the routing-aware regularization (RAR), we achieve load balance and efficient FL training. We theoretically prove the $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1})$ convergence rate. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks compared with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate the superiority of \textsc{FedFMX}.

AI.tldr: FedFMX is a federated class-incremental learning framework that uses Fisher-Routed Mixture of Experts to address capacity conflict, catastrophic forgetting, data heterogeneity, and class misalignment through adaptive expert specialization, achieving superior performance with theoretical convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: Extending FL to class incremental learning faces three key challenges: (1) capacity conflict and catastrophic forgetting due to shared model overloading, (2) Non-IID data heterogeneity, and (3) synchronized class misalignment. Existing FL methods struggle with these simultaneously.
AI.method: FedFMX introduces three core modules: (1) Fisher-Routed Expert Scoring (FRES) estimating expert importance via Fisher-based stability cost and gradient-based plasticity gain, (2) Adaptive Expert Selection (AES) quantifying marginal contributions for adaptive expert subset determination, and (3) Routing-Aware Regularization (RAR) for load balance and efficient FL training. A theoretical O(T^{-1}) convergence rate is proved.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate superiority over state-of-the-art methods, with theoretical proof of O(T^{-1}) convergence rate.
AI.conclusion: FedFMX effectively addresses the unique challenges of federated class-incremental learning through adaptive expert specialization, balancing knowledge acquisition and retention while handling non-IID data.

title: Animation2Code: Evaluating Temporal Visual Reasoning in Video-to-Code Generation
summary: While recent vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved significant improvements on static visual-to-code tasks such as generating code for webpages, charts, or SVGs, it remains unclear whether they can recover temporal dynamics when motion is present. To this end, we introduce Animation2Code, a benchmark for evaluating temporal visual reasoning via reconstructing executable web animation code from videos. Animation2Code consists of 1,069 web animation videos with diverse visual appearances and motion patterns, paired with corresponding HTML/CSS/JavaScript implementations. We propose two human-aligned metrics, appearance similarity and temporal similarity, which allow us to disentangle visual fidelity from temporal alignment when comparing rendered animations against ground-truth samples. Benchmarking state-of-the-art VLMs on this dataset shows that current VLMs struggle to maintain temporal consistency in reconstruction, even when achieving high appearance similarity, including under finetuning and iterative refinement settings. Code and data are available at https://anya-ji.github.io/animation2code-website .

AI.tldr: Animation2Code is a benchmark evaluating whether VLMs can reconstruct executable web animation code from videos, finding they struggle with temporal consistency despite decent appearance similarity.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs excel at static visual-to-code tasks (webpages, charts, SVGs), but it's unclear if they can recover temporal dynamics from motion. This work fills the gap by testing temporal visual reasoning.
AI.method: Created a dataset of 1,069 web animation videos with diverse motion patterns paired with HTML/CSS/JS code. Proposed two disentangled metrics: appearance similarity (visual fidelity) and temporal similarity (motion alignment). Evaluated state-of-the-art VLMs with finetuning and iterative refinement.
AI.result: Current VLMs achieve high appearance similarity but struggle significantly with temporal consistency. This limitation persists even with finetuning and iterative refinement.
AI.conclusion: Temporal dynamics remain a major challenge for VLMs in code generation tasks, highlighting the need for better temporal reasoning capabilities.

title: Selective Memory Retention for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: When does retention matter for memory-augmented LLM agents? We study this with TraceRetain, a lightweight framework for bounded external memory in frozen LLM agents that scores entries by interpretable features (success, age, access frequency, redundancy, specificity, similarity, downstream utility) and evicts the lowest-scoring ones at capacity. On clean ALFWorld with gpt-5-mini, external memory robustly improves over no memory across two seeds, but differences among bounded retention policies fall within Wilson 95% CIs: clean ALFWorld at T=100 to T=200 does not naturally exhibit the memory pollution retention is designed to address. Under a controlled noisy-write stress (75% synthetic distractors), unbounded memory and FIFO-K50 degrade on Precision@5 (20.2% to 12.4% and 15.8% to 3.8%) while TraceRetain-CEM is essentially unchanged (16.9% to 16.6%) and preserves 97/100 task success. The mechanism: unbounded memory has the highest mean similarity (0.87) but lowest precision, indicating failed distractors close to the query in embedding space. Held-out in-distribution evaluation shows memory-augmented policies solving 47 to 49 of 50 tasks vs. 39/50 for no memory. Bounded retention buys memory and step efficiency on saturated clean benchmarks at no task-success cost, and only differentiates from cache heuristics when streams contain noise.

AI.tldr: TraceRetain is a lightweight framework for bounded external memory in frozen LLM agents that scores entries by interpretable features and evicts low-scoring ones at capacity. It shows that external memory robustly improves over no memory on clean ALFWorld, but bounded retention policies only differentiate from cache heuristics when streams contain noise (e.g., distractors). Under noisy-write stress, TraceRetain-CEM preserves performance while unbounded memory and FIFO-K50 degrade significantly.
AI.motivation: To study when retention matters for memory-augmented LLM agents and understand whether bounded external memory policies are beneficial, especially in the presence of noise in the memory stream.
AI.method: TraceRetain is a lightweight framework for bounded external memory in frozen LLM agents that scores entries by interpretable features (success, age, access frequency, redundancy, specificity, similarity, downstream utility) and evicts the lowest-scoring ones at capacity. Experiments are conducted on clean ALFWorld with gpt-5-mini and under controlled noisy-write stress with 75% synthetic distractors.
AI.result: On clean ALFWorld, external memory robustly improves over no memory, but differences among bounded retention policies fall within Wilson 95% CIs. Under noisy-write stress, unbounded memory and FIFO-K50 degrade significantly (Precision@5 drops from 20.2% to 12.4% and 15.8% to 3.8%), while TraceRetain-CEM is essentially unchanged (16.9% to 16.6%) and preserves 97/100 task success. Held-out in-distribution evaluation shows memory-augmented policies solving 47-49/50 tasks vs. 39/50 for no memory.
AI.conclusion: Bounded retention buys memory and step efficiency on saturated clean benchmarks at no task-success cost, and only differentiates from cache heuristics when streams contain noise.

title: AnyBody: Free-Form Whole-Body Humanoid Control from Arbitrary Keypoint Guidance
summary: We present AnyBody, a unified whole-body humanoid controller driven by an arbitrary subset of body keypoints chosen at deploy time. Prior physics-based trackers either rely on expensive full-body motion capture and error-prone trajectory retargeting, which bottleneck scalable data collection and policy learning, or decompose upper- and lower-body control into separate hierarchical representations, sacrificing the coordinated whole-body motions that loco-manipulation requires. We close this gap by learning a single latent motion representation that any keypoint subset can address. To achieve this, we first train a privileged teacher tracker on a large unstructured motion corpus and distill it online into a deterministic encoder-decoder student whose latent space is a unit sphere. We then train a transformer keypoint encoder that admits any subset of body keypoints through masked self-attention, aligning it to the privileged latent. Additionally, we treat the frozen decoder as a motor prior and specialize downstream tasks with a lightweight residual corrector in the latent space. We demonstrate the effectiveness of AnyBody by tracking large-scale human motions from arbitrary keypoint subsets, free-form control, flexibly teleoperating, and learning downstream behaviors including locomotion, in-air writing, and obstacle-reach.

AI.tldr: AnyBody is a unified whole-body humanoid controller that can be driven by any subset of body keypoints at deploy time, using a privileged teacher-student distillation framework with a latent motion representation and decoder residual corrector for downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing physics-based trackers either require expensive full-body motion capture with error-prone retargeting (bottlenecking data collection), or decompose upper/lower-body control separately (sacrificing coordinated whole-body motions needed for loco-manipulation).
AI.method: Train a privileged teacher tracker on large unstructured motion data, distill it online into a deterministic encoder-decoder student with a unit sphere latent space, train a transformer keypoint encoder (with masked self-attention) to align any keypoint subset to this latent, then use frozen decoder as motor prior with lightweight residual corrector for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness on tracking large-scale motions from arbitrary keypoint subsets, free-form control, flexible teleoperation, and downstream behaviors (locomotion, in-air writing, obstacle reach).
AI.conclusion: AnyBody closes the gap between full-body mocap dependence and decomposed upper/lower control, enabling scalable whole-body control from any keypoint subset.

title: The Contagion Tensor: A Framework for Measuring Output-Distribution Coupling in Multi-Agent LLM Systems -- and Auditing the Claims It Enables
summary: We introduce the Contagion Tensor, a measurement framework for quantifying how large language model (LLM) output distributions couple across modalities, agents, and time steps. From the tensor we derive the Coupling Amplification Factor (CAF), a family of ratio-based metrics sharing the form CAF = E[T_condition] / E[T_baseline], providing unitless, baseline-referenced measurement with bootstrap confidence intervals. We instantiate CAF in four variants and evaluate the strongest in a complete 2x2x2 block-orthogonal simulation design with modality-specific ablation. The ablation reveals that an apparent image-condition super-linear effect (CAF = 1.40) collapses to sub-linear (CAF = 0.87) when the image perturbation module is disabled, a shift of -0.53 with zero effect on text conditions. We supplement with real-API experiments across two model families: DeepSeek-Chat (R=30) and GPT-4o-mini (R=15, real vision). Under uniform personas, text-only communication produces CAF approx 1.0 in both models. Diverse personas drive convergence (CAF = 0.88). A within-model comparison on GPT-4o-mini reveals: C3 (text) CAF = 1.02 vs. C5 (real vision, R=30) CAF = 1.72 [1.700, 1.733], delta = +0.70, validating the simulation's super-linear image-condition prediction. Of 11 conditions, 5 have been tested on real APIs and 6 remain unverified. Our contribution is two-layered: (1) a measurement instrument that makes output-distribution coupling quantitatively falsifiable; and (2) a transferable ablation protocol that any modular multi-agent simulator can adopt to distinguish genuine coupling from design artifacts.

AI.tldr: Introduces Contagion Tensor and Coupling Amplification Factor (CAF) to measure how LLM outputs couple across modalities, agents, and time; validates with simulations and real APIs (DeepSeek-Chat, GPT-4o-mini).
AI.motivation: Existing methods lack a falsifiable, unitless, baseline-referenced metric to quantify output distribution coupling in multi-agent, multi-modal LLM systems.
AI.method: Proposes Contagion Tensor + CAF (ratio-based metric with bootstrap CIs), instantiated in 4 variants; uses 2x2x2 block-orthogonal simulation design with modality-specific ablation and real-API experiments.
AI.result: Image-condition super-linear effect (CAF=1.40) collapses to sub-linear (CAF=0.87) with image module ablated; real-API on GPT-4o-mini confirms super-linearity (CAF=1.72 vs text CAF=1.02).
AI.conclusion: Provides a falsifiable measurement instrument and a transferable ablation protocol to distinguish genuine coupling from design artifacts in multi-agent LLM simulations.

title: IMU-HOI: A Symbiotic Framework for Coherent Human-Object Interaction and Motion Capture via Contact-Conscious Inertial Fusion
summary: Capturing full-body human motion with object interactions is crucial for AR/VR and robotics applications, yet it remains challenging for conventional vision-based methods due to occlusions and constrained capture volumes. Inertial measurement units (IMUs) offer a compelling alternative without line-of-sight requirements, but existing IMU-based motion capture assumes an isolated human and ignores object contacts and dynamics. To bridge this gap, we present IMU-HOI, a novel framework that jointly recovers full-body human pose and 6-DoF object trajectory from sparse IMUs on the body and object, explicitly modeling human-object interaction. Our approach first infers probabilistic hand-object contacts directly from IMU streams and uses them as a high-level signal to route between kinematic and inertial reasoning. These contact cues drive a three-stage fusion pipeline that refines human pose and root translation, and fuses hand-based forward kinematics with object-IMU integration for object motion, yielding coherent, drift-resilient trajectories for both human and object. Experiments on challenging human-object interaction scenarios demonstrate substantial accuracy gains over prior inertial motion capture methods. Moreover, IMU-HOI can be plugged into existing sparse-IMU mocap backbones with minimal changes, effectively extending the scope of purely inertial motion capture from isolated humans to full human-object interaction and joint motion estimation.

AI.tldr: IMU-HOI jointly recovers full-body human pose and 6-DoF object trajectory from sparse IMUs by modeling human-object interaction, using probabilistic hand-object contacts to drive a three-stage fusion pipeline that produces coherent, drift-resilient motion.
AI.motivation: Vision-based methods struggle with occlusions and limited capture volumes for human-object interaction, while existing IMU-based motion capture ignores object contacts and dynamics, assuming an isolated human.
AI.method: Infer probabilistic hand-object contacts from IMU streams, use contact cues to route between kinematic and inertial reasoning, then employ a three-stage fusion pipeline refining human pose, root translation, and fusing hand FK with object-IMU integration for object motion.
AI.result: Substantial accuracy gains over prior inertial motion capture methods on challenging human-object interaction scenarios; can be plugged into existing sparse-IMU mocap backbones.
AI.conclusion: IMU-HOI effectively extends purely inertial motion capture from isolated humans to full human-object interaction and joint motion estimation.

title: Measuring Graph-to-Graph Semantic Similarity in Knowledge Graphs: An Empirical Evaluation of Knowledge Graph Embeddings
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 6 tables. Accepted as a poster at The 2nd Frontiers in Graph Machine Learning for the Large Model Era (GMLLM'26) Workshop, co-located with KDD 2026
summary: A Knowledge Graph (KG) represents facts as structured triples and is widely used to organize relational knowledge across diverse domains. Just as textual information ranges from words and sentences to complete documents, KG information can be interpreted at multiple levels, from entities, relations, and triples to subgraphs and entire KGs. However, existing KG embedding methods mainly focus on entities, relations, and triples, leaving graph-level semantics largely unaddressed. Conventional graph-level methods, which typically compare graphs based on structural patterns, are also insufficient because structural similarity alone cannot guarantee semantic similarity between KGs. To evaluate how well different methods capture such graph-level semantic information, we study graph-to-graph semantic similarity, which determines whether a pair of KGs represents semantically corresponding underlying information. To obtain reliable ground-truth correspondences, we construct a semantic matching dataset by modifying text documents, extracting KGs from both original and modified documents, and transferring their known correspondences to KG pairs. We compare text-based, structure-based, and KG embedding-based approaches on each dataset. For the KG embedding-based approach, we introduce two scoring functions: \textit{EmbPairSim}, which uses maximal pairwise entity similarity, and \textit{AvgEmbSim}, which uses a frequency-weighted centroid. Experiments on WikiText-2 and CC-News show that \textit{EmbPairSim} achieves up to 5.3 pp higher MRR than Sentence-BERT while using substantially fewer parameters. These results suggest that KGE representations can serve as compact and effective signals for graph-to-graph semantic similarity in KGs. Our code is available at https://github.com/SeungRyeolBaek/KG-to-KG-Semantic-Similarity.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses graph-to-graph semantic similarity in Knowledge Graphs (KGs), proposing KG embedding-based scoring functions (EmbPairSim and AvgEmbSim) that outperform text-based methods like Sentence-BERT in capturing graph-level semantics, with up to 5.3 pp higher MRR on WikiText-2 and CC-News datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing KG embedding methods focus on entities, relations, and triples but neglect graph-level semantics. Structural similarity alone cannot guarantee semantic similarity between KGs, motivating the need for methods that capture graph-to-graph semantic correspondence.
AI.method: The authors construct a semantic matching dataset by modifying text documents, extracting KGs from original and modified documents, and transferring correspondences to KG pairs. They compare text-based, structure-based, and KG embedding-based approaches, introducing EmbPairSim (maximal pairwise entity similarity) and AvgEmbSim (frequency-weighted centroid) as scoring functions.
AI.result: EmbPairSim achieves up to 5.3 percentage points higher MRR than Sentence-BERT while using substantially fewer parameters. Experiments were conducted on WikiText-2 and CC-News datasets.
AI.conclusion: KGE representations can serve as compact and effective signals for graph-to-graph semantic similarity in KGs, offering a parameter-efficient alternative to text-based methods.

title: CORE Planner: Contextual-memory Oriented Reinforcement-learning in Unknown Environments for Robot Navigation
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
summary: Autonomous navigation in unknown environments requires a robot to efficiently reach a predefined goal while exploring without prior maps. Although progress has been made in this area, most existing works still rely on traditional planning methods with hand-crafted rules, while learning-based methods often suffer from limited environmental memory and challenges in simulation-to-real (sim-to-real) transfer. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Contextual-memory Oriented Reinforcement-learning (CORE) planner for robot navigation in unknown environments. The proposed CORE planner effectively combines the core advantages of traditional and learning-based methods. Specifically, our method uses a sparse visibility graph for structured environment representation, reducing the computational overhead of dense grid maps, and employs a Transformer network to achieve a holistic environmental understanding, thereby significantly improving navigation efficiency. Moreover, we introduce a visibility graph-based graph sparsification method and a contextual memory mechanism, which alleviates local optima and enhances computational performance in large-scale scenes. Finally, our approach achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer after training solely on image-based environments, requiring no fine-tuning. Experimental results show that CORE Planner consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, including the traditional FAR Planner and all learning-based baselines, across representative environments, reducing travel distance by 13\% over traditional FAR Planner and by up to 48\% relative to learning-based baselines, with larger gains observed in more complex environments. In real-world scenarios, CORE successfully navigates without human intervention, showcasing zero-shot sim-to-real transfer. Code is available at https://github.com/BBD00/core_planner.

AI.tldr: CORE Planner combines reinforcement learning with Transformer-based contextual memory and sparse visibility graphs for efficient robot navigation in unknown environments, achieving zero-shot sim-to-real transfer and outperforming baselines by 13-48% in travel distance.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely either on traditional planning with hand-crafted rules (limited adaptability) or learning-based methods (poor memory and sim-to-real transfer challenges). The authors aim to bridge these gaps.
AI.method: (1) Sparse visibility graph for structured environment representation (replacing dense grid maps). (2) Transformer network for holistic environmental understanding. (3) Graph sparsification and contextual memory mechanism to avoid local optima. (4) Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer after training on image-based environments.
AI.result: CORE Planner reduces travel distance by 13% over traditional FAR Planner and up to 48% over learning-based baselines, with larger gains in complex environments. Achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer without fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The CORE Planner effectively combines traditional and learning-based method strengths, offering superior navigation efficiency and robust real-world transferability.

title: Analysis of Adam Algorithms for Stochastic Dynamic Systems
summary: The adaptive moment estimation algorithm, known as Adam, is widely used in modern machine learning, owing to its low per-iteration complexity and strong empirical performance. Despite its prevalent use, the theoretical foundation of Adam remains largely unexplored for time-varying and nonstationary systems. In fact, the existing theoretical analyses of Adam-type algorithms are primarily concerned with time-invariant model parameters and explicitly or implicitly rely on independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data assumptions, under which the learning taskcan be formulated as minimizing a fixed expected objective with a static minimizer. However, such assumptions are often violated in time-varying and nonstationary systems, thereby calling for a theoretical investigation beyond the conventional yet idealized i.i.d. setting. The main objective of this paper is to solve this challenging problem by establishing a general theory of Adam for time-varying and nonstationary stochastic systems. We will introduce some new techniques for analyzing the products of nonstationary and dependent random matrices induced by Adam's coupled first- and second-moment recursions, and will construct a new stochastic Lyapunov function that blends these two moment dynamics. Under a stochastic excitation condition that allows nonstationary and dependent data, we will derive both parameter tracking and output prediction error bounds explicitly, quantifying the effects of stepsize, first- and second-momentum parameters, gradient noise and parameter drift. These bounds not only provide guarantees for Adam performance, but also provide guidelines for hyperparameter selection. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world data validate our theory and design guidelines.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes the first theoretical analysis of Adam for time-varying and nonstationary stochastic systems, deriving tracking and prediction error bounds under non-i.i.d. data conditions, with guidelines for hyperparameter selection validated by experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing Adam theory assumes static, i.i.d. settings, which are violated in time-varying and nonstationary systems. The paper aims to bridge this gap by providing theoretical guarantees for Adam in such realistic scenarios.
AI.method: Introduces new techniques for analyzing products of nonstationary dependent random matrices from Adam's coupled first/second-moment recursions, and constructs a stochastic Lyapunov function blending both moment dynamics. Derives bounds under a stochastic excitation condition for nonstationary/dependent data.
AI.result: Derives explicit parameter tracking and output prediction error bounds that quantify the effects of stepsize, momentum parameters, gradient noise, and parameter drift. These bounds provide performance guarantees and hyperparameter selection guidelines.
AI.conclusion: Adam is theoretically justified for time-varying and nonstationary systems beyond the i.i.d. setting; the derived bounds offer practical guidelines for hyperparameter tuning, validated on synthetic and real-world data.

title: Meshtryoshka: Differentiable Rendering of Real-World Scenes via Mesh Rasterization
comment: Daniel Xu and David Charatan contributed equally; author order decided by coin flip. Project website: https://danielxu9393.github.io/meshtryoshka-website/
summary: Differentiable rendering has emerged as a powerful approach for 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis. State-of-the-art differentiable rendering methods combine a variety of custom representations of 3D geometry and appearance with specialized renderers. However, most downstream tasks in computer graphics rely on 3D meshes. While prior work has attempted differentiable rendering with mesh representations, these approaches are limited to object-centric scenes and fail to reconstruct large-scale, unbounded scenes. In this work, we introduce Meshtryoshka, a novel mesh differentiable rendering framework that combines an off-the-shelf triangle rasterizer with a 3D representation that consists of nested mesh shells which resemble a matryoshka doll. In every forward pass, the mesh shells are extracted anew from a 3D signed distance function via iso-surface extraction, and the opacities for each vertex are computed as a function of signed distance.
