Finally, white-box probe signals are consistently stronger than black-box behavioural expression, and the relationship between the two varies by family in ways not predicted by probe AUROC alone.

AI.tldr: Language models show evaluation-awareness that moves from late to early layers as models scale, with white-box probes outperforming black-box behavioral signals.
AI.motivation: To determine if language models can recognize when they are being tested, which has implications for AI safety and the interpretability of benchmarks.
AI.method: Analyzed 11 models from Qwen 2.5, Gemma 2, and Llama 3.2 families, measuring evaluation-awareness via linear probe recovery across network layers.
AI.result: Found a systematic size-dependent shift: evaluation-awareness moves from late to early layers in larger models. Within-family scaling is non-monotonic, and white-box probes are stronger than black-box behavioral measures.
AI.conclusion: Scale changes both the strength and location of evaluation-awareness in the network; simple universal power-law scaling is not supported.

title: On Test-Time Scaling for Vision-Language Models
summary: Test-time scaling is a paradigm where large models use additional compute at inference to achieve better performance, without changing model weights. While it has been widely studied for Large Language Models (LLMs), its applicability to Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) remains less explored and analyzed, with limited analysis of whether, when, and to what extent these approaches transfer to LVLMs. In this work, we ask a simple but fundamental question: can conventional test-time scaling methods developed for LLMs be directly applied to LVLMs? We present the first comprehensive study of test-time scaling for LVLMs, spanning multiple models and model sizes, nine test-time scaling methods, and six diverse benchmarks. Our main findings is that 1) different from previous findings, small, well-performing models benefit the most from test-time scaling, enabling performance improvements of up to around 30\%, reaching large models performance, and often outperforming them, 2) LVLMs lose focus when given more compute than necessary, and 3) Visual information is encoded early in the reasoning chain, after which the chain is dominated by text-only reasoning and the contribution of image tokens drops significantly. Finally, we also provide a global and fine-grained analysis on the quality and information sufficiency of the reasoning chains produced. Overall, our findings and analysis provide practical guidance and insights into LVLMs and their deployment in research and industry.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first comprehensive study of test-time scaling for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), examining whether methods developed for LLMs transfer to LVLMs. Key findings: (1) small, well-performing models benefit most, improving up to ~30% and often outperforming larger models; (2) LVLMs lose focus with excessive compute; (3) visual information is encoded early in reasoning, after which text-only reasoning dominates and image token contribution drops significantly.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether conventional test-time scaling methods developed for LLMs can be directly applied to LVLMs, and to understand when, whether, and to what extent these approaches transfer, since test-time scaling in LVLMs remains less explored.
AI.method: A comprehensive study spanning multiple models and model sizes, nine test-time scaling methods, and six diverse benchmarks, with global and fine-grained analysis of reasoning chain quality and information sufficiency.
AI.result: (1) Small, well-performing models benefit most from test-time scaling (up to ~30% improvement), often reaching or surpassing large model performance. (2) LVLMs lose focus when given more compute than necessary. (3) Visual information is encoded early in the reasoning chain, after which text-only reasoning dominates and image token contribution drops significantly.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide practical guidance and insights into LVLMs and their deployment in research and industry, highlighting that test-time scaling can help smaller LVLMs close the gap with larger models, but with important caveats about diminishing returns and the role of visual information in reasoning.

title: Toward Secure and Reliable PDDL Formalization of Large Language Models with Planner-in-the-Loop Feedback
summary: Planning often requires symbolic specifications that are both executable and verifiable. For large language models deployed in autonomous or decision-support systems, failures in such formalization may lead to unverifiable decisions, execution failures, or unsafe downstream behavior. We present NL-PDDL-Bench, a multi-domain benchmark for natural-language-to-PDDL specification construction with planner-verified executability and controlled difficulty scaling by object count. We further propose a planner-in-the-loop framework that uses validator and planner diagnostics to revise non-executable specifications through localized edits. Building on this infrastructure, we develop a planner-grounded optimization recipe that combines parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adaptation supervised fine-tuning, offline planner-derived preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization, and inference-time planner-in-the-loop repair, without requiring online planner calls during training. We also provide a unified evaluation suite for parseability, solvability, specification similarity, and outcome-aware plan-level consistency against planner references. Experiments on representative model families show substantial gains in planner success and plan-level agreement, with improved robustness under difficulty scaling and cross-domain variation. These results highlight the value of externally verifiable formalization for reliable deployment of LLMs in safety- or security-sensitive planning systems. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/ibasicplan/NL-PDDL-Bench

AI.tldr: NL-PDDL-Bench is a benchmark and framework for converting natural language to PDDL planning specifications, using planner-in-the-loop techniques (SFT, DPO, inference-time repair) to improve executability, verifiability, and plan-level consistency across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: LLMs deployed in autonomous or decision-support systems often fail to produce formal planning specifications that are both executable and verifiable, leading to unverifiable decisions, execution failures, or unsafe behavior. There is a need for a systematic benchmark and training approach that ensures formalization quality.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) NL-PDDL-Bench, a multi-domain benchmark with difficulty scaling by object count; (2) a planner-in-the-loop framework using validator/planner diagnostics for revision; (3) a training recipe combining LoRA SFT, offline DPO from planner-derived preference pairs, and inference-time planner-in-the-loop repair without online planner calls during training; (4) a unified evaluation suite covering parseability, solvability, similarity, and plan-level consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on representative model families show substantial gains in planner success rate and plan-level agreement, with improved robustness under difficulty scaling and cross-domain variation.
AI.conclusion: Externally verifiable formalization is valuable for reliable deployment of LLMs in safety- or security-sensitive planning systems.

title: Sphere-VIO: Fast and Robust Visual-Inertial Odometry via Unified Spherical Representation for Heterogeneous Multi-Camera Systems
summary: Multi-camera visual-inertial odometry (VIO) overcomes the inherent limitations of pure visual systems by expanding the field of view. However, existing algorithms are typically tailored for fixed camera setups and lack unified compatibility with heterogeneous multi-camera systems. Meanwhile, due to the absence of a unified cross-camera representation and association mechanism, current methods struggle to achieve a balance among robust cross-camera feature tracking, stable depth estimation, and reliable real-time performance. To address these issues, we present Sphere-VIO, a lightweight filter-based VIO framework with unified spherical representation for heterogeneous multi-camera systems. Specifically, we first propose a Unified Spherical Panorama Model (USPM) that supports all standard camera models and enables bidirectional fast mapping between multi-camera images and a shared spherical space without sequential stitching, simplifying cross-camera feature management and improving triangulation efficiency. Second, we design a parallel-accelerated depth-guided semi-direct tracking pipeline, namely Hierarchical Omnidirectional Feature Alignment (HOFA), with global spherical constraints for robust cross-camera matching, and fuse multi-camera depth observations into a standard depth filter for stable initialization. Finally, we develop a multi-camera-adapted ESKF backend that employs spherical bearing residuals and Schur complement marginalization to minimize computational overhead, enabling accurate real-time state estimation on resource-constrained devices. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and a custom omnidirectional dataset show that Sphere-VIO achieves superior trade-offs between accuracy, robustness, efficiency, and cross-camera generality.

AI.tldr: Sphere-VIO is a filter-based VIO framework using unified spherical representation for heterogeneous multi-camera systems, achieving robust cross-camera tracking, stable depth estimation, and real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-camera VIO algorithms lack unified compatibility with heterogeneous camera setups and fail to balance robust cross-camera feature tracking, stable depth estimation, and real-time performance due to the absence of a unified cross-camera representation and association mechanism.
AI.method: 1) Unified Spherical Panorama Model (USPM) for bidirectional mapping between multi-camera images and shared spherical space. 2) Hierarchical Omnidirectional Feature Alignment (HOFA) for depth-guided semi-direct tracking with global spherical constraints. 3) Multi-camera-adapted ESKF backend with spherical bearing residuals and Schur complement marginalization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and a custom omnidirectional dataset show Sphere-VIO achieves superior trade-offs between accuracy, robustness, efficiency, and cross-camera generality.
AI.conclusion: Sphere-VIO effectively addresses heterogeneous multi-camera VIO challenges by providing a unified spherical representation framework that balances accuracy, robustness, efficiency, and cross-camera compatibility.

title: BrainRiem: Riemannian Prototype Learning for Source-Free Cross-Site Brain Network Diagnosis
summary: Multi-site functional MRI (fMRI) studies are essential for robust neuropsychiatric diagnosis yet suffer severe domain shifts from scanner heterogeneity, demographics, and site-specific acquisition protocols. Traditional domain adaptation requires concurrent source and target data access, violating clinical privacy regulations. Moreover, functional connectivity matrices lie on the Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) manifold, where Euclidean operations cause geometric distortions corrupting diagnostic patterns. We propose BrainRiem, a source-free domain adaptation framework learning compact Riemannian brain prototypes via manifold-aware bi-level optimization. It employs the Log-Euclidean Metric to ensure prototypes remain valid SPD matrices, while Dirichlet Energy spectral calibration aligns their frequency characteristics with real brain networks. Only anonymized prototypes are transmitted to target sites, serving as stable anchors for training local models without source data access and reducing leakage under the evaluated attacks. Comprehensive experiments on ABIDE and REST-meta-MDD show BrainRiem consistently outperforms state-of-the-art source-free, traditional, and graph domain adaptation methods across diverse scanners and demographics. Notably, learned prototypes exhibit biologically interpretable connectivity patterns aligning with established neuroscience findings, validating the necessity of Riemannian geometry for brain network analysis.

AI.tldr: BrainRiem is a source-free domain adaptation framework for multi-site fMRI studies that learns compact Riemannian brain prototypes via Log-Euclidean Metric and Dirichlet Energy calibration, enabling private, geometrically valid adaptation across clinical sites without sharing raw data.
AI.motivation: Multi-site fMRI studies suffer from domain shifts due to scanner heterogeneity and site-specific protocols, while traditional domain adaptation violates clinical privacy regulations by requiring concurrent data access. Additionally, functional connectivity matrices reside on the SPD manifold where Euclidean operations cause geometric distortions.
AI.method: BrainRiem uses manifold-aware bi-level optimization with Log-Euclidean Metric to learn compact SPD prototypes, and Dirichlet Energy spectral calibration to align frequency characteristics with real brain networks. Only anonymized prototypes are transmitted to target sites.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art source-free, traditional, and graph domain adaptation methods across diverse scanners and demographics on ABIDE and REST-meta-MDD datasets. Learned prototypes exhibit biologically interpretable connectivity patterns.
AI.conclusion: Riemannian geometry is necessary for brain network analysis, and source-free prototype-based adaptation is effective for privacy-preserving multi-site fMRI studies.

title: Projection-based coupling of infrared thermography and stereocorrelation-based digital image correlation
summary: Full-field measurement techniques such as digital image correlation and infrared thermography are prevalent in experimental solid mechanics. Digital image correlation is used to analyze surface deformation, while infrared thermography quantifies surface temperature fields. However, sophisticated procedures are necessary to express both datasets in the same Lagrangian frame, especially when analyzing non-flat surfaces. In this study, we propose an external projection-based coupling that uses the pinhole camera model to relate two-dimensional temperature data measured by infrared thermography to three-dimensional point coordinates from stereocorrelation-based digital image correlation. Unlike existing multiview approaches, we utilize two independently calibrated industrial-grade systems and augment the experimental evaluation with the pinhole camera model. The projection matrix of the camera model is calibrated using a single image of a reference object. Through this projection, temperature fields are accurately represented at material points. Our method is particularly suited for, but not restricted to, curved surfaces and straightforward to embed in existing experimental protocols, as the image registration is kept as is. Additionally, we propose using radial basis functions as a global interpolation ansatz in both space and time to compute in-plane temperature gradients and even temperature rates on curved surfaces, thereby providing an extensive and information-rich full-field dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an external projection-based coupling method using the pinhole camera model to align 2D infrared thermography temperature data with 3D digital image correlation deformation data on curved surfaces, enabling comprehensive full-field analysis.
AI.motivation: Current full-field measurement techniques (DIC and IRT) provide complementary data but require complex procedures to express both datasets in the same Lagrangian reference frame, especially for non-flat surfaces.
AI.method: Uses the pinhole camera model to project 2D IRT temperature data onto 3D DIC coordinates, calibrated via a single reference object image. Radial basis functions are employed for global spatiotemporal interpolation to compute temperature gradients and rates.
AI.result: Temperature fields are accurately mapped to material points on curved surfaces without altering existing image registration protocols, providing rich full-field datasets including in-plane temperature gradients and rates.
AI.conclusion: The method is straightforward to integrate into existing experimental workflows, suitable for curved surfaces, and enables comprehensive thermo-mechanical analysis from two independently calibrated industrial systems.

title: GUICrafter: Weakly-Supervised GUI Agent Leveraging Massive Unannotated Screenshots
summary: Data, as the fundamental substrate of modern intelligence, has greatly driven the development of current foundation models. Naturally, researchers aim to extend this paradigm to the domain of GUI agents, hoping to build strong GUI agents through a similar paradigm. However, GUI agent data cannot be directly harvested from the internet, making it costly and difficult to collect at scale. As a result, current GUI agents suffer from poor cross-device generalization and limited visual grounding ability for fine-grained GUI elements. As an attempt to address data challenge in GUI agents, we propose GUICrafter, a weakly-supervised GUI agent leveraging massive unannotated screenshots to substantially reduce the reliance on expensive human annotations. GUICrafter explores a curriculum learning framework for training GUI agents through two progressive stages. First, the model learns visual grounding from large-scale unannotated screenshots and webpages, leveraging the rich contextual signals inherent in GUI interactions without human annotations. Then, in Stage 2, we leverage a small amount of high-quality data to calibrate the model via reinforcement learning. Experiments show that GUICrafter achieves competitive, or even superior, performance to advanced systems like UI-TARS while using only 0.1% of its data. Furthermore, under the same amount of annotated data, GUICrafter surpasses all previous methods such as GUI-R1. Code, data, and models are available at https://github.com/fansunqi/GUICrafter.

AI.tldr: GUICrafter is a weakly-supervised GUI agent that leverages massive unannotated screenshots through a two-stage curriculum learning framework, achieving competitive performance to UI-TARS using only 0.1% of its data and surpassing all previous methods under the same annotated data budget.
AI.motivation: Current GUI agents suffer from poor cross-device generalization and limited visual grounding for fine-grained GUI elements because GUI agent data cannot be directly harvested from the internet, making it costly and difficult to collect at scale.
AI.method: GUICrafter proposes a two-stage curriculum learning framework: Stage 1 learns visual grounding from large-scale unannotated screenshots and webpages using rich contextual signals inherent in GUI interactions; Stage 2 uses a small amount of high-quality data to calibrate the model via reinforcement learning.
AI.result: GUICrafter achieves competitive or superior performance to UI-TARS while using only 0.1% of its data. Under the same amount of annotated data, GUICrafter surpasses all previous methods such as GUI-R1.
AI.conclusion: Weakly-supervised learning from unannotated screenshots can substantially reduce reliance on expensive human annotations for GUI agents, enabling strong performance with minimal labeled data.

title: Flying to Image-Specified Objects: 3D Quadrotor Navigation via Cross-Graph Memory and Viewpoint Planning
summary: Instance-Specific Image-Goal Navigation (InstanceImageNav) requires a robot to navigate toward the exact object instance depicted in a query image. Extending this task to quadrotors is challenging due to continuous 3D control, limited field of view (FOV), and safety constraints, which make successful navigation highly dependent on selecting informative viewpoints. We propose a hierarchical navigation framework for quadrotor InstanceImageNav that separates high-level decision making from low-level motion execution. Instead of navigating directly to spatial locations, the system generates viewpoint-aware action nodes around frontier regions and potential target objects, enabling the robot to explore while maintaining informative viewpoints for detecting the target instance. A lightweight semantic memory maintains object-level and observation-level context, allowing semantic cues to propagate to candidate action nodes for decision making. A learning-based policy selects the most promising action node, and a trajectory planner generates dynamically feasible 3D flight paths for safe execution. Experiments in simulation demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, and real-world quadrotor flights validate the practicality and robustness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical navigation framework for quadrotors to perform Instance-Specific Image-Goal Navigation, using viewpoint-aware action nodes, semantic memory, and a learning-based policy to achieve robust 3D navigation in both simulation and real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Extending InstanceImageNav to quadrotors is challenging due to continuous 3D control, limited FOV, and safety constraints, making viewpoint selection critical for success.
AI.method: A hierarchical framework with high-level decision making and low-level motion execution: generates viewpoint-aware action nodes around frontiers and target candidates, uses lightweight semantic memory for context, and a learning-based policy to select optimal action nodes, with a trajectory planner for safe 3D flight.
AI.result: Simulation experiments show consistent improvements over strong baselines; real-world quadrotor flights validate practicality and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the challenges of quadrotor InstanceImageNav by separating decision making from motion execution and leveraging viewpoint-aware exploration.

title: What Color is the Sky (for a non-human) ?
summary: The light of the daytime sky contains a mixture of many colors yet is perceived as blue by human observers. This is largely due to the particular response functions of the human cones. Under these response functions skylight and blue light are metamers: they yield the exact same excitation of the cones. In this paper we ask: is it possible to define the ``color'' of the sky for other visual systems? We present a simple computational method to determine monochromatic metamers to a given input light for arbitrary visual systems. Using published values on spectral sensitivity functions of various species, we use our method to determine the dominant wavelength of monochromatic metamers to skylight. For a wide range of species (bichromats, trichromats and tetrachormats) we find monochromatic metamers to skylight but the dominant wavelength of the metamer can vary drastically between species and be very different from the color perceived by humans.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how different species perceive sky color by finding monochromatic metamers (wavelengths that produce identical photoreceptor responses) to skylight, and shows that the perceived "color" of the sky varies drastically across visual systems.
AI.motivation: To understand whether it's possible to define the "color" of the sky for non-human visual systems, given that human perception of sky as blue is a consequence of specific cone response functions.
AI.method: A computational method to determine monochromatic metamers for arbitrary visual systems, applied to skylight using published spectral sensitivity functions of various species (bichromats, trichromats, and tetrachromats).
AI.result: Monochromatic metamers to skylight exist for all examined species, but the dominant wavelength varies drastically across species and can be very different from human-perceived blue.
AI.conclusion: The color of the sky is not a fixed property but rather depends on the observer's visual system; different species experience sky color differently based on their photoreceptor sensitivities.

title: DeepTrans Studio: Turning Expert Interventions into Shared Team Knowledge in Agentic Translation Workflows
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures. Accepted to CSCW 2026 Demo. Code and demo video: https://github.com/hint-lab/deeptrans-studio, https://youtu.be/cNpafhHAEjg
summary: Professional translation is often a team-based process: translators, reviewers, and project managers must coordinate terminology, legal force, and accountability across documents. Yet many LLM-based translation tools treat human corrections as isolated edits. Expert decisions made in one segment or by one member are rarely captured as reusable knowledge for the rest of the team. We present DeepTrans Studio, a collaborative translation workspace that lets professionals intercept selected nodes in an agentic translation workflow, review evidence, revise AI outputs, and save approved decisions to a shared team memory. During the demo, attendees will role-play translators and reviewers, resolve preset terminology and legal-modal risks, and see how their decisions are propagated to downstream segments and surfaced in a teammate's workspace as reusable precedents. The demo illustrates how human interventions in AI-mediated work can become shared, traceable knowledge rather than one-off corrections.

AI.tldr: DeepTrans Studio is a collaborative translation workspace where professional translators, reviewers, and project managers can intercept AI translation decisions, review evidence, edit outputs, and save approved decisions to a shared team memory, transforming one-off corrections into reusable knowledge across the team.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based translation tools treat human corrections as isolated edits, failing to capture expert decisions as reusable knowledge for the team, despite translation being inherently collaborative across translators, reviewers, and project managers.
AI.method: The system allows professionals to intercept nodes in an agentic translation workflow, review evidence, revise AI outputs, and save approved decisions to shared team memory. The demo involves role-playing as translators and reviewers resolving terminology and legal-modal risks.
AI.result: Decisions made by one team member are propagated to downstream segments and surfaced in teammates' workspaces as reusable precedents, enabling shared, traceable knowledge rather than one-off corrections.
AI.conclusion: Human interventions in AI-mediated translation work can become shared, traceable knowledge, improving team coordination and consistency across documents.

title: RoamFlow: Reinforcement-Aligned One-Step Action MeanFlow Policy for Image-Goal Navigation
summary: Image-goal navigation is a key challenge in embodied robotics, where an agent must reach a target specified solely by a goal image. While existing reinforcement learning approaches map perceptual observations directly to actions, they struggle to model long-horizon dependencies, often leading to suboptimal trajectories. To address this limitation, we propose RoamFlow, a generative navigation framework that leverages MeanFlow to predict the average velocity field for trajectory synthesis, enabling efficient few-step generation and reducing inference latency. We further adopt a two-stage training strategy that combines expert imitation for stable initialization with reinforcement learning for task-specific policy refinement. Extensive experiments in both Habitat simulation and real-world robotic platforms demonstrate that RoamFlow achieves efficient inference while maintaining strong navigation performance under real-time constraints.

AI.tldr: RoamFlow is a generative navigation framework using MeanFlow for trajectory prediction and two-stage training (expert imitation + RL) to achieve efficient, high-performance image-goal navigation in both simulation and real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Existing reinforcement learning approaches for image-goal navigation struggle to model long-horizon dependencies, often leading to suboptimal trajectories.
AI.method: Proposes RoamFlow, which leverages MeanFlow to predict the average velocity field for trajectory synthesis, enabling efficient few-step generation. Uses a two-stage training strategy combining expert imitation (stable initialization) with reinforcement learning (task-specific policy refinement).
AI.result: RoamFlow achieves efficient inference while maintaining strong navigation performance under real-time constraints, validated in both Habitat simulation and real-world robotic platforms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed generative framework effectively addresses long-horizon dependency issues in image-goal navigation and is practical for real-time deployment.

title: Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models
summary: Block Diffusion Language Models (BD-LMs) improve diffusion-based text generation with KV caching and flexible-length generation. A natural next step is to extend them from Single-Block Diffusion (SingleBD) to Multi-Block Diffusion (MultiBD), where a \textit{running-set} of consecutive blocks is decoded concurrently for inter-block parallelism. However, existing BD-LMs are mostly trained under teacher forcing, where the model observes only one noisy block conditioned on a clean prefix. While the recent diffusion forcing strategy introduces visibility among multiple noisy blocks, its training states still differ from MultiBD inference, where decoding operates on a bounded \textit{running-set} with heterogeneous slot-wise noise patterns. To bridge this gap, we propose \textit{Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models} (MBD-LMs), obtained by post-training BD-LMs with \textit{Multi-block Teacher Forcing} (MultiTF). MultiTF integrates teacher forcing and diffusion forcing by training on bounded \textit{noise-groups} conditioned on clean prefixes, with randomized \textit{noise-schedulers} that better match MultiBD inference states. To make MultiBD practically executable, we further introduce an optimized decoding algorithm based on the \textit{Block Buffer} mechanism that preserves prefix-cache reuse, keeps input shapes static, and translates increased decoding parallelism into wall-clock acceleration. Empirically, MBD-LLaDA2-Mini increases average Tokens Per Forward pass (TPF) from 3.47 to \textbf{6.19} and improves average accuracy from 79.95\% to \textbf{81.03\%}; when combined with DMax, MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax reaches an average TPF of \textbf{9.34} with only a 1.02\% accuracy drop on math and code benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Multi-Block Diffusion Language Models (MBD-LMs), extending from single-block to multi-block diffusion for text generation. It introduces Multi-block Teacher Forcing (MultiTF) to bridge the training-inference gap and a Block Buffer mechanism for efficient parallel decoding, achieving higher token throughput and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing Block Diffusion Language Models (BD-LMs) are trained under teacher forcing with only one noisy block conditioned on a clean prefix, which mismatches the multi-block inference setting where multiple blocks are decoded concurrently with heterogeneous noise patterns. This gap limits performance and parallelism.
AI.method: The authors propose Multi-block Teacher Forcing (MultiTF) which trains on bounded noise-groups conditioned on clean prefixes with randomized noise-schedulers. They also introduce a Block Buffer mechanism for efficient decoding that preserves prefix-cache reuse, maintains static input shapes, and enables wall-clock acceleration.
AI.result: MBD-LLaDA2-Mini increases average Tokens Per Forward pass (TPF) from 3.47 to 6.19 and improves accuracy from 79.95% to 81.03%. MBD-LLaDA2-Mini-DMax reaches an average TPF of 9.34 with only a 1.02% accuracy drop on math and code benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Multi-block diffusion with proper training alignment and an optimized decoding algorithm significantly improves both efficiency and quality in diffusion-based language generation, demonstrating the potential for scaling text diffusion models.

title: ExACT: Exemplar-Driven Calibrated Refinement for Training-Free Visual Grounding in Remote Sensing Images
summary: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to locate specific objects in high-resolution RS imagery using free-form natural language descriptions. While recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) show great potential for such open-vocabulary RSVG, their training-free adaptation is hindered by the modality gap between abstract linguistic semantics and fine-grained visual cues. In cluttered RS scenes, this gap inevitably causes severe localization drift. To bridge this gap, we propose Exemplar-driven Calibrated Refinement (ExACT), a novel training-free framework driven by a one-shot visual prompting mechanism to explicitly provide discriminative structural guidance for precise pixel-level localization. Specifically, we propose a Vision Exemplar-based Calibrator (VEC) that extracts fine-grained visual correspondences from the given exemplar to rectify the rough cross-modal priors from frozen MLLMs, effectively suppressing background artifacts and accurately outlining target boundaries. Subsequently, a Structure-Aware Refiner (SAR) employs an iterative merge-and-select clustering strategy to consolidate the calibrated priors into high-quality positive and negative geometric prompts. These prompts then guide the Segment Anything Model (SAM) to achieve precise pixel-level predictions. Extensive experiments confirm the superiority of ExACT over existing training-free and weakly-supervised methods.

AI.tldr: ExACT is a training-free framework that uses a one-shot exemplar to bridge the modality gap in RSVG, employing a Vision Exemplar-based Calibrator and Structure-Aware Refiner to improve pixel-level localization with frozen MLLMs and SAM.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs for open-vocabulary RSVG suffer from a modality gap between abstract language and fine-grained visual cues, causing severe localization drift in cluttered RS scenes.
AI.method: ExACT consists of two components: (1) Vision Exemplar-based Calibrator (VEC) extracts fine-grained visual correspondences from a given exemplar to rectify coarse cross-modal priors from frozen MLLMs; (2) Structure-Aware Refiner (SAR) uses iterative merge-and-select clustering to form positive/negative prompts for SAM to produce precise pixel-level segmentation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ExACT outperforms existing training-free and weakly-supervised methods on RSVG benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The one-shot visual prompting mechanism effectively bridges the modality gap without requiring additional training, enabling precise open-vocabulary visual grounding in remote sensing imagery.

title: DEEPMED Search: An Open-Source Agentic Platform for Medical Deep Research with Introspective Verification
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted to IJCAI-ECAI 2026 Demo Track. Project website: https://www.deepmedsearch.cloud. Demo video: https://youtu.be/4U4aok8yLpk
summary: Navigating the deluge of heterogeneous medical data, from academic literature (PubMed) to clinical guidelines (Web) and private knowledge bases, remains a critical bottleneck for evidence-based medicine. While commercial black-box tools lack transparency, standard open-source RAG implementations frequently suffer from reasoning drift when handling complex, long-tail queries. We present DEEPMED Search, a fully open-source, agentic platform designed for transparent medical deep research. Built on a high-performance Next.js architecture, DEEPMED Search features a source-adaptive router that autonomously dispatches sub-queries to PubMed, web search, or local graph-based knowledge bases based on information density. Crucially, the platform integrates an introspective verification module, powered by a causal-consistent multi-agent debate framework, to validate retrieved evidence against diagnostic logic before synthesis. To demonstrate its robustness, we showcase DEEPMED Search's ability to autonomously decompose high-difficulty rare disease queries, filter out confounding noise, and generate structured, citation-backed research reports in minutes. By open-sourcing this software, we provide the community with a robust infrastructure to democratize access to trustworthy, glass-box medical reasoning in research and prototyping settings.

AI.tldr: DEEPMED Search is an open-source, agentic platform for transparent medical deep research that uses a source-adaptive router and introspective multi-agent verification to handle complex medical queries, producing citation-backed reports.
AI.motivation: Current medical information retrieval faces a critical bottleneck: commercial tools are black-box and lack transparency, while standard open-source RAG implementations suffer from reasoning drift on complex, long-tail medical queries.
AI.method: Built on Next.js architecture with a source-adaptive router that dispatches sub-queries to PubMed, web, or local graph KBs. Features an introspective verification module using causal-consistent multi-agent debate to validate evidence against diagnostic logic before synthesis.
AI.result: Demonstrates ability to autonomously decompose rare disease queries, filter confounding noise, and generate structured, citation-backed research reports in minutes.
AI.conclusion: Open-sourcing DEEPMED Search democratizes access to transparent, trustworthy medical reasoning for research and prototyping, providing a robust glass-box alternative to commercial black-box tools.

title: OpenSPM: An Environment-Transferable Robotic Key Spatial Pose Memory and Closed-Loop High-Frequency Flow-Matching Action Generation Model
summary: Open-environment tabletop robotic manipulation requires systems to possess semantic understanding, precise geometric pose estimation, and high-frequency action generation. While end-to-end vision-language-action (VLA) models excel at semantic generalization, they often lack explicit geometric constraints for fine-grained tasks and require costly training. To bridge the gap between high-level semantics and low-level physical execution, we propose OpenSPM, an open environment spatial persistent memory framework consisting of spatial pose memory and flow-matching action generation model. OpenSPM first leverages semantically conditioned 3D perception and Kalman filtering to track continuous 6D poses. It then extracts key spatial poses from human demonstrations, keeping them as transferable, object-centric spatial persistent memory entries. During inference, OpenSPM retrieves relevant memory entries in terms of natural language instructions, transfers the spatial poses to new scenes using SE(3) transformations, and generates high-frequency action chunks via a lightweight conditional flow-matching model. Combined with real-time proprioceptive state feedback and terminal residual correction, the system effectively suppresses trajectory error accumulation. Evaluated on ten LIBERO-GOAL tasks, OpenSPM achieves an 85.6% success rate and an equivalent control frequency of 1033.3 Hz, while requiring minimal inference AI computing power. Extensive ablations illustrate that structured spatial persistent memory and closed-loop residual correction play a crucial role in reliable, high-frequency robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: OpenSPM is a spatial persistent memory framework combining semantic 3D perception, Kalman filtering, and flow-matching action generation to achieve high-frequency (1033.3 Hz) robotic manipulation with 85.6% success on LIBERO-GOAL tasks, bridging the gap between high-level semantics and low-level control without costly VLA training.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models offer semantic understanding but lack explicit geometric constraints for fine-grained manipulation and require expensive training, creating a gap between high-level semantics and low-level physical execution.
AI.method: OpenSPM uses semantically conditioned 3D perception with Kalman filtering for continuous 6D pose tracking, extracts key spatial poses from demonstrations as transferable memory entries, retrieves relevant memories via natural language, applies SE(3) transformations to new scenes, and generates high-frequency action chunks using a lightweight conditional flow-matching model with real-time proprioceptive feedback and terminal residual correction.
AI.result: 85.6% success rate on ten LIBERO-GOAL tasks, 1033.3 Hz equivalent control frequency, minimal AI inference compute power.
AI.conclusion: Structured spatial persistent memory and closed-loop residual correction are crucial for reliable, high-frequency robotic manipulation in open environments.

title: A Linear Matching Bandit Approach to Online Multi-Human Multi-Robot Teaming
summary: We address the problem of online multi-human multi-robot teaming through the lens of a linear matching bandit framework, where a learner assigns robots with unknown features from a fixed pool to distinct sets of human agents over multiple rounds. To solve this problem, we propose LinMatch, an online learning algorithm that updates the confidence intervals of the unknown features and makes the optimistic matching under uncertainty. The contributions and novelty of this work are twofold. First, we recast the optimistic matching problem in each round as a linear program of maximum weighted matching, efficiently solvable by the celebrated Hungarian algorithm. Second, we provide novel bounds for matching with linear feature problems, showing an upper bound of $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{MKT})$ and a minimax lower bound of $Ω(d\sqrt{MKT})$, establishing a tight optimal regret rate of $\tildeΘ(d\sqrt{MKT})$. This demonstrates that LinMatch achieves strictly optimal achievable regret with respect to the total number of rounds $T$, the feature dimension $d$, and the matching parameters $M$ and $K$. The proposed algorithm and bounds apply to a wide range of matching problems with applications beyond human-robot matching, such as housing allocation, recommendation systems, and more.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LinMatch, a linear matching bandit algorithm for online multi-human multi-robot teaming that achieves tight optimal regret bounds of $\tilde{\Theta}(d\sqrt{MKT})$ by reformulating optimistic matching as a linear program solvable via the Hungarian algorithm.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of online multi-human multi-robot teaming, where a learner must repeatedly assign robots with unknown features to human agents over multiple rounds while minimizing regret.
AI.method: The authors propose LinMatch, which uses confidence intervals to handle unknown robot features and performs optimistic matching by recasting each round's matching problem as a linear program of maximum weighted matching, solved efficiently via the Hungarian algorithm.
AI.result: Provides an upper bound of $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{MKT})$ and a minimax lower bound of $\Omega(d\sqrt{MKT})$, establishing a tight optimal regret rate $\tilde{\Theta}(d\sqrt{MKT})$ that is strictly optimal in $T$, $d$, $M$, and $K$.
AI.conclusion: LinMatch achieves provably optimal regret for linear matching bandits, with applicability extending beyond human-robot teaming to domains like housing allocation and recommendation systems.

title: Character Recognition of Nepali Number Plate
comment: Accepted at World Conference on Information Systems for Business Management (ISBM) 2025
summary: This paper presents a robust Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) system tailored for Nepali license plates written in Devanagari script. In this paper, a pipelined model was used that integrates YOLO-based models for license plate and character detection, followed by a CNN classifier trained on 34 Devanagari characters. Two publicly available data sets were used that incorporate diverse lighting, fonts, and structural variations. Data augmentation and additional training on embossed plates enhanced the generalizability of the model. The system achieved a recognition accuracy of up to 93\%, demonstrating strong performance under real-world conditions and providing a scalable solution for traffic management in Nepal. Code: https://github.com/Satyasakhadka/Nepali-NumberPlate-Character-Recognition

AI.tldr: This paper develops a robust ANPR system for Nepali license plates using a pipelined approach with YOLO for detection and CNN for character recognition, achieving 93% accuracy on Devanagari script plates.
AI.motivation: To address the need for an automated traffic management system tailored specifically for Nepali license plates written in Devanagari script, which have unique structural and font characteristics not covered by existing ANPR systems.
AI.method: A pipelined model integrating YOLO-based models for license plate and character detection, followed by a CNN classifier trained on 34 Devanagari characters. Data augmentation and additional training on embossed plates were used to improve generalizability.
AI.result: The system achieved up to 93% recognition accuracy under real-world conditions with diverse lighting, fonts, and structural variations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ANPR system demonstrates strong real-world performance and provides a scalable solution for traffic management in Nepal, with code publicly available.

title: Rethinking Generative Reconstruction Attacks against Graph Neural Network Models
summary: The application of graph data in numerous disciplines raises the need for gathering and analyzing huge volumes of data, some of which is private and sensitive. The non-Euclidean nature of the graph data makes the analysis computationally challenging, leading to the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in the age of AI. GNNs may inadvertently leak sensitive data they are trained on, which raises serious data security issues, including the model inversion attack. In this study, we analyze GNNs' vulnerabilities by introducing two novel graph inversion (i.e., reconstruction) attacks: graph-label conditioned (GLC) attack and embedding-label conditioned (ELC) attack, utilizing targetmodel predictions and their intermediate representations, respectively. We perform a comprehensive analysis of our introduced privacy attacks and compare them with existing baselines across three benchmark graph datasets (i.e., NCI1, PROTEINS, and AIDS) and four graph distributional/structural metrics (i.e., FGD, EGD, MMD, and GKS). Our work demonstrates that an adversary can use the generator-discriminator technique to reconstruct high-quality graphs in real-world black-box attack scenarios against GNNs. Additionally, we present a variant of our attacks (Ours--) with 50% reduced queries, achieving good or comparable reconstruction attack performance. In addition, we show that GNNs are highly vulnerable to privacy attacks, varying Laplacian noise-scales.

AI.tldr: This paper studies privacy vulnerabilities in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) by introducing two novel graph reconstruction attacks (GLC and ELC) that can reconstruct high-quality graphs using target model predictions and intermediate representations, demonstrating GNNs' susceptibility to model inversion attacks even with reduced queries and varying noise scales.
AI.motivation: Graph data from various disciplines contains private and sensitive information, and GNNs may leak this training data through privacy attacks like model inversion, motivating the need to analyze these vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Proposes two novel graph inversion attacks: Graph-Label Conditioned (GLC) attack using target model predictions, and Embedding-Label Conditioned (ELC) attack using intermediate representations, both employing a generator-discriminator technique for black-box attack scenarios.
AI.result: The attacks successfully reconstruct high-quality graphs across three benchmark datasets (NCI1, PROTEINS, AIDS) measured by four metrics (FGD, EGD, MMD, GKS). A variant with 50% reduced queries achieves comparable performance. GNNs show high vulnerability to privacy attacks under varying Laplacian noise-scales.
AI.conclusion: GNNs are highly vulnerable to graph reconstruction privacy attacks, and adversaries can effectively reconstruct sensitive graph data even with limited query budgets and noise perturbations.

title: REPAIR-Bench: A Benchmark for Robot Error Perception And Interaction Recovery
summary: Understanding how users perceive and respond to robot failures is essential for building robust and trustworthy robot systems. Prior work, however, (i) often treats failures as independent events, (ii) emphasizes binary failure detection, (iii) with rule-based recovery modeling. We present REPAIR-Bench, built on 214 interaction trials from 41 participants, the benchmark spans four induced failure types and provides synchronized facial action units, head pose, speech transcripts, and post-interaction affect and recovery reports. The benchmark spans three novel evaluation tasks that jointly capture the lifecycle of failure in human-robot interaction (HRI): (i) failure detection over inter-dependent interaction sessions, modeling longitudinal user adaptation across repeated failures; (ii) visual failure-type classification beyond binary success/failure formulations; and (iii) user-centered recovery prediction, inferring users' preferred recovery strategies from interaction context rather than relying on manually designed or rule-based strategies. In baseline experiments, hierarchical recurrent modeling improved failure detection over a single-session model (strict F1: 0.80 vs. 0.68), achieved a failure localization mean signed error of -0.51 s, median absolute error of 2.97 s and, for recovery prediction, a QLoRA-tuned Mistral-7B reached Hit@5=0.76 and F1@5=0.32. REPAIR-Bench provides both the HRI and Medical HRI communities with a standardized framework for (1) evaluating robot failures and (2) building transparent, adaptive, and trustworthy recovery systems.

AI.tldr: REPAIR-Bench is a benchmark built from 214 interaction trials (41 participants) with four induced failure types, featuring synchronized multimodal data. It introduces three evaluation tasks—longitudinal failure detection, visual failure-type classification, and user-centered recovery prediction—moving beyond independent failure events, binary detection, and rule-based recovery. Baseline results show hierarchical models improve detection (F1: 0.80), and QLoRA-tuned Mistral-7B achieves Hit@5=0.76 for recovery prediction.
AI.motivation: Prior work treats failures as independent events, uses binary success/failure detection, and relies on rule-based recovery modeling—none of which capture the real-world lifecycle of failures in HRI where users adapt and have varied preferences.
AI.method: Built from 214 interaction trials with 41 participants, the benchmark induces four failure types, collects synchronized facial action units, head pose, speech transcripts, and affect/recovery reports. It defines three tasks: (1) hierarchical recurrent modeling for longitudinal failure detection, (2) visual failure-type classification beyond binary, and (3) QLoRA-tuned Mistral-7B for user-centered recovery strategy prediction.
AI.result: Hierarchical recurrent modeling improved strict F1 from 0.68 to 0.80 vs. single-session model; failure localization mean signed error of -0.51 s, median absolute error 2.97 s; QLoRA-tuned Mistral-7B achieved Hit@5=0.76 and F1@5=0.32 for recovery prediction.
AI.conclusion: REPAIR-Bench provides a standardized, multimodal benchmark for evaluating robot failures across their lifecycle, enabling transparent, adaptive, and trustworthy recovery systems for both HRI and Medical HRI communities.

title: Depth Exploration for LLM Decoding
summary: Autoregressive LLM decoding evaluates every generated token through the full layer stack, even though many tokens become predictable at intermediate depths. Existing lossless depth-adaptive methods exploit this redundancy by choosing a single non-final exit depth and verifying its prediction with the final-depth model. However, our measurements show that this selection-based strategy leaves substantial headroom: choosing an exit too late wastes computation, while choosing one too early triggers fallback and discards dependent drafts. We propose Depth Exploration Decoding (DEX), a lossless decoding algorithm that replaces single-depth selection with parallel exploration over multiple candidate depths. At each commit position, DEX validates candidates against the final-depth reference, commits exactly the final-depth token, and collapses the exploration lattice to retain only reusable branch states. This expand--commit--collapse procedure preserves equivalence to standard autoregressive decoding while reducing the cost of committing each token. Across early-exit-trained and standard LLMs, DEX outperforms representative depth-selection baselines and achieves competitive end-to-end throughput against speculative and distributed decoding methods. Moreover, DEX improves as the explored depths become finer, showing that parallel depth exploration provides a scalable way to exploit the underused depth axis of LLM decoding.

AI.tldr: DEX replaces single-depth selection with parallel exploration over multiple candidate depths during LLM decoding, using an expand-commit-collapse procedure that preserves lossless equivalence to standard decoding while reducing computational cost.
AI.motivation: Existing depth-adaptive LLM decoding methods use single-depth selection (choose one exit), which leaves headroom: exiting too late wastes computation, exiting too early triggers fallback and discards dependent drafts.
AI.method: DEX performs parallel exploration over multiple candidate depths at each commit position, validates candidates against the final-depth reference, commits the final-depth token, and collapses the exploration lattice to retain only reusable branch states.
AI.result: DEX outperforms representative depth-selection baselines across early-exit-trained and standard LLMs, achieves competitive end-to-end throughput against speculative and distributed decoding methods, and improves as explored depths become finer.
AI.conclusion: Parallel depth exploration provides a scalable way to exploit the underused depth axis of LLM decoding, and the expand-commit-collapse procedure is a lossless improvement over single-depth selection strategies.

title: Self-Evolving Agentic Image Restoration via Deliberate Planning and Intuitive Execution
summary: Real-world image restoration (IR) remains challenging due to complex and coupled degradations. While recent agentic IR frameworks leverage Large Language Models for flexible tool planning, they face two critical limitations. First, from a search scheme perspective, excessive reliance on greedy strategies fails to balance exploration and exploitation. Second, existing agentic systems underutilize information, exhibiting episodic amnesia. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{Self-Evolving Agentic Image Restoration (SEAR)}, which formulates restoration as a sequential decision-making problem. Inspired by the dual-process theory, SEAR comprises an Intuitive Executor and a Deliberate Planner, respectively following the fast-thinking \textit{System 1} and slow-thinking \textit{System 2} principles. The Deliberate Planner employs Pruning-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search for long-horizon reasoning, utilizing a hybrid no-reference reward and a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based tournament to prevent metric exploitation. Complementarily, the Intuitive Executor leverages a self-evolving episodic memory indexed by degradation-aware state fingerprints. This mechanism distills expensive search trajectories into adaptive expertise, overcoming episodic amnesia while progressively amortizing cold-start exploration costs through memory reuse. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate its strong perceptual and quantitative performance.

AI.tldr: SEAR is a self-evolving agentic image restoration framework that uses Monte Carlo Tree Search with pruning and dual-process theory (System 1/System 2) to overcome greedy search limitations and episodic amnesia in real-world image restoration.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic IR frameworks rely too heavily on greedy search strategies (poor exploration-exploitation balance) and suffer from episodic amnesia (underutilizing past information), limiting their effectiveness on complex, coupled degradations in real-world images.
AI.method: SEAR formulates IR as sequential decision-making using dual-process theory: (1) Deliberate Planner (System 2) employs Pruning-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search with hybrid no-reference reward and MLLM-based tournament for long-horizon reasoning; (2) Intuitive Executor (System 1) uses self-evolving episodic memory indexed by degradation-aware state fingerprints to distill search trajectories into reusable expertise.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate strong perceptual and quantitative performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-process framework with memory reuse effectively overcomes greedy search limitations and episodic amnesia, progressively amortizing cold-start exploration costs while achieving superior image restoration quality.

title: CLQT: A Closed-Loop, Cost-Aware, Strategy-Consistent Benchmark for Diagnostic Evaluation of LLM Portfolio-Management Agents
summary: LLM agents are increasingly cast as autonomous portfolio managers, and benchmarks have moved from financial question-answering to sequential trading. Yet most still rank agents by returns over a fixed window -- a weak proxy, since a period's return is dominated by the market path and apparent alpha can dissolve once look-ahead leakage is controlled. Such a ranking certifies neither sound reasoning, nor a consistent strategy, nor a durable edge. We introduce CLQT, which reframes closed-loop trading evaluation as diagnosis rather than ranking: an instrument that localizes where and why an agent's process succeeds or fails. CLQT is a fully closed-loop, cost-aware, strategy-consistent, temporally-gated environment whose agents run a five-stage cycle: gather, synthesize, allocate, execute, reflect. Each round emits a complete DecisionRound sealed into a recompute-verifiable hash chain, so every metric is reconstructable from the trail. Six pillars form the substrate: a hard TimeGate, institutional transaction- and financing-cost modeling, strategy-consistency scoring, three-tier memory, a Model-Context-Protocol tool layer, and mandate-aware synthesis. The same agent runs as a constrained committee of specialized roles or a single full-autonomy orchestrator, making process scaffolding an experimental variable. From the audit trail we compute a five-axis capability scorecard (APM-CS: Coherence, Acuity, Composure, Discipline, Reliability), with Coherence judged partly by a held-out, out-of-cohort LLM to curb self-preference bias. We validate it on a contamination-controlled multi-model backtest with an ablation grid and a live broker track on unseen, post-cutoff data, against a repeated-run noise floor. CLQT separates outcome from capability, yielding not a model ranking but a durable, extensible map of agent competencies and limitations.

AI.tldr: CLQT reframes LLM agent trading evaluation from simplistic return-based ranking to a diagnostic framework that uses a closed-loop, auditable environment with five-axis capability scoring to separate outcome from true agent competence.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks rank agents by returns over fixed windows, which is a weak proxy: returns are dominated by market paths, look-ahead leakage inflates apparent alpha, and rankings don't certify sound reasoning, consistent strategy, or durable edge.
AI.method: CLQT implements a closed-loop, cost-aware, strategy-consistent, temporally-gated environment with a five-stage agent cycle (gather, synthesize, allocate, execute, reflect), a verifiable hash chain audit trail, six pillars (TimeGate, cost modeling, strategy-consistency scoring, three-tier memory, MCP tool layer, mandate-aware synthesis), and a five-axis capability scorecard (APM-CS: Coherence, Acuity, Composure, Discipline, Reliability) with cross-model coherence evaluation to avoid self-preference bias.
AI.result: Validated on contamination-controlled multi-model backtest with ablation grid and live broker track on unseen post-cutoff data against repeated-run noise floor; produces a diagnostic map of agent competencies and limitations rather than a simple ranking.
AI.conclusion: CLQT separates outcome from capability, yielding a durable, extensible map of agent strengths and weaknesses instead of a fragile model ranking.

title: WARP: Whole-Body Retargeting for Learning from Offline Human Demonstrations
summary: Direct transfer from human demonstration to learnable robot action is a crucial step towards scalable whole-body mobile manipulation. While human data scales better than mobile teleoperation, it requires overcoming significant embodiment gaps. Existing retargeting methods yield imprecise or inconsistent solutions, causing action multi-modality that prevents supervised policies from reliably converging. We present Whole-body-Aware Retargeting from human Pose (WARP), an offline pipeline that explicitly models embodiment differences to extract precise, unique whole-body actions. WARP leverages a closed-form Shoulder-Elbow-Wrist (SEW) geometric solver for exact end-effector tracking while preserving whole-body structural intent. Paired with lazy mobile-base control, it extracts accurate, consistent robot trajectories. Evaluations show WARP provides highly reliable data for open-loop real-world replay. To our knowledge, WARP is the first framework to achieve zero-shot whole-body mobile manipulation directly from offline human demonstrations, eliminating the need for human-in-the-loop teleoperation action data. More details on https://warp-retarget.github.io/

AI.tldr: WARP is an offline pipeline that retargets human poses to whole-body robot actions via a closed-form SEW geometric solver and lazy mobile-base control, enabling zero-shot whole-body mobile manipulation directly from human demonstration without teleoperation.
AI.motivation: Human demonstration data scales better than teleoperation but suffers from embodiment gaps that cause action multi-modality and unreliable policy convergence. Existing retargeting methods are imprecise or inconsistent.
AI.method: WARP uses a closed-form Shoulder-Elbow-Wrist (SEW) geometric solver for precise end-effector tracking while preserving whole-body structural intent, paired with lazy mobile-base control to extract consistent robot trajectories.
AI.result: WARP provides highly reliable data for open-loop real-world replay and achieves zero-shot whole-body mobile manipulation directly from offline human demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: WARP eliminates the need for human-in-the-loop teleoperation action data, marking the first framework to achieve zero-shot whole-body mobile manipulation from offline human demos.

title: On the Policy Gradient Foundations of Group Relative Policy Optimization: Credit Assignment, Gradient Sparsity, and Rank Collapse
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) eliminates the learned critic in PPO by using the mean reward of grouped rollouts as a baseline. We provide a rigorous derivation of GRPO from first principles of the policy gradient theorem, revealing a fundamental credit assignment failure: under output-only reward, every token in a rollout receives identical advantage, collapsing token-level credit to a single scalar. We prove this induces gradient sparsity that intensifies over training, and demonstrate empirically via SVD analysis of GRPO gradients on Nemotron-4B/GSM8K that the gradient matrix has effective rank $\approx$ 2 regardless of group size $R \in \{2, 4, 8\}$. We formalize this as an intrinsic rank-2 structure arising from the zero-sum constraint on advantages and derive conditions under which GRPO's baseline is optimal. Our results characterize when GRPO's simplicity is theoretically justified and identify the credit assignment bottleneck as the key limitation for multi-step reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously analyzes Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), proving it suffers from a fundamental credit assignment failure where every token in a rollout receives identical advantage. This creates a rank-2 gradient structure regardless of group size, limiting its effectiveness for multi-step reasoning.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous theoretical derivation of GRPO from policy gradient first principles and identify its fundamental limitations in credit assignment, particularly for multi-step reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors derive GRPO from the policy gradient theorem, prove gradient sparsity theoretically, and validate empirically using SVD analysis of GRPO gradients on Nemotron-4B/GSM8K with varying group sizes (R ∈ {2,4,8}).
AI.result: The gradient matrix has effective rank ≈ 2 regardless of group size, revealing an intrinsic rank-2 structure resulting from zero-sum constraints on advantages. Under output-only reward, every token receives identical advantage, collapsing token-level credit to a single scalar.
AI.conclusion: GRPO's simplicity is theoretically justified only under certain conditions, and the identified credit assignment bottleneck is the key limitation for multi-step reasoning tasks.

title: Evidence-Based Text-Conditioned 3D CT Synthesis for Ovarian Cancer
summary: Ovarian cancer is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage, making preoperative contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) central to staging and surgical planning; yet the scarcity of annotated imaging data, compounded by privacy regulations, limits the development of generalizable computational models in this domain. Text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis has shown promise, but existing pipelines depend on paired radiology reports and have been evaluated only on chest CT. We propose OvESyn (Ovarian Evidence-based Synthesis), a framework that constructs standardized Findings and Impression sections directly from CT-derived imaging descriptors and routine clinical metadata, without any original radiology report, and uses them to condition a latent diffusion model adapted to 493 high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma patients. This is the first text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis framework adapted to an abdomino-pelvic oncologic setting. A systematic ablation over two adaptation axes, vision-language encoder alignment and generator fine-tuning, identifies generator domain adaptation as the operative mechanism for crossing the domain gap and establishing the target anatomy: without it, synthesis remains anchored to the thoracic pretraining domain, with Precision and Recall collapsing to zero and FID2.5D exceeding 140, regardless of encoder alignment. Encoder alignment instead refines intensity and fine detail. The full OvESyn attains the best distributional and intensity fidelity (FID2.5D 29.35, Precision 0.671, Wasserstein-1 0.044), while the generator-only variant maximizes coverage (Recall 0.645), reflecting a fidelity/coverage trade-off governed by encoder adaptation. Requiring only automatic segmentations and routine preoperative metadata, OvESyn supports transferability to report-scarce settings and provides a foundation for synthetic cohort generation in abdomino-pelvic oncologic imaging.

AI.tldr: OvESyn is the first text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis framework for abdomino-pelvic oncology, generating synthetic ovarian cancer CT scans using only automated segmentations and routine clinical metadata — without needing original radiology reports.
AI.motivation: Ovarian cancer is often diagnosed late; annotated CT data is scarce due to privacy constraints, limiting model generalization. Existing text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis relies on paired radiology reports and is only validated on chest CT, not abdomino-pelvic domains.
AI.method: OvESyn constructs standardized Findings and Impression sections from CT-derived imaging descriptors and routine clinical metadata (no radiology reports), then conditions a latent diffusion model adapted from a thoracic pretraining setting to 493 high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma patients. Systematic ablation studies over vision-language encoder alignment and generator fine-tuning were conducted.
AI.result: Generator domain adaptation is the critical mechanism — without it, synthesis remains in the thoracic domain (Precision/Recall collapse to zero, FID2.5D>140). Encoder alignment refines intensity and fine details. Full OvESyn achieves best fidelity (FID2.5D 29.35, Precision 0.671, Wasserstein-1 0.044); generator-only variant maximizes coverage (Recall 0.645), revealing a fidelity/coverage trade-off.
AI.conclusion: OvESyn enables transferable synthetic CT generation in report-scarce, abdomino-pelvic oncologic settings, providing a foundation for synthetic cohort generation without requiring paired radiology reports.

title: The CRISTAL Method: Neurosymbolic analysis from AI-synthesized world models
summary: This project introduces the CRISTAL Method (Coherent Reliable Intentional Synthesis of Truthful Analysis Logic), a neurosymbolic framework for automating complex analysis workflows, with fundamental investment analysis as a primary use case. This domain poses major challenges: high structural uncertainty, noisy and subjective data, tight attention budgets, and the need for justified, reproducible decisions. Human analysts often struggle in this domain due to cognitive biases and limitations, suggesting significant value in automation. But while LLM-based agents have been proposed as analytical aids, their limitations -- poor numerical reasoning, unawareness of uncertainty, and lack of reproducibility -- hinder their effectiveness in this context. CRISTAL addresses these gaps through a principled blend of statistical model synthesis, continuous learning, and active learning. Starting from a natural-language prior knowledge curriculum, CRISTAL builds a dynamic, interpretable probabilistic program that enables full Bayesian inference, including uncertainty quantification and budget-aware data acquisition. CRISTAL continually refines its world model during analysis, leveraging LLMs for code synthesis and learning. We validate CRISTAL on a novel benchmark of synthetic equities with rich financial and textual data. On a company classification task, CRISTAL achieves Bayes-optimal accuracy with just 5 examples and a 5-second budget, outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs that plateau around 40\% accuracy even with order-of-magnitude more input data and compute.

AI.tldr: CRISTAL is a neurosymbolic framework that blends statistical modeling, continuous learning, and active learning to automate complex analysis. It achieves Bayes-optimal accuracy with minimal data and compute, vastly outperforming LLMs on an investment analysis task.
AI.motivation: Human analysts in investment analysis suffer from cognitive biases; LLM-based agents struggle with numerical reasoning, uncertainty, and reproducibility. There is a clear need for principled automation that is accurate, interpretable, and data-efficient.
AI.method: CRISTAL uses a neurosymbolic approach: starting from natural-language prior knowledge, it builds a dynamic, interpretable probabilistic program enabling Bayesian inference with uncertainty quantification and budget-aware active learning. It continuously refines its world model using LLMs for code synthesis.
AI.result: On a synthetic equities classification benchmark, CRISTAL achieves Bayes-optimal accuracy with only 5 examples and a 5-second budget, while state-of-the-art LLMs plateau at ~40% accuracy even with far more data and compute.
AI.conclusion: CRISTAL demonstrates that principled neurosymbolic methods can vastly outperform pure LLM approaches in domains requiring numerical reasoning, uncertainty handling, and reproducibility, offering a promising direction for automating complex analytical workflows.

title: Seeing Touch from Motion: A Unified Modality-Aware Visuo-Tactile Policy with Tactile Motion Correlation
summary: Visuo-Tactile policies leveraging optical tactile sensors have shown great promise in contact-rich manipulation. These sensors achieve high spatial resolution and multi-dimensional force sensing by utilizing an internal camera to monitor the deformation of their elastic gel surface, thereby indirectly inferring tactile cues. Despite their advantages, extracting fine-grained contact states necessary for contact-rich manipulation remains an open challenge. Existing methods typically use either raw images or cumulative motion fields to represent tactile cues. However, both are prone to perception ambiguity. Raw tactile images mainly capture appearance changes, while cumulative motion fields only reflect the aggregate gel deformation. Consequently, distinct fine-grained contact states can exhibit highly similar patterns, making it difficult to explicitly distinguish subtle contact variations. To address this issue, we explore the dynamic priors of tactile motion and discover that the correlation between transient and cumulative motion can explicitly distinguish fine-grained contact states. Based on this insight, we propose a motion-aware tactile representation to facilitate contact-rich manipulation. Beyond tactile representation, effective fusion of tactile and visual modalities is also critical. Most existing fusion methods either directly concatenate features from each modality or train modality-specific networks separately and fuse their outputs. However, these strategies struggle to simultaneously model cross-modal interactions and preserve modality-specific characteristics. In this work, we take advantage of the Mixture-of-Transformers architecture and propose a unified modality-aware visuo-tactile policy that captures cross-modal complementarity while maintaining modality-specific properties.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a motion-aware tactile representation based on the correlation between transient and cumulative motion to resolve perception ambiguity in fine-grained contact states, and introduces a Mixture-of-Transformers-based visuo-tactile policy that captures cross-modal interactions while preserving modality-specific properties for contact-rich manipulation.
AI.motivation: Existing tactile representations (raw images or cumulative motion fields) suffer from perception ambiguity, where distinct fine-grained contact states exhibit similar patterns, making it difficult to distinguish subtle contact variations in contact-rich manipulation. Additionally, current multimodal fusion methods struggle to simultaneously model cross-modal interactions and preserve modality-specific characteristics.
AI.method: 1) A motion-aware tactile representation that leverages the correlation between transient and cumulative motion to explicitly distinguish fine-grained contact states. 2) A Mixture-of-Transformers architecture for unified modality-aware visuo-tactile policy that captures cross-modal complementarity while maintaining modality-specific properties.
AI.result: The proposed motion-aware tactile representation effectively resolves perception ambiguity by exploiting dynamic priors of tactile motion, distinguishing contact states that appear similar under conventional representations. The Mixture-of-Transformers fusion approach successfully models cross-modal interactions without losing modality-specific characteristics.
AI.conclusion: The dynamic correlation between transient and cumulative motion provides explicit cues for fine-grained contact state discrimination. The Mixture-of-Transformers architecture offers an effective paradigm for visuo-tactile policy learning that balances cross-modal fusion and modality preservation.

title: Blackknife: Hard-Label Query-Limited Black-Box Attacks on Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks
summary: Heterogeneous graph neural networks (HGNNs) have achieved strong performance in modeling complex graph-structured data with multiple node and relation types. However, their robustness under realistic black-box adversarial settings remains insufficiently explored. Existing attacks on HGNNs usually assume access to model gradients, soft prediction scores, or the complete graph structure, which is often unavailable when HGNN-based services are deployed as closed systems. In this paper, we propose Blackknife, a hard-label, query-limited, and structure-limited black-box evasion attack framework for heterogeneous graph neural networks. Blackknife assumes no access to the victim model architecture, parameters, gradients, logits, confidence scores, or the full graph structure. Instead, it only relies on locally observable one-hop heterogeneous structures and a small number of hard-label queries. To generate effective perturbations under these strict constraints, Blackknife first constructs a local relation-aware surrogate model from observable heterogeneous neighborhoods. It then relaxes discrete edge addition and deletion operations into continuous soft weights and optimizes them through projected gradient descent. Finally, the optimized perturbations are discretized into relation-preserving structural rewiring operations and verified using limited hard-label feedback from the victim model. Extensive experiments on three benchmark heterogeneous graph datasets, including ACM, DBLP, and IMDB, demonstrate that Blackknife consistently achieves strong attack success rates against representative HGNN models. The results further show that Blackknife remains effective under topology-based defense strategies, revealing the vulnerability of HGNNs to local structure-limited black-box attacks.

AI.tldr: Blackknife is a hard-label, query-limited, and structure-limited black-box evasion attack framework for heterogeneous graph neural networks that only relies on locally observable one-hop structures and limited hard-label queries to generate effective perturbations via relation-aware surrogate modeling and projected gradient descent.
AI.motivation: Existing attacks on HGNNs assume access to model gradients, soft prediction scores, or complete graph structure, which is unrealistic when HGNN services are deployed as closed systems. The paper aims to explore HGNN robustness under more restrictive and realistic black-box settings.
AI.method: Blackknife constructs a local relation-aware surrogate model from observable heterogeneous neighborhoods, relaxes discrete edge operations into continuous soft weights optimized via projected gradient descent, then discretizes perturbations into relation-preserving structural rewiring operations verified with limited hard-label feedback.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ACM, DBLP, and IMDB datasets show Blackknife achieves strong attack success rates against representative HGNN models and remains effective under topology-based defense strategies.
AI.conclusion: HGNNs are vulnerable to local structure-limited black-box attacks, revealing a significant robustness gap that needs to be addressed.

title: Beyond Triplet Plausibility: Relation Set Completion in Knowledge Graphs
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) organize real-world knowledge as triplets and underpin many downstream applications. Due to their inherent incompleteness, knowledge graph completion (KGC) is widely studied and is typically formulated as triplet prediction, with link prediction as the dominant paradigm.
