However, this formulation focuses on the incompleteness of triplet-wise information and overlooks the incompleteness of entity-relation compatibility information. To address this limitation, we introduce a relation set completion task (RSC), which complements the link prediction task and aims to reason about missing relations that are semantically compatible with a given entity. We further propose a Relation Set Embedding model (RelSetE), which models latent patterns among the observed relations of entities to infer missing ones. To evaluate RelSetE, we derive three benchmark datasets from standard KG benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RelSetE effectively captures entity-relation compatibility patterns and performs favorably in inferring missing relations of entities. Code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Relation Set Completion (RSC) task to address entity-relation compatibility incompleteness in KGs, proposing RelSetE to model observed relation patterns and infer missing compatible relations, with effective results on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing KGC methods focus on triplet-wise incompleteness (link prediction) but overlook the incompleteness of entity-relation compatibility information.
AI.method: Proposes a Relation Set Embedding model (RelSetE) that models latent patterns among observed relations of entities to infer missing compatible relations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show RelSetE effectively captures entity-relation compatibility patterns and performs well in inferring missing relations of entities on three benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: The RSC task complements link prediction, and RelSetE successfully addresses the overlooked entity-relation compatibility incompleteness in KGs.

title: Heterogeneous Tactile Transformer
summary: Tactile sensors are inherently heterogeneous: a model trained on one sensor cannot be directly used on another, which limits learning contact-rich manipulation policies from diverse tactile data at scale. To bridge this gap, we propose the Heterogeneous Tactile Transformer (HTT), a framework that learns shared tactile representations across heterogeneous sensors. HTT consists of sensor-specific encoders and a shared transformer trunk, and is pretrained with per-modality masked reconstruction together with cross-modal alignment between paired sensors. Pretraining uses our novel Heterogeneous Paired Tactile (HPT) dataset, containing 1.6M synchronized paired frames across four vision- and array-based tactile sensors. Across distinct tactile perception and real-world manipulation tasks, HTT is shown to learn transferable representations that adapt to new tasks and previously unseen sensors. Dataset, code, and model checkpoints will be released upon publication at https://jxbi1010.github.io/htt-gh-page/.

AI.tldr: HTT is a framework that learns shared tactile representations across heterogeneous sensors using sensor-specific encoders, a shared transformer, masked reconstruction pretraining, and cross-modal alignment on a 1.6M paired frame dataset (HPT), achieving transferable representations for perception and manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Tactile sensors are heterogeneous, so models trained on one sensor cannot be directly transferred to another, limiting scalable learning of contact-rich manipulation policies from diverse tactile data.
AI.method: HTT uses sensor-specific encoders + a shared transformer trunk, pretrained with per-modality masked reconstruction and cross-modal alignment between paired sensors, using the novel HPT dataset (1.6M synchronized paired frames across four vision- and array-based tactile sensors).
AI.result: HTT learns transferable representations that adapt to both new tasks and previously unseen sensors across tactile perception and real-world manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: HTT effectively bridges the heterogeneity gap in tactile sensing, enabling representation transfer across diverse sensors and tasks, with dataset, code, and checkpoints to be released.

title: Towards Evaluating Data Priors for Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Data-generating priors are a central component of tabular foundation models because they define the task distribution used during pretraining. However, priors are rarely evaluated as independent components, making it difficult to understand how much they affect downstream model behavior. This raises a methodological question: how can priors from different tabular foundation models be compared independently of the architectures and training protocols they were introduced with? To study this question, we implement a unified interface for publicly available priors from recent tabular foundation models and priors constructed from real datasets. We generate training tasks from each prior, train the same model architecture under a fixed training protocol, and evaluate the resulting models on shared downstream classification tasks. We compare priors through both generated-task statistics and downstream predictive performance. Our results show that different priors favor different downstream behaviors, with some achieving stronger absolute performance and others exhibiting more consistent relative rankings across datasets. We further find that data-level similarity only partially explains downstream behavior. Our code is available at https://github.com/automl/TFM-Playground/tree/prior-dev.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a unified framework to compare data-generating priors from different tabular foundation models independently of their architectures and training protocols, showing that priors significantly influence downstream model behavior and that data similarity only partially explains these effects.
AI.motivation: Data-generating priors are critical for tabular foundation models but are rarely evaluated as independent components, making it unclear how much they affect downstream behavior. There is a need for a methodology to compare priors separately from architectures and training protocols.
AI.method: The authors implement a unified interface for publicly available priors, generate training tasks from each prior, train the same model architecture under a fixed training protocol, and evaluate on shared downstream classification tasks. They compare priors using both generated-task statistics and predictive performance.
AI.result: Different priors favor different downstream behaviors; some priors achieve stronger absolute performance while others produce more consistent relative rankings across datasets. Data-level similarity only partially explains downstream behavior.
AI.conclusion: Priors should be evaluated as independent components to understand their impact on model behavior. The proposed framework enables fair comparison and reveals that prior choice significantly shapes downstream outcomes beyond what data similarity metrics capture.

title: SciFlow: Semantic Cross Interference for Self-Supervised Optical Flow Domain Generalization
summary:
Motions of objects and scenes carry essential intelligence in video understanding, offering rich cues for interpreting dynamic settings and interactions. Due to the cost and scarcity of high-quality annotation or ground truth of pixel-wise optical flow, however, motion estimation models are typically trained in synthetic domains while deployed in real-world domains. Addressing synthetic-to-real domain generalization challenges has been crucial for developing practical solutions in diverse open-world use cases.
  This paper introduces SciFlow, a simple yet effective, network-agnostic, training-based approach that leverages self-supervised learning to generalize motion estimation across synthetic and open-world domains. Specifically, SciFlow imposes semantic interference from open-world images onto synthetic images during training, blending indomain features with cross-domain interference, which enables the network to adapt to the real-world domains. Additionally, SciFlow utilizes geometric consistency to ensure validity of the self-supervision. Our experiment results show that SciFlow not only significantly enhances model robustness amidst domain variations, but also remarkably enables synthetic-to-real domain generalization without requiring any ground truth in the open world.

AI.tldr: SciFlow is a training-based, network-agnostic approach using self-supervised learning to generalize motion estimation from synthetic to real-world domains by blending semantic interference from open-world images with synthetic data, further improved with geometric consistency.
AI.motivation: Motion estimation models suffer from synthetic-to-real domain generalization issues due to the cost and scarcity of high-quality optical flow annotations in real-world domains, limiting practical deployment.
AI.method: SciFlow imposes semantic interference from open-world images onto synthetic images during training, blending in-domain features with cross-domain interference, and utilizes geometric consistency to ensure validity of self-supervision.
AI.result: SciFlow significantly enhances model robustness amidst domain variations and enables synthetic-to-real domain generalization without requiring any ground truth in the open world.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is simple, effective, network-agnostic, and provides a practical solution for motion estimation in diverse open-world use cases.

title: AI Training Manager: Bounded Closed-Loop Control of Adaptive Training Recipes
summary: We present the AI Training Manager, a bounded LLM-based supervisory controller for adaptive machine learning training. Standard training pipelines often rely on fixed recipes or single-axis schedulers, which can struggle with mid-run failures such as severe overfitting, loss imbalance, exploration collapse, or unsafe exploration. Rather than replacing mathematical optimizers or acting as an unconstrained coding agent, the manager operates through a schema-conditioned interface: it reads structured telemetry snapshots from an active run, audits a constrained action space, and returns validated updates to training parameters such as learning rate, regularization strength, loss-weight coefficients, and exploration settings. We evaluate this architecture across supervised language modeling and reinforcement learning. On TinyStories, the manager detects and corrects overfitting, achieving a validation loss 60% lower than the baseline while producing auditable intervention logs. In this supervised setting, we additionally show that manager inference does not need to block the training loop: training can continue while a manager response is pending, and validated updates can be applied asynchronously once available. In a robotic manipulation reinforcement-learning task, we use the same bounded decision interface in an episodic closed-loop setting, where manager updates are applied at evaluation or checkpoint boundaries. The manager mitigates both conservative and unsafe exploration regimes. These results suggest that schema-conditioned LLMs can serve as bounded supervisory managers for live training runs, complementing conventional optimizers and schedulers with interpretable, multi-axis intervention capabilities

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an AI Training Manager—a bounded LLM-based controller that supervises ML training by reading telemetry, auditing a constrained action space, and returning validated parameter updates (e.g., learning rate, regularization). It corrects overfitting, loss imbalance, and exploration issues in both supervised learning and RL, achieving 60% lower validation loss on TinyStories and stable exploration in robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard training pipelines rely on fixed recipes or single-axis schedulers that fail to handle mid-run issues like overfitting, loss imbalance, exploration collapse, or unsafe exploration. The authors aim to create an interpretable, multi-axis supervisory system that complements—rather than replaces—existing optimizers.
AI.method: A schema-conditioned LLM-based "manager" reads structured telemetry snapshots, audits a constrained action space, and returns validated updates to hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization, loss weights, exploration settings). In supervised learning, updates can be applied asynchronously without blocking training; in RL, updates occur at evaluation/checkpoint boundaries in an episodic closed-loop fashion.
AI.result: On TinyStories, the manager detects and corrects overfitting, achieving 60% lower validation loss than baseline with auditable intervention logs. In robotic manipulation RL tasks, the manager mitigates both conservative and unsafe exploration regimes.
AI.conclusion: Schema-conditioned LLMs can serve as bounded supervisory managers for live training runs, providing interpretable, multi-axis intervention capabilities that complement conventional optimizers and schedulers without replacing them.

title: SIR: Structured Image Representations for Explainable Robot Learning
summary: Existing robot policies based on learned visual embeddings lack explicit structure and are sensitive to visual distractions. Thus, the representations that drive their behaviour are often opaque, making their decision-making process difficult to interpret. To address this, we introduce Structured Image Representations (SIR), a method that leverages Scene Graphs (SGs) as an intermediate representation for robot policy learning. Our approach first constructs a fully connected graph, using image-derived features as initial node representations. Then, a module learns to sparsify this graph end-to-end, creating a task-relevant sub-graph that is passed to the action generation model. This process makes our model intrinsically explainable. Evaluations on RoboCasa show that our sparse graph policies outperform image-based baselines on average with 19.5% vs 14.81% success rate. Most importantly, we show that the learned sparse graphs are a powerful tool for model analysis. By analysing when the model's sub-graph deviates from human expectation, such as by including distractor nodes or omitting key objects, we successfully uncover dataset biases, including spurious correlations and positional biases. https://github.com/intuitive-robots/SIR_Model

AI.tldr: SIR uses Scene Graphs as intermediate representations to create interpretable, sparse graph policies for robot learning, outperforming image-based baselines and enabling detection of dataset biases.
AI.motivation: Existing robot policies based on learned visual embeddings lack explicit structure, are sensitive to visual distractions, and produce opaque decision-making processes that are difficult to interpret.
AI.method: SIR first constructs a fully connected graph from image features, then learns to sparsify this graph end-to-end to create task-relevant sub-graphs, which are passed to the action generation model for intrinsically explainable behavior.
AI.result: On RoboCasa, sparse graph policies achieve 19.5% success rate vs 14.81% for image-based baselines. The learned sparse graphs also enable uncovering dataset biases like spurious correlations and positional biases.
AI.conclusion: Structured Image Representations provide both improved performance and interpretability, with the sparse graphs serving as a powerful tool for model analysis and bias detection in robot learning.

title: KrishokChat: A Citation-Grounded Dataset and Benchmark for Bengali Agricultural Advisory
summary: We present KrishokChat, the first citation-grounded Bengali agricultural instruction-tuning dataset for crop advisory in low-resource settings. We establish a foundation of 290 hierarchical Knowledge Nodes, extracting disease symptoms, management practices, chemical dosages, and verbatim citations from 129 domain-filtered agricultural manuals. Every training instance inherits a verified citation header, guaranteeing 100% citation provenance. Using a Partitioned Seed Generation Matrix, these nodes are expanded into 139,200 supervised fine-tuning pairs, and augmented with 5,300 chemical safety and 1,000 adversarial safety instances, yielding 145,500 QA pairs across 18 crop categories. To evaluate real-world performance, we introduce the Farmer Benchmark, comprising 1,001 authentic farmer queries curated from field surveys and digital portals. Empirical evaluation on Gemma-4-E2B reveals that while fine-tuning on KrishokChat vastly improves structured formatting, standalone models still struggle with exact chemical dosage generalization. This highlights the dataset's true value as a verified knowledge base for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) rather than mere parametric memorization. All data, code, and benchmarks are released under CC-BY-4.0.

AI.tldr: KrishokChat is the first citation-grounded Bengali agricultural QA dataset with 145,500 pairs across 18 crops, built from 129 manuals via knowledge nodes and seed generation. Evaluation shows fine-tuning improves formatting but struggles with dosage generalization, positioning the dataset as a verified RAG knowledge base rather than for parametric memorization.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of citation-grounded, instruction-following agricultural datasets for low-resource settings (Bengali), enabling trustworthy crop advisory systems with verifiable provenance.
AI.method: (1) Extracted 290 hierarchical Knowledge Nodes from 129 domain-filtered agricultural manuals; (2) Used a Partitioned Seed Generation Matrix to expand nodes into 139,200 SFT pairs; (3) Augmented with 5,300 chemical safety + 1,000 adversarial safety instances; (4) Created Farmer Benchmark (1,001 real farmer queries); (5) Evaluated on Gemma-4-E2B.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on KrishokChat significantly improves structured formatting; however, standalone models still struggle with exact chemical dosage generalization, suggesting the dataset is better suited as a verified knowledge base for RAG rather than parametric memorization.
AI.conclusion: KrishokChat provides 100% citation provenance, is released under CC-BY-4.0, and is positioned as a foundational resource for retrieval-augmented generation in low-resource agricultural question answering.

title: Mural: Transferring LLM knowledge to image generation via Mixture-of-Transformers
summary: Leveraging capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in text-to-image (T2I) synthesis is an important research direction. In this work we investigate whether the knowledge of a frozen LLM can be effectively utilized in T2I generation when trained exclusively on standard text-image pairs. We integrate a frozen, reasoning-capable LLM with a diffusion-based image generator via shared attention within the Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture. Our experiments span two critical questions: (1) what degree of the LLM's intrinsic knowledge remains accessible during T2I training, and (2) what novel capabilities emerge in the resulting system. Across established benchmarks, our models achieve strong performance among unified understanding-generation systems: 0.85 on GenEval, 86.75 on DPG-Bench, and 0.66 on WISE with inference-time reasoning, using only text-image data. Remarkably, we uncover emergent behaviors absent from training data, including cross-lingual image generation, color-guided composition, emoji / ASCII scene construction, and generation directed by world knowledge. These results demonstrate that pretrained LLM knowledge can guide image synthesis under standard text-to-image training paradigms, without interleaved multimodal signals or explicit reasoning supervision. Our findings open new avenues for harnessing frozen model capabilities in resource-constrained multimodal learning.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that a frozen LLM, integrated with a diffusion model via Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, can be trained on standard text-image pairs to achieve strong T2I performance and unlock emergent capabilities like cross-lingual generation and world knowledge-guided synthesis without explicit multimodal or reasoning supervision.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether frozen, reasoning-capable LLMs can effectively guide text-to-image synthesis when trained only on standard text-image pairs, without interleaved multimodal data or explicit reasoning supervision.
AI.method: A frozen LLM is integrated with a diffusion-based image generator via shared attention within the Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, trained exclusively on standard text-image pairs.
AI.result: Achieved 0.85 on GenEval, 86.75 on DPG-Bench, and 0.66 on WISE with inference-time reasoning. Emergent capabilities include cross-lingual generation, color-guided composition, emoji/ASCII scene construction, and world knowledge-driven generation.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained frozen LLM knowledge can effectively guide image synthesis under standard T2I training paradigms, opening new avenues for resource-constrained multimodal learning.

title: SafePyramid: A Hierarchical Benchmark for In-context Policy Guardrailing
summary: In real-world applications, guardrails are often expected to identify unsafe user-model interactions according to application-specific safety policies, rather than relying on predefined risk taxonomies. In this work, we study this setting under the paradigm of in-context policy guardrailing, where guardrails predict safety violations based on policy specifications provided in context. To systematically evaluate this capability, we introduce SafePyramid, a safety benchmark comprising 1,000 multi-turn conversations across 10 domains and 3,000 corresponding application-specific policies, which together contain 61,699 distinct natural-language rules. SafePyramid organizes the evaluation into three difficulty levels: L0 evaluates individual-rule understanding, L1 evaluates reasoning over rule dependencies, and L2 evaluates adaptation of full novel policy frameworks defined in context. To ensure benchmark quality, we employ a rigorous multi-stage pipeline to construct and validate the benchmark. Using SafePyramid, we evaluate 10 frontier LLMs and 5 policy-configurable guardrails and find that in-context policy guardrailing remains highly challenging: even the best-performing model, GPT-5.5, exactly identifies the full set of violated rules in only 54.0%, 35.3%, and 12.9% cases on L0, L1, and L2, respectively. These results highlight the limitations of current guardrails and call for stronger in-context policy guardrails that can reliably execute policies, resolve rule dependencies, and adapt to novel policy frameworks.

AI.tldr: SafePyramid is a benchmark with 1,000 multi-turn conversations, 3,000 policies, and 61,699 rules across 3 difficulty levels (L0-L2) for evaluating in-context policy guardrailing. Even the best model (GPT-5.5) achieves only 54.0%, 35.3%, and 12.9% accuracy on L0, L1, and L2 respectively, showing this remains a highly challenging task.
AI.motivation: To address the need for guardrails that can identify unsafe interactions based on application-specific policies rather than predefined risk taxonomies, and to systematically evaluate this capability.
AI.method: Constructs SafePyramid benchmark with 1,000 multi-turn conversations across 10 domains, 3,000 policies, and 61,699 rules. Organizes evaluation into L0 (individual-rule understanding), L1 (rule dependency reasoning), and L2 (novel policy framework adaptation). Uses a rigorous multi-stage pipeline for construction/validation. Evaluates 10 frontier LLMs and 5 policy-configurable guardrails.
AI.result: Best model (GPT-5.5) achieves only 54.0% (L0), 35.3% (L1), and 12.9% (L2) in exactly identifying all violated rules. In-context policy guardrailing remains highly challenging.
AI.conclusion: Current guardrails have significant limitations; there is a need for stronger in-context policy guardrails that can reliably execute policies, resolve rule dependencies, and adapt to novel policy frameworks.

title: TacEvo: Self-Evolving Architecture Discovery for Robotic Tactile Perception via LLM-Driven Quality-Diversity Search
summary: Vision-based tactile sensing converts contact-induced surface deformation into images, enabling robots to infer contact forces and fine surface textures that are not accessible through conventional vision alone. However, tactile images are sensor- and physics-specific, so effective architectures often require expert intuition and extensive manual iteration. Existing neural architecture search (NAS) pipelines can reduce this burden, but they are often computationally expensive and restricted to hand-designed search spaces, which limits architectural novelty and diversity. We introduce TacEvo, a self-evolving architecture discovery framework that improves network designs from downstream feedback. TacEvo uses an LLM to generate code-level mutations and crossovers, and a MAP-Elites quality-diversity loop that preserves diverse elite architectures while preferentially reusing prompts that consistently yield improvements. Exploration is guided by two behavioural descriptors, Architectural Diversity and Efficiency Ratio, which encourage coverage across structural variations and compute-size trade-offs. On ViTacTip force regression and grating classification, TacEvo achieves high autonomous generation reliability (96.0%/94.5% trainable) and improves best validation fitness over 20 generations by 56.1%/96.1%. In a 20-seed post-search high-fidelity evaluation, TacEvo matches the expert baseline on force prediction and outperforms it on fine-grained grating classification. These results suggest that LLM-driven self-evolving search constitutes a practical paradigm for AI-assisted scientific discovery in specialised robotic sensing.

AI.tldr: TacEvo is an LLM-driven, self-evolving neural architecture search framework for vision-based tactile sensing that uses code-level mutations, MAP-Elites quality-diversity optimization, and behavioral descriptors to autonomously discover improved network architectures, achieving 96%/94.5% generation reliability and 56.1%/96.1% fitness improvements over 20 generations.
AI.motivation: Vision-based tactile sensing requires specialized network architectures, but designing them relies on expert intuition and manual iteration. Existing NAS methods are computationally expensive and restricted to hand-designed search spaces, limiting architectural novelty.
AI.method: TacEvo combines an LLM for generating code-level mutations and crossovers with a MAP-Elites quality-diversity loop that preserves diverse elite architectures. Two behavioral descriptors (Architectural Diversity and Efficiency Ratio) guide exploration across structural variations and compute-size trade-offs.
AI.result: Achieves 96.0%/94.5% trainable generation reliability, 56.1%/96.1% best validation fitness improvement over 20 generations on force regression and grating classification. In 20-seed evaluation, matches expert baseline on force prediction and outperforms on fine-grained grating classification.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven self-evolving search is a practical paradigm for AI-assisted scientific discovery in specialized robotic sensing, reducing reliance on human expertise and manual architecture iteration.

title: When Prices Double in a Week: Forecasting of Agricultural Volatility in Import-Isolated Markets
summary: Vegetable prices in Sri Lanka are highly volatile because the market is largely import-isolated, so supply disruptions quickly drive prices up. This study develops a machine learning framework to forecast such volatility by incorporating supply-chain-aware features and explicitly modelling the country's two cultivation seasons, Maha (October-April) and Yala (May-September). An integrated dataset was constructed by combining retail and farmer-gate prices with origin-aligned weather variables, diesel costs, and exchange rates across 12 vegetable varieties and 14 market centres from 2013 to 2019. A gradient-boosted ensemble model (XGBoost and LightGBM) was trained and optimised using Optuna, and unified and season-specific configurations were compared. Results show that season-specific models improve within-season fit, with the Yala-specific model achieving the highest R2 of 0.9420 (95% CI [0.690, 1.000]), while the unified model delivers the best overall predictive accuracy of 90.84% (95% CI [88.34%, 91.52%]) and an R2 of 0.9281 (95% CI [0.760, 1.000]). Notably, the unified model maintains 85.96% accuracy on a completely unseen 2024 hyperinflationary period without retraining, successfully tracking major price surges. These findings suggest that agricultural price movements in import-constrained markets are meaningfully predictable when models capture supply-chain dynamics, offering practical value for early warning and decision making by farmers, traders, and policymakers. Existing studies on Sri Lankan vegetable prices are confined to Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) and Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (GARCH) applied to single markets, with no supply-chain features, seasonal segmentation, or cross-regime validation.

AI.tldr: This study develops a machine learning framework (XGBoost + LightGBM) to forecast volatile vegetable prices in Sri Lanka's import-isolated market, using supply-chain-aware features across two cultivation seasons. Season-specific models improve within-season fit (Yala model R²=0.942), while the unified model achieves best overall accuracy (90.84%, R²=0.928) and maintains 85.96% accuracy on a 2024 hyperinflationary period without retraining.
AI.motivation: Vegetable prices in Sri Lanka are highly volatile due to import isolation, causing supply disruptions to rapidly drive up prices. Existing research only uses ARIMA/GARCH models on single markets without supply-chain features, seasonal segmentation, or cross-regime validation, leaving a gap for more sophisticated forecasting.
AI.method: A gradient-boosted ensemble (XGBoost and LightGBM) is trained and optimized via Optuna. An integrated dataset combines retail/farmer-gate prices with origin-aligned weather, diesel costs, and exchange rates across 12 vegetable varieties and 14 market centers (2013-2019). Both unified and season-specific (Maha/Yala) model configurations are compared.
AI.result: Season-specific models improve within-season fit; Yala-specific model achieves highest R²=0.942 (CI: 0.690-1.000). Unified model delivers best overall accuracy (90.84%, CI: 88.34%-91.52%) and R²=0.928 (CI: 0.760-1.000). It maintains 85.96% accuracy on an unseen 2024 hyperinflationary period without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Agricultural price movements in import-constrained markets are meaningfully predictable when supply-chain dynamics are captured, offering practical value for early warning systems and decision-making by farmers, traders, and policymakers.

title: Semantic-Aware, Physics-Informed, Geometry-Grounded Weather Video Synthesis
summary: Weather synthesis aims to add weather effects to input videos while preserving scene identity, structure, and motion. The key limitation of existing methods is the lack of diversity in weather appearance and effective control over weather dynamics (e.g., temporal evolution and particle motion). Most approaches rely on text prompts, which are inherently underspecified and often fail to produce detailed weather characteristics. Additionally, general-purpose video editors optimized for clean and aesthetic outputs tend to suppress heavy weather phenomena, making dense particle effects difficult to generate. To address these, we propose a Semantic-Aware, Physics-Informed, and Geometry-Grounded framework that steers an off-the-shelf video editor to synthesize diverse global appearances and detailed particle dynamics. We factorize the synthesis into three conditional signals, so that each provides a distinct and stable source of guidance: semantics specifies what the weather should look like, dynamics governs how it evolves over time, and geometry determines where it should appear in the scene. Specifically, we introduce (1) semantic-aware appearance anchoring to establish the target appearance from scene semantics and user input; (2) physics-informed dynamic simulation to generate particle effects by simulating a Gaussian-represented particle field under gravity, wind, and turbulence; and (3) geometry-grounded video synthesis to align the simulated particles with target scene geometry and synthesize the final video. Experiments demonstrate that our method produces diverse, physically and visually realistic weather effects. Furthermore, we show that our synthesized data significantly improves the robustness of autonomous driving semantic segmentation under adverse weather conditions. Project page: https://jumponthemoon.github.io/w-crafter/.

AI.tldr: A Semantic-Aware, Physics-Informed, Geometry-Grounded framework (W-Crafter) that steers video editors to synthesize diverse and realistic weather effects by factorizing synthesis into semantics (what), dynamics (how), and geometry (where).
AI.motivation: Existing weather synthesis methods lack diversity in weather appearance and effective control over weather dynamics (temporal evolution, particle motion). Text prompts are underspecified, and general video editors suppress heavy weather phenomena.
AI.method: (1) Semantic-aware appearance anchoring for target appearance from semantics + user input; (2) Physics-informed dynamic simulation (Gaussian particle field under gravity, wind, turbulence); (3) Geometry-grounded video synthesis aligning particles with scene geometry.
AI.result: Produces diverse, physically and visually realistic weather effects. Synthesized data significantly improves robustness of autonomous driving semantic segmentation under adverse weather.
AI.conclusion: Factorizing weather synthesis into three conditional signals (semantics, dynamics, geometry) enables controllable, realistic, and diverse weather effects that benefit downstream tasks like autonomous driving.

title: A causal modeling perspective on decision theory
summary: Decision theory provides a formal framework for how agents should make choices under uncertainty, drawing on ideas from philosophy, probability, and causality. Despite significant progress, the field still lacks a unified modeling language, and key concepts - such as the distinction between subjective and objective elements, or what it means for a decision theory to perform well - are often left implicit. This can make it difficult to evaluate and compare competing theories, particularly in controversial cases. In this paper, we address these issues by introducing a formal framework for decision theory based on nonparametric structural equation models (NPSEMs), a well-established tool in causal inference. NPSEMs provide a unified foundation for representing agents, counterfactuals, and causal relationships, allowing for unambiguous definitions of EDT and CDT. Building on this foundation, we propose a novel decision theory - personal decision theory - which instructs agents to maximize a subjective model of their own counterfactual utility. We introduce a formal performance metric based on hypothetical interventions that enforce a given decision theory across a population - such as might be achieved through education or policy -- and show that, under certain assumptions, personal decision theory is optimal with respect to this metric. Throughout, we use the smoking lesion problem as a running example and conclude with a formal analysis of Newcomb's problem. Our aim is to provide decision theory with a clearer modeling language and firmer evaluative ground, thereby enabling more rigorous comparisons and facilitating conceptual progress in the field.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a formal framework for decision theory using NPSEMs, proposes a new "personal decision theory" that maximizes subjective counterfactual utility, and shows its optimality under a population-level intervention-based performance metric.
AI.motivation: Decision theory lacks a unified modeling language, and key concepts (subjective vs. objective elements, performance evaluation) are often left implicit, making it difficult to evaluate and compare competing theories.
AI.method: The authors use nonparametric structural equation models (NPSEMs) as a unified foundation to represent agents, counterfactuals, and causal relationships, enabling unambiguous definitions of EDT and CDT. They then propose "personal decision theory" which instructs agents to maximize a subjective model of their own counterfactual utility.
AI.result: Under certain assumptions, personal decision theory is shown to be optimal with respect to a formal performance metric based on hypothetical interventions that enforce a given decision theory across a population.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides decision theory with a clearer modeling language and firmer evaluative ground, enabling more rigorous comparisons and facilitating conceptual progress.

title: Automating the Design of Embodied AgentArchitectures
summary: Embodied agents are typically built as hand-designed compositions of perception, memory, planning, and action modules. This modularity exposes a large architectural design space, but current systems still rely on researcher intuition to choose where information is stored, how observations are processed, and how model calls are connected. Agent Architecture Search (AAS) automates such design for text-domain agents, but has not been systematically evaluated on perceptual embodied agents through simulator rollouts. We study this transfer. We introduce AgentCanvas, a typed-graph runtime that hosts embodied executors as editable node-and-wire programs with simulator-aware execution and episode-level logs, and KDLoop, a coding-agent search procedure that cycles through proposal, critique, experiment, and distillation, with triggered reflection after stalls. We evaluate three AAS variants across four embodied executors spanning vision-language navigation, embodied question answering, and language-conditioned manipulation. The resulting 3x4 matrix shows that architecture-level search can produce deployable and directional success-rate gains on embodied tasks, while one apparent high-scoring candidate is rejected as leak-bearing. At the same time, the experiments expose constraints that are muted in text-domain AAS: optimization signals can be masked by rollout noise, search can become trapped in local edit basins, and episode-level credit assignment only partially emerges even when detailed logs are available. These results characterize both the promise and the current limits of automated architecture search for embodied agents.

AI.tldr: This paper studies transferring Agent Architecture Search (AAS) from text-domain to embodied agents, introducing AgentCanvas (a typed-graph runtime for embodied executors) and KDLoop (a search procedure). Through evaluations across 4 embodied tasks, it shows architecture search can improve success rates but faces challenges like rollout noise, local edit traps, and limited credit assignment.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents are manually designed with hand-crafted perception, memory, planning, and action modules, relying on researcher intuition. AAS automates this for text-domain agents but hasn't been systematically evaluated on embodied agents with simulator rollouts.
AI.method: Introduces AgentCanvas (typed-graph runtime with simulator-aware execution and episode-level logs) and KDLoop (coding-agent search procedure cycling through proposal, critique, experiment, and distillation with triggered reflection). Evaluates 3 AAS variants across 4 embodied executors (VLN, EQA, manipulation).
AI.result: Architecture-level search can produce deployable success-rate gains on embodied tasks. However, one high-scoring candidate was rejected as leak-bearing. Optimization signals are masked by rollout noise, search gets trapped in local edit basins, and episode-level credit assignment only partially emerges.
AI.conclusion: Automated architecture search for embodied agents shows promise (directional gains) but faces significant constraints not seen in text-domain AAS, including noise masking, local traps, and limited credit assignment.

title: Learning to Bid in Discriminatory Auctions with Budget Constraints
summary: We study repeated bidding in multi-unit discriminatory (pay-as-bid) auctions for a single bidder with per-round utility equal to value minus $α$ times payment, where $α\in[0,1]$ is a cost-of-capital parameter. The bidder aims to maximize cumulative utility over $T$ rounds subject to a total budget $B$. The problem is challenging even without budgets: the action space is exponential in $M$, the maximum demand of the bidder and the valuation vector (context) varies over time. Exploiting a decomposition of utility across units, we develop polynomial-time learning algorithms based on shortest paths in a directed acyclic graph, obtaining sublinear regret under both full-information and bandit feedback. In the bandit setting, the regret is independent of the number of contexts due to complete cross-learning: observing the utility of the chosen action under the realized context reveals the utility for the same action under all counterfactual contexts. With budget constraints, when the average normalized per-round budget $ρ=\frac{B}{MT}<1$, we design a coupled primal-dual algorithm in which the DAG-based procedure uses dual-adjusted edge weights for primal updates, while online gradient descent updates the dual variable, yielding $ρ$-approximate sublinear regret. Finally, we give implementations whose per-round time and space are independent of the number of contexts, enabling scalability to large or even infinite context spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper develops polynomial-time learning algorithms for repeated multi-unit discriminatory auctions with cost-of-capital and budget constraints, achieving sublinear regret in both full-information and bandit settings, with per-round complexity independent of context space size.
AI.motivation: Repeated bidding in multi-unit pay-as-bid auctions is challenging due to exponentially large action spaces (in maximum demand M), time-varying valuation vectors, and budget constraints. The problem is unsolved even without budgets, motivating efficient algorithms with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: The authors decompose utility across units and develop polynomial-time learning algorithms based on shortest paths in a directed acyclic graph (DAG). For bandit settings, they exploit complete cross-learning (observing utility under realized context reveals utility under all counterfactual contexts). For budget constraints, they design a coupled primal-dual algorithm where the DAG-based procedure uses dual-adjusted edge weights, and online gradient descent updates the dual variable.
AI.result: The algorithms achieve sublinear regret under both full-information and bandit feedback. In bandit setting, regret is independent of the number of contexts due to complete cross-learning. With budget constraints, when average normalized per-round budget ρ < 1, the algorithm yields ρ-approximate sublinear regret. Per-round time and space complexity are independent of the number of contexts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithms are efficient, scalable to large or even infinite context spaces, and provide strong theoretical guarantees for repeated multi-unit discriminatory auctions under both unconstrained and budget-constrained settings.

title: Efficient Spatio-Temporal Grounding with Multimodal Large Models via Second-Level Tracking and RL Verification
summary: Spatio-temporal grounding in long videos requires precise temporal localization and robust object tracking conditioned on natural-language queries. While recent vision-language models (VLMs) show strong reasoning ability, directly applying frame-by-frame inference to long sequences is computationally expensive and unstable. We propose a practical pipeline that shifts from frame-level to second-level tracking and performs cross-second smoothing to preserve continuity while reducing sequence length. To improve reasoning supervision, we synthesize chain-of-thought style trajectories using advanced multimodal models for temporal localization and target selection, and replace generated spatio-temporal coordinates with ground-truth annotations to avoid noisy supervision. We further optimize the policy with reinforcement learning using a verifier based on $t\_\mathrm{IoU}+mv\_\mathrm{IoU}$. Experiments across multiple FPS settings show that our method achieves a strong trade-off between efficiency and localization quality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an efficient pipeline for spatio-temporal grounding in long videos by shifting from frame-level to second-level tracking with cross-second smoothing, using chain-of-thought trajectory synthesis, ground-truth annotation substitution, and reinforcement learning optimization, achieving a good efficiency-quality trade-off.
AI.motivation: Direct frame-by-frame inference with VLMs on long videos is computationally expensive and unstable; there is a need for a more efficient yet accurate approach for spatio-temporal grounding in long video sequences.
AI.method: (1) Shifts from frame-level to second-level tracking with cross-second smoothing; (2) Synthesizes chain-of-thought style trajectories using multimodal models for temporal localization and target selection; (3) Replaces generated coordinates with ground-truth annotations to avoid noisy supervision; (4) Optimizes policy with RL using a verifier based on t_IoU + mv_IoU.
AI.result: The method achieves a strong trade-off between efficiency and localization quality across multiple FPS settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline offers a practical and efficient solution for spatio-temporal grounding in long videos, balancing computational cost with accurate localization performance.

title: HippoSpark: An On-Demand Experience System for LLM Reasoning
summary: Distilling historical trajectories into reusable experience to enhance future problem-solving has become a focal point of recent LLM research. However, existing methods predominantly operate at the task level, leveraging general summaries or rules under the assumption that analogous tasks share universal solution patterns. This approach often fails in complex reasoning, which typically falters at local bottlenecks that require precise, state-specific guidance rather than broad heuristics. We introduce HippoSpark, a state-level experience system that performs on-demand retrieval tailored to the immediate needs of the current reasoning state. Across mathematical, scientific, and programming benchmarks, HippoSpark consistently outperforms both standard prompting and task-level experience baselines. Our findings reveal that the most effective experience systems are those that provide actionable guidance at critical bottlenecks rather than serving as generic task-level context. Our code is available at https://github.com/DanlingMeng/HippoSpark.

AI.tldr: HippoSpark introduces a state-level experience system that retrieves precise, state-specific guidance at reasoning bottlenecks, outperforming task-level experience methods across math, science, and programming benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM experience systems operate at the task level with general summaries/rules, which fail in complex reasoning because problems occur at local bottlenecks needing precise, state-specific guidance rather than broad heuristics.
AI.method: HippoSpark is a state-level experience system that performs on-demand retrieval tailored to the immediate needs of the current reasoning state, providing actionable guidance at critical bottlenecks.
AI.result: HippoSpark consistently outperforms standard prompting and task-level experience baselines across mathematical, scientific, and programming benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The most effective experience systems provide actionable guidance at critical bottlenecks rather than serving as generic task-level context.

title: SA-VLA: State-aware tokenizer for improving Vision-Language-Action Models' performance
summary: Discrete action tokenization provides a compact interface for autoregressive VLA policies, but accurately recovering continuous robot actions from discrete codes remains challenging. Existing tokenizers typically map each discrete code to a fixed continuous action prototype, ignoring the robot's current proprioceptive state. This limitation is particularly pronounced in manipulation, where the same action token may require different continuous controls under different joint configurations, object poses, and contact conditions. We therefore propose SA-VLA, a state-aware action tokenizer that conditions action decoding on robot state. We study two state-injection mechanisms for VQ-based action tokenization: cross-attention between state and action features, and a lightweight state adapter that predicts action-wise modulation factors for state-conditioned action modulation and reconstruction. The adapter formulation expands the effective support of a finite codebook by allowing each discrete token to represent a family of state-dependent continuous actions, while preserving the efficiency and compatibility of discrete action modeling. Integrated into an LLM-based VLA policy, SA-VLA supports both autoregressive and parallel action-token decoding with minimal changes to the model interface. On 12 RoboTwin manipulation tasks, SA-VLA improves the average success rate from 0.29 to 0.56 over the strongest tokenizer baseline. In zero-shot sim-to-real experiments on three real-world tasks, it further improves average success from 0.15 to 0.33 over the strongest tokenizer baseline. These results demonstrate that state-conditioned action decoding is a simple and effective mechanism for reducing the compression gap in discrete VLA policies.

AI.tldr: SA-VLA introduces a state-aware action tokenizer that conditions discrete action decoding on robot proprioceptive state, significantly improving manipulation success rates across simulated and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing discrete action tokenizers map each discrete code to a fixed continuous action prototype, ignoring the robot's current proprioceptive state (joint configurations, object poses, contact conditions), which is particularly problematic for manipulation tasks.
AI.method: SA-VLA proposes two state-injection mechanisms for VQ-based action tokenization: (1) cross-attention between state and action features, and (2) a lightweight state adapter that predicts action-wise modulation factors for state-conditioned action modulation and reconstruction, allowing each discrete token to represent a family of state-dependent continuous actions.
AI.result: On 12 RoboTwin tasks, SA-VLA improves average success rate from 0.29 to 0.56 over strongest baseline. In zero-shot sim-to-real on three real-world tasks, it improves from 0.15 to 0.33.
AI.conclusion: State-conditioned action decoding is a simple and effective mechanism for reducing the compression gap in discrete VLA policies, preserving efficiency and compatibility while significantly improving performance.

title: Nonlinear mixture model motivated subspace clustering
summary: We derive the linear union-of-subspaces (UoS) model for subspace clustering (SC) from the nonlinear mixture model (NMM) used in blind source separation (BSS) to represent a D-dimensional observation vector as an unknown multivariate nonlinear mapping of C latent variables. Assuming the mapping is differentiable up to an unknown order K, we approximate NMM by a K-th order Taylor expansion, yielding a model equivalent to the linear UoS framework underlying SC. This establishes that: (i) the smoothness order K corresponds to the unknown subspace dimension d; (ii) KC equals the number of anchors; and (iii) the sparsity of the representation vector equals K (i.e., d). These relationships enable estimation of bounds on subspace dimension, and that is validated on six benchmark datasets using five established SC algorithms. Established theoretical results are important for post-processing of self-representation matrices estimated by SC algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper derives the linear union-of-subspaces (UoS) model for subspace clustering from the nonlinear mixture model used in blind source separation. By approximating the nonlinear mapping via Taylor expansion, the authors establish theoretical links between the smoothness order, subspace dimension, number of anchors, and sparsity. These relationships enable estimation of subspace dimension bounds, validated on six benchmark datasets with five SC algorithms.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between nonlinear mixture models (BSS) and linear subspace clustering (SC), providing a unified theoretical framework that explains key SC parameters (subspace dimension, anchors, sparsity) through the lens of nonlinear smoothness.
AI.method: Approximates the nonlinear mixture model using a K-th order Taylor expansion, which yields a linear UoS model. Derives correspondences: smoothness order K ↔ subspace dimension d; KC ↔ number of anchors; sparsity ↔ K (d). Validates estimated bounds on six benchmark datasets with five SC algorithms.
AI.result: Established theoretical relationships between NMM smoothness and SC parameters; validated bounds on subspace dimension across six datasets using five algorithms, confirming practical applicability for post-processing self-representation matrices.
AI.conclusion: The derived connections provide important theoretical grounding for subspace clustering, particularly useful for post-processing steps in SC algorithms, and offer a principled way to estimate subspace dimension bounds.

title: Adaptive Spectrum-Aware Feature Disentangled Network for Small Object Detection
summary: Small Object Detection (SOD) is a fundamental yet challenging problem in computer vision due to its limited spatial resolution and weak visual cues. Although recent approaches have achieved remarkable advances, the background distractors in different frequency spectra still degrade the performance. In this paper, we propose a novel small object detection framework termed SFDNet, which is capable of detecting small objects via efficient spectrum-aware feature disentanglement. Specifically, we propose an Adaptive Spectrum Disentanglement (ASD) module that decomposes backbone features into multiple complementary spectral components, aiming to construct discriminative object-relevant representations by discarding the background distractors for each component. Afterwards, to strengthen the semantic consistency of the similar objects in the same class, we propose a Class-Wise Prototype Distillation (CPD) procedure, which establishes class prototypes for the object instances and enforces the compact representation by efficient prototype distillation. Extensive experiments on multiple challenging benchmarks show that SFDNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods by a large margin. Our code is available at https://github.com/ManOfStory/SFDNet.

AI.tldr: SFDNet is a small object detection framework that uses spectrum-aware feature disentanglement (ASD module) to separate object-relevant features from background distractors, plus Class-Wise Prototype Distillation (CPD) to enforce intra-class compactness. It achieves SOTA results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Small object detection suffers from limited spatial resolution and weak visual cues, and existing methods degrade due to background distractors across different frequency spectra.
AI.method: (1) Adaptive Spectrum Disentanglement (ASD): decomposes backbone features into complementary spectral components and discards background distractors per component. (2) Class-Wise Prototype Distillation (CPD): establishes class prototypes and enforces compact representation via distillation.
AI.result: Outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods by a large margin on multiple challenging benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectrum-aware disentanglement and prototype distillation framework effectively addresses the small object detection problem.

title: SAGA: Scene-Aware, Goal-Evolving Agents for Long-Horizon CivRealm Strategy Planning
summary: Long-horizon strategic planning in complex strategy games demands concurrent reasoning across multiple decision domains under imperfect information and sparse reward. Existing LLM-based agents suffer from three systematic failures: scene blindness from raw tile coordinates, context overflow and domain coupling from monolithic state dumps, and shallow cross-game learning that treats each episode in isolation. We present SAGA, an LLM multi-agent framework with three mechanisms each directly targeting one class of failure: (i) a Map-Semantic Scene Graph that encodes typed spatial relations among game entities into per-unit natural-language context, resolving spatial blindness without global token inflation; (ii) a Tool-Augmented Planner that pulls fine-grained domain state on demand and dispatches per-domain directives to dedicated specialist controllers, eliminating context overflow, domain coupling, and mechanical constraint violations; and (iii) a Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop that combines periodic within-game goal generation with structured cross-game causal post-mortem, enabling principled strategic evolution without manual reward engineering. Evaluated on FreeCiv, SAGA attains the highest mean civilization score -- the environment's sole sparse objective reward -- with lower variance than the two strongest baselines, and is the only method that significantly surpasses every baseline on infrastructure construction, the resource axis most readily sacrificed under multi-objective conflict. It outscores the two strongest baselines in most head-to-head games while cutting output tokens (the dominant decoding cost) by 27%. Equipped with the cross-game evolution module, SAGA reaches the highest end-of-chain score across five successive episodes. Ablation studies confirm that each architectural component contributes independently to this advantage.

AI.tldr: SAGA is an LLM multi-agent framework that addresses three key failures (scene blindness, context overflow/domain coupling, shallow cross-game learning) in long-horizon strategic planning for complex strategy games, achieving superior performance on FreeCiv with 27% fewer output tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based agents suffer from scene blindness (raw tile coordinates), context overflow and domain coupling (monolithic state dumps), and shallow cross-game learning (episode isolation). The paper aims to resolve these systematic failures for long-horizon strategic planning under imperfect information and sparse rewards.
AI.method: SAGA introduces three mechanisms: (i) Map-Semantic Scene Graph encoding spatial relations into per-unit context; (ii) Tool-Augmented Planner for on-demand domain state retrieval with per-domain specialist controllers; (iii) Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop combining within-game goal generation with cross-game causal post-mortem for strategic evolution.
AI.result: On FreeCiv, SAGA achieves the highest mean civilization score with lower variance than top baselines, surpasses every baseline on infrastructure construction, outscores strongest baselines in head-to-head games, and cuts output tokens by 27%. Cross-game evolution module reaches highest end-of-chain score across five episodes.
AI.conclusion: Each architectural component contributes independently to SAGA's advantage, validated by ablation studies. The framework provides principled strategic evolution without manual reward engineering.

title: AERIS: Aerial-Edge Role-Driven Intelligence at Runtime via Orchestrated Language-Model Swarm
comment: 10 pages, 11 figures. Preprint version of the submitted manuscript
summary: Integrating large language models into robotic systems holds promise for enhancing autonomy, yet practical deployment remains constrained by strict heartbeat-constrained scheduling and limited computational power. We propose AERIS: an edge deployment framework for aerial platforms. It organizes dedicated small language models combined with lightweight perception and control modules into roles that can be instantiated at runtime, and dynamically rebinds them across different executors as resources change, thereby pushing intelligent capabilities to the edge. AERIS achieves long-horizon instruction decomposition through an attention-subgoal alignment mechanism, which involves annotating the currently active instruction step in messages, thereby progressively approaching long-term objectives. We evaluate AERIS on a high-fidelity UAV Vision-and-Language Navigation benchmark. Under a heartbeat-timed execution mechanism, AERIS maintains a stable perception-decision-control loop between a low-frequency planner and a high-frequency controller, supporting real-time closed-loop operation. We further validate its deployability through two real-world experiments focused on planning and fast response. A demonstration video is provided in the supplementary materials.

AI.tldr: AERIS is an edge deployment framework for aerial robots that uses dedicated small language models with lightweight perception/control modules, dynamically reassigns roles across executors, and employs attention-subgoal alignment for long-horizon task decomposition, achieving real-time closed-loop operation under heartbeat-timed execution.
AI.motivation: To enable practical deployment of LLM-integrated robotic systems on aerial platforms despite tight heartbeat-constrained scheduling and limited computational power at the edge.
AI.method: AERIS organizes small language models and lightweight modules into runtime-instantiated roles, dynamically rebinds them across executors as resources change, and uses an attention-subgoal alignment mechanism (annotating the active instruction step in messages) for long-horizon instruction decomposition.
AI.result: Maintains stable perception-decision-control loop between low-frequency planner and high-frequency controller under heartbeat-timed execution on a UAV VLN benchmark; validated via two real-world planning and fast-response experiments.
AI.conclusion: AERIS effectively pushes intelligent capabilities to the edge for aerial robotic systems, enabling real-time closed-loop operation despite resource constraints.

title: PCGD: Physics-Guided Conditional Graph Diffusion for TCAD Device Simulation
summary: Technology computer-aided design (TCAD) semiconductor device simulation is fundamentally constrained by the high computational cost of iteratively solving coupled drift-diffusion equations. Existing ML surrogates either reduce internal physics to macroscopic scalar regressions, or rely on single-step mappings that lack the iterative refinement required to resolve stiff, coupled fields. To address this, we introduce PCGD, a Physics-Guided Conditional Graph Diffusion framework operating natively on unstructured TCAD meshes to predict coupled electrostatic and carrier density fields. PCGD employs a Condition-Aware MeshGraphNet denoiser that explicitly injects boundary conditions and device structure context via global cross-attention. By augmenting data-driven denoising with a physics-guided hybrid objective that integrates exponent-free quasi-Fermi gradient matching with noise-aware PDE residuals, PCGD progressively enforce physical constraints in the iterative diffusion trajectory. This strategy successfully bypasses the numerical instabilities typical of stiff drift-diffusion equations. Evaluated on a challenging mixed PN/MOS benchmark, PCGD significantly outperforms deterministic one-step regression (1.207% error) and local diffusion (1.585% error) baselines by achieving a sub-percent mean relative field error of 0.835%, while concurrently reducing maximum PDE residual errors by nearly three orders of magnitude compared to pure diffusion. It also transfers robustly to unseen SOI topologies (0.815% error) via LoRA adaptation, using 5.30$\times$ less data and 14.34$\times$ fewer parameters than full fine-tuning. Ultimately, PCGD bridges the computational efficiency of generative surrogates with the rigorous physical fidelity of traditional TCAD, unlocking highly scalable, field-level analysis for robust device engineering.

AI.tldr: PCGD is a physics-guided conditional graph diffusion framework for TCAD semiconductor simulation that predicts coupled electrostatic and carrier density fields on unstructured meshes, achieving sub-percent error (0.835%) while reducing PDE residuals by nearly three orders of magnitude compared to pure diffusion baselines, and transferring robustly to unseen topologies via LoRA with 14.34× fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: TCAD semiconductor device simulation suffers from high computational cost due to iteratively solving coupled drift-diffusion equations. Existing ML surrogates either oversimplify physics to scalar regressions or lack iterative refinement for stiff, coupled fields.
AI.method: PCGD uses a Condition-Aware MeshGraphNet denoiser with global cross-attention for boundary/device context, and a physics-guided hybrid objective combining exponent-free quasi-Fermi gradient matching with noise-aware PDE residuals to enforce physical constraints during the diffusion trajectory.
AI.result: PCGD achieves 0.835% mean relative field error (vs. 1.207% for one-step regression, 1.585% for local diffusion), reduces maximum PDE residual errors by nearly three orders of magnitude, and transfers to unseen SOI topologies with 0.815% error using 5.30× less data and 14.34× fewer parameters via LoRA.
AI.conclusion: PCGD bridges computational efficiency of generative surrogates with physical fidelity of traditional TCAD, enabling scalable, field-level analysis for robust device engineering.

title: Flow Matching in Feature Space for Stochastic World Modeling
summary:
World modeling requires forecasting uncertain futures while preserving information useful for
  downstream perception. Existing visual world models often struggle to satisfy both goals:
  VAE-based stochastic models operate in low-dimensional reconstruction latents, which can
  limit perception performance, while deterministic predictors using strong pretrained features
  collapse multimodal futures into a single blurry mean. In this work, we propose FlowWM, a
  stochastic world model that performs flow matching directly within pretrained feature space
  (e.g., DINOv3). This is challenging because pretrained features are substantially
  high-dimensional, making standard diffusion recipes suboptimal. To address this, we
  investigate the design choices needed for feature-space flow matching and introduce a
  differentiable one-step projection mechanism that enables efficient training with temporal
  consistency and task-driven objectives. We evaluate FlowWM on two benchmarks: a synthetic
  benchmark for systematic evaluation of accuracy and diversity, and a real-world benchmark
  FuturePerception. FlowWM improves perception performance, mode coverage, and horizon
  robustness, validating our proposed design for stochastic world modeling in high-dimensional
  feature spaces.

AI.tldr: FlowWM is a stochastic world model that performs flow matching directly in high-dimensional pretrained feature spaces (e.g., DINOv3) to jointly achieve accurate future prediction and strong downstream perception performance, outperforming prior VAE-based or deterministic approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing visual world models face a trade-off: VAE-based stochastic models use low-dimensional reconstruction latents limiting perception, while deterministic predictors using pretrained features collapse multimodal futures into a single blurry mean. The goal is to achieve both uncertainty-aware forecasting and preserved perception utility.
AI.method: FlowWM performs flow matching directly within high-dimensional pretrained feature spaces (e.g., DINOv3). To handle the challenge of high-dimensionality, it introduces a differentiable one-step projection mechanism, enabling efficient training with temporal consistency and task-driven objectives.
AI.result: Evaluated on a synthetic benchmark and the real-world FuturePerception benchmark, FlowWM improves perception performance, mode coverage, and horizon robustness compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: FlowWM validates that flow matching in high-dimensional pretrained feature spaces is an effective design for stochastic world modeling, overcoming the accuracy-diversity trade-off faced by prior approaches.

title: First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks
summary: Most of the existing neuro-symbolic AI methods focus on the scenario of static knowledge where objects do not change according to a temporal dimension. Temporal neuro-symbolic works are still under explored and are mainly developed for time-interval logic or propositional linear temporal logic. There is a lack of models studying linear temporal logics with predicates that deal with objects whose properties and relations change through the time. We present First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks (FOT-LTN) that is an extension of Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) that fills this gap by considering a linear-temporal dimension. In particular, FOT-LTN joins the syntax of First-Order Linear Temporal Logic with the fuzzy (and real-valued) semantics of LTN obtaining a framework that supports both temporal operators and quantifiers and is totally differentiable. A first evaluation regards a temporal knowledge graph completion task on two synthetic datasets showing better performance of FOT-LTN with respect to dedicated (purely neural) methods.

AI.tldr: FOT-LTN extends Logic Tensor Networks to handle first-order linear temporal logic with predicates, enabling temporal reasoning about changing objects while remaining fully differentiable. It outperforms purely neural methods on temporal knowledge graph completion tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic AI methods primarily focus on static knowledge without temporal dimensions. Temporal neuro-symbolic approaches are under-explored and limited to time-interval logic or propositional linear temporal logic, lacking models that handle predicates whose properties and relations change over time.
AI.method: FOT-LTN extends Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) by incorporating a linear-temporal dimension. It combines First-Order Linear Temporal Logic syntax with fuzzy, real-valued semantics from LTN, supporting both temporal operators and quantifiers while maintaining differentiability.
AI.result: Evaluation on temporal knowledge graph completion tasks using two synthetic datasets shows FOT-LTN achieves better performance compared to dedicated purely neural methods.
AI.conclusion: FOT-LTN successfully fills the gap in temporal neuro-symbolic AI by providing a differentiable framework for first-order linear temporal logic with changing predicates, demonstrating superior performance on temporal reasoning tasks.

title: Self-supervised Geometry Reasoning for LiDAR Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
summary: LiDAR simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) relies on local geometric quantities such as covariances, correspondences, and surface structures. However, most existing pipelines rely on hand-crafted estimates of local geometry and use them as fixed inputs to LiDAR SLAM, which can make the estimated local geometry noisy and unstable in sparse regions of a point cloud or when using low-resolution LiDAR. To address this issue, this paper introduces a self-supervised framework that learns an explicit symbolic representation of local geometry and uses it to improve LiDAR SLAM recursively. Specifically, each point is represented as a Gaussian distribution, allowing local geometry to be described by a covariance. Without dense geometry labels or ground-truth poses, the framework learns by maximizing the likelihood of local geometry, with self-supervision derived from consistency relations over symbolic geometric representations, including predicted covariances, correspondences, and trajectory from SLAM. The learned geometry is then fed back into LiDAR SLAM, forming a reciprocal loop in which improved geometry enhances localization and mapping, and improved localization provides cleaner supervision for subsequent geometry reasoning. This framework is backend-agnostic and can be plugged into existing LiDAR SLAM pipelines without architectural changes. Experiments on KITTI under varying LiDAR resolutions show that the proposed method improves both odometry and global registration.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a self-supervised framework that learns explicit symbolic representations of local geometry (as Gaussian distributions/covariances) for LiDAR SLAM, using consistency relations over covariances, correspondences, and trajectory for supervision, forming a reciprocal loop that improves both geometry estimation and SLAM performance iteratively.
AI.motivation: Existing LiDAR SLAM pipelines rely on hand-crafted estimates of local geometry (covariances, correspondences, surface structures), which are noisy and unstable in sparse point cloud regions or with low-resolution LiDAR.
AI.method: The framework represents each point as a Gaussian distribution, learns local geometry (covariances) via self-supervised likelihood maximization using consistency relations over predicted covariances, correspondences, and SLAM trajectory, then feeds learned geometry back into LiDAR SLAM in a reciprocal loop.
AI.result: Experiments on KITTI under varying LiDAR resolutions show improvements in both odometry and global registration. The method is backend-agnostic and pluggable into existing pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised learning of local geometry representations can effectively replace hand-crafted estimates in LiDAR SLAM, improving robustness in sparse/low-resolution conditions without requiring dense labels or ground-truth poses.

title: Adaptive Block Diffusion: Resolving Training-Inference Mismatch in Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) are typically trained under fixed context structures, restricting denoising to predetermined token subsets. This creates a mismatch between training and inference, where models must operate over arbitrary configurations, leading to degradation off the training grid. We propose Adaptive Block Diffusion (ABD), which resolves this mismatch by optimizing denoising risk over a distribution of prefix-window configurations. By treating the configuration as a stochastic variable, ABD trains a single model over the full configuration space without architectural changes. We show that generalization across decoding strategies is governed by the support of the training distribution, and that ABD guarantees denoising optimality for any inference policy whose configurations are covered during training. Empirically, ABD exhibits structural invariance across decoding scales, avoiding off-grid collapse and recovering a monotonic relationship between block size and perplexity, while matching or outperforming fixed-block specialists at their target scales.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Block Diffusion (ABD) trains diffusion language models over variable prefix-window configurations, resolving the mismatch between fixed training and arbitrary inference setups, ensuring denoising optimality across decoding strategies.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Language Models suffer from a mismatch between training (fixed context structures) and inference (arbitrary configurations), causing performance degradation off the training grid.
AI.method: ABD treats the prefix-window configuration as a stochastic variable and optimizes denoising risk over a distribution of configurations, training a single model over the full configuration space without architectural changes.
AI.result: ABD exhibits structural invariance across decoding scales, avoids off-grid collapse, recovers monotonic relationship between block size and perplexity, and matches/outperforms fixed-block specialists at target scales.
AI.conclusion: Generalization across decoding strategies depends on the support of the training distribution; ABD guarantees denoising optimality for any inference policy covered during training.

title: From Fog Chamber to Aircraft Window: Pixel-Registered Imaging and Synthetic Fine-Tuning Enable Cross-Domain Defogging
summary: A deep defogging pipeline pretrained on controlled laboratory fog and fine-tuned with domain-randomized synthetic fog applied to clear outdoor scenes generalizes across a graded sequence of out-of-distribution settings with no target-domain training, from chamber-free free-flowing fog to iPhone video recorded through an aircraft cabin window in flight, an entirely unseen sensor, scene, and optical path. This directly addresses an open transfer limitation reported for real-world binocular defogging. Two design choices support the transfer. First, a single-camera fog imager photographs a flat-panel display through an artificial-fog enclosure with a fixed 114~mm scattering path, producing 5{,}495 pixel-aligned foggy/clear pairs. Exact registration permits a paired Laplacian ratio that predicts per-image restoration quality far better than single-image proxies (Spearman $ρ= 0.632$ versus $0.399$) and supports pixel-exact $L_1$ reconstruction training that avoids adversarial hallucination. Second, the fog-chamber checkpoint is fine-tuned on Mapillary Vistas crops overlaid with on-the-fly randomized synthetic fog spanning a broad range of strengths, spatial variations, airlights, and noise conditions. On a 552-image held-out split, a uniform comparison of 30 restoration backbones places NAFNet at the top (24.33~dB~/~0.7912~SSIM), with a compact alternative within 1.29~dB at 3\% of the parameter count, and a ResNet-50 classifier confirms that the restoration preserves semantic content rather than only pixel-level structure. On unpaired aircraft-window video, NIQE decreases from a mean of 6.22 to 4.97 after fine-tuning, with temporally stable output across full-motion sequences. The same backbone, under paired supervision, also reaches 20.71~dB~/~0.683~SSIM on a non-overlapping O-HAZE/NH-HAZE split (a transferability check rather than a competitive ranking).

AI.tldr: A deep defogging pipeline trained on controlled lab fog and fine-tuned with domain-randomized synthetic fog generalizes to unseen real-world settings (including aircraft window video), addressing a known transfer limitation in binocular defogging.
AI.motivation: To overcome the open transfer limitation in real-world binocular defogging, where models fail to generalize to unseen sensors, scenes, and optical paths without target-domain training.
AI.method: (1) A single-camera fog imager captures 5,495 pixel-aligned foggy/clear pairs through a fixed 114mm scattering path. (2) Fine-tuning with on-the-fly domain-randomized synthetic fog on Mapillary Vistas (varying strength, spatial variation, airlight, noise). (3) Uses paired Laplacian ratio for quality prediction and pixel-exact L1 reconstruction to avoid adversarial hallucination.
AI.result: NAFNet achieves 24.33dB/0.7912 SSIM (top among 30 backbones). A compact model performs within 1.29dB at 3% params. On aircraft-window video, NIQE drops from 6.22 to 4.97. On O-HAZE/NH-HAZE: 20.71dB/0.683 SSIM. Spearman ρ=0.632 for paired Laplacian ratio vs 0.399 for single-image proxies.
