We study the measurement properties of the benchmark through judge calibration and cross-family inter-judge agreement. We also illustrate EMPATH on three frontier models, one of them open-weight. Aggregate scores sit within 0.74 points of one another, but per-metric profiles diverge by up to six points in model-specific places. Under the standard rubric, both the ranking and the weak spots are stable across a second, cross-family judge: 93% of scores fall within plus or minus 1. A five-run test-retest adds a second axis: even the steadiest model swings from 2 to 10 on a crisis metric across identical re-runs, and deepseek-v4-pro returns a different conversation on every run even at temperature 0. Run-to-run reliability is therefore a per-model safety property, not noise to average away. EMPATH is system-agnostic; the pipeline, seeds, personas, and rubrics are released for reuse.

AI.tldr: EMPATH is a benchmark for evaluating emotional-support chatbot safety across multilingual, multi-turn crisis conversations. It uses an auditor to generate conversations and a calibrated judge to score them on 19 metrics. Key findings include score inflation under standard rubrics, per-model safety variation, and significant run-to-run instability even at temperature zero.
AI.motivation: Existing safety benchmarks fix prompts, languages, and turn structures, which hides safety failures in emotional-support chatbots—especially across multilingual, multi-turn crisis scenarios. The authors argue that these failures are precisely where safety matters most.
AI.method: EMPATH uses an auditor model role-playing help-seekers to generate multi-turn conversations from 140 seed instructions and 34 personas. A judge model (from a different family) scores transcripts against 19 metrics across five dimensions. The judge is calibrated rather than trusted, and a strict per-criterion rubric is used to correct score inflation.
AI.result: Score inflation was found on 10 of 19 metrics under standard rubrics. Aggregate scores across three frontier models are within 0.74 points, but per-metric profiles diverge by up to 6 points. 93% of scores show ±1 agreement across judges. Run-to-run reliability varies dramatically—even the steadiest model swings 2-10 on a crisis metric across identical re-runs.
AI.conclusion: Run-to-run reliability is a per-model safety property, not noise to average away. The benchmark is system-agnostic and released publicly. Safety evaluation of emotional-support chatbots requires calibrated judges, fine-grained metrics, and awareness of temporal instability.

title: Do Models Read What They Write? Causal Registers in Scratchpad Reasoning
summary: A central hope behind process supervision is that models can expose intermediate variables that matter for their later behavior. For this to help with alignment, a scratchpad must be tied to the computation: when the model writes a state, later steps should compute from that state. To test this requirement, we use a controlled state-tracking task with a known update rule, comparing models trained to report only the final state with models trained to write intermediate states before giving the final answer. At evaluation, we edit the internal representation of one written state while leaving the visible scratchpad text fixed. Because the transition rule is known, the edit has a single correct downstream consequence. In Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, the state-writing model predicts the next phase bit implied by the edited state on 80% and 91% of held-out examples across the two task variants, while pretrained and final-answer-only controls remain near baseline. Additional controls rule out generic next-token steering and copying another continuation: the prediction depends on both the edited state and the current move. The same causal-use pattern replicates across model families. Together, these results suggest a sharper goal for scratchpad oversight: not just to make intermediate reasoning legible, but to train written states that the model uses as part of its computation.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether models actually use intermediate scratchpad states as part of their computation. By editing internal representations of written states in a controlled task, they find that state-writing models do causally depend on these states for downstream predictions, while pretrained and final-answer-only models do not. This suggests alignment efforts should focus on training models to *use* written states, not just to make reasoning legible.
AI.motivation: Process supervision for alignment relies on the assumption that intermediate reasoning variables matter for a model's later behavior. The authors want to test whether scratchpad states are causally tied to computation (i.e., later steps compute from those states) rather than being mere epiphenomenal artifacts.
AI.method: Uses a controlled state-tracking task with a known update rule. Compares models trained to report only the final state vs. models trained to write intermediate states. At evaluation, the internal representation of one written state is edited while the visible scratchpad text is fixed, and the model's downstream prediction is checked against the single correct consequence implied by the edited state.
AI.result: In Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, the state-writing model predicts the next phase bit implied by the edited state on 80% and 91% of held-out examples across two task variants. Pretrained and final-answer-only controls remain near baseline. Controls rule out generic next-token steering and copying. The pattern replicates across model families.
AI.conclusion: Scratchpad oversight for alignment should aim not just to make intermediate reasoning legible, but to train written states that the model actually uses as part of its computation.

title: D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR: Degradation-Disentangled Representation for Real-World Omnidirectional Image Super-Resolution
summary: With the growing demand for immersive visual experiences, high-quality omnidirectional images (ODIs) have become increasingly important. However, limitations in imaging devices and transmission bandwidth often lead to low-resolution ODIs, hindering the rendering of fine-grained 360° details, especially in the presence of real-world degradations and geometric distortions. Existing real-world super-resolution (Real-SR) methods are inadequate for ODIs, as their degradation models fail to account for the complex imaging pipeline involving fisheye capture and Equirectangular Projection (ERP), introducing severe aliasing and projection-specific distortions. To address these challenges, we propose D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR, a Degradation-Disentangled Representation framework for Real-world Omnidirectional image Super-Resolution. D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR explicitly models degradations arising from both fisheye imaging and ERP projection, guided by two key insights: (1) projection priors play a critical role in shaping real-world degradations, and (2) human perception in immersive environments is inherently viewpoint-centric. Accordingly, we introduce a Perspective Projection Representation (PPR) operating alongside the ERP branch to capture viewpoint-aware features, together with a Degradation-Specific Module (DSM) that jointly models ERP-induced geometric distortions and PPR-specific real-world degradations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D$^{2}$R$^{2}$OSR achieves state-of-the-art performance and produces visually compelling, high-fidelity omnidirectional Real-SR results while maintaining favorable computational efficiency for low-resource deployment.

AI.tldr: D²R²OSR is a novel framework for real-world omnidirectional image super-resolution that explicitly models degradations from both fisheye imaging and Equirectangular Projection (ERP), using a viewpoint-aware Perspective Projection Representation branch and a Degradation-Specific Module to achieve state-of-the-art performance with high computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing real-world super-resolution methods fail for omnidirectional images because they don't account for the complex imaging pipeline involving fisheye capture and ERP projection, which introduces severe aliasing and projection-specific distortions.
AI.method: Proposes D²R²OSR with: (1) a Perspective Projection Representation (PPR) branch alongside the ERP branch for viewpoint-aware features, and (2) a Degradation-Specific Module (DSM) that jointly models ERP-induced geometric distortions and PPR-specific real-world degradations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on real-world omnidirectional image super-resolution, producing visually compelling high-fidelity results while maintaining favorable computational efficiency for low-resource deployment.
AI.conclusion: The degradation-disentangled representation approach, guided by projection priors and viewpoint-centric perception, effectively addresses the unique challenges of omnidirectional Real-SR under complex real-world degradations.

title: PromptGNN-sim: Deep Fusion and Alignment of GNN and LLMs for Text-Attributed Graph Learning
summary: Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) combine textual semantics with graph structure and are central to many graph learning tasks. However, existing fusion methods often treat text and structure as separate inputs in a shallow, one-way pipeline, which limits deep interaction between modalities and weakens performance under sparse connectivity or cross-graph generalisation. To address this issue, we propose PromptGNN-sim, a bi-directional structure-semantic fusion framework for collaborative GNN-LLM learning. PromptGNN-sim uses a Graph Attention Network (GAT) for semantically aware neighborhood selection by combining structural attention with textual similarity. The selected structural context is then used to generate structure-aware prompts for an LLM, including the target node summary, label categories, and representative keywords from similar neighbors. During training, bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention are introduced to jointly optimize the GNN and LLM components. Experiments on six public datasets, including Cora, Pubmed, and WikiCS, evaluate accuracy, generalisation, and robustness under cross-task transfer, cross-dataset generalisation, and sparse perturbations. Results show that PromptGNN-sim outperforms classical GNNs, LLMs, and recent GNN-LLM fusion methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of interactive structure-semantic collaboration for text-attributed graph learning.

AI.tldr: PromptGNN-sim proposes a bi-directional structure-semantic fusion framework using GAT for semantically aware neighborhood selection and structure-aware prompts for LLM, with cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention for joint optimization, outperforming existing methods on TAG benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing fusion methods for Text-Attributed Graphs treat text and structure as separate inputs in a shallow, one-way pipeline, limiting deep interaction between modalities and weakening performance under sparse connectivity or cross-graph generalization.
AI.method: PromptGNN-sim uses a GAT for semantically aware neighborhood selection (combining structural attention with textual similarity), generates structure-aware prompts for LLM (target node summary, label categories, representative keywords), and employs bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning with cross-attention to jointly optimize GNN and LLM components.
AI.result: Outperforms classical GNNs, LLMs, and recent GNN-LLM fusion methods on six public datasets (Cora, Pubmed, WikiCS, etc.) in accuracy, generalisation, and robustness under cross-task transfer, cross-dataset generalisation, and sparse perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Interactive structure-semantic collaboration is effective for text-attributed graph learning, and PromptGNN-sim demonstrates the value of bi-directional fusion between GNN and LLM.

title: Event-VLA: Action-Conditioned Event Fusion for Robust Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have become an important paradigm of embodied AI. However, existing VLA models typically assume well-lit and stable indoor settings, while real-world embodied manipulation may involve degraded RGB observations caused by illumination shifts, posing critical challenges for robust robotic manipulation. To address this gap, we propose \textbf{Event-VLA}, an event-enhanced VLA framework for generalizable manipulation across varying illumination conditions. We formulate VLA-based manipulation under degraded visibility as a practical robustness problem for RGB-centric policies, and introduce event streams as an illumination-robust, motion-sensitive complementary observation to improve robustness across visibility levels. Specifically, unlike conventional multimodal fusion that directly merges event features into the global semantic token space, Event-VLA injects event information through an action-query routing pathway. It uses learnable action queries to extract task-relevant semantics from the VLA reasoning process, and selectively aggregates event tokens via gated cross-attention to construct event-aware action representations. This design preserves the pretrained RGB-language semantic priors while effectively leveraging event information for robust action prediction. Experiments in simulation and real-world deployment show that Event-VLA maintains strong manipulation performance under normal lighting and improves success rates under low-light degradation and near-dark real-world settings.

AI.tldr: Event-VLA is an event-enhanced VLA framework that uses event streams (illumination-robust, motion-sensitive) to improve robotic manipulation robustness under degraded lighting conditions, via an action-query routing pathway with gated cross-attention that preserves pretrained RGB-language priors.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models assume well-lit, stable indoor settings, but real-world manipulation encounters illumination shifts (e.g., low-light, near-dark) that degrade RGB observations, posing robustness challenges.
AI.method: Event-VLA formulates degraded-visibility manipulation as a robustness problem. It injects event streams (illumination-robust, motion-sensitive) through an action-query routing pathway: learnable action queries extract task-relevant semantics from VLA reasoning, and selectively aggregate event tokens via gated cross-attention to build event-aware action representations, preserving RGB-language priors.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments show Event-VLA maintains strong manipulation performance under normal lighting and improves success rates under low-light degradation and near-dark settings.
AI.conclusion: Event-VLA demonstrates that event streams can effectively complement RGB inputs for robust VLA-based manipulation across varying illumination conditions, with minimal disruption to pretrained semantic priors.

title: The Mirage of Optimizing Training Policies: Monotonic Inference Policies as the Real Objective for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has gained growing attention in large language model (LLM) post-training, yet RL training remains fragile and can suffer from instability or collapse. One vital cause is training-inference mismatch: LLM adopts separate inference and training engines for generation efficiency and training precision, which in practice exhibits inconsistent probabilities for the same trajectories on training and inference sides, even with synchronized model parameters. This naturally induces a special type of off-policyness ever existing and poisoning the training. Prior works have made various efforts in addressing the off-policyness to stabilize the training policies under the mismatch. In this paper, we point out the objective misalignment neglected by existing works that an effective update to the policy in the training engine not necessarily ensures the improvement of the inference policy, i.e., the one used in deployment. To this end, we propose a new policy optimization objective for LLM RL, named Monotonic Inference Policy Improvement (MIPI). Following this principle, we introduce Monotonic Inference Policy Update (MIPU), a two-step LLM RL framework that constructs sampler-referenced candidate updates and selectively accepts synchronized candidates using an inference-side gap proxy. Experiments conducted on two model scales under high mismatch show that MIPU improves average reasoning performance and training stability.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies training-inference mismatch in LLM RL due to separate engines, causing off-policyness and instability. The authors propose MIPI (Monotonic Inference Policy Improvement) objective and MIPU framework to ensure policy updates in the training engine actually improve the inference (deployment) policy, improving reasoning performance and training stability.
AI.motivation: RL training for LLMs suffers from instability caused by training-inference mismatch (separate engines with inconsistent probabilities for same trajectories). Existing off-policyness solutions fail to address the objective misalignment—an effective update to the training engine policy does not guarantee improvement in the inference/deployment policy.
AI.method: Proposes MIPI (Monotonic Inference Policy Improvement) objective, and introduces MIPU (Monotonic Inference Policy Update), a two-step LLM RL framework: (1) constructs sampler-referenced candidate updates, (2) selectively accepts synchronized candidates using an inference-side gap proxy.
AI.result: Experiments at two model scales under high mismatch conditions show MIPU improves average reasoning performance and training stability.
AI.conclusion: The MIPI/MIPU approach effectively addresses the objective misalignment between training and inference policies in LLM RL, leading to more stable and better-performing training under engine mismatch conditions.

title: FDM-MFVT: Few-step Sampling Diffusion Model for Mask-Free Virtual Try-On
summary: Image-based Virtual Try-On (IVTON) has greatly advanced through diffusion models, yet existing methods require many sampling steps and depend on masks with costly auxiliary networks. In addition, the absence of large-scale mask-free paired datasets further limits the development of mask-free IVTON. We propose FDM-MFVT, a few-step diffusion model for mask-free IVTON, integrating an Outfit-aware Noise Optimization Module (OANO) and an Instruction-driven Try-on Module (IDT) to enhance efficiency and flexibility.The OANO module initializes the alignment space with noise using the input image and only needs 6 steps to generate a higher-fidelity try-on image compared to 30 steps.The IDT module uses virtual try-on prompts and efficient adaptation to generate high-quality results from garment and person images alone. We further introduce MFVT, a 30,000-pair mask-free IVTON dataset. Experiments show that FDM-MFVT achieves superior quantitative and qualitative results with fewer inference steps than mask-based and mask-free baseline methods.

AI.tldr: FDM-MFVT is a few-step diffusion model for mask-free virtual try-on that uses only 6 inference steps (vs. 30 in prior work), integrates two novel modules (OANO and IDT), and introduces a new 30K-pair mask-free dataset (MFVT), achieving superior results over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing IVTON methods rely on many sampling steps, costly masks, and auxiliary networks, and lack large-scale mask-free paired datasets, which limits efficiency and scalability.
AI.method: Proposes FDM-MFVT with two modules: (1) Outfit-aware Noise Optimization Module (OANO) for fast 6-step alignment, and (2) Instruction-driven Try-on Module (IDT) using prompts and efficient adaptation. Also introduces MFVT, a 30K-pair mask-free dataset.
AI.result: FDM-MFVT achieves superior quantitative and qualitative results compared to both mask-based and mask-free baselines, while requiring far fewer inference steps (6 vs. 30).
AI.conclusion: Mask-free few-step diffusion is viable and effective for virtual try-on; the proposed method and dataset advance the field by improving efficiency and removing mask dependencies.

title: Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering
comment: Preprint. 4 figures, 1 algorithm, 5 tables. Systems paper with a preliminary six-session case study on four deployed applications; full benchmark protocol proposed, corpus run to appear in a later revision
summary: Live product demonstrations are a recurring, high-cost activity in software organizations: a human presenter must select features, dispatch the corresponding interactions on a running application, narrate them coherently, and answer questions in real time. Existing automation addresses only fragments -- generalist browser agents target instruction-conditioned task completion, and demo-video tools produce fixed MP4 artifacts that cannot be questioned and silently break under interface drift. We propose Rhetor, a multi-agent system that takes a running web application and its source-code repository as input and produces a rehearsed live demonstration with segment-synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. The architectural contributions are a cross-modal feature representation that merges UI exploration with source-code analysis into features tagged with discrete focus tiers, a grounded scripter constrained to UI elements observed during exploration and dispatched through multi-strategy semantic locators, a pre-presentation rehearsal loop with explicit convergence and graceful degradation to narration-only segments, and a runtime synchronization invariant that ties each browser action to the audio-end event of its narration segment. Across six pipeline sessions on four deployed applications -- including the public-domain whiteboard application Excalidraw -- the rehearser's internal locator-firing rate (sigma-bar) spans 0.31-1.00 over 147 scripted actions; on the substantial workload (53 actions, full tier differentiation), sigma-bar is approximately 0.92, and on the public-domain reference point the locator-repair step drives convergence to sigma-bar = 1.00 at iteration 2. We additionally define a benchmark protocol of ten metrics across six application categories that would establish, beyond the case study, whether each design choice contributes positively.

AI.tldr: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that automates live software demonstrations by combining code analysis with UI exploration to produce narrated, queryable demos that gracefully degrade under failures, with strong locator accuracy (sigma-bar ~0.92) on complex workflows.
AI.motivation: Live demos in software organizations are expensive and repetitive; existing tools only handle fragments (task-completion agents or static MP4 videos), and static videos cannot be questioned and break under UI changes.
AI.method: Rhetor introduces: (1) a cross-modal feature representation merging UI exploration with source-code analysis tagged by focus tiers; (2) a grounded scripter using multi-strategy semantic locators; (3) a rehearsal loop with convergence detection and graceful degradation to narration-only segments; (4) a runtime synchronization invariant linking browser actions to audio-end events.
AI.result: Across 6 sessions on 4 apps (including Excalidraw), locator firing rate spans 0.31-1.00 over 147 actions; on the full 53-action differentiated workload sigma-bar ≈ 0.92; locator-repair achieves sigma-bar = 1.00 at iteration 2 on the public-domain reference.
AI.conclusion: Rhetor demonstrates feasibility of end-to-end automated live demos; authors propose a 10-metric benchmark across 6 app categories for future rigorous validation.

title: VISTA-DZ: Visual Semantic Trajectory Adaptation for Personalized Dilemma Zone Prediction
comment: This manuscript is currently under review
summary: Driver decision making in the dilemma zone at signalized intersections is safety critical, as vehicles approaching a yellow signal must decide whether to stop or proceed within limited time and distance margins. Accurate prediction of both stop-go decisions and decision timing is important for adaptive signal control, advanced driver assistance systems, and human-centered intelligent transportation applications. However, dilemma zone behavior is strongly driver dependent. Similar approach trajectories may lead to different decisions across drivers because of differences in risk preference, braking habit, and decision threshold. Existing personalized models often rely on handcrafted scalar descriptors, which provide useful but limited summaries of individual behavior. This paper proposes VISTA-DZ, a semantic-profile-conditioned framework for personalized stop-go and decision-time prediction. Historical trajectories are converted into visual representations, interpreted by a vision-language model to generate behavioral profiles, and encoded as semantic embeddings to condition a dual-output prediction network. The final model combines a bidirectional GRU encoder, driver-conditioned multi-head cross-attention, and Feature-wise Linear Modulation for temporal evidence selection and feature adaptation. Experiments on the SDZ dataset and a newly collected FDZ dataset show that VISTA-DZ outperforms trajectory-only and handcrafted personalization baselines, achieving 93.26% in-domain simulation accuracy and 90.22% mean accuracy across 20 held-out simulation drivers. Cross-domain results further show feasible zero-shot simulation-to-real transfer and better real-world generalization when simulation data are combined with limited field data.

AI.tldr: VISTA-DZ uses vision-language models to convert historical driving trajectories into behavioral profiles for personalized stop-go and decision-time prediction at signalized intersections, outperforming traditional methods with 93.26% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Dilemma zone decision making is safety-critical but highly driver-dependent; existing personalized models rely on handcrafted scalar descriptors that provide limited summaries of individual behavior.
AI.method: Converts historical trajectories into visual representations, uses a vision-language model to generate behavioral profiles, then encodes them as semantic embeddings to condition a dual-output prediction network combining bidirectional GRU, driver-conditioned multi-head cross-attention, and Feature-wise Linear Modulation.
AI.result: 93.26% in-domain simulation accuracy, 90.22% mean accuracy across 20 held-out simulation drivers, and feasible zero-shot simulation-to-real transfer with better real-world generalization.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-profile-conditioned personalization significantly improves dilemma zone behavior prediction over trajectory-only and handcrafted baselines, with promising cross-domain transfer capability.

title: RAGA: Real Time Ray Traced Gaussian Shadow Casting for 3DGS Avatar-Scene Interaction
summary: We study the problem of physically plausible shadow casting when animating 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) avatars, either individually or in multi-avatar and object-interaction scenarios, within existing 3DGS scenes. In contrast to prior methods that rely on binary hit tests and mesh-based shadow casters, our method performs shadow computation entirely in Gaussian space, without requiring any mesh reconstruction. We introduce RAGA, a Ray-Traced Gaussian Shadow Casting formulation based on exact ray-Gaussian line integrals. For each occluding Gaussian, we integrate the opacity profile along the shadow ray and normalize by the theoretical maximum integral, producing a weight that captures how the ray traverses the occluder rather than merely whether an intersection occurred. To reduce temporal variance from clothing deformations in animated avatars, we further introduce an avatar proxy representation that stabilizes shadow casting while preserving visual fidelity. We implement RAGA using custom CUDA kernels integrated with the NVIDIA OptiX framework; as such, our shadow tracer runs at rates of about 50 FPS. We evaluate on single-avatar, multi-avatar, and avatar-object interaction scenarios across multiple datasets, demonstrating substantially improved shadow realism, temporal stability, and scene coherence. Our project page is available at https://miraymen.github.io/raga/.

AI.tldr: RAGA is a ray-traced shadow casting method for 3D Gaussian Splatting avatars that computes physically plausible shadows entirely in Gaussian space using exact ray-Gaussian line integrals, achieving ~50 FPS with improved realism and temporal stability across single/multi-avatar and object interaction scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on binary hit tests and mesh-based shadow casters, which are incompatible with the pure Gaussian representation of 3DGS avatars. The authors aim to achieve physically plausible shadow casting directly in Gaussian space without mesh reconstruction, supporting avatars with deformable clothing and multi-avatar/object interactions.
AI.method: (1) Exact ray-Gaussian line integral formulation that integrates opacity along shadow rays and normalizes by the theoretical maximum, producing continuous occlusion weights. (2) An avatar proxy representation to stabilize shadow casting against temporal clothing deformation variance. (3) Custom CUDA kernels integrated with NVIDIA OptiX for real-time (~50 FPS) ray tracing.
AI.result: The method achieves substantially improved shadow realism, temporal stability, and scene coherence across single-avatar, multi-avatar, and avatar-object interaction scenarios on multiple datasets, running at about 50 FPS.
AI.conclusion: RAGA demonstrates that high-quality, physically plausible shadow casting for 3DGS avatars can be achieved entirely in Gaussian space without mesh reconstruction, with real-time performance and robustness to deformable clothing dynamics.

title: ManimAgent: Self-Evolving Multimodal Agents for Visual Education
summary: Multi-round reflection lets agents built on large language models recover from failures within a single task, but each task remains an isolated episode: lessons learned across many reflection rounds on one task are discarded before the next begins. We study this gap on a code-generation task: from a scientific paper section, the agent writes Python in the open-source Manim library to render a mathematical animation. We present ManimAgent, a self-evolving multimodal agent that carries reflection experience across tasks through a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank grown entirely from its own task stream, with no weight updates and no human seeds. After each animation converges, a vision-language model scores the rendered keyframes; the resulting signals populate a positive channel M+ that stores success rationales as soft Reference Examples, and a negative channel M- that stores validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls. On a fixed-probe evaluation against no-memory, matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and shuffled-memory baselines, blind human Pass@1 rises and reflection rounds fall as memory size grows. We will release the code, frozen memory snapshots, and the task stream.

AI.tldr: ManimAgent is a self-evolving multimodal agent that uses a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank to carry reflection experience across code-generation tasks, improving Pass@1 rates while reducing reflection rounds as memory grows.
AI.motivation: Multi-round reflection helps agents recover within a single task, but knowledge gained across reflections is discarded between tasks, preventing continuous improvement.
AI.method: ManimAgent uses a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank with a positive channel (M+) storing success rationales as Reference Examples and a negative channel (M-) storing validated failure patterns as Known Pitfalls, grown entirely from its own task stream without weight updates or human seeds.
AI.result: Blind human Pass@1 increases and reflection rounds decrease as memory size grows, outperforming no-memory, matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and shuffled-memory baselines.
AI.conclusion: Carrying reflection experience across tasks via structured episodic memory improves agent performance on code-generation for mathematical animations.

title: MF-UAVPose6D: A Model-Free Monocular 6-DoF Pose Estimation Framework for Fixed-Wing UAVs
summary: For uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), estimating six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) poses is essential for airspace situational awareness, target tracking, and counter-UAV operations. However, non-cooperative targets usually lack computer-aided design (CAD) models and keypoint priors, making existing model-based or keypoint-matching methods difficult to apply reliably. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MF-UAVPose6D, a model-free monocular 6-DoF pose estimation framework for fixed-wing UAVs. During inference, the method takes only a single red-green-blue (RGB) image and camera intrinsics as input. It first obtains a stable target anchor through heatmap-guided center localization, introduces a Perspective-Aware Module (PAM) to model observation-ray priors, exploits Dynamic Topological Sampling (DTS) to complement weak structural cues from the wings, fuselage, and tail, and adopts a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism to estimate the 6-DoF pose. In addition, we construct the FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset, which covers fixed-wing UAV observations across diverse distances, viewpoints, and poses. Experimental results show that MF-UAVPose6D achieves accurate and efficient monocular 6-DoF pose estimation without requiring CAD models, and demonstrates strong robustness in long-range rotation estimation, depth recovery, and joint pose evaluation.

AI.tldr: MF-UAVPose6D is a model-free monocular 6-DoF pose estimation framework for fixed-wing UAVs that uses only a single RGB image and camera intrinsics at inference, employing heatmap-guided center localization, a Perspective-Aware Module, Dynamic Topological Sampling, and decoupled translation-rotation decoding to achieve accurate pose estimation without requiring CAD models or keypoint priors.
AI.motivation: Estimating 6-DoF poses for non-cooperative UAV targets is challenging because they lack CAD models and keypoint priors, making traditional model-based or keypoint-matching methods unreliable.
AI.method: The framework uses (1) heatmap-guided center localization for stable target anchoring, (2) a Perspective-Aware Module (PAM) for observation-ray priors, (3) Dynamic Topological Sampling (DTS) to capture weak structural cues from wings, fuselage, and tail, and (4) a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism.
AI.result: Achieves accurate and efficient monocular 6-DoF pose estimation without CAD models, with strong robustness in long-range rotation estimation, depth recovery, and joint pose evaluation on the constructed FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset.
AI.conclusion: MF-UAVPose6D provides a reliable model-free solution for fixed-wing UAV pose estimation, overcoming the limitations of existing methods that depend on CAD models or keypoint annotations.

title: Optimizer Memory Makes Shuffle Order a First-Order Source of Fine-Tuning Noise
summary: Shuffle order can be a larger source of fine-tuning noise than a memoryless analysis predicts: fixed-clock optimizer memory makes local equal-multiset contrasts first order in the learning rate rather than second order, and the resulting order channel can be large enough for a single seed to flip a close A/B comparison. We isolate this mechanism and derive a fit-free way to size the noise it produces. For a memoryless optimizer, reordering an equal multiset has no first-order endpoint term; the leading local contrast is the $O(η^2)$ gradient bracket. Fixed-clock optimizers such as AdamW are different. Their moment buffers, preconditioner state, and de-biasing counters advance with the step index rather than with the learning-rate-scaled time $τ=ηk$, so the same gradient can receive a position-dependent endpoint weight. For any fixed finite measurement window, a lifted-state expansion gives an $O(η)$ equal-multiset contrast whenever the first-order replay coefficient is nonzero, while regular and clock-matched controls remain $O(η^2)$; a bare fixed-$β$ momentum buffer is already enough. A bitwise-deterministic replay from one warmed optimizer state isolates the mechanism, giving order-variance slopes 1.83 for AdamW, 2.00 for fixed-$β$ momentum, and 4.00 for SGD; matching the memory clock to $τ$ restores the regular exponent. For AdamW with a frozen preconditioner, the same impulse-weight kernel gives a closed-form asymptotic order-variance floor after the local potentials are measured, with no fitted coefficients. The result is local to the measurement window (independent reshuffling can average the channel across windows), but it yields order-noise error bars, positional attribution weights, and a seed-budget criterion for fine-tuning comparisons.

AI.tldr: Shuffle order creates significant fine-tuning noise in fixed-clock optimizers like AdamW, producing O(η) contrasts rather than the expected O(η²), with measurable order-variance slopes and a closed-form asymptotic floor.
AI.motivation: To identify and quantify an overlooked source of noise in neural network fine-tuning—shuffle order—that can flip A/B comparisons, and to develop a theory-driven, fit-free method for sizing this noise.
AI.method: Uses a lifted-state expansion to analyze equal-multiset gradient contrasts under fixed-clock optimizers (AdamW, momentum, SGD); derives order-variance slopes analytically and validates with bitwise-deterministic replay experiments.
AI.result: Order-variance slopes: 1.83 for AdamW, 2.00 for fixed-β momentum, 4.00 for SGD. A closed-form asymptotic order-variance floor is derived for AdamW with frozen preconditioner, requiring no fitted coefficients.
AI.conclusion: Shuffle order noise is a first-order effect in fixed-clock optimizers and must be accounted for in fine-tuning comparisons; the paper provides error bars, attribution weights, and a seed-budget criterion to manage this noise.

title: HiReFF: High-Resolution Feedforward Human Reconstruction from Uncalibrated Sparse-View Video
summary: Uncalibrated volumetric video streaming for human reconstruction is essential for holographic communication and AR/VR, yet remains challenging due to the need for temporal consistency and computational efficiency from sparse-view inputs. Existing methods rely on per-scene optimization or calibrated cameras, while recent feed-forward models are limited to low-resolution (0.5K) single-frame synthesis. We present HiReFF, a feed-forward method for 2K-resolution 360° human video reconstruction from uncalibrated sparse-view videos. Our framework decomposes the problem into two key tasks: foreground 3D Gaussian reconstruction from sparse-view videos (four views separated by 90°) and computationally efficient high-resolution synthesis. To enable the former, we propose Scale-synchronized Camera Calibration to resolve scale ambiguity for multi-view supervision, and Gaussian-wise Foreground Masking to reconstruct clean foregrounds by modulating Gaussian parameters. For efficient high-resolution synthesis, our High-resolution Side-tuning achieves 2K rendering by augmenting the Gaussian head with supplementary features while keeping the backbone at 0.5K, drastically reducing computational overhead. Experiments demonstrate that HiReFF significantly outperforms existing methods in high-resolution streaming volumetric video reconstruction. https://iridescentjiang.github.io/HiReFF

AI.tldr: HiReFF is a feed-forward method achieving 2K-resolution 360° human video reconstruction from uncalibrated sparse-view (four views) videos, using Scale-synchronized Camera Calibration, Gaussian-wise Foreground Masking, and High-resolution Side-tuning for efficient high-resolution volumetric video streaming.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for volumetric video reconstruction suffer from per-scene optimization, reliance on calibrated cameras, or low-resolution (0.5K) single-frame synthesis. The goal is to enable high-resolution (2K) temporally consistent human reconstruction from uncalibrated sparse-view inputs for practical AR/VR and holographic communication.
AI.method: HiReFF decomposes the problem into (1) foreground 3D Gaussian reconstruction from sparse-view videos (four views at 90° separation) using Scale-synchronized Camera Calibration to resolve scale ambiguity and Gaussian-wise Foreground Masking for clean foregrounds, and (2) efficient high-resolution synthesis via High-resolution Side-tuning, which augments the Gaussian head with supplementary features while keeping the backbone at 0.5K to reduce computation.
AI.result: HiReFF significantly outperforms existing methods in high-resolution streaming volumetric video reconstruction, achieving 2K rendering quality from uncalibrated sparse-view inputs.
AI.conclusion: HiReFF provides an effective feed-forward solution for high-resolution 360° human video reconstruction without requiring calibration or per-scene optimization, advancing practical volumetric video streaming applications.

title: BayesEvolve: Explicit Belief States for Autonomous Scientific Discovery
summary: Autonomous scientific discovery systems increasingly use large language models (LLMs) to propose new hypotheses, but many such systems condition primarily on experimental memory: archives of high-scoring candidates or heuristic summaries of recent trials. We argue that discovery agents should instead maintain explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality. We introduce BayesEvolve, a belief-guided discovery framework that converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state and uses this belief to guide future experimentation. As a controlled testbed for belief-guided discovery, we evaluate BayesEvolve on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks, leaving program and laboratory discovery domains to future work. BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under a fixed evaluation budget. We further show that the belief state is predictive on held-out candidate pools, that controlled decision-rule ablations favor belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus, and that BayesEvolve exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.

AI.tldr: BayesEvolve is a belief-guided discovery framework that uses explicit uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality (rather than just experimental memory) to guide LLM-based scientific discovery, improving sample efficiency on black-box optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based discovery systems rely primarily on experimental memory (high-scoring archives or heuristic summaries) rather than maintaining explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality, which limits their efficiency.
AI.method: BayesEvolve converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state and uses this belief (with an annealed uncertainty bonus) to guide future experimentation. It is evaluated on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks.
AI.result: BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under a fixed evaluation budget. The belief state is predictive on held-out pools, and belief-guided selection with annealed uncertainty bonus outperforms controlled ablations. It exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.
AI.conclusion: Discovery agents should maintain explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality instead of relying solely on experimental memory, as belief-guided frameworks like BayesEvolve achieve better sample efficiency.

title: OP3DSG: Open-Vocabulary Part-Aware 3D Scene Graph Generation for Real-World Environments
summary: 3D scene graphs (3DSGs) provide a compact and structured abstraction of 3D environments. Although advances in foundation models have enabled open-vocabulary 3DSG generation, existing approaches remain object-centric and encode limited relational information -- restricting their applicability in real-world scenarios that require fine-grained understanding. We propose OP3DSG, an open-vocabulary part-aware 3DSG generation framework that constructs unified graphs that jointly model objects, interactive parts, spatial relations, functional relations, and affordances. OP3DSG integrates object-part knowledge-guided detection with part-aware 3D fusion to preserve small and interaction-relevant components, and employs a geometry-initialized prior graph with LLM-based refinement to reduce spurious relational predictions while enabling efficient graph construction. To systematically evaluate unified 3D scene graph construction, we introduce UniGraph3D, a benchmark designed for part-aware perception and multi-level relational reasoning. Experimental results show that OP3DSG achieves state-of-the-art performance and demonstrates its effectiveness as a perception backbone in diverse real-world robotics tasks.

AI.tldr: OP3DSG is an open-vocabulary framework that generates part-aware 3D scene graphs integrating objects, interactive parts, spatial/functional relations, and affordances, achieving SOTA performance on the new UniGraph3D benchmark and real-world robotics tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D scene graph approaches are object-centric with limited relational information, lacking fine-grained part-level understanding needed for real-world applications.
AI.method: Integrates object-part knowledge-guided detection with part-aware 3D fusion, and uses a geometry-initialized prior graph with LLM-based refinement to reduce spurious predictions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the UniGraph3D benchmark and demonstrates effectiveness as a perception backbone in diverse robotics tasks.
AI.conclusion: OP3DSG advances 3D scene understanding by jointly modeling objects, parts, and multi-level relations, enabling fine-grained structured perception for robotics.

title: Persona-Trained Monte Carlo: Estimating Market-Outcome Distributions via Swarms of Persona-Conditioned Neural Policy Bots in a Limit Order Book
comment: 58 pages, 3 figures, 9 tables, 3 algorithms. Survey and proposed framework; no implementation or empirical results
summary: We propose Persona-Trained Monte Carlo (PTMC), a method for estimating distributions of market-outcome statistics by repeatedly simulating limit-order-book interaction among swarms of persona-conditioned neural-policy trading bots. Each run instantiates many bots sharing one trained policy network but conditioned on heterogeneous, individually sampled persona parameters drawn from a learned trader-heterogeneity distribution; the bots interact in a continuous double auction, and the resulting price path is one Monte Carlo sample. Repeating this over independent persona-population draws yields an ensemble from which a target market statistic is estimated. Randomness enters through persona draws, within-run action sampling, and optional exogenous shocks, not solely through price as in classical Monte Carlo. We distinguish PTMC from adjacent paradigms, including classical Monte Carlo, hand-coded agent-based models, single-agent reinforcement learning, and large-language-model-based generative agents. To justify the design, we survey cross-disciplinary foundations -- agent-based computational economics, market microstructure, behavioral finance, deep reinforcement learning, generative/LLM-based agents, news-driven trading, systemic risk, econophysics, and game theory -- connecting each literature to a specific design choice in the policy network, training data, or validation protocol. We formalize the PTMC estimator and its convergence properties, specify a candidate bot architecture and training objective, and propose a four-level validation methodology: stylized-fact matching, microstructure- and agent-level checks, and historical stress-test comparison against a zero-intelligence baseline. The framework is proposed but not implemented: we contribute a formal estimator, a cross-disciplinary design justification, and a validation roadmap, and conclude with open research questions.

AI.tldr: PTMC is a proposed-but-not-yet-implemented framework that estimates market-outcome statistics by simulating limit-order-book interactions among swarms of persona-conditioned neural-policy trading bots, combining Monte Carlo sampling with heterogeneous trader heterogeneity.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between classical Monte Carlo methods (which assume price dynamics exogenously) and realistic market simulations that capture heterogeneous trader behavior, behavioral biases, and complex agent interactions in limit-order-book markets.
AI.method: Persona-Trained Monte Carlo (PTMC): repeated simulations of limit-order-book interactions among many neural-policy bots sharing one trained policy network but conditioned on heterogeneous persona parameters sampled from a learned trader-heterogeneity distribution. Randomness comes from persona draws, action sampling, and optional exogenous shocks.
AI.result: Not yet implemented; the paper contributes a formal estimator with convergence properties, a candidate bot architecture and training objective, and a four-level validation methodology (stylized-fact matching, microstructure checks, agent-level checks, historical stress-test comparison).
AI.conclusion: PTMC is proposed as a novel cross-disciplinary framework for market simulation, with a formal estimator and validation roadmap identified, while implementation and empirical testing remain as open research questions.

title: Multi-scale Object-Aware Gaze Estimation via Geometric Reasoning
summary: Gaze target estimation aims to predict the semantic object an observer fixates upon within an image, a task deeply rooted in the object-oriented nature of human gaze. Observers tend to select a specific semantic entity as the attentional target, rather than responding randomly across arbitrary regions of the image. However, existing methods typically model this task as a direct mapping from global features to gaze heatmaps, essentially treating it as a pixel-level regression problem. This approach fails to explicitly represent the gazed object as a distinct entity, making it difficult to produce stable and semantically consistent predictions in complex scenes. To address this, we propose a two-stage gaze estimation framework guided by object semantics, reformulating gaze target estimation as a hierarchical reasoning process. Our method incorporates object-level representations during feature encoding to align image features with discrete semantic entities, then introduces multi-scale feature fusion and geometric constraints from head pose and gaze direction for fine-grained localization and object-level discrimination. Extensive experiments on GazeFollow, VideoAttentionTarget, ChildPlay, and GOO-Real demonstrate that our method achieves AUC of 0.961, 0.948, 0.987, and 0.977 respectively, delivering strong performance across all benchmarks while maintaining a compact parameter size of 7.1M.

AI.tldr: A two-stage gaze target estimation framework using object semantics, achieving state-of-the-art performance (0.961-0.987 AUC) with only 7.1M parameters across four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing gaze estimation methods treat the task as pixel-level regression from global features, failing to explicitly model gazed objects as discrete semantic entities, leading to unstable and semantically inconsistent predictions in complex scenes.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) object-level feature encoding aligning image features with discrete semantic entities, (2) multi-scale feature fusion with geometric constraints from head pose and gaze direction for fine-grained localization and object-level discrimination.
AI.result: Achieves AUC of 0.961 (GazeFollow), 0.948 (VideoAttentionTarget), 0.987 (ChildPlay), and 0.977 (GOO-Real) with only 7.1M parameters.
AI.conclusion: Reformulating gaze target estimation as hierarchical object-semantic reasoning significantly outperforms pixel-level regression approaches, delivering compact yet strong performance.

title: Sequential Fairness Auditing with Limited Output Access
summary: External evaluations are becoming increasingly central to the governance of AI systems. In practice, however, independent auditors often have limited access to deployed models and must rely on query-based interactions. Most existing fairness evaluation methods assume static datasets and fixed-sample statistical tests, making them poorly suited to real-world auditing scenarios in which evidence must be collected sequentially under query constraints. In this work, we formulate fairness auditing as a tolerance-aware sequential hypothesis-testing problem under limited model output access. We develop a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio framework that allows auditors to accumulate evidence from a finite audit pool and stop once sufficient support for compliance or violation has been obtained. The framework is instantiated for decision-based Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity audits, and extended to score- and logit-based proxy audits when richer observables are available. Our results show that both the fairness metric and the level of model access significantly affect audit efficiency, and that the benefits of richer output information are not uniform across auditing settings. In particular, richer outputs can substantially reduce the number of queries required for some fairness metrics and operating regimes, while offering limited gains in near-threshold cases. This work provides a practical statistical framework for sequential fairness auditing under realistic deployment constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio framework for fairness auditing under query constraints, enabling auditors to sequentially collect evidence and stop once sufficient support for compliance or violation is obtained.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness evaluation methods assume static datasets and fixed-sample statistical tests, making them unsuitable for real-world auditing where evidence must be collected sequentially under query constraints and limited model access.
AI.method: Formulates fairness auditing as a tolerance-aware sequential hypothesis-testing problem; develops a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio framework instantiated for Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity audits, extended to score/logit-based proxy audits.
AI.result: Fairness metric and model access level significantly affect audit efficiency; richer outputs substantially reduce queries for some metrics/regimes but offer limited gains in near-threshold cases.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical statistical framework for sequential fairness auditing under realistic deployment constraints.

title: Speculative Pre-Positioning: Decoding Stateful Sessions to the Next Decision Point Off the Critical Path
summary: A stateless inference server (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM) idles between requests while the accelerator waits; a stateful session reclaims that idle time. Speculative pre-positioning decodes the session forward to its next decision point with the target model's own forward pass and no draft model, moving the cross-request prefill and entry-decode off the critical path: the next request resumes from a pre-paid entry on its delta, or, when a confidence gate fires, is answered from a cached distribution in one near-constant vocabulary scan with no decode, at a cost only of energy and a rare, bounded false accept. The payoff is conditional on capability: a capable model fires the gate at near-full coverage and about 87% precision (a smaller one never clears it), returning the first token in about 1.0 ms versus the 39 ms decode a prefix cache still pays.

AI.tldr: A technique called speculative pre-positioning uses idle time in stateless inference servers to pre-compute future inference steps, enabling near-instantaneous first-token responses by skipping decode at high confidence.
AI.motivation: Stateless inference servers waste idle accelerator time between requests; the goal is to reclaim this idle time to reduce latency for subsequent requests.
AI.method: Speculative pre-positioning pre-decodes the session forward to the next decision point using the target model's own forward pass (no draft model), moving prefill and entry-decode off the critical path. The next request resumes from a pre-paid entry, and if a confidence gate fires, it answers from a cached distribution via a vocabulary scan with no decode.
AI.result: A capable model achieves near-full coverage with ~87% precision, returning the first token in ~1.0 ms compared to 39 ms with a prefix cache. A smaller model never clears the confidence gate.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively eliminates decode latency on the critical path for capable models, trading energy for dramatically lower first-token latency, but is conditional on model capability.

title: W4A4 Quantization for Inference on Wan2.2-I2V-A14B
comment: 4 pages, 8 figures; ICME 2026 Low-Bit-width Large-Model Quantization Challenge submission
summary: We summarize our submission to Sub-Challenge 1: W4A4 Quantization for Inference (HiF4 / MXFP4) of the ICME 2026 Low-Bit-width Large-Model Quantization Challenge. The sub-challenge targets 4-bit weight and 4-bit activation inference on Wan-AI/Wan2.2-I2V-A14B under HiF4 or MXFP4 numerical formats. We adapt two complementary ideas from LLM quantization, MixQ-style mixed precision for sparse activation outliers and SmoothQuant-style per-channel smoothing, together with block-wise HiF4 packing for Wan2.2 feed-forward linear layers. Calibration on representative OpenS2V-5M batches identifies heavy-tailed activation channels; smoothing rebalances dynamic range before W4A4 rounding; and a dual-branch GEMM preserves outlier columns in higher precision while the bulk of channels use strict W4A4. On official VBench I2V metrics, our pipeline stays within 2-3.5 percent of FP16 on most quality axes and improves motion smoothness, outperforming a native HiFloat4 baseline that degrades roughly 5 percent relative to FP16 across all reported scores.

AI.tldr: A quantization pipeline combining MixQ-style mixed precision, SmoothQuant-style smoothing, and block-wise HiF4 packing for W4A4 inference on Wan2.2-I2V-A14B, achieving within 2-3.5% of FP16 on VBench I2V metrics.
AI.motivation: To enable 4-bit weight and 4-bit activation (W4A4) inference on the large Wan2.2-I2V-A14B model under HiF4/MXFP4 formats while minimizing accuracy degradation relative to FP16.
AI.method: Combines (1) MixQ-style mixed precision for sparse activation outliers, (2) SmoothQuant-style per-channel smoothing to rebalance dynamic range, and (3) block-wise HiF4 packing for feed-forward linear layers. A dual-branch GEMM preserves outlier columns in higher precision while most channels use strict W4A4.
AI.result: Stays within 2-3.5% of FP16 on most VBench quality axes, improves motion smoothness, and outperforms a native HiFloat4 baseline that degrades roughly 5% relative to FP16.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantization pipeline effectively bridges the gap between FP16 and W4A4 inference for large video generation models, with minimal quality loss.

title: Using Large Language Models as Low-Cost Statistical Estimators for Human-Response Data
summary: Quantitative research across the social and behavioral sciences depends on human subject experiments that are expensive, slow, and subject to sampling bias. Here we show that pretrained large language models induce risk-equivalent estimators of conditional expectations under squared loss, establishing restricted functional risk equivalence: under squared loss, the LLM induces an estimator whose risk matches the Bayes optimal risk for squared-loss prediction of conditional expectations for any inference that depends on the data only through the conditional mean. We formalize the LLM as a misspecified functional estimator $T(\hat{P}_n)$ trained on i.i.d.\ data, decompose the estimation error into representation bias $ε_{\mathrm{rep}}$ and optimization error, and prove that under mild regularity conditions the LLM's expected error converges to the irreducible population variance plus the squared representation bias, with the representation bias bounded by the Pinsker inequality. The identifiability error $δ$ propagates into the effective bias, inflating the asymptotic risk floor. We establish restricted functional risk equivalence via a bidirectional Le Cam deficiency analysis: the forward deficiency vanishes asymptotically while the reverse deficiency is exactly zero. We provide finite-sample concentration bounds and a calibration protocol with explicit decision rules. The result is a precise, provable statement: a well-calibrated LLM achieves the Bayes-optimal risk for conditional-mean-dependent inference, bounded by explicit scope conditions. In practical applications, this means that under satisfied conditions and well-calibrated models, large language models can be used in many prediction and decision-making tasks that originally relied on human experiments, approximating near-optimal statistical inference at lower cost.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that pretrained large language models can achieve Bayes-optimal risk for conditional mean estimation under squared loss, establishing restricted functional risk equivalence through Le Cam deficiency analysis. The authors show that with proper calibration, LLMs can replace human subject experiments in quantitative research by providing near-optimal statistical inference at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Human subject experiments are expensive, slow, and suffer from sampling bias. The authors aim to establish theoretical foundations for using LLMs as substitutes in quantitative research, proving they can achieve near-optimal statistical inference.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the LLM as a misspecified functional estimator trained on i.i.d. data, decomposes estimation error into representation bias and optimization error, and uses bidirectional Le Cam deficiency analysis to establish restricted functional risk equivalence.
AI.result: A well-calibrated LLM achieves Bayes-optimal risk for conditional-mean-dependent inference, with representation bias bounded by Pinsker inequality, finite-sample concentration bounds, and a calibration protocol with explicit decision rules.
AI.conclusion: Under satisfied conditions and well-calibrated models, LLMs can replace human experiments in prediction and decision-making tasks that depend only on conditional means, approximating optimal inference at lower cost.

title: The Joint Effect of Quantization and Sampling Temperature on LLM Safety Alignment: A Factorial Analysis
summary: Modern LLM deployments routinely compress models and raise sampling temperature to reduce cost, latency, or repetition, yet safety evaluations usually treat these choices as fixed implementation details. This leaves a practical uncertainty: does a model that is safe at FP16 and greedy decoding remain safe after it is quantized and sampled stochastically, or do the two deployment knobs amplify one another? We study this question with a factorial evaluation of 9 instruction-tuned models from six families, 3 precisions (FP16, GPTQ INT8, AWQ INT4), and 6 temperatures ($T{=}0$ to $1.0$), yielding 161 configurations and $\approx$322k responses judged by a six-model safety ensemble. Contrary to the concern that low-bit deployment broadly erodes alignment, standard non-adversarial quantization is usually safety-neutral: INT4 keeps or lowers attack success for 7 of 9 models, with clear degradation concentrated in the weakest baseline model, SmolLM3-3B ($18.5\%{\to}36.0\%$). The larger risk comes from sampling: higher temperature sharply increases decision instability for vulnerable models, with DFR reaching 53.0\% at $T{=}1.0$, even when average ASR changes modestly. Finally, the interaction is not a ``double penalty'': our Compound Degradation Index remains largely sub-additive ($-0.195$ to $+0.045$), indicating that quantization and temperature do not systematically compound. These results suggest a deployment rule of thumb: standard INT4/INT8 quantization can be reasonable for strongly aligned models, but safety claims at elevated temperature should report multi-sample stability, not only average attack success.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether LLM safety degrades under combined quantization and high-temperature sampling. Testing 9 models across 161 configurations, it finds quantization is mostly safety-neutral, while higher temperature sharply increases safety instability, but the two factors do not compound each other.
AI.motivation: Real-world LLM deployments routinely use quantization and higher sampling temperatures to reduce cost/latency/repetition, but safety evaluations typically fix these settings, creating uncertainty about whether a model safe under one configuration remains safe under realistic deployment conditions.
AI.method: Factorial evaluation of 9 instruction-tuned models (6 families), 3 precisions (FP16, GPTQ INT8, AWQ INT4), 6 temperatures (T=0 to 1.0), yielding 161 configurations and ~322k responses judged by a six-model safety ensemble.
AI.result: Quantization is safety-neutral: INT4 keeps or lowers ASR for 7/9 models. Degradation only appears in the weakest model (SmolLM3-3B: 18.5%→36.0%). Higher temperature causes decision instability (DFR up to 53.0% at T=1.0). Interaction is sub-additive (Compound Degradation Index: -0.195 to +0.045), meaning no systematic compounding.
AI.conclusion: Standard INT4/INT8 quantization is reasonable for strongly aligned models, but safety claims at elevated temperature should report multi-sample stability, not just average attack success rate.

title: Fast Enough to Act: Spatio-Temporal Visual Token Merging for Low-Latency Robotic VLMs and VLAs
summary: Vision-language models and vision-language action models endow the robot with unprecedented capabilities. However, the input of video and high-resolution images yields a massive number of visual tokens, leading to extremely high inference latency and severely hindering the robot's real-time control. To break through this computational bottleneck, we propose ST-Merge, a plug-and-play, training-free framework that efficiently fuses redundant tokens directly during the visual encoding phase. By explicitly constructing 3D spatiotemporal coordinates, it employs a multi-queue parallel matching and weighted aggregation mechanism to achieve efficient and geometrically consistent fusion of redundant tokens across frames. In addition, we introduce a post-merge positional correction mechanism that effectively eliminates spatial deviation caused by merging by dynamically re-evaluating the rotational position code of the weighted centroid of the vision token, thereby ensuring the high-precision spatial awareness required for dexterous operation. In the Video Question Answering task on the mainstream VLM, Qwen2.5-VL, ST-Merge achieves a 2$\times$ inference speedup with only a tiny 1\% loss in precision. When deployed on the $π_{0.5}$ VLA policy, ST-Merge achieves an 8.3$\times$ speedup at 1024 $\times$ 1024 resolution and matches the baseline success rate at this high-resolution setting. At lower resolutions, it introduces a small drop in accuracy.

AI.tldr: ST-Merge is a plug-and-play, training-free framework that accelerates vision-language and vision-language action models by merging redundant visual tokens during encoding, achieving 2x speedup on VLMs and 8.3x on VLAs with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models for robotics suffer from high inference latency due to massive visual tokens from video/high-res images, hindering real-time robot control. The paper aims to break this computational bottleneck.
AI.method: Proposes ST-Merge: a training-free, plug-and-play framework that (1) constructs 3D spatiotemporal coordinates for tokens, (2) performs multi-queue parallel matching and weighted aggregation to fuse redundant tokens across frames during visual encoding, and (3) introduces a post-merge positional correction mechanism using rotational position encoding of weighted centroids to preserve spatial awareness.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5-VL (VQA task): 2x inference speedup with only 1% precision loss. On π0.5 VLA policy: 8.3x speedup at 1024x1024 resolution with matched success rate; minor accuracy drop at lower resolutions.
AI.conclusion: ST-Merge effectively reduces inference latency in vision-language and vision-language action models while maintaining high precision, enabling real-time robotic control with high-resolution visual input.

title: Whose Side Is Your Agent On? Multi-Party Principal Loyalty in LLM Agents
summary: A rapidly growing class of LLM agents is multi-party: the agent acts for a principal (who briefs it, sends follow-ups, and receives results) while also conversing in a separate channel with a counterparty whose interests may diverge (negotiating with a vendor, screening inbound requests, or mediating between employees). Here "help whoever you are talking to" is the wrong objective. The agent must stay loyal to the principal it represents without over-refusing the principal's own cooperative asks. We study this multi-party loyalty problem and contribute a measurement instrument, two mechanisms, and a structural lesson. PrincipalBench is a 75-item multi-turn benchmark with leak probes, dual judges, and an integrity-audit gate. Across 13 frontier subjects it exposes a sharp split (<=20% vs. 53.6-75.3% harm) invisible to single-turn safety evaluations: a selective cluster that declines adversarial probes while still following the principal's legitimate requests, and an over-refusing cluster that refuses broadly. (M1) A prompt-time loyalty scaffold (a fixed system prompt of seven prioritized rules, open-coded from 50+ failure trajectories) holds Claude-Sonnet to 19.4% harm and all nine selective subjects to <=20%. (M2) A per-token-KL distillation recipe transfers a prompted Qwen3-32B teacher into 8B Qwen3 and Llama-3.1 students, the strongest open-weight recipe we measure. (Lesson) Both mechanisms only move along a common leak/over-refusal trade-off rather than crossing it: improving one axis costs the other, and the jointly favorable outcome stays out of reach.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the "multi-party loyalty problem" in LLM agents that must serve a principal while interacting with a counterparty. It introduces PrincipalBench (75-item benchmark with leak probes and dual judges), finds a sharp split between selective and over-refusing agents across 13 frontier models, proposes two mitigation mechanisms (a fixed loyalty scaffold system prompt and KL distillation), and reveals a fundamental trade-off between leaking and over-refusing that neither mechanism can overcome.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety evaluations focus on single-turn, single-user scenarios. However, multi-party agents (e.g., negotiating with a vendor while serving a principal) face a unique loyalty problem where "help whoever you are talking to" is the wrong objective. The agent must stay loyal to its principal without over-refusing legitimate requests — a tension not captured by standard safety benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors construct PrincipalBench, a 75-item multi-turn benchmark with leak probes and dual judges. They evaluate 13 frontier LLM subjects, revealing a "selective" vs. "over-refusing" split. They then propose two mechanisms: (M1) a prompt-time loyalty scaffold with 7 prioritized rules, and (M2) a per-token-KL distillation recipe from prompted Qwen3-32B to 8B students (Qwen3 and Llama-3.1).
AI.result: PrincipalBench exposes a sharp split: some models are selective (decline adversarial probes but follow legitimate requests, ≤20% harm) while others over-refuse broadly (53.6-75.3% harm). M1 holds Claude-Sonnet to 19.4% harm and all selective subjects to ≤20%. M2 produces the strongest open-weight recipe measured. However, both mechanisms reveal a fundamental leak/over-refusal trade-off — improving one axis costs the other.
AI.conclusion: The multi-party loyalty problem is structurally challenging: neither prompt engineering nor distillation can cross the leak/over-refusal trade-off. The jointly favorable outcome remains out of reach, suggesting deeper architectural or training innovations may be needed.

title: Bilevel Optimization for Neural Architecture Search
summary: Bilevel optimization has become an influential and widely adopted framework for addressing hierarchical optimization problems in machine learning, providing an effective approach to modeling the interaction between two levels of optimization, with applications such as hyperparameter tuning, meta-learning, adversarial training, and data poisoning. Neural Architecture Search (NAS), a subfield of hyperparameter optimization, is a prime example of a bilevel optimization problem, with architecture parameters optimized at the outer-level and network weights optimized at the inner level. This paper presents a structured overview of NAS through the lens of bilevel optimization. We categorize existing NAS approaches into two main classes: sampling-based methods, which search optimal architectures using different architecture samplers, and bilevel theory-based methods, which solve the architecture search problem using bilevel optimization principles. We further highlight our current research direction, wherein the bilevel NAS formulation is addressed through an auxiliary mathematical programming framework. This framework enables the systematic integration of second-order information from the model's training loss function and ensures the optimality of the model parameters while modifying architecture parameters. By simultaneously updating the architecture and model parameters along their respective optimal descent directions derived from the auxiliary mathematical program, these methods achieve more principled and theoretically consistent results. The same auxiliary program can also be used for simultaneous hyperparameter and model fine-tuning. A comparative analysis shows that bilevel theory-based approaches generally outperform sampling-based methods, both in accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a structured overview of Neural Architecture Search (NAS) through the lens of bilevel optimization, categorizing methods into sampling-based and bilevel theory-based approaches, and presents an auxiliary mathematical programming framework that achieves more principled and theoretically consistent results by jointly optimizing architecture and model parameters along optimal descent directions.
AI.motivation: Bilevel optimization is a powerful framework for hierarchical problems in ML (hyperparameter tuning, meta-learning, etc.), and NAS is a key instance. The authors aim to provide a structured understanding of NAS through this lens and address limitations of existing approaches.
AI.method: Two main categories: (1) Sampling-based methods (search using architecture samplers) and (2) Bilevel theory-based methods (solve using bilevel optimization principles). The authors highlight an auxiliary mathematical programming framework that integrates second-order information from training loss and ensures optimality, updating both architecture and model parameters along optimal descent directions.
AI.result: Bilevel theory-based methods generally outperform sampling-based methods in both accuracy and efficiency. The auxiliary programming framework enables principled joint optimization and can also be used for simultaneous hyperparameter and model fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The bilevel optimization lens provides a structured and theoretically sound approach to NAS, with bilevel theory-based methods (especially the auxiliary mathematical programming framework) offering superior performance and consistency over sampling-based alternatives.

title: Dynamic Parsing and Updating Natural Language Specification using VLMs for Robust Vision-Language Tracking
summary: Vision-language tracking guided by natural language specifications leverages high-level semantic cues of target objects to substantially boost tracking accuracy and robustness. Existing studies have verified that adaptively optimizing textual descriptions throughout the tracking process can effectively mitigate the semantic-visual mismatch induced by dynamic variations in target appearance, position, and other inherent attributes. Nevertheless, mainstream methods that directly generate textual information via sequence models or large language models inevitably suffer from inherent defects, including erroneous target updating, excessive background distraction, and pervasive hallucination artifacts. To address the aforementioned limitations, this paper proposes a novel language dependency parsing mechanism to precisely distill core tracking principal components, encompassing target objects, semantic concepts, and background contextual information. On this basis, we perform component-aware adaptive textual description updates by exploiting the powerful cross-modal understanding capability of the pre-trained vision-language model Qwen-VL. By integrating the proposed elaborately designed modules into the baseline framework, our method achieves consistent and superior tracking performance on multiple large-scale vision-language tracking benchmarks, including TNL2K, LaSOT, TNLLT, and OTB-LANG. The source code and pre-trained models will be released at https://github.com/Event-AHU/Open_VLTrack.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a language dependency parsing mechanism with Qwen-VL to address semantic-visual mismatch in vision-language tracking, outperforming existing methods on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language tracking methods using sequence models or LLMs suffer from erroneous target updating, excessive background distraction, and hallucination artifacts due to semantic-visual mismatch caused by dynamic target variations.
AI.method: The paper introduces a language dependency parsing mechanism to distill core tracking components (target objects, semantic concepts, background context) and performs component-aware adaptive textual description updates using the pre-trained vision-language model Qwen-VL.
AI.result: The method achieves consistent and superior tracking performance on multiple benchmarks including TNL2K, LaSOT, TNLLT, and OTB-LANG.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dependency parsing and component-aware updating approach effectively mitigates semantic-visual mismatch and outperforms existing vision-language tracking methods.

title: ENC-ODE: Event-level Neurodegenerative Modeling in Continuous Time with Neural ODEs
summary: Accurately predicting the temporal evolution of clinical biomarkers is crucial for the early diagnosis and management of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease. However, this relies on longitudinal data to capture biomarker changes over time, which is often sparse and irregular due to the high cost, labor-intensive nature, and patient burden. To address these challenges, we propose ENC-ODE, an Event-level Neurodegenerative modeling in Continuous time with neural Ordinary Differential Equations. ENC-ODE predicts future biomarker evolution by modeling clinical events through diagnosis-conditioned continuous dynamics.
