A target-conditioned attention mechanism weights and aggregates event-level predictions for the target time and modality without history compression. Extensive experiments on Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset demonstrate that ENC-ODE outperforms representative sequence models while offering a scalable and neuroscientifically grounded solution for clinical support. The code is available at https://github.com/JardinDelSol/enc-ode.

AI.tldr: ENC-ODE uses neural ODEs with diagnosis-conditioned dynamics and attentive aggregation to predict Alzheimer's biomarker trajectories from sparse, irregular clinical data.
AI.motivation: Longitudinal biomarker data for neurodegenerative diseases is sparse and irregular due to high costs and patient burden, making accurate temporal prediction challenging.
AI.method: ENC-ODE models clinical events via diagnosis-conditioned continuous dynamics using neural ODEs, with a target-conditioned attention mechanism to weight and aggregate event-level predictions for the target time and modality.
AI.result: Outperforms representative sequence models on the ADNI dataset, demonstrating scalable and neuroscientifically grounded clinical support.
AI.conclusion: ENC-ODE offers an effective, scalable solution for predicting Alzheimer's biomarker evolution from sparse longitudinal data, with code publicly available.

title: STEMGym: Benchmarking Sequential Decision-Making under Dose Budgets in Autonomous Electron Microscopy
summary: A central premise of autonomous scientific imaging is that smarter navigation, whether Bayesian, RL-based, or otherwise adaptive, is the principal lever for sample-efficient acquisition. We present evidence to the contrary in scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), an atomic-resolution imaging modality whose every measurement deposits damaging electron dose. We introduce STEMGym, an open-source Gymnasium benchmark of 15 physics-simulated STEM worlds spanning five materials, three difficulty levels, and four characterisation tasks, scored by the Dose-Efficiency Curve area (DEC-AUC), a single scalar capturing the information-vs-dose Pareto frontier. Across 33 agent configurations under realistic dose budgets, the dominant determinant of dose efficiency is the analyst (perception) pipeline, not the navigator: pairing a trained CNN analyst with naïve raster scanning raises DEC-AUC by 5.5x over a CNN-free raster baseline (0.287 vs.\ 0.052), while substituting Bayesian or adaptive finite-state-machine navigation for raster yields no statistically significant further gain. Production-tier vision-language models further underperform task-specific CNNs by {\sim}13x on crystallographic defect analysis. By decoupling perception, navigation, and planning under a unified dose budget, STEMGym reframes where ML effort should be invested in autonomous electron microscopy and provides the measurement infrastructure to test it.

AI.tldr: STEMGym benchmark reveals that perception (analyst) pipeline, not navigation strategy, is the dominant factor for dose efficiency in autonomous STEM imaging, with CNN analysts improving performance 5.5x over non-CNN baselines while adaptive navigation offers no significant gains.
AI.motivation: To challenge the prevailing assumption that smarter navigation (Bayesian, RL-based, adaptive) is the key to sample-efficient acquisition in autonomous scientific imaging, specifically in STEM where electron dose is damaging.
AI.method: Introduced STEMGym, an open-source Gymnasium benchmark with 15 physics-simulated STEM worlds across 5 materials, 3 difficulty levels, and 4 tasks, scored by Dose-Efficiency Curve area (DEC-AUC). Tested 33 agent configurations comparing different perception (CNN, VLM) and navigation (raster, Bayesian, FSM) combinations.
AI.result: CNN analyst with raster scanning achieved 5.5x higher DEC-AUC (0.287 vs 0.052) over raster without CNN. No statistically significant gain from Bayesian or adaptive navigation over raster. Vision-language models underperformed task-specific CNNs by ~13x.
AI.conclusion: ML effort in autonomous electron microscopy should focus on perception/analyst pipelines rather than navigation strategies. STEMGym provides infrastructure to decouple and test these components under unified dose budgets.

title: SAFE-DiT: Semantics-Aware Fast-path Execution for High-Resolution Diffusion Transformers
summary: High-resolution Diffusion Transformer (DiT) inference contains substantial spatial redundancy, but many spatially adaptive implementations encode regional computation as attention masks, which can inadvertently move scaled dot-product attention (SDPA) away from FlashAttention fast paths. We identify this avoidable systems bottleneck as Mask-Induced Dispatch Tax (MIDT) and show that it grows with latent sequence length. We introduce SAFE-DiT, a training-free Semantics-Aware Fast-path Execution framework that separates exact mask elision from approximation-based spatial scheduling. SAFE-DiT removes only provenance-certified image self-attention masks that induce a row-wise constant shift in attention logits, preserves semantics-bearing masks such as text-padding masks, and realizes spatial adaptation through prompt-conditioned token partitioning, selective state updates with global context, and periodic context refresh. We call this acceleration-only configuration SAFE-Core and report sensitivity-weighted classifier-free guidance separately as SAFE-DiT+SW. On the evaluated PyTorch SDPA stack, redundant masks make long-sequence attention $4.1\times$ to $5.8\times$ slower than the mask-free path. On Lumina-Next, SAFE-DiT achieves $2.69\times$ end-to-end acceleration at $1024^2$ resolution and $5.09\times$ at $2560^2$, reduces peak memory at $2560^2$ from 94.1 to 27.9 GB, and enables $3072^2$ generation when dense inference runs out of memory. Paired metrics, component ablations, and a blinded human study support visual non-inferiority of SAFE-Core to the dense fast-path baseline, while SAFE-DiT+SW provides a separate prompt-alignment operating point without reintroducing spatial self-attention masks. Code is available at https://github.com/xuanhuayin/SAFE-DiT.

AI.tldr: SAFE-DiT is a training-free framework that accelerates High-resolution Diffusion Transformer (DiT) inference by removing redundant attention masks that cause systems bottlenecks (MIDT), achieving up to 5.09× speedup and 3.4× memory reduction without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Spatially adaptive DiT implementations encode regional computation as attention masks, which inadvertently push scaled dot-product attention (SDPA) away from FlashAttention fast paths, creating a systems bottleneck called Mask-Induced Dispatch Tax (MIDT) that grows worse with longer sequences.
AI.method: SAFE-DiT separates exact mask elision (removing only row-wise constant shift masks) from approximation-based spatial scheduling. It uses prompt-conditioned token partitioning, selective state updates with global context, and periodic context refresh. SAFE-Core is the acceleration-only configuration, while SAFE-DiT+SW adds sensitivity-weighted classifier-free guidance.
AI.result: On Lumina-Next: 2.69× speedup at 1024², 5.09× at 2560²; peak memory reduced from 94.1GB to 27.9GB at 2560²; enables 3072² generation where dense inference runs OOM; redundant masks make long-sequence attention 4.1× to 5.8× slower than mask-free path.
AI.conclusion: SAFE-DiT achieves major acceleration and memory savings without training or visual quality loss. Ablations and human study confirm visual non-inferiority of SAFE-Core to dense baseline, and SAFE-DiT+SW offers prompt-alignment tuning without reintroducing spatial self-attention masks.

title: The FIL Hypothesis: Inductive Biases Help with Kernel Engineering
summary: The Bitter Lesson, which posits that general-purpose methods that scale with computation and data ultimately outperform those with built-in human knowledge, has become a dominant paradigm in the era of Large Language Models. We revisit this principle by observing a new and critical scaling dimension: the duration of the Feedback Information Loop (FIL), the time required for a system to receive a verification signal after generating a prediction. Most historic successes in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have benefited from near instantaneous feedback (e.g., games or classification tasks), but we argue that future AI applications in science and the physical world will inherently involve FILs ranging from hours to weeks. This trend poses a fundamental scaling limit, as obtaining enough verification steps required by purely data-driven methods becomes practically impossible. Additionally, we propose a method that is orthogonal to purely data-driven approaches, based on human-inspired expert knowledge. The method relies on inductive biases and constraining the solution space. We provide an initial validation of the hypothesis and the method, by studying the real-world GPU programming task, a domain with non-trivial FIL, and demonstrate that incorporating inductive biases yields superior performance over data-driven approaches. The code is released under: https://github.com/ai-nikolai/robust_kernelbench

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the "Bitter Lesson" principle by introducing a critical scaling dimension—Feedback Information Loop (FIL) duration—and argues that purely data-driven methods fail for tasks with long FILs (hours to weeks). The authors propose incorporating human-inspired expert knowledge and inductive biases as an alternative, validating it on GPU programming tasks where it outperforms data-driven approaches.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the dominant "Bitter Lesson" paradigm (general-purpose scaling methods outperform human-knowledge-based ones) by identifying a critical limitation: future AI applications in science and the physical world involve long Feedback Information Loops (FILs) from hours to weeks, making pure data-driven scaling practically impossible due to insufficient verification steps.
AI.method: The authors propose a method orthogonal to data-driven scaling that incorporates human-inspired expert knowledge through inductive biases and solution space constraints. They validate this approach on a real-world GPU programming task with non-trivial FIL duration.
AI.result: Initial validation shows that incorporating inductive biases yields superior performance over purely data-driven approaches in the GPU programming task domain with non-trivial FIL.
AI.conclusion: The Bitter Lesson's data-driven scaling paradigm faces fundamental limits when applied to real-world scientific and physical AI applications due to long feedback loops. Methods based on human expert knowledge and inductive biases offer a complementary and potentially superior alternative for such domains.

title: How AI settled the complexity of the oldest SGD algorithm
summary: In 1937, Stefan Kaczmarz proposed a simple algorithm for solving systems of linear equations. This algorithm turned out to be the earliest known example of stochastic gradient descent, a ubiquitous computing paradigm that drives the training of modern AI models such as ChatGPT and Gemini. Now, those AI models have joined forces to discover the worst-case complexity of the Kaczmarz algorithm. This paper tells the story of how it happened.

AI.tldr: This paper describes how AI models discovered the worst-case complexity of the 1937 Kaczmarz algorithm (an early form of stochastic gradient descent), telling the story of this discovery.
AI.motivation: To understand the worst-case complexity of the Kaczmarz algorithm, a foundational method equivalent to stochastic gradient descent that powers modern AI training.
AI.method: AI models (like ChatGPT and Gemini) were used to analyze and discover the worst-case complexity bound of the Kaczmarz algorithm.
AI.result: The worst-case complexity of the Kaczmarz algorithm was discovered through AI assistance.
AI.conclusion: AI models can contribute to discovering theoretical properties of classical algorithms, bridging historical methods with modern AI capabilities.

title: L2D2-GS: Learning to Densify for Feedforward Dynamic Gaussian Scene Reconstruction
summary: High-fidelity reconstruction of dynamic urban environments is a cornerstone of autonomous driving simulation and large-scale world modeling. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has established a new standard for real-time rendering, its reliance on expensive per-scene optimization limits scalability. Conversely, recent feedforward methods that infer Gaussian parameters offer faster speed but face fundamental bottlenecks: they are memory-prohibitive at high resolutions and struggle to fuse dense multi-view observations consistently. This paper presents L2D2-GS, a unified framework that reformulates generalizable reconstruction not as a one-shot regression, but as a robust iterative process of optimization and densification. To resolve the ambiguity of supervision in primitive generation, we propose a self-supervised densification policy that derives explicit reward signals from global reconstruction gains to guide local densification. Furthermore, we mitigate irreversible early-stage artifacts through a geometric regularization mechanism, utilizing reparameterization to constrain the optimization manifold and prevent convergence to poor local optima. Extensive experiments on the PandaSet and Waymo datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity and strong zero-shot generalization, while using fewer primitives than competing baselines.

AI.tldr: L2D2-GS reformulates generalizable 3D reconstruction from multi-view images as an iterative optimization and densification process, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity with fewer primitives via self-supervised densification and geometric regularization.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS methods face a dilemma: per-scene optimization is expensive and not scalable, while feedforward approaches are memory-prohibitive at high resolutions and struggle to fuse dense multi-view observations consistently.
AI.method: (1) Reformulates reconstruction as iterative optimization and densification rather than one-shot regression; (2) Self-supervised densification policy using global reconstruction gains as rewards; (3) Geometric regularization via reparameterization to constrain the optimization manifold and avoid poor local optima.
AI.result: State-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity on PandaSet and Waymo datasets, strong zero-shot generalization, and uses fewer primitives than competing baselines.
AI.conclusion: An iterative, generalizable approach to dynamic urban scene reconstruction can overcome the scalability and fidelity bottlenecks of both per-scene optimization and feedforward methods.

title: Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents
summary: Tool-augmented language-model agents are often evaluated by whether they select the correct tool, produce valid API arguments, and complete the requested task. However, an agent may choose the right tool and still act on the wrong external entity. For example, a request to "email Alex about the launch" may lead the agent to contact the wrong Alex, attach the wrong launch document, reply in the wrong thread, or update the wrong customer account. We call these errors entity binding failures. This paper studies entity binding failures as a distinct reliability and safety problem in tool-augmented agents. We formalize the separation between tool correctness and entity correctness, introduce a taxonomy of wrong-entity failures in enterprise workflows, and evaluate entity-aware execution mechanisms including entity-resolution preconditions, confidence-gated binding, clarification under ambiguity, and provenance tracking. In a controlled diagnostic evaluation across 60 tasks, five model backends, and six tool-use methods, all methods achieved 0.0 percent wrong-tool error, yet action-oriented baselines still produced wrong-entity actions in 24.0-26.0 percent of runs. Entity-aware methods eliminated wrong-entity actions and risk-weighted wrong-entity exposure in this setting, but reduced direct task completion by deferring under ambiguity. These findings show that safe tool use requires not only selecting the correct tool, but also reliably binding natural-language references to the correct real-world entity before action.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies and formalizes "entity binding failures" in tool-augmented language agents, where agents select the correct tool but act on the wrong real-world entity (e.g., emailing the wrong Alex). It proposes entity-aware execution mechanisms and shows that while standard methods achieve 0% wrong-tool errors, they still produce 24-26% wrong-entity errors, which entity-aware methods can eliminate.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation of tool-augmented agents focuses narrowly on tool selection correctness, overlooking errors where the agent acts on the wrong external entity despite choosing the right tool. This gap poses reliability and safety risks in enterprise workflows.
AI.method: The authors formalize the separation between tool correctness and entity correctness, introduce a taxonomy of wrong-entity failures, and evaluate entity-aware execution mechanisms (entity-resolution preconditions, confidence-gated binding, clarification under ambiguity, provenance tracking) across 60 tasks, 5 model backends, and 6 tool-use methods.
AI.result: All methods achieved 0.0% wrong-tool error, but action-oriented baselines produced wrong-entity actions in 24.0-26.0% of runs. Entity-aware methods eliminated wrong-entity actions and risk-weighted exposure, though at the cost of reduced direct task completion due to deferring under ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Safe and reliable tool use requires not just correct tool selection but also robust entity binding—correctly mapping natural-language references to real-world entities before action execution.

title: Mechanistically Eliciting Latent Behaviors in Language Models
summary: We aim to discover diverse, generalizable perturbations of LLM internals that can surface hidden behavioral modes. Such perturbations could help reshape model behavior and systematically evaluate potential risks. We introduce Causal Perturbative Elicitation (CPE), an unsupervised method for discovering interpretable low-rank adapters (LoRAs) that can elicit these latent behaviors. CPE decomposes the computations of a deep transformer slice using a heuristic tensor-decomposition-based algorithm. CPE exhibits remarkable data efficiency, learning a large number of interpretable LoRAs from a single example. Even though CPE is unsupervised, we find that in some cases it can be competitive with supervised elicitation methods via brute-force enumerative search over weight space. For instance, CPE performs similarly to matched-wall-clock-time GRPO on the Countdown task for Qwen3-8B (85% vs 87%), demonstrating that CPE can efficiently elicit complex multi-token behaviors. Since CPE is unsupervised, it can also surface hidden failure modes, such as sandbagging, restoring 85% of locked BigCodeBench performance on a password-locked version of Llama3-70B introduced by Taylor et al. (2025). Additionally, since CPE explores behaviors in weight-space rather than token-space it can potentially ameliorate exploration hacking, a misalignment failure which may arise in sufficiently self-aware AI models (Ngo, 2022). In fact, we find that CPE virtually eliminates alignment-faking (Greenblatt et al., 2024) behavior in a Llama3-70B-based model organism developed by Hughes et al. (2025). Finally, we find that CPE can be used to initialize GPT-OSS-20B in an aligned basin when running GRPO on an environment prone to reward-hacking. By providing a data-efficient method to systematically explore the space of latent model behaviors, CPE yields a powerful tool for aligning AI systems and evaluating their safety.

AI.tldr: CPE is an unsupervised method that discovers interpretable low-rank adapters (LoRAs) from a single example to elicit diverse latent behaviors in LLMs, matching or exceeding supervised methods while uncovering hidden failure modes like sandbagging and alignment-faking.
AI.motivation: To discover diverse, generalizable perturbations of LLM internals that can surface hidden behavioral modes, enabling systematic risk evaluation and model behavior reshaping without requiring large datasets or supervision.
AI.method: Causal Perturbative Elicitation (CPE) uses a heuristic tensor-decomposition-based algorithm to decompose computations of a deep transformer slice, learning interpretable low-rank adapters (LoRAs) from a single example in an unsupervised manner.
AI.result: CPE achieves 85% on Countdown task (vs 87% for GRPO), restores 85% of locked BigCodeBench performance on password-locked Llama3-70B, virtually eliminates alignment-faking in Llama3-70B, and initializes GPT-OSS-20B in an aligned basin for GRPO.
AI.conclusion: CPE provides a data-efficient, unsupervised tool for systematically exploring latent model behaviors, offering powerful capabilities for AI alignment and safety evaluation.

title: SAD-GS: Learning Reliable 3D Semantic Gaussian Fields via Dynamic Geo-Semantic Anchoring
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D semantic Gaussian field learning relies on multi-view 2D supervision, whose semantic targets and spatial assignments are often unreliable. Across varying viewpoints, view-dependent features cause semantic identity drift, while propagated tracker masks introduce boundary leakage and identity switches. Directly optimizing against these unreliable 2D targets forces the 3D representation to absorb multi-view contradictions, leading to severe error accumulation. To resolve this limitation, we propose SAD-GS, a framework for learning reliable 3D semantic Gaussian fields via dynamic geo-semantic anchoring. Specifically, Semantic Anchor Distillation (SAD) distills per-view visual embeddings into consensus text anchors to establish a viewpoint-invariant semantic identity. Concurrently, the Geo-Semantic Feedback Loop (GSFL) leverages the evolving 3D field to actively filter tracker anomalies and refine spatial mask assignments via a conservative three-gate update rule. Extensive evaluations on LERF-OVS, 3D-OVS, and Mip-NeRF360 show that SAD-GS consistently achieves the best overall performance in both open-vocabulary localization and semantic segmentation. These comprehensive improvements validate the effectiveness and robustness of dynamic geo-semantic anchoring for reliable 3D semantic Gaussian field learning.

AI.tldr: SAD-GS proposes dynamic geo-semantic anchoring with Semantic Anchor Distillation and Geo-Semantic Feedback Loop to learn reliable 3D semantic Gaussian fields, achieving state-of-the-art performance in open-vocabulary localization and segmentation.
AI.motivation: Multi-view 2D supervision for 3D semantic Gaussian fields suffers from view-dependent feature drift, boundary leakage, and identity switches, causing error accumulation in 3D representation learning.
AI.method: Semantic Anchor Distillation (SAD) distills per-view embeddings into consensus text anchors for viewpoint-invariant identity; Geo-Semantic Feedback Loop (GSFL) filters tracker anomalies and refines spatial masks via a conservative three-gate update rule.
AI.result: SAD-GS achieves best overall performance on LERF-OVS, 3D-OVS, and Mip-NeRF360 in both open-vocabulary localization and semantic segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic geo-semantic anchoring effectively resolves multi-view contradictions and ensures reliable 3D semantic Gaussian field learning.

title: Latent Actions from Factorized Transition Effects under Agent Ambiguity
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop on Compositional Learning. Project Page: https://hazel-heejeong-nam.github.io/LAM/
summary: Latent Action Models (LAMs) learn action-like proxies from observation transitions. However, in multi-object or distractor-rich scenes, these visual effects mix agent motion with distractors, camera dynamics, and background changes, making the underlying action source ambiguous without supervision. Structuring this mixture as reusable transition effects provides an intermediate representation from which action-like latents can be more robustly formed. We introduce Observed Transition Factorization (OTF), which decomposes each transition into a sparse set of observed transition primitives. Using these primitives as the transition interface, we propose OTF-LAM, which abstracts motion primitives into action-like latents within the standard inverse-forward dynamics framework, and OTF-LAM-Dino, a decoder-free variant that predicts future states in a frozen DINOv2 representation space. Empirically, OTF primitives transfer zeroshot across controlled carrier and morphology shifts, showing reusability. Furthermore, downstream policy learning results match or outperform baselines under complex transition ambiguity.

AI.tldr: OTF-LAM decomposes observation transitions into reusable primitives to form robust action-like latents, improving policy learning in complex scenes with distractors and dynamics changes.
AI.motivation: Latent Action Models struggle in multi-object or distractor-rich scenes because visual effects mix agent motion with distractors, camera dynamics, and background changes, making action sources ambiguous without supervision.
AI.method: OTF (Observed Transition Factorization) decomposes each transition into sparse observed transition primitives. OTF-LAM abstracts these primitives into action-like latents within an inverse-forward dynamics framework, and OTF-LAM-Dino is a decoder-free variant predicting future states in a frozen DINOv2 space.
AI.result: OTF primitives transfer zero-shot across controlled carrier and morphology shifts. Downstream policy learning matches or outperforms baselines under complex transition ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Structuring mixed transition effects as reusable primitives yields more robust action-like latents and improves policy learning in ambiguous scenarios.

title: Does Role Specialization Matter for Explanation Faithfulness in Mixture-of-Experts?
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have recently been extended with role-based mechanisms for interpretability. This is typically done by assigning semantic roles to individual expert components, for example roles like synergy, redundancy, and uniqueness in multimodal settings. However, whether such structural role decomposition preserves explanation faithfulness of the overall architecture remains largely underexplored. We hypothesize that inter-expert representation overlap weakens effective role separation and degrades attribution-based faithfulness, even when semantic roles are explicitly defined. To address this limitation, we introduce representation-level decorrelation regularization to explicitly reduce inter-expert similarity in latent space. Using representation decorrelation objectives, we encourage clearer specialization among experts by minimizing representation overlap. Our experiments show that across multiple multimodal benchmarks, this separation consistently improves explanation faithfulness, as measured by comprehensiveness, sufficiency, and their Area Over the Perturbation Curve (AOPC) summaries, while preserving task performance. We further show that these improvements are not limited to role-based architectures such as Interpretable Multimodal Interaction-aware MoE (I2MoE). Similar trends are observed in a standard sparse MoE baseline, suggesting that representation-level separation may provide a more general mechanism for enhancing explanation faithfulness in MoE systems. Overall, our findings suggest that structural role decomposition alone may be insufficient to guarantee faithful explanations and that representation-level separation helps improve explanation faithfulness. To support reproducibility, the source code and supplementary material are publicly available at https://github.com/dut0817/FL-I2MoE_Decor.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes representation-level decorrelation regularization to reduce inter-expert similarity in MoE architectures, improving explanation faithfulness (measured by comprehensiveness, sufficiency, AOPC) while preserving task performance—showing that structural role decomposition alone is insufficient for faithful explanations.
AI.motivation: While MoE architectures have been extended with role-based mechanisms (e.g., assigning semantic roles like synergy, redundancy, uniqueness) for interpretability, whether such structural role decomposition preserves explanation faithfulness remains underexplored. The authors hypothesize that inter-expert representation overlap weakens effective role separation and degrades attribution-based faithfulness.
AI.method: The authors introduce representation-level decorrelation regularization to explicitly reduce inter-expert similarity in latent space, encouraging clearer specialization among experts by minimizing representation overlap.
AI.result: Across multiple multimodal benchmarks, the decorrelation approach consistently improves explanation faithfulness (comprehensiveness, sufficiency, AOPC) while preserving task performance. Improvements are observed in both role-based I2MoE and standard sparse MoE baselines, suggesting general applicability.
AI.conclusion: Structural role decomposition alone is insufficient to guarantee faithful explanations; representation-level separation helps improve explanation faithfulness in MoE systems.

title: DR-GS: Physically-Based Deformable and Relightable 2D Gaussians
summary: Gaussian splatting (GS) has garnered significant attention in VR/AR and digital content creation due to its explicit parameterization and efficient rendering capabilities. However, existing GS-based methods for deformable objects face two key limitations: (i) illumination is erroneously baked into textures, causing physically inconsistent responses under dynamic deformations and lighting changes; (ii) snapshot-based reconstruction restricts post-reconstruction material editing. To address these challenges, we propose Deformable and Relightable GS (DR-GS), a unified Gaussian framework that integrates physically-based inverse rendering, relighting, and deformation-aware manipulation. Through explicitly disentangling geometry, illumination, and material representations, DR-GS overcomes the limitations of static snapshots, resolving unrealistic appearance under varying conditions while enabling post-reconstruction parameter editing. Extensive experiments show that DR-GS achieves leading visual quality across static reconstruction, dynamic deformation, and relighting, reliably preserving reflections and specular highlights on glossy surfaces. It further establishes a fully decoupled geometry-illumination-material pipeline, enabling high-quality 3D asset creation and comprehensive post-editing.

AI.tldr: DR-GS is a unified Gaussian framework that disentangles geometry, illumination, and material for deformable objects, enabling physically-based inverse rendering, relighting, and post-reconstruction material editing.
AI.motivation: Existing GS-based methods for deformable objects bake illumination into textures, causing physically inconsistent responses under dynamic deformations/lighting changes, and lack post-reconstruction material editing capabilities.
AI.method: DR-GS integrates physically-based inverse rendering with Gaussian splatting, explicitly disentangling geometry, illumination, and material representations to enable deformation-aware manipulation and relighting.
AI.result: Achieves leading visual quality across static reconstruction, dynamic deformation, and relighting, reliably preserving reflections and specular highlights on glossy surfaces.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a fully decoupled geometry-illumination-material pipeline for high-quality 3D asset creation and comprehensive post-editing.

title: Linguistic Firewall: Geometry as Defense in Multi-Agent Systems Routing
comment: 8 pages (9 more for appendix), 3 figures. Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: The rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven the evolution of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), where specialized agents collaborate to execute complex workflows. Effective orchestration in these environments requires robust routing mechanisms to efficiently allocate tasks to the most suitable agent. However, existing routers fundamentally rely on unverified proxies, ranging from textual self-descriptions to static surrogate representations, to gauge an agent's competence. This reliance on non-empirical data creates a critical gap between an agent's projected profile and its actual operational capabilities, introducing severe security vulnerabilities. Malicious agents can easily misrepresent their proficiencies or harbor covert backdoors that evade both standard external analysis and static representation-learning techniques. In this work, we introduce ANTAP (Automatic Non-Textual Agent Picker), an evaluation-driven routing architecture that discards indirect proxies in favor of active capability testing. By dynamically querying agents to ascertain their true competencies empirically, ANTAP distills performance into fixed behavioral operators within a shared semantic space. At inference time, routing is performed via a purely non-textual algebraic projection, establishing a "linguistic firewall" that renders metadata-based attacks inexpressible. In our experiments, ANTAP achieves near-zero ASR against description-based injection attacks, compared to 67.3\% and above for the description-based router baseline. Against adaptive embedding attacks, ANTAP achieves substantially lower ASR than the embedding-based baseline, with a 20\% reduction, while remaining resilient to description manipulation by design.

AI.tldr: ANTAP is an evaluation-driven routing architecture for Multi-Agent Systems that discards unverified textual/embedding proxies and instead uses active capability testing to assess agents, routing via non-textual algebraic projection to create a "linguistic firewall" against attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing routers rely on unverified proxies (textual self-descriptions, static surrogate representations) to gauge agent competence, creating a gap between projected profiles and actual capabilities. This introduces security vulnerabilities where malicious agents can misrepresent proficiencies or harbor backdoors.
AI.method: ANTAP dynamically queries agents to empirically ascertain their true competencies, distills performance into fixed behavioral operators within a shared semantic space, and performs routing via non-textual algebraic projection at inference time.
AI.result: Near-zero ASR against description-based injection attacks (vs. 67.3%+ for baselines). Against adaptive embedding attacks, 20% reduction in ASR compared to embedding-based baseline. Resilient to description manipulation by design.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation-driven routing that discards indirect proxies in favor of active capability testing significantly improves security in multi-agent systems by establishing a "linguistic firewall" against metadata-based attacks.

title: t-STEP: An interpretable model for Total Electron Content predictions and irregularities estimations
comment: 40 pages, 15 figures. Note that the article has been published in the Earth Science Informatics Journal
summary: Earth system infrastructures relying on satellite-based technologies, such as Global Positioning System (GPS) communications, are affected by ionospheric Total Electron Content (TEC) gradients. Modeling these gradients under physical constraints remains challenging due to their dynamic and transient nature. While existing machine learning (ML) models can predict hourly TEC variations, it remains unclear whether their temporal resolution is sufficient to preserve small-scale TEC irregularities within predicted signals. To address this gap, we introduce an interpretable ML-based model, t-STEP, designed to predict TEC at a 30-second resolution and estimate irregularity signatures from the modeled signals. This high cadence enables the derivation of Rate of TEC changes (ROT) and the ROT Index (ROTI) as diagnostic indicators of ionospheric variability. The model is developed using GPS observations from solar cycle 24 at a station located at 5.49°S, 47.49°W. A multi-metric evaluation framework, including dynamic time warping, is used for robustness assessment, while SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides insight into feature contributions. The 30-second TEC predictions achieve 91% accuracy with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 4.38 TECU during high solar activity (2015). Compared with the International Reference Ionosphere (IRI-2020), the hourly model improves accuracy by 35%, reduces absolute errors by 57%, and increases prediction skill by 54%. More importantly, the 30-second model captures TEC irregularity dynamics and morphologies during geomagnetic storms of different intensities, outperforming an attention-based Long Short-Term Memory model under the same experimental conditions. This study demonstrates the potential of a single TEC prediction framework for scalable irregularity monitoring without requiring separate models for individual transient events.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces t-STEP, an interpretable ML model predicting Total Electron Content (TEC) at 30-second resolution, achieving 91% accuracy and capturing small-scale ionospheric irregularities during geomagnetic storms better than existing models and the IRI-2020 reference.
AI.motivation: Existing machine learning models can predict hourly TEC variations but cannot preserve small-scale TEC irregularities within predicted signals. Modeling ionospheric TEC gradients under physical constraints remains challenging due to their dynamic and transient nature.
AI.method: The authors developed t-STEP, an interpretable ML-based model that predicts TEC at 30-second resolution using GPS observations from solar cycle 24. They used multi-metric evaluation including dynamic time warping and SHAP for interpretability. Performance was compared against IRI-2020 and an attention-based LSTM model.
AI.result: 30-second TEC predictions achieved 91% accuracy with MAE of 4.38 TECU during high solar activity (2015). Compared to IRI-2020, the hourly model improved accuracy by 35%, reduced errors by 57%, and increased prediction skill by 54%. The model outperformed attention-based LSTM under same conditions.
AI.conclusion: A single TEC prediction framework (t-STEP) can serve scalable irregularity monitoring without requiring separate models for individual transient events, and can capture TEC irregularity dynamics during geomagnetic storms of different intensities.

title: The Human Creativity Benchmark
summary: Modern AI evaluation frameworks treat evaluator disagreement as noise to be resolved. In creative domains, professional disagreement reflects genuine differences in taste, not measurement error. We argue that evaluating creative AI requires preserving two distinct signals: convergence, where professionals align around shared best practices, and divergence, where individual taste legitimately varies. We present the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), a benchmark that operationalizes this separation by collecting pairwise preferences, scalar ratings on prompt adherence, usability, and visual appeal, and qualitative rationale from domain professionals. Across 15,000 professional judgments spanning five creative domains and three workflow phases (ideation, mockup, refinement), we find that convergence concentrates on verifiable dimensions like technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence concentrates on taste-driven dimensions like aesthetic direction and conceptual risk. No model excels uniformly across all phases. Collapsing these signals into a single quality metric discards the most actionable information: where models must be correct versus where they should remain steerable.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current AI evaluation frameworks that treat disagreement as noise, particularly for creative domains. It proposes the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB) that separates convergence (shared best practices) from divergence (legitimate taste differences). Based on 15,000 professional judgments across five creative domains and three workflow phases, they find convergence on technical dimensions and divergence on aesthetic ones. No model excels uniformly, and collapsing these signals loses actionable information.
AI.motivation: Current AI evaluation frameworks treat evaluator disagreement as noise to be resolved, but in creative domains, professional disagreement reflects genuine differences in taste, not measurement error.
AI.method: The Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB) collects pairwise preferences, scalar ratings on prompt adherence, usability, and visual appeal, and qualitative rationale from domain professionals, analyzing 15,000 professional judgments across five creative domains and three workflow phases (ideation, mockup, refinement).
AI.result: Convergence concentrates on verifiable dimensions (technical correctness, visual hierarchy), while divergence concentrates on taste-driven dimensions (aesthetic direction, conceptual risk). No model excels uniformly across all phases.
AI.conclusion: Collapsing convergence and divergence signals into a single quality metric discards actionable information about where models must be correct versus where they should remain steerable.

title: Fuzzing Large Language Models to Elicit Hidden Behaviours
summary: Sleeper agents are the canonical model organism of deception: models trained to behave normally but to emit an unsafe behaviour on a specific trigger. Eliciting that behaviour without knowing the trigger has not been studied systematically. We study fuzzing: injecting Gaussian noise into a model's weights or residual-stream activations and checking whether the perturbed outputs reveal the behaviour. On 6 backdoored models (7B-13B) we compare both forms of fuzzing head-to-head against temperature-sampling baselines. Fuzzing elicits the hidden behaviour more often than temperature sampling on 4 of 6 models (up to ~6x on OpenHermes-13B), and which form wins depends on the task, so both are worth running. Elicitation is uneven across each method's hyperparameter grid: a uniform sweep gives only a few percent on most models, while the best cell is 2-10x higher, so the bottleneck is hyperparameter selection, not the technique. To select hyperparameters without ground-truth access, we use a cheap proxy task (in-context secret elicitation, where a base64-encoded secret is placed in the system prompt for the model to hide) and run Thompson sampling on it to pick candidate cells, which we evaluate on the real backdoor. On the four models that can decode the secret, proxy-selected cells raise activation-fuzzing elicitation ~4x over the uniform-sweep mean (recovering ~70% of the best-cell rate on the best performing model) and weight-fuzzing by 1.3-1.8x. To our knowledge this is the first systematic study of fuzzing on sleeper-agent backdoors and the first to show proxy-task hyperparameter selection transferring to real-task elicitation. We also propose reporting such results as a (uniform-baseline, proxy-selected, oracle) triple, since these are three distinct claims that prior work has often blurred.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies fuzzing (injecting Gaussian noise into weights or activations) to elicit hidden behaviors from backdoored sleeper-agent LLMs (7B-13B). Fuzzing outperforms temperature sampling on 4/6 models. The key bottleneck is hyperparameter selection; the authors propose using a cheap proxy task (base64 secret elicitation) with Thompson sampling to select hyperparameters, which transfers effectively to real backdoor elicitation. They recommend reporting results as (uniform-baseline, proxy-selected, oracle) triples.
AI.motivation: Eliciting unsafe hidden behaviors from deceptive models without knowing the trigger has not been studied systematically. Understanding how to reliably surface such behaviors is critical for AI safety evaluations.
AI.method: The authors compare weight-fuzzing vs activation-fuzzing (injecting Gaussian noise) against temperature-sampling baselines on 6 backdoored models. For hyperparameter selection without ground truth, they design a proxy task (in-context secret elicitation with base64-encoded secrets) and use Thompson sampling to select candidate hyperparameters, then evaluate on the real backdoor.
AI.result: Fuzzing elicits hidden behavior more often than temperature sampling on 4/6 models (up to ~6x on OpenHermes-13B). Uniform hyperparameter sweeps yield only a few percent, while best cells are 2-10x higher. Proxy-selected cells raise activation-fuzzing elicitation ~4x over uniform mean (recovering ~70% of best-cell rate) and weight-fuzzing by 1.3-1.8x.
AI.conclusion: This is the first systematic study of fuzzing on sleeper-agent backdoors and the first to show proxy-task hyperparameter selection transferring to real-task elicitation. The authors recommend reporting results as (uniform-baseline, proxy-selected, oracle) triples to avoid blurring distinct claims.

title: NaLA: A 3D Native LLM Layout Agent for High-quality 3D Scene Generation
summary: Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as promising layout agents for 3D scene generation. Existing layout agents still suffer from implausible layout generation because most of them convert 3D assets and 3D layouts into textual descriptions as inputs and outputs, which involves severe information loss due to the modality gap between texts and 3D assets and 3D layouts. We propose NaLA, a native 3D LLM layout Agent for high-quality 3D scene generation by placing 3D assets in the scene. For the inputs, NaLA encodes 3D scene boundaries and 3D assets directly into the LLM, preserving fine-grained geometry and enabling explicit reasoning over relationships like collisions, surface supporting, and containment. To accurately output the positions and orientations of assets, NaLA adopts a coarse-to-fine prediction mechanism that first predicts discrete poses in an autoregressive manner and then refines the discrete poses with a continuous regression. Trained on diverse layout datasets, NaLA attains strong geometric perception and layout coherence. Experiments demonstrate that NaLA outperforms prior layout agents in both generation quality and inference efficiency, with comprehensive ablation studies to verify each component's effectiveness.

AI.tldr: NaLA is a native 3D LLM layout agent that directly encodes 3D scene boundaries and assets into an LLM, uses coarse-to-fine pose prediction, and outperforms prior methods in 3D scene generation quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based layout agents for 3D scene generation suffer from information loss due to converting 3D assets and layouts into textual descriptions, creating a modality gap between text and 3D data.
AI.method: NaLA directly encodes 3D scene boundaries and assets into the LLM to preserve geometry; uses a coarse-to-fine mechanism that first predicts discrete poses autoregressively, then refines with continuous regression.
AI.result: Outperforms prior layout agents in both generation quality and inference efficiency; ablation studies verify effectiveness of each component.
AI.conclusion: NaLA demonstrates that native 3D understanding in LLMs significantly improves layout plausibility and efficiency for 3D scene generation.

title: DOPD: Dual On-policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) offers superior capacity transfer by supervising student-sampled trajectories with dense token-level signals. To furnish high-quality supervision sources and thereby elevate the performance frontier of distillation, an intuitive direction is to infuse privileged information to either teacher or student itself. However, this additional input induces a potential failure mode we dub privilege illusion: a pattern that conflates the transferable capability gap that students are meant to close, and the information asymmetry gap that can only be mimicked but never replicated. This issue is further amplified by the inherent non-uniformity of token-level supervision, where only a small subset of tokens carries pivotal capability-bearing signals. To this end, we propose DOPD, an advantage-aware dual distillation paradigm that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student policies based on their advantage gap and relative probabilities. Each token receives supervision of different strength, objective, and strategy from either teacher or student itself, which transfers credible capability while simultaneously receiving auxiliary signals, to alleviate privilege illusion. Extensive experiments on both large language model (LLM) and vision-language model (VLM) settings demonstrate that DOPD consistently outperforms Vanilla OPD and other counterparts. Further results on stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution tasks validate its superiority.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "privilege illusion" in on-policy distillation where privileged information creates an unbridgeable gap between teacher and student, and proposes DOPD—a dual distillation paradigm that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and student policies based on advantage gaps and relative probabilities.
AI.motivation: To enhance OPD performance, researchers intuitively add privileged information to teacher/student, but this creates "privilege illusion"—confusing the transferable capability gap (students can close) with the information asymmetry gap (can only mimic). This issue is worsened by token-level supervision non-uniformity where only few tokens carry key capability signals.
AI.method: DOPD uses an advantage-aware dual distillation paradigm that routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student policies, dynamically adjusting based on advantage gaps and relative probabilities. Each token receives supervision of different strength, objective, and strategy from either teacher or student itself.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms Vanilla OPD and other counterparts on both LLM and VLM settings. Additional experiments on stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution tasks validate superiority.
AI.conclusion: DOPD effectively alleviates privilege illusion by dynamically routing supervision, enabling credible capability transfer while receiving auxiliary signals, achieving superior performance across diverse settings.

title: I-BBS: Coordinate-Free Inference of Latent Sub-Manifolds Using Random Distance Matrix Theory
summary: Bogomolny, Bohigas and Schmit (BBS) found that the spectrum of the pairwise distance matrix on N points sampled from a smooth d-dimensional manifold encodes a signature of the underlying geometry. We develop I-BBS (Inference-BBS), a coordinate-free method that identifies a low-dimensional latent sub-manifold embedded in a high-dimensional ambient distance matrix alone, without accessing an ambient high-dimensional vector space. It therefore applies even when that space is only partly observable or undefined. We model the ambient embedding by two classes of generative noise, model-based and model-free. The noise mixes the latent signal with off-manifold components, so the eigenvalues reorganise collectively and the latent geometry cannot be read off eigenvalue by eigenvalue. We recover it instead from two integer-stable signatures that survive the noise: the multiplicity of the top non-Perron multiplet, which fixes $d$, and a parameter-free law for how the multiplet positions shrink as the noise grows. On synthetic spheres $S^1$, $S^2$ and $S^3$ these integer signatures are far more stable under noise than the continuous spectral slope, and a blind test recovers both the manifold and the noise model from a single distance matrix. Applications to neural-network representations and to the dynamic training regime are developed in two companion papers.

AI.tldr: I-BBS is a coordinate-free method that recovers latent low-dimensional manifold geometry (dimension d) from noisy distance matrices using integer-stable spectral signatures (multiplet multiplicity and noise-driven shrinkage law), outperforming continuous spectral methods under noise.
AI.motivation: To infer latent geometric structure from pairwise distance matrices when the ambient high-dimensional space is only partially observable or undefined, and conventional eigenvalue-by-eigenvalue analysis fails due to noise mixing signal with off-manifold components.
AI.method: Extends the BBS framework to noisy settings by modeling ambient embedding via two noise classes (model-based and model-free). Recovers latent geometry from two integer-stable signatures: (1) multiplicity of the top non-Perron eigenvalue multiplet (estimates dimension d), (2) a parameter-free law for how multiplet positions shrink with noise.
AI.result: On synthetic spheres S¹, S², S³, integer signatures are far more stable under noise than continuous spectral slope. Blind test recovers both the manifold type and noise model from a single distance matrix.
AI.conclusion: I-BBS provides a robust, coordinate-free framework for latent manifold inference from distance matrices alone, with applications to neural network representations and dynamic training regimes (developed in companion papers).

title: Learning to Adaptively Allocate Gaussians for Arbitrary-Scale Image Super-Resolution
summary: In computer graphics, visual content is continuously warped, zoomed and resampled. This occurs when engines upscale frames, users zoom into 3D scenes, or foveated VR applies varying scaling. Handling these transformations requires Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution (ASR). Traditional models, designed for fixed scales, typically predict at a lower integer scale (e.g., x4) and rely on sub-optimal interpolation for continuous resolutions, compromising quality. Furthermore, most methods process pixels uniformly. Since fine details are sparse, this creates overhead; efficiency dictates concentrating resources only where structural complexity demands it. While implicit models and Gaussian Splatting (GS) enable continuous representation, GS is advantageous due to adaptive densification. However, transitioning GS into a feed-forward model for ASR is non-trivial. Standard GS optimization needs high-resolution gradients to drive primitive growth, which are unavailable during inference. Thus, the network must autonomously predict GS densification from low-resolution inputs. To solve this, we propose QuADA-GS. After encoding inputs into a latent space, a Neural Routing Architecture evaluates local complexity to distribute a global budget, assigning specific upsampling factors to features to avoid redundant processing. Features are dynamically densified based on these factors, forming an irregular topology decoded into 2D Gaussian primitives. To coordinate features before decoding, we introduce Hierarchical Pointer Convolution. This non-grid operator achieves O(1) neighbor lookup complexity, facilitating efficient spatial communication and bypassing dense bottlenecks. Experiments show QuADA-GS achieves state-of-the-art ASR performance, maintaining low latency and a lean memory footprint.

AI.tldr: QuADA-GS proposes a Gaussian Splatting-based feed-forward model for Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution that uses a Neural Routing Architecture to adaptively allocate computational resources based on local complexity, achieving state-of-the-art performance with low latency and memory usage.
AI.motivation: Existing ASR methods struggle with continuous resolutions due to reliance on interpolation, and they process pixels uniformly despite sparse fine details, creating inefficiency. Gaussian Splatting offers adaptive densification but is difficult to convert into a feed-forward model because it requires high-resolution gradients unavailable during inference.
AI.method: QuADA-GS encodes inputs into a latent space, uses a Neural Routing Architecture to evaluate local complexity and distribute a global budget, assigns upsampling factors to features dynamically, densifies features into an irregular topology, decodes them into 2D Gaussian primitives, and employs Hierarchical Pointer Convolution for O(1) neighbor lookup and efficient spatial communication.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution performance while maintaining low latency and a lean memory footprint.
AI.conclusion: QuADA-GS successfully adapts Gaussian Splatting into a feed-forward ASR model, overcoming the gradient limitation by having the network autonomously predict densification from low-resolution inputs, and demonstrates superior efficiency and quality.

title: Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning
summary: World models offer a principled way to equip long-horizon LLM agents with foresight: predictions of action consequences before execution. However, unreliable foresight can be ignored, misused, or even degrade downstream decision-making. In this paper, we introduce WorldEvolver, a self-evolving world model framework that revises its deployment-time context while keeping the downstream agent and all model parameters frozen. WorldEvolver integrates three modules: (i) Episodic Memory, which exploits real action transitions through retrieval-based simulation; (ii) Semantic Memory, which extracts persistent heuristic rules from prediction-observation mismatches; and (iii) Selective Foresight, which filters low-confidence predictions before integrating them into agent reasoning context. We evaluate WorldEvolver on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld, measuring world model prediction accuracy on Word2World and downstream agent success rate on AgentBoard. Extensive experiments show that WorldEvolver achieves the highest prediction accuracy across three backbones and leads other world model baselines on downstream agent success rate, demonstrating that test-time memory revision enhances both predictive fidelity and planning performance.

AI.tldr: WorldEvolver is a self-evolving world model framework that improves LLM agents' foresight by revising deployment-time context through episodic memory, semantic memory, and selective foresight, achieving superior prediction accuracy and downstream success rates on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents need reliable foresight (predicting action consequences) for better planning, but existing world models suffer from unreliable predictions that can degrade decision-making.
AI.method: WorldEvolver integrates three modules: (i) Episodic Memory (retrieval-based simulation from real action transitions), (ii) Semantic Memory (extracts heuristic rules from prediction-observation mismatches), and (iii) Selective Foresight (filters low-confidence predictions). All model parameters remain frozen.
AI.result: WorldEvolver achieves the highest prediction accuracy across three backbones on Word2World and leads other baselines on downstream agent success rate on AgentBoard.
AI.conclusion: Test-time memory revision enhances both predictive fidelity and planning performance without altering model parameters.

title: Learning as Observable Matrix Dynamics: Diffusive Relaxations versus Phase Transitions
summary: Observable Matrix Dynamics (OMD) is a diagnostic framework that probes the dynamics of high-dimensional internal representations of inputs by a neural network via a fixed-size $N \times N$ distance matrix $M(t)$ on a held set of $N$ inputs. OMD uses methods of random matrix theory and particle dynamics to explore spectral reorganisations that are missed by scalar loss functions, but are informative of the training process. We read $M(t)$ against a perturbative ambient-versus-latent decomposition extending the Bogomolny--Bohigas--Schmit (BBS) theory of random distance matrices, with per-snapshot diagnostics for the top-of-spectrum band structure and ambient noise, trajectory-level observables linking snapshots, and a 3D MDS embedding (bottom-three eigenvectors) rendering training as a moving particle cloud. Across seven experiments, diffusive regimes lack stable top-of-spectrum band structure, while sharp endogenous or externally driven reorganisations produce stable fingerprints: consistent with smooth or product latent geometries in BBS-adjacent cases, and with finite-cluster or Fourier-soliton structures otherwise. OMD thus reads the geometric regime of a representation rather than reporting a single intrinsic dimension.

AI.tldr: OMD is a diagnostic framework that uses distance matrices and random matrix theory to probe neural network internal representations, revealing geometric regimes of learning beyond scalar loss.
AI.motivation: Standard scalar loss functions miss important spectral reorganizations in high-dimensional neural network representations during training.
AI.method: Uses N×N distance matrices with BBS theory decomposition, per-snapshot diagnostics, trajectory-level observables, and 3D MDS embedding to analyze training dynamics.
AI.result: Diffusive regimes lack stable band structure; sharp reorganizations produce stable fingerprints indicating specific latent geometries (smooth, product, cluster, or soliton).
AI.conclusion: OMD reads the geometric regime of representations rather than reporting a single intrinsic dimension, enabling richer understanding of neural network training dynamics.

title: FiRe: Frequency Reparameterization as a Preconditioner for Periodic Implicit Neural Representations
summary: Periodic Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) such as SIREN and FINER assign every neuron, the same global frequency, spending the representational budget inefficiently when local signal content varies. We introduce FiRe (Frequency Reparameterization), that accelerates optimization by reparameterizing per-neuron frequency of periodic INRs without changing their underlying activation function. FiRe gives each neuron a bounded, input-dependent frequency via a separate low-rank gating path and is applicable to any periodic activation function. The gate acts as an implicit preconditioner that improves optimization conditioning at initialization via the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). This better-conditioned initialization makes optimization converge faster, and the high-frequency content of the reconstruction tracks the target more closely at a fixed computational budget. On 2D image fitting, FiRe increases PSNR over a parameter-matched baseline (up to +1 dB at short training budgets), with gains that vary with resolution and diminish at full convergence. We characterize how performance depends on resolution, rank, and training budget, and give an NTK account that predicts these trends.

AI.tldr: FiRe reparameterizes per-neuron frequencies in periodic INRs via a low-rank gating path, acting as an implicit preconditioner that improves NTK conditioning and accelerates optimization convergence.
AI.motivation: Periodic INRs (SIREN, FINER) assign all neurons the same global frequency, wasting representational capacity when local signal content varies across the domain.
AI.method: Each neuron receives a bounded, input-dependent frequency through a separate low-rank gating path, applicable to any periodic activation function. The gate acts as an implicit preconditioner improving NTK conditioning at initialization.
AI.result: Up to +1 dB PSNR gain on 2D image fitting at short training budgets, with gains varying by resolution and diminishing at full convergence. Performance trends predicted by NTK analysis.
AI.conclusion: FiRe enables faster convergence and better high-frequency reconstruction under fixed computational budget by reparameterizing per-neuron frequencies without altering the activation function.

title: Tool-Augmented Spatiotemporal Reasoning for Streamlining Video Question Answering Task
summary: Video Question Answering (VideoQA) task serves as a critical playground for evaluating whether foundation models can effectively perceive, understand, and reason about dynamic real-world scenarios. However, existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with simultaneously modeling spatial relationships within video frames and understanding the causal dynamics of temporal evolution on complex and reasoning-intensive VideoQA task. In this work, we equip MLLM with a comprehensive and extensible Video Toolkit, to enhance MLLM's spatiotemporal reasoning capabilities and ensure the harmony between the quantity and diversity of tools. To better control the tool invocation sequence and avoid toolchain shortcut issues, we propose a Spatiotemporal Reasoning Framework (STAR) that strategically schedules temporal and spatial tools, thereby progressively localizing the key area in the video. Our STAR framework enhances GPT-4o using lightweight tools, achieving an 8.2% gain on VideoMME and 4.6% on LongVideoBench. We believe that our proposed Video Toolkit and STAR framework make an important step towards building autonomous and intelligent video analysis assistants. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/fansunqi/VideoTool.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Video Toolkit and a Spatiotemporal Reasoning Framework (STAR) to enhance MLLMs' spatiotemporal reasoning for VideoQA, achieving significant gains on benchmarks (8.2% on VideoMME, 4.6% on LongVideoBench) by strategically scheduling temporal and spatial tools.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle to simultaneously model spatial relationships within video frames and understand causal temporal dynamics in complex, reasoning-intensive VideoQA tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) a comprehensive and extensible Video Toolkit to augment MLLM capabilities, and (2) a Spatiotemporal Reasoning Framework (STAR) that strategically schedules temporal and spatial tools to progressively localize key areas in video, avoiding toolchain shortcut issues.
AI.result: STAR enhances GPT-4o using lightweight tools, achieving an 8.2% gain on VideoMME and 4.6% gain on LongVideoBench.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Video Toolkit and STAR framework represent an important step toward building autonomous and intelligent video analysis assistants, with code publicly available.

title: CAREBench: A Child-Safety Risk Benchmark for Language Models
summary: How can we evaluate whether frontier AI systems recognize child-safety risks before they escalate into explicit harm? Existing child safety evaluations focus on child sexual abuse material, yet many child-safety failures begin earlier: in model assistance that helps adults manipulate, impersonate, profile, or isolate minors, and in model responses that deepen children's emotional dependence on AI systems rather than redirecting them toward human support. We introduce CAREBench (Child AI Risk Evaluation), a benchmark to assess such upstream child-safety risks in language models. CAREBench contains 500 prompts spanning twelve risk categories, including grooming and relationship engineering, deception and impersonation, surveillance and privacy, sextortion and sexual abuse, AI anthropomorphization, emotional dependency, and mental illness sensitivity. Developed with response annotations from parents and clinicians, the benchmark excludes explicit abuse material and imagery; instead, it evaluates whether models recognize, refuse, de-escalate, or redirect risky interactions before harm becomes overt. Evaluating seven frontier models on our benchmark, we find failure rates ranging from 2% to 58%, with failure patterns that vary across risk categories. CAREBench provides a responsibly scoped evaluation for LLM developers to identify and close gaps in child safety policies.

AI.tldr: CAREBench is a benchmark with 500 prompts across 12 child-safety risk categories that evaluates whether frontier AI models recognize, refuse, de-escalate, or redirect upstream child-safety risks before explicit harm occurs. Testing seven frontier models reveals failure rates from 2% to 58%.
AI.motivation: Existing child safety evaluations focus narrowly on explicit CSAM, but many child-safety failures begin earlier with model assistance in manipulation, impersonation, profiling, isolation, or fostering emotional dependency. There is a gap in evaluating these upstream risks.
AI.method: Created 500 prompts spanning 12 risk categories (grooming, deception, surveillance, sextortion, AI anthropomorphization, emotional dependency, etc.) with annotations from parents and clinicians, excluding explicit abuse material. Tested seven frontier language models on refusal, de-escalation, and redirection behaviors.
AI.result: Failure rates across frontier models ranged from 2% to 58%, with varying failure patterns across different risk categories, indicating significant gaps in current child safety policies.
AI.conclusion: CAREBench provides a responsibly scoped evaluation tool for LLM developers to identify and close gaps in child safety policies by focusing on upstream risks before harm becomes overt.

title: Can Machines Really See Objects in Images? A Study Based on Syntactic Distance and Visual Self-Referential Instances
summary: Can a vision model truly see an object, or does it only fit surface-level visual cues? Following Wittgenstein's view that the limits of language are the limits of the world, we view a model's recognition ability as bounded by the descriptive system it has learned. In current vision models, this system is often realized through learned feature representations that exploit local statistical cues. We therefore ask whether a model can still classify correctly when such local cues provide no stable basis for distinction. We formalize this question with syntactic distance, which measures class separability through the symmetry of the operations mapping one class to the other: positive distance exposes exploitable local features, whereas zero distance requires global semantics rather than local rules. We construct a visual self-referential task in maximum-variance binary noise: positive samples contain a closed square, while negative samples contain an otherwise identical square with one flipped boundary pixel. The two classes differ in global semantics but have zero syntactic distance, making local statistical shortcuts unreliable. Experiments on ResNets and Vision Transformers reveal a consistent phase-transition phenomenon, with accuracy collapsing to random guessing once the image scale crosses a critical point and does not recover within the tested range. Larger training sets and models only delay this collapse, while globally attentive ViTs reach it earlier. These results reveal a structural capability boundary of current architectures on global-concept tasks, suggesting that general intelligence may require creating new language, not reusing an existing one.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether vision models genuinely understand objects or merely exploit surface-level statistical cues. The authors introduce "syntactic distance" to measure class separability and construct a visual task where global semantics differ but local shortcuts are unavailable. Experiments on ResNets and Vision Transformers show a phase-transition phenomenon where accuracy collapses to random guessing beyond a critical image scale, revealing fundamental capability boundaries in current architectures.
AI.motivation: To challenge whether vision models truly comprehend objects (global semantics) or only fit surface-level visual cues (local statistical patterns), motivated by Wittgenstein's philosophy that recognition is bounded by the descriptive system learned.
AI.method: Formalizes "syntactic distance" measuring class separability via symmetry of operations mapping classes. Constructs a visual self-referential task with maximum-variance binary noise where positive samples contain a closed square and negative samples have one flipped boundary pixel (zero syntactic distance). Tests ResNets and Vision Transformers across varying scales.
AI.result: A consistent phase-transition phenomenon: accuracy collapses to random guessing once image scale crosses a critical point, with no recovery in tested range. Larger training sets and models only delay collapse; globally attentive ViTs reach it earlier.
AI.conclusion: Current vision architectures have structural capability boundaries on global-concept tasks, suggesting general intelligence may require creating new language rather than reusing existing descriptive systems.

title: IG-Lens: Exact Additive Probability Attribution Across Transformer Layers via Telescoping Integrated Gradients
summary: We ask a simple question about decoder-only transformers: \emph{between which two layers is the probability of a predicted token actually produced?} Existing layer-wise readout tools answer only approximately. The logit lens and its trained variant report a per-layer \emph{level} of probability but give no additive decomposition; their estimates are biased and non-monotone across depth. Direct Logit Attribution and related residual-stream methods are additive, but only in \emph{logit} space -- the softmax nonlinearity breaks additivity in probability space, precisely the quantity one usually cares about. Layer Conductance integrates gradients per layer, but attributes each to its own baseline and so does not sum to the total change in prediction. We introduce \textbf{IG-Lens}, a telescoping application of Integrated Gradients along a single path through the hidden states from a baseline to the final layer. Crediting each segment to the layer it terminates at yields a layer-wise attribution whose sum is \emph{exactly} the change in target probability, with the softmax inside the integration path rather than linearized away. Our default estimator credits each integration step its \emph{observed} change in target probability -- a prediction-aware reweighting in the spirit of IDGI -- rather than its raw gradient. Because the readout is a one-dimensional probability, this collapses each segment to a telescoping sum of endpoint values, so completeness holds exactly (to floating point) at \emph{any} step count, removing Riemann discretization error while suppressing steps that show gradient sensitivity without a change in output. We give the telescoping identity and its proof, verify completeness to floating point, and describe a single-pass batched implementation computing the full token-by-layer map without any backward call. Code: https://github.com/anhnda/IGLens.

AI.tldr: IG-Lens introduces a telescoping Integrated Gradients method for decoder-only transformers that provides exact, additive, layer-wise attributions of predicted token probability, with completeness holding precisely at any step count and a single-pass batched implementation requiring no backward calls.
AI.motivation: Existing layer-wise readout tools (Logit Lens, Direct Logit Attribution, Layer Conductance) are either approximate, biased, non-additive in probability space, or fail to sum to the total change in prediction. The authors aim to provide an exact additive decomposition of predicted token probability across layers.
AI.method: IG-Lens applies Integrated Gradients telescopically along the hidden state path from a baseline to the final layer, crediting each segment to the layer it terminates at. It uses prediction-aware reweighting (inspired by IDGI) where each integration step is credited its observed change in target probability rather than raw gradient. The one-dimensional probability collapses each segment to a telescoping sum of endpoint values, achieving exact completeness at any step count. Implementation is single-pass batched without backward calls.
AI.result: The method achieves exact completeness (to floating point) at any step count, removes Riemann discretization error, and suppresses steps showing gradient sensitivity without output change. Verified against the telescoping identity.
AI.conclusion: IG-Lens provides the first exact, additive layer-wise attribution of token probability in decoder-only transformers, with practical computational benefits (no backward pass required).

title: Bit-ViP: Leveraging Bit-planes to Preserve Visual Privacy in Images through Obfuscation
summary: The unprecedented growth of computer vision applications, such as surveillance systems and social media, raises security and visual privacy concerns, especially when data is stored on cloud servers. Image obfuscation offers a way to preserve visual privacy while maintaining an adequate level of usability; thus, it has been a topic of great interest in recent years. However, prior obfuscation schemes are either vulnerable to malicious attacks, such as model inversion to reconstruct original images from obfuscated images, or generate non-trainable obfuscated images, making them unusable for achieving reasonable accuracy. This paper proposes a novel bit-plane-based image obfuscation scheme, {\em Bit-ViP}, to preserve visual privacy for image-based recognition tasks. The Bit-ViP scheme produces secure, usable images by incorporating an innovative end-to-end obfuscation function. While doing so, the obfuscated image would contain non-invertible noise (generated by Lorenz's chaotic system and differential privacy), making it hard for an adversary to reconstruct the original image. We conduct extensive experiments on two popular activity recognition datasets, namely UCF101 and HMDB51, to validate the effectiveness of Bit-ViP. In the face of attacks on reconstruction, pixel frequency, information entropy, and pixel inter-correlation, we present a rigorous security analysis demonstrating tangible improvements over existing schemes.

AI.tldr: Bit-ViP is a bit-plane-based image obfuscation scheme that uses Lorenz's chaotic system and differential privacy to create secure, non-invertible, and trainable obfuscated images, demonstrating strong privacy-utility trade-offs on activity recognition datasets.
AI.motivation: Prior obfuscation methods either are vulnerable to model inversion attacks (which reconstruct original images) or produce non-trainable obfuscated images that fail to maintain reasonable recognition accuracy.
AI.method: A bit-plane-based end-to-end obfuscation function incorporating Lorenz's chaotic system and differential privacy to generate non-invertible noise, producing secure yet usable (trainable) images.
AI.result: Extensive validation on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets shows tangible improvements over existing schemes against reconstruction, pixel frequency, information entropy, and pixel inter-correlation attacks.
AI.conclusion: Bit-ViP effectively balances visual privacy and recognition utility, outperforming prior obfuscation schemes in security and usability.

title: A Diagnostic Framework and Multi-Evaluator Audit of Evaluator-Driven Preference Dynamics in Self-Adapting LLM Agents
summary: Measurements of proprietary LLM evaluators can become invalid within weeks -- we document one case and provide the diagnostic framework to detect it. We introduce EPC -- comprising the Multimodal Preference Collapse Index (MPCI), evaluator-indexed coupling matrix, and Jensen-Shannon divergence (JSD) -- and apply it across eight experimental conditions (N=112 main + N=10 ablation = 122 unique repetitions, all reported).
