AI.conclusion: T3R demonstrates strong potential for developing an adaptation pipeline for graph-based systems where conventional fine-tuning or retraining is infeasible.

title: Early Warning Signals for OpenVLA Failure under Visual Distribution Shift
summary: Vision Language Action models combine perception, language grounding, and control in a single policy, but their failures are hard to diagnose once visual conditions shift. We test whether OpenVLA feedforward activations contain linearly decodable information about near term task failure in LIBERO manipulation rollouts. The policy is fixed throughout. We log internal activations during execution and fit lightweight monitors after the rollouts are collected. Occlusion is the main controlled stress test. It reduces OpenVLA success from $57\%$ to $17\%$ over $100$ episodes per condition. Under this shift, a logistic probe at layer 16 reaches AUROC $0.972$ and AUPRC $0.352$ for predicting failure within a $15$ step horizon. It outperforms both a mean difference direction and an action disagreement baseline. A sparse layer sweep finds uneven decodability across depth: layer 16 is strongest among the tested layers, layer 8 remains informative, and layer 10 is weaker. To check whether the monitor is just an occlusion detector, we also evaluate color shift and camera jitter without refitting. Color shift produces no failures in this setting, so it is a benign control rather than a failure benchmark. Camera jitter does induce failures, and the occlusion trained monitor remains above random. The result is deliberately limited: OpenVLA internal states contain failure relevant structure under controlled perceptual shift, but these experiments do not establish a causal mechanism, task held out generalization, or a deployable recovery system.

AI.tldr: OpenVLA internal activations at layer 16 can predict near-term task failure under occlusion (AUROC 0.972), outperforming baselines, but the study is limited to controlled shifts with no causal or generalization claims.
AI.motivation: Vision Language Action models are hard to diagnose when visual conditions shift; the authors aim to test whether internal activations of a fixed OpenVLA policy contain linearly decodable signals about imminent task failure.
AI.method: Fixed OpenVLA policy logs internal activations during LIBERO manipulation rollouts under occlusion (and later color shift, camera jitter). Lightweight logistic probes are fit post-hoc to predict failure within a 15-step horizon. Layer sweep across depths and comparison to baselines (mean difference direction, action disagreement) are performed.
AI.result: Occlusion drops success from 57% to 17%. Layer 16 logistic probe achieves AUROC 0.972, AUPRC 0.352, outperforming baselines. Layer 16 is strongest, layer 8 informative, layer 10 weaker. Occlusion-trained monitor transfers partially to camera jitter but not to color shift (benign control).
AI.conclusion: OpenVLA internal states contain failure-relevant structure under perceptual shift, but results are limited: no causal mechanism, no held-out task generalization, and no deployable recovery system established.

title: Heads, Not Backbones: Output Heads Dominate Architectures on Fat-Tailed Returns
summary: In a deep forecasting pipeline for fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons, which matters more - the backbone architecture or the output head? We compare four modern backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) under three output heads: a point head, a single-Gaussian density head, and a Gaussian mixture density head with K=4 components. On S and P 500 monthly log-returns (1871-2023) under anchored walk-forward validation, the three heads form a strict gradient: switching from point to Gaussian improves CRPS by about 1.3 percent; switching from Gaussian to mixture adds a further about 2.4 percent. Switching between backbones, in contrast, changes CRPS by less than 1.5 percent on the point-head row and on the backbone-mean axis; density-head backbone spread is larger (up to 5.1 percent on the h=1 Gaussian row, driven by N-BEATS) but the head gradient (3.7 percentage points) still dominates. The Model Confidence Set on squared errors does not exclude any of the 12 variants at the 5 percent level: the head separates them only on distributional metrics (CRPS, pinball, coverage), not on squared error. The mixture head incremental value over a single Gaussian is largest in the highest-volatility regimes (13.9 percent in 1970s stagflation at h=12), confirming the mixture captures tail risk beyond what a unimodal Gaussian can express. The picture is horizon-dependent: the head dominates at short horizons, but at long horizons (h >= 6) the backbone re-takes the lead - an h-split we document against classical baselines (section 5.1). We conclude that on fat-tailed returns at short horizons, the head dominates the backbone, and the mixture distribution adds genuine value over a single Gaussian during crisis periods when risk-management decisions actually matter.

AI.tldr: This paper compares four deep learning backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) and three output heads (point, Gaussian, Gaussian mixture) for forecasting fat-tailed financial returns. The output head dominates performance at short horizons, with mixture heads adding significant value during high-volatility crisis periods.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the backbone architecture or the output head matters more in a deep forecasting pipeline for fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons.
AI.method: Compare four modern backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) under three output heads (point, single-Gaussian, Gaussian mixture with K=4) on S&P 500 monthly log-returns (1871-2023) using anchored walk-forward validation.
AI.result: Heads form a strict gradient: point→Gaussian improves CRPS by ~1.3%, Gaussian→mixture adds ~2.4%. Backbone changes affect CRPS by <1.5% (point-head row). At short horizons, head dominates backbone; at long horizons (h≥6), backbone re-takes the lead. Mixture head adds 13.9% improvement during 1970s stagflation at h=12.
AI.conclusion: On fat-tailed returns at short horizons, the output head dominates the backbone architecture. Mixture distributions add genuine value over single Gaussian during crisis periods when risk management decisions matter most.

title: UniVAD v2: Unified Visual Anomaly Detection via Support-Conditioned Boundary Construction
summary: Unified visual anomaly detection seeks to train a single detector that can be deployed across categories, domains, and application scenarios. In the few-shot transfer regime, the key challenge is to estimate an episode-specific boundary for an unseen target category from a small support set. Existing approaches mainly infer this boundary from normal-side evidence and provide limited abnormal-side evidence for deployment-specific tolerance. Within the normal side, they often struggle to jointly capture local correspondences and global support-query relations, making their boundaries less reliable for unseen anomalies. To address these issues, we propose UniVAD v2, a two-sided support-conditioned boundary construction framework for unified visual anomaly detection. Built on the component-patch divide-and-conquer framework of UniVAD, UniVAD v2 strengthens the normal side with an Optimal Transport-based Relational Modeling module (OTRM), which complements retrieval with support-query matching through transport-style allocation, and an Adaptive Coordination mechanism for Retrieval and Relational Modeling (ACRRM), which estimates episode-conditioned reliabilities to fuse the two sources of evidence. On the abnormal side, a Few-Shot Abnormal Reference module (FAR) converts optional abnormal references into rejection-side evidence for boundary adjustment. Experiments on six datasets spanning industrial, logical, and medical anomaly detection demonstrate strong cross-domain generalization. Under the 1N-shot protocol, UniVAD v2 improves the mean image-level AUC over UniVAD from 83.0\% to 84.5\%, and further reaches 85.7\% in the 1N+1A-shot setting. On the MVTec-AD Severity Split (MVTec-AD-SS), UniVAD v2 achieves 96.2\% image-level AUC and 96.9\% pixel-level AUC, showing that abnormal references enable controllable boundary customization without retraining.

AI.tldr: UniVAD v2 improves unified visual anomaly detection by constructing two-sided (normal + abnormal) support-conditioned boundaries, using Optimal Transport-based relational modeling and adaptive coordination for normal-side evidence, plus a few-shot abnormal reference module for controllable boundary adjustment, achieving new SOTA across six datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing few-shot unified anomaly detection methods rely only on normal-side evidence, struggle to jointly capture local correspondences and global support-query relations, and provide limited abnormal-side evidence for deployment-specific tolerance.
AI.method: Proposes UniVAD v2 with: (1) OTRM for optimal-transport-based support-query matching, (2) ACRRM for adaptive fusion of retrieval and relational evidence, and (3) FAR module that converts optional abnormal references into rejection-side evidence for boundary adjustment.
AI.result: Improves mean image-level AUC from 83.0% to 84.5% (1N-shot) and 85.7% (1N+1A-shot) across six datasets. On MVTec-AD-SS, achieves 96.2% image-level and 96.9% pixel-level AUC.
AI.conclusion: Abnormal references enable controllable boundary customization without retraining, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization across industrial, logical, and medical anomaly detection.

title: Building Multi-Task Agentic LLMs via Two-Phase Distillation
summary: A key step toward artificial general intelligence is to train models that can perform multiple tasks. In this paper, we study how to build such models by first training separate RL experts for individual tasks and then consolidating them via distillation, as an alternative to directly training a single model on mixed tasks. We show that off-policy distillation degrades in multi-task settings due to the mode-covering nature of forward KL: aggregating data from multiple tasks introduces a large number of behavioral modes that can exceed the student's capacity, forcing it to average across behaviors and leading to degraded performance. In contrast, on-policy distillation is mode-seeking but requires strong initialization. Inspired by these observations, we propose a two-phase approach: off-policy distillation followed by on-policy refinement. Evaluation across conversational agents and text-based games confirms that this two-phase approach matches single-task RL expert performance for each individual task, whereas off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails to match this performance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a two-phase distillation approach (off-policy then on-policy) to consolidate multiple RL experts into a single multi-task model, outperforming either distillation method alone.
AI.motivation: To build models capable of performing multiple tasks (toward AGI), the paper explores consolidating separate RL experts via distillation as an alternative to direct multi-task training.
AI.method: A two-phase approach: (1) off-policy distillation (forward KL) for initial consolidation, followed by (2) on-policy refinement (mode-seeking) to address the mode-covering degradation in multi-task off-policy distillation.
AI.result: The two-phase approach matches single-task RL expert performance across conversational agents and text-based games, while off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails.
AI.conclusion: Combining off-policy distillation (initialization) with on-policy refinement (fine-tuning) effectively consolidates multi-task RL experts into a single model without performance loss.

title: Accurate Recognition of Pneumonia and COVID-19 by Geometric Shape Normalization of Lung Region using Automatic Landmark Detection and Piecewise Affine Warping
summary: This paper presents an automatic system for recognizing pulmonary diseases in chest X-rays using geometric normalization of the lung region. The method combines three modules: (1) a ResNet-18 landmark detector with coordinate attention that predicts 15 lung-contour landmarks, achieving a mean localization error of 3.61 pixels through an ensemble of four models with test-time augmentation; (2) a geometric normalizer based on Generalized Procrustes Analysis, Delaunay triangulation, and piecewise affine warping to map each lung region to a standardized shape; and (3) a ResNet-18 classifier with transfer learning and SAHS contrast enhancement to classify images as COVID-19, Viral Pneumonia, or Normal. On the COVID-19 Radiography Database, the normalized-image classifier achieved 98.60+/-0.26% accuracy and 98.00% F1-Macro using five-fold cross-validation. Although original images produced slightly higher raw accuracy, Grad-CAM and cropping experiments suggest that this advantage is partly influenced by acquisition artifacts. In contrast, geometrically normalized images outperformed artifact-masked/cropped unaligned images on both the COVID-19 Radiography Database (98.60% vs. 96.24%) and a balanced adult-pediatric mixed dataset including pediatric cases from the Kermany dataset (94.67% vs. 94.17%). These results suggest that anatomical alignment can provide a more controlled and artifact-resistant representation for pulmonary disease recognition.

AI.tldr: A pulmonary disease recognition system for chest X-rays combining lung landmark detection, geometric normalization via Procrustes analysis and warping, and a ResNet-18 classifier achieves 98.6% accuracy on COVID-19 detection, with normalization reducing artifact bias compared to raw images.
AI.motivation: Standard chest X-ray classification is susceptible to acquisition artifacts (e.g., patient positioning, machine variation) rather than true pathological features. The authors aim to provide an artifact-resistant representation through anatomical alignment of lung regions.
AI.method: Three-module pipeline: (1) ResNet-18 landmark detector with coordinate attention predicting 15 lung-contour landmarks (ensemble of 4 models + TTA, 3.61px error); (2) geometric normalizer using Generalized Procrustes Analysis, Delaunay triangulation, and piecewise affine warping; (3) ResNet-18 classifier with transfer learning and SAHS contrast enhancement for COVID-19, Viral Pneumonia, or Normal classification.
AI.result: 98.60+/-0.26% accuracy, 98.00% F1-Macro on COVID-19 Radiography Database. Geometrically normalized images outperformed artifact-masked/cropped unaligned images (98.60% vs. 96.24% on COVID-19 DB; 94.67% vs. 94.17% on mixed adult-pediatric dataset). Grad-CAM revealed raw-image advantage partly due to artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Anatomical alignment via geometric normalization provides a more controlled, artifact-resistant representation for pulmonary disease recognition, reducing reliance on spurious correlations from acquisition artifacts.

title: From Failure Taxonomy to Intervention: A Diagnostic Methodology for Industry-Scale AVLM in Video and Live-Streaming Platform Moderation
summary: Industry-scale video and live-streaming moderation imposes requirements that are difficult to satisfy with generic pretrained public models or external APIs, including adaptation to platform-specific data distributions, policy-specific objectives, and product-level safety constraints. As a result, platforms must undertake internal model development, naturally turning to shared public research for guidance. However, existing multimodal foundation-model studies primarily report architectures, training recipes, data scaling strategies, and benchmark results, but provide less systematic guidance on how failures should be localized and translated into targeted model-development interventions. Interventions are essential because deployment failures are rarely self-explanatory. Similar failures can originate from different causes. Without targeted interventions, improvement reduces to heuristic trial-and-error, where benchmark improvements are weakly attributable, and failures are difficult to trace to their underlying causes. To address this gap, we present a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Models AVLM development. The methodology maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures and links each class of failure to an intervention space. We instantiate this methodology across the development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model for a large-scale video and live-streaming platform. The resulting system supports over 100 regions and is designed for noisy, ambiguous, and highly diverse content drawn from global platform traffic.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Models (AVLM), mapping failures into a taxonomy of observable signatures linked to intervention spaces, instantiated for a large-scale video/live-streaming platform across 100+ regions.
AI.motivation: Industry-scale content moderation requires adaptation to platform-specific data, policies, and safety constraints. Existing multimodal foundation-model research lacks systematic guidance on localizing failures and translating them into targeted model-development interventions, leading to heuristic trial-and-error.
AI.method: A diagnostic methodology that maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures, with each failure class linked to a corresponding intervention space. Applied across the full development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model.
AI.result: The system supports over 100 regions and handles noisy, ambiguous, and highly diverse platform traffic content. It provides a structured failure-to-intervention mapping for targeted model improvement.
AI.conclusion: Systematic failure diagnosis enables targeted interventions over heuristic trial-and-error, improving attribution and traceability of failures in industrial-scale multimodal model development.

title: AerialMetric: Benchmarking and Adapting UAV Monocular Metric Depth Estimation in the Real World
summary: This paper addresses the problem of monocular metric depth estimation in aerial UAV imagery. Although recent data-driven methods have achieved remarkable progress in ground-level scenarios, models trained primarily on street-view and indoor datasets exhibit significant domain gaps when applied to aerial viewpoints. To tackle these challenges, we introduce AerialMetric, a benchmark dataset designed to evaluate and facilitate the adaptation of monocular metric depth estimation under UAV aerial viewpoints. The dataset consists of four complementary subsets collected from different sources, jointly covering real-world photogrammetry data, controlled aerial acquisition settings, photorealistic synthetic scenes, and in-the-wild Internet imagery. Totally, AerialMetric provides 52K real-world and 16K synthetic image-depth pairs with reliable metric ground truth. Based on this dataset, we conduct systematic evaluations of existing state-of-the-art models under aerial settings and investigate the impact of viewpoint, altitude, and camera parameters on metric depth prediction. In addition, by fine-tuning representative metric depth model on our dataset, we establish a comprehensive aerial benchmark and achieve state-of-the-art performance across diverse aerial imagery. Our dataset, code, and model weight are publicly available at https://kuieless.github.io/AerialMetric-ECCV2026-page/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces AerialMetric, a benchmark dataset for monocular metric depth estimation in aerial UAV imagery, comprising 52K real and 16K synthetic image-depth pairs from diverse sources. Through systematic evaluations and fine-tuning, the authors establish a comprehensive aerial benchmark and achieve state-of-the-art performance across diverse aerial viewpoints.
AI.motivation: Existing monocular metric depth estimation models trained on street-view and indoor datasets suffer from significant domain gaps when applied to aerial UAV viewpoints. There is a lack of dedicated benchmark datasets to evaluate and adapt these models for aerial scenarios.
AI.method: The authors construct AerialMetric, a dataset with four subsets covering real-world photogrammetry, controlled aerial acquisition, photorealistic synthetic scenes, and in-the-wild Internet imagery. They conduct systematic evaluations of state-of-the-art models and fine-tune a representative metric depth model on the dataset.
AI.result: AerialMetric provides 52K real-world and 16K synthetic image-depth pairs with reliable metric ground truth. Fine-tuning on this dataset achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse aerial imagery.
AI.conclusion: The AerialMetric dataset effectively bridges the domain gap for monocular metric depth estimation in aerial UAV imagery, enabling adaptation and improved performance. The dataset, code, and model weights are publicly released.

title: Data-Driven Energy-Based Learning via Gibbs Measures on Hierarchical Structures
summary:
We introduce a data-driven probabilistic framework for learning systems based on Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures. Unlike standard empirical risk minimization, where a dataset is used to identify a single optimal parameter, our approach transforms the empirical loss function into an interaction potential defining an energy-based model. The resulting Gibbs distribution describes a family of equilibrium learning states generated by the data.
  We formulate the consistency conditions of the associated finite-volume distributions and derive nonlinear integral fixed-point equations whose solutions characterize the admissible learning states. These equations provide a rigorous connection between empirical loss landscapes and probabilistic inference on trees. For translation-invariant solutions, the problem reduces to the analysis of positive compact operators induced by data-dependent kernels, allowing us to establish existence and uniqueness conditions in the one-dimensional setting.
  Furthermore, we show that hierarchical learning systems may exhibit phase-transition phenomena: for certain empirical kernels on Cayley trees, multiple Gibbs measures emerge beyond a critical inverse temperature, corresponding to distinct equilibrium prediction regimes. Numerical experiments with non-separable kernels illustrate the appearance of multiple solution branches and demonstrate the coexistence of several data-induced learning states.
  Our results provide a new perspective on energy-based learning, where data do not merely determine an optimal model through minimization but define an entire probabilistic landscape of possible inference states.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a probabilistic framework using Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures, transforming empirical loss into an interaction potential to define a family of learning states. It derives consistency conditions and fixed-point equations, shows existence/uniqueness conditions for one-dimensional translation-invariant solutions, and demonstrates phase transitions on Cayley trees where multiple Gibbs measures emerge beyond a critical temperature. Numerical experiments confirm multiple solution branches and coexisting learning states.
AI.motivation: Standard empirical risk minimization identifies a single optimal parameter from data. The authors aim to move beyond this limitation by modeling learning as a probabilistic landscape of possible inference states, where data define an entire family of equilibrium learning states rather than a unique solution.
AI.method: The method transforms empirical loss functions into interaction potentials of energy-based models, employing Gibbs measures on hierarchical (tree-like) structures. Consistency conditions lead to nonlinear integral fixed-point equations. For translation-invariant cases, analysis reduces to positive compact operators induced by data-dependent kernels.
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness conditions are established for one-dimensional translation-invariant settings. Phase transitions are discovered on Cayley trees: multiple Gibbs measures emerge beyond a critical inverse temperature. Numerical experiments with non-separable kernels confirm multiple solution branches and coexisting learning states.
AI.conclusion: Data define a probabilistic landscape of possible inference states rather than a single optimal model via minimization. This offers a new perspective on energy-based learning, where phase transitions and multiple equilibrium prediction regimes naturally arise from the hierarchical structure of the learning system.

title: HTC-SGA Former: A Hybrid Transformer-CNN Network with Self-Guided Attention and a New Boundary-Weighted Adaptive Loss for Coronary DSA Vessel Segmentation
comment: 20 pages, 10 figures, 3 tables. Submitted for journal review
summary: Accurate coronary Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) vessel segmentation is essential for computer-aided diagnosis and treatment planning of coronary artery disease (CAD). However, thin low-contrast vessels, background interference, and severe vessel-background class imbalance make reliable segmentation of weak distal branches and vessel boundaries challenging. Existing methods struggle to balance global contextual reasoning with preservation of weak vessels, vessel continuity, and fine boundaries. To address these limitations, we propose HTC-SGA Former, a lightweight hybrid Transformer-CNN framework for coronary DSA vessel segmentation. It employs a CNN encoder for local vessel morphology extraction and a Transformer decoder for contextual feature modeling. A Multi-Scale Global-Local Window Attention (MS-GLWA) block performs efficient global-local contextual modeling, while a Self-Guided Feature Attention (SGFA) module enhances weak-vessel responses. In addition, a Boundary-Weighted Adaptive Compound Loss (BWACL) emphasizes thin-vessel boundaries and adaptively balances vessel recovery and boundary refinement. Experiments on private right and left coronary artery DSA subsets show that HTC-SGA Former outperforms 14 state-of-the-art segmentation methods while maintaining a compact architecture with only 0.81M parameters. BWACL also improves performance over binary cross-entropy and Dice losses across four encoder-decoder architectures, demonstrating strong cross-backbone applicability. HTC-SGA Former improves thin-vessel recovery, vessel continuity, and boundary localization through complementary global-local contextual modeling, vessel-focused refinement, and adaptive optimization, supporting reliable and computationally efficient coronary vessel analysis for future computer-assisted cardiovascular interventions.

AI.tldr: HTC-SGA Former is a lightweight hybrid Transformer-CNN framework (0.81M parameters) for coronary DSA vessel segmentation that outperforms 14 SOTA methods by combining multi-scale global-local attention, self-guided feature attention, and boundary-weighted adaptive loss for improved thin-vessel and boundary segmentation.
AI.motivation: Existing DSA vessel segmentation methods struggle with thin low-contrast vessels, background interference, severe class imbalance, and cannot balance global contextual reasoning with preservation of weak vessels, vessel continuity, and fine boundaries.
AI.method: A lightweight hybrid Transformer-CNN framework with: (1) CNN encoder for local vessel morphology + Transformer decoder for contextual features; (2) Multi-Scale Global-Local Window Attention (MS-GLWA) for efficient context modeling; (3) Self-Guided Feature Attention (SGFA) for weak-vessel enhancement; (4) Boundary-Weighted Adaptive Compound Loss (BWACL) emphasizing thin-vessel boundaries.
AI.result: Outperforms 14 SOTA methods on private right/left coronary DSA datasets with only 0.81M parameters. BWACL improves over BCE and Dice losses across four encoder-decoder architectures, demonstrating cross-backbone applicability.
AI.conclusion: HTC-SGA Former improves thin-vessel recovery, vessel continuity, and boundary localization via complementary global-local modeling, vessel-focused refinement, and adaptive optimization, supporting reliable and computationally efficient coronary vessel analysis for future computer-assisted cardiovascular interventions.

title: Neural Subspace Reallocation: Continual Learning as Retrieval-Based Subspace Memory Management
summary: We introduce Neural Subspace Reallocation (NSR), which reframes continual learning as memory management over parameter subspaces. Instead of treating Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules as disposable per-task adapters, NSR manages them as compressible, retrievable memory units on a frozen backbone through a recurring cycle: (1) compress learned LoRAs via SVD, (2) reserve them in a TaskKnowledgeBank, (3) recall related past LoRAs by embedding similarity to warm-start new or returning tasks, and (4) reallocate the active subspace accordingly, with distillation protecting prior tasks. We prove that in cyclic environments any memoryless allocation policy incurs cumulative regret Omega(T(M-1)Delta_switch) relative to a history-aware policy backed by the Bank (Theorem 1). Empirically, on Split-CIFAR-100 the Bank reduces cyclic recovery time by 10x, exactly as predicted, and on the heterogeneous 5-Datasets benchmark NSR achieves the highest accuracy and the least forgetting, about 9x closer to zero backward transfer than the memoryless heuristics. Crucially, we run a controlled study that isolates which component matters: holding the Bank fixed and varying only the allocation rule, we find that a simple similarity-based retrieval rule matches or beats a learned reinforcement-learning controller (recovering recurring tasks in 0 vs 1.8 steps and reaching equal accuracy). Our central, honest finding is therefore that the memory mechanism -- compression and similarity retrieval -- rather than a learned allocation policy, drives continual-learning performance under fixed capacity. A memory-budget analysis confirms the compressed Bank stays small -- 0.29 MB of parameter memory per task -- so a top-K retention cap bounds the total footprint while preserving fast recovery for retained tasks.

AI.tldr: NSR reframes continual learning as memory management over parameter subspaces using compressible, retrievable LoRA units in a TaskKnowledgeBank, achieving faster recovery and less forgetting than memoryless allocation policies.
AI.motivation: Existing methods treat LoRA modules as disposable per-task adapters, ignoring the potential to reuse learned knowledge across recurring tasks. The paper aims to show that memory mechanisms (compression and similarity retrieval) rather than learned allocation policies drive continual learning performance.
AI.method: NSR operates via a recurring cycle: (1) compress learned LoRAs via SVD, (2) reserve them in a TaskKnowledgeBank, (3) recall related past LoRAs by embedding similarity, and (4) reallocate the active subspace with distillation. The method includes theoretical regret analysis against memoryless policies.
AI.result: On Split-CIFAR-100, the Bank reduces cyclic recovery time by 10x. On 5-Datasets, NSR achieves highest accuracy and ~9x less backward transfer vs memoryless heuristics. A controlled study shows similarity-based retrieval matches or beats a learned RL controller (0 vs 1.8 recovery steps). Compressed Bank uses only 0.29 MB per task.
AI.conclusion: The memory mechanism (compression + similarity retrieval) is the key driver of continual learning performance under fixed capacity, not the learned allocation policy.

title: LEIQ-Assessor: Multi-dimensional Quality Assessment of Low-light Enhanced Images via Multi-task Learning
comment: The paper achieved second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge on Low-light Enhanced Image Quality Assessment
summary: Low-light image enhancement algorithms (LIEAs) aim to improve the visibility of images captured under poor illumination. However, the enhancement process often introduces artifacts such as noise amplification, color shift, structural damage, and over-exposure, which degrade the perceptual quality of the enhanced images. Therefore, a reliable image quality assessment (IQA) metric for evaluating enhancement effects is of great importance for both the development of LIEAs and their practical applications. In this paper, we present \textbf{LEIQ-Assessor}, a multi-dimensional quality assessment model for low-light image enhancement based on multi-task learning, developed for the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge on Low-light Enhanced Image Quality Assessment. Specifically, our method leverages a pre-trained SigLIP2 Vision Transformer as the backbone and simultaneously predicts the overall Mean Opinion Score (MOS) together with six perceptual sub-attributes: lightness, color fidelity, noise level, exposure quality, naturalness, and content recovery. By jointly optimizing these correlated objectives via the PLCC loss, the shared representation captures richer quality-aware features than its single-task counterpart. Experiments on the MLE benchmark demonstrate that LEIQ-Assessor significantly outperforms existing no-reference IQA models and hand-crafted quality descriptors. Our method achieved second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge on Low-light Enhanced Image Quality Assessment. The code is available at https://github.com/sunwei925/LEIQ-Assessor.

AI.tldr: LEIQ-Assessor is a multi-task learning model for assessing low-light image enhancement quality, using a SigLIP2 Vision Transformer backbone to predict MOS and six perceptual sub-attributes, achieving second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge.
AI.motivation: Low-light image enhancement algorithms often introduce artifacts (noise, color shift, structural damage, over-exposure), so a reliable IQA metric is needed to evaluate enhancement effects for both algorithm development and practical applications.
AI.method: Uses a pre-trained SigLIP2 Vision Transformer as backbone with multi-task learning to simultaneously predict overall MOS and six perceptual sub-attributes (lightness, color fidelity, noise level, exposure quality, naturalness, content recovery), jointly optimized via PLCC loss.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing no-reference IQA models and hand-crafted quality descriptors on the MLE benchmark, achieving second place in the QoMEX 2026 Grand Challenge.
AI.conclusion: Multi-task learning with shared representations captures richer quality-aware features than single-task approaches, proving effective for low-light enhanced image quality assessment.

title: Predictive Objectives Discard Exogenous Control-Relevant Features: A Controlled Mechanistic Study
comment: 15 pages 3 tables 5 figures for associated github repo see https://github.com/bushesarebetter/jepa_research_project
summary: Joint-embedding predictive (JEPA-style) objectives learn representations by predicting future latents. In doing so they can discard features that are exogenous (uncontrollable by the agent) yet control-relevant, even when those features are trivially encodable. This occurs because the objective optimizes temporal predictability rather than control-relevance. We isolate this failure mode in a controlled 2x2 experimental design that varies feature controllability and relevance independently, using a predictability knob that decouples a feature's temporal predictability from its control-relevance. Comparing six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under a random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA, we observe that all evaluated reward-free predictive objectives leave the exogenous control-relevant feature near chance accuracy, while a reward-grounded variant retains it selectively. The remedy is label-efficient and robust: as little as 2% of reward-labeled transitions recovers the feature, the effect holds across two environments with different surface forms, and it persists across latent dimensions from 16 to 1024. Comparing the learned latent geometry against bisimulation theory's prediction, the JEPA latent realizes only a small fraction of the class separation a supervised reference attains.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that JEPA-style predictive objectives fail to retain exogenous but control-relevant features, while reward-grounded variants can selectively recover them with as little as 2% labeled data.
AI.motivation: To identify and isolate a failure mode in JEPA-style objectives where they discard exogenous yet control-relevant features due to optimizing temporal predictability over control-relevance.
AI.method: Controlled 2x2 experimental design varying feature controllability and relevance independently, with a predictability knob. Compare six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA.
AI.result: All reward-free predictive objectives leave exogenous control-relevant features near chance accuracy. Reward-grounded variant retains it selectively. 2% reward-labeled transitions suffices. Effect holds across environments and latent dimensions (16-1024). JEPA latent achieves only small fraction of class separation vs supervised reference.
AI.conclusion: Reward-free predictive objectives fundamentally fail on exogenous control-relevant features; reward-grounded objectives offer a label-efficient and robust remedy.

title: MR-IQA: A Unified Margin View of Regression and Ranking for Blind Image Quality Assessment
summary: Blind image quality assessment (BIQA) is commonly built on two basic learning paradigms: regression and ranking. Regression calibrates absolute scores, whereas ranking recovers quality structure from ordinal relations. Although joint regression-ranking supervision often improves BIQA, the relation between the two paradigms remains largely empirical and underexplored. In this work, we revisit what underlies regression and ranking and identify pairwise relational distance, termed quality margin, as their common bridge. Our derivation shows that, at the objective-optimization level, both paradigms fit quality margins: regression fits margins induced by score endpoints, while ranking fits transformed or sign-level margins through preference probabilities. Motivated by this insight, we propose MR-IQA, a direct quality-margin optimization framework for reinforcement learning (RL)-based BIQA. MR-IQA samples quality scores and optimizes pairwise margin errors as policy rewards, thereby modeling quality structure more explicitly. Experiments on six BIQA benchmarks show competitive general performance, and controlled comparisons demonstrate that MR-IQA achieves the strongest average PLCC/SRCC over regression- or ranking-based RL methods. Our findings provide a new insight into unifying regression and ranking, offering a theoretical basis for understanding quality-structure modeling in BIQA and beyond.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that both regression and ranking in BIQA fundamentally fit quality margins, and proposes MR-IQA, a reinforcement learning framework that directly optimizes pairwise margin errors, achieving strong performance across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The relation between regression and ranking in BIQA remains empirical and underexplored; the authors aim to theoretically unify these two paradigms and leverage their common ground for better quality assessment.
AI.method: MR-IQA is a direct quality-margin optimization framework using reinforcement learning: it samples quality scores and optimizes pairwise margin errors as policy rewards, explicitly modeling quality structure.
AI.result: Competitive general performance on six BIQA benchmarks; strongest average PLCC/SRCC compared to regression- or ranking-based RL methods in controlled comparisons.
AI.conclusion: The work unifies regression and ranking under a quality-margin perspective, providing a theoretical basis for understanding quality-structure modeling in BIQA and beyond.

title: Online Data Selection for Instruction Tuning via Gaussian Processes
summary: With Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training and fine-tuning shifting its focus from data volume to data quality, quality data selection has emerged as a critical research topic. Existing online data selection methods for LLM training are typically "batch-constrained", limiting optimization to local utility within random batches. To overcome this, we propose GAIA (Global Adaptive Instruction tuning via GAussian processes), a framework that formulates data valuation as a global estimation process. GAIA employs Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across the semantic space, utilizing an adaptive strategy fusion mechanism to dynamically prioritize high-utility samples. By casting the strategy-posterior update as an instance of the classical fixed-share Hedge framework for tracking the best expert, we inherit a dynamic-regret guarantee that characterizes GAIA's robustness under non-stationary quality scores during training. Empirical evaluations on three datasets demonstrate that GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like \greats, establishing our method as a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning.

AI.tldr: GAIA uses Gaussian Processes to globally model and dynamically select high-quality data for LLM instruction tuning, outperforming batch-constrained methods with theoretical robustness guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing online data selection methods for LLM training are "batch-constrained" (limited to local utility within random batches), failing to globally optimize data quality selection.
AI.method: GAIA employs Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across semantic space, with an adaptive strategy fusion mechanism (formulated as fixed-share Hedge framework) for dynamic prioritization of high-utility samples.
AI.result: GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like \greats on three datasets, with dynamic-regret guarantees for robustness under non-stationary quality scores.
AI.conclusion: GAIA provides a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning, shifting from local batch-constrained optimization to global adaptive data valuation.

title: TopoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Automated Topology Learning in Medical Imaging
summary: Topological data analysis (TDA), particularly persistent homology (PH), captures geometric structural properties in medical images (e.g., connected components, loops, shape characteristics), which conventional pixel-level deep learning approaches often neglect. While many topological descriptors are known for converting persistence diagrams (PDs) or raw images into topological feature vectors, existing methods mostly default to a single fixed descriptor (e.g., persistence images), leaving the diversity of topological representations largely unexplored. To the best of our knowledge, there is no known large language model (LLM)-based agentic framework that can automatically determine the most suitable topological descriptors for a given image dataset and produce the corresponding topological feature vectors for downstream tasks. To fill this gap, we propose \textbf{TopoAgent}, an LLM-based agentic framework that automates topology learning for medical image analysis.TopoAgent operates through a Perception--Reasoning--Action--Reflection loop supported by 21 domain-specific tools and dual memory that accumulates experience across runs. Its skill set is distilled from systematic evaluation of 15 topological descriptors across 26 datasets with six classifiers. TopoAgent analyzes input images and their topological characteristics, reasons about which topological descriptors best suit the input, and determines the optimal descriptor and its configuration, all without task-specific training.

AI.tldr: TopoAgent is an LLM-based agentic framework that automatically selects optimal topological descriptors for medical image analysis, using a Perception-Reasoning-Action-Reflection loop with 21 tools and dual memory, eliminating the need for task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Existing topological data analysis methods default to single fixed descriptors (e.g., persistence images) and lack automated systems to select the most suitable topological representation for a given medical image dataset.
AI.method: TopoAgent uses a Perception-Reasoning-Action-Reflection loop with 21 domain-specific tools and dual memory, drawing from systematic evaluation of 15 topological descriptors across 26 datasets with six classifiers to automatically select and configure optimal descriptors.
AI.result: The framework can analyze input images, reason about suitable topological descriptors, and determine optimal descriptor configurations without task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: TopoAgent is the first known LLM-based agentic framework to automate topology learning by intelligently selecting topological descriptors for downstream medical image analysis tasks.

title: Query-Aware Spreading Activation for Multi-Hop Retrieval over Knowledge Graphs
comment: Accepted for publication in Cybernetics and Systems Analysis (Springer). Not yet published
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation built on knowledge graphs (Graph RAG) outperforms flat passage retrieval on multi-hop question answering by leveraging graph structure. In most existing systems, however, the question only sets the seed nodes; the subsequent traversal becomes "query-blind", depending solely on the graph structure. The exception is QAFD-RAG, which implements query-aware traversal via a flow-diffusion solver with combined edge re-weighting. This architecture requires loading the full graph into Python memory and an iterative solver with a variable number of iterations complicating integration with the graph database. We propose a spreading-activation method that achieves the same query-aware traversal with a single per-step semantic gate: the step weight is the cosine similarity between the candidate entity's description and the question, and the number of iterations is fixed. The whole retrieval procedure - seed mapping, propagation, top-K selection and context assembly - is expressed as a single Cypher query executed in one round-trip to Neo4j; the graph never leaves the database. On MuSiQue our method matches QAFD-RAG by exact match (32.80 vs 33.50) and outperforms the strongest purely-structural baseline in our comparison, HippoRAG, by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1; on 2WikiMultiHopQA HippoRAG and QAFD-RAG retain an advantage due to their phrase-node architectures. An ablation with the gate disabled confirms that the gate is the source of a simultaneous F1 gain of 3.6 to 7.4 points and a retrieval-latency reduction by a factor of 1.5 to 4.9.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a spreading-activation method for query-aware graph traversal in Retrieval-Augmented Generation that runs entirely inside Neo4j via a single Cypher query, matching or outperforming existing approaches while reducing latency.
AI.motivation: Existing Graph RAG systems either use query-blind traversal or require loading the full graph into Python memory with iterative solvers (like QAFD-RAG), which complicates integration with graph databases.
AI.method: A spreading-activation method with a single per-step semantic gate: step weight = cosine similarity between candidate entity description and question, with fixed iteration count. The entire retrieval is expressed as one Cypher query in a single round-trip to Neo4j.
AI.result: Matches QAFD-RAG on MuSiQue (32.80 vs 33.50 EM), outperforms HippoRAG by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1. Ablation shows gate provides 3.6-7.4 F1 gain and 1.5-4.9x latency reduction.
AI.conclusion: Query-aware traversal via semantic gating can be efficiently implemented entirely within a graph database, achieving competitive accuracy with substantial latency improvements over both query-blind and full-graph-loading approaches.

title: UrbanCDNet: Appearance-Robust and Boundary-Aware Bitemporal Change Detection for Korean Urban Building Monitoring
summary: Urban building change detection from bi-temporal aerial imagery is important for redevelopment monitoring, infrastructure management, and unauthorized-construction screening, but Korean urban scenes remain difficult because changed regions are often sparse, appearance varies strongly between acquisition dates, and useful outputs must follow building footprints rather than coarse blobs. This paper presents UrbanCDNet, a task specific Siamese CNN that combines appearance-robust multi-cue comparison, alignment-aware middle-scale differencing, lightweight context refinement, scene calibration, and auxiliary boundary supervision. Experiments use a corrected AIHub-based Korean benchmark with 3,998 training, 503 validation, and 499 test pairs, and report changed-class precision, recall, F1, and IoU. On the locked test split, UrbanCDNet achieves 0.7335 precision, 0.7696 recall, 0.7511 F1, and 0.6014 IoU, outperforming a strong Siamese U-Net baseline (0.7108 F1, 0.5514 IoU) and the strongest external competitor, ChangeFormer-MIT-B0 (0.7107 F1, 0.5512 IoU). Additional diagnostic slicing shows that the gain is concentrated in the operating regimes that motivated the design: on the sparse-change subset with less than 5% changed area, F1 improves from 0.4765 to 0.6175, and on the high photometric-gap subset it improves from 0.6349 to 0.7285. Boundary F1 at 3-pixel tolerance rises from 0.3445 to 0.4447, while object F1 at IoU 0.3 rises from 0.0690 to 0.2258. These results indicate that, on this Korean benchmark, task-shaped temporal comparison and boundary-aware supervision matter more than generic model scale alone

AI.tldr: UrbanCDNet is a Siamese CNN for building change detection from aerial imagery in Korean urban scenes, achieving superior performance (0.7511 F1, 0.6014 IoU) over baselines by combining multi-cue comparison, alignment-aware differencing, and boundary supervision, particularly excelling on sparse-change and high-photometric-gap cases.
AI.motivation: Urban building change detection in Korean scenes is challenging due to sparse changed regions, strong appearance variations between acquisition dates, and the need for building-footprint-level outputs rather than coarse blobs.
AI.method: UrbanCDNet is a task-specific Siamese CNN combining appearance-robust multi-cue comparison, alignment-aware middle-scale differencing, lightweight context refinement, scene calibration, and auxiliary boundary supervision.
AI.result: On a corrected Korean benchmark (3,998/503/499 train/val/test pairs), UrbanCDNet achieves 0.7335 precision, 0.7696 recall, 0.7511 F1, and 0.6014 IoU, outperforming Siamese U-Net (0.7108 F1) and ChangeFormer-MIT-B0 (0.7107 F1). Gains are most pronounced on sparse-change (F1: 0.4765→0.6175) and high-photometric-gap (F1: 0.6349→0.7285) subsets, with boundary F1 improving from 0.3445 to 0.4447 and object F1 from 0.0690 to 0.2258.
AI.conclusion: Task-shaped temporal comparison and boundary-aware supervision are more important than generic model scale for building change detection in this Korean benchmark, validating the proposed design choices.

title: Robust Strategic Classification under Decision-Dependent Cost Uncertainty
comment: 29 pages, 7 figures, accepted for publication at ICML 2026
summary: Humans facing algorithmic decision systems have been found to ``game'' them by altering their input data (at a cost to them) in order to favorably change the algorithmic outcomes they receive (at a cost to the algorithm). The growing literature on strategic classification seeks to develop robust machine learning algorithms that account for, and reduce, unwanted strategic behavior. A limitation of these existing works is that they assume the cost of strategic behavior to be fixed and independent of the classifier's decision. In practice, however, manipulation costs evolve and depend on past algorithmic decisions: today's decisions influence tomorrow's costs. This paper proposes and analyzes a two-stage robust optimization framework with a decision-dependent uncertainty set to capture such dependencies. We highlight that awareness of policy-dependent costs not only reduces uncertainty, but also better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time.

AI.tldr: This paper extends strategic classification by introducing decision-dependent manipulation costs, proposing a two-stage robust optimization framework to reduce gaming over time.
AI.motivation: Existing strategic classification literature assumes manipulation costs are fixed and independent of the classifier's decisions, which is unrealistic. In practice, past algorithmic decisions influence future manipulation costs, leading to evolving strategic behavior.
AI.method: A two-stage robust optimization framework with decision-dependent uncertainty sets is proposed to model how past decisions affect future manipulation costs and strategic behavior.
AI.result: Awareness of policy-dependent costs reduces uncertainty and better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for evolving, decision-dependent manipulation costs improves robustness against strategic behavior in sequential settings.

title: Graph-GSReg: Leveraging 3D Scene Graphs for Gaussian Splatting Registration
summary: Merging multiple 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes into a single unified Gaussian representation is essential for large-scale 3D mapping and long-term map management. Despite its importance, this area remains underexplored, and existing solutions exhibit several limitations. Learning-based methods attempt direct correspondence between Gaussian primitives and require training on large 3DGS datasets. Image-based optimization methods depend heavily on coarse initialization from generic foundation models and often incur expensive refinement. We present \ourmodel. Our method constructs a 3D scene graph from a 3DGS and its rendered images, \textit{reformulating 3DGS registration as a graph registration problem}. The proposed 3D scene graph represents each 3DGS at a higher-level representation, enabling a globally consistent understanding of semantic information and structural context for accurate registration. To further construct a seamless unified scene, we introduce a Self-Supervised Test-Time Optimization. Naively merging two 3D Gaussian scenes often suffers from occlusion artifacts such as hollows and floaters. To alleviate this issue, we refine the merged Gaussians to preserve visual consistency between the original scenes and the merged scene. We evaluate our method on real and synthetic benchmarks, demonstrating competitive registration accuracy and merged scene rendering quality.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method for merging multiple 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes by reformulating 3DGS registration as a graph registration problem using a 3D scene graph, plus a self-supervised test-time optimization to reduce occlusion artifacts during merging.
AI.motivation: Merging multiple 3DGS scenes is essential for large-scale 3D mapping and long-term map management, yet existing solutions are limited: learning-based methods need large datasets, and image-based optimization methods rely on coarse initialization and expensive refinement.
AI.method: Constructs a 3D scene graph from a 3DGS and its rendered images, reformulating 3DGS registration as a graph registration problem. The scene graph captures semantic and structural context. A Self-Supervised Test-Time Optimization is introduced to refine merged Gaussians, reducing occlusion artifacts like hollows and floaters by preserving visual consistency.
AI.result: Evaluated on real and synthetic benchmarks, demonstrating competitive registration accuracy and merged scene rendering quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively merges 3DGS scenes with improved registration accuracy and reduced artifacts, offering a robust alternative to existing learning-based and optimization-based approaches.

title: Federated Learning with Energy-Based Structured Probabilistic Inference
comment: Accepted to the Structured Probabilistic Inference Generative Modeling workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Federated learning typically aggregates client updates using fixed or heuristic weighting rules, which can be suboptimal when clients have heterogeneous data and varying contributions to the global model. We propose a framework that refines client aggregation weights using Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). Our method defines unary potentials for individual clients and pairwise potentials for all client pairs, allowing the server to model both client-specific reliability and interactions between clients. The resulting CRF inference produces aggregation weights that enable better convergence of the global training objective. Experiments show that, under non-IID heterogeneity, our approach consistently improves performance over well-established federated learning baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes using Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to learn optimal client aggregation weights in federated learning, improving over fixed/heuristic weighting under heterogeneous data.
AI.motivation: Fixed or heuristic client aggregation weights are suboptimal when clients have heterogeneous data and varying contributions to the global model.
AI.method: Uses Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to model unary potentials (individual client reliability) and pairwise potentials (client interactions), producing learned aggregation weights via CRF inference.
AI.result: Under non-IID heterogeneity, the method consistently outperforms well-established federated learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: CRF-based aggregation weight refinement enables better convergence and performance in heterogeneous federated learning settings.

title: Beyond Drug Discovery: The Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark
summary: Generative molecular design is shaped by simple proxy benchmarks for drug-like properties and models pretrained on large pharmaceutical datasets. This combination yields strong benchmark metrics but limits transferability to domains structurally distinct from drug discovery. To overcome this limitation and drive discovery toward real, scientifically grounded targets, we introduce the Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark, which bridges machine learning (ML) and quantum materials science. NMO acts simultaneously as a rigorous testbed for the ML community and a discovery engine for nanotechnology research. The suite replaces proxy oracles with quantum simulations and introduces strict protocols that prioritize scientific utility over leaderboard-oriented overfitting. The physics-based NMO tasks impose hard structural constraints and rugged fitness landscapes, posing fundamentally new requirements on generative models. Notably, advanced molecular optimization methods underperform much simpler approaches on the NMO tasks. We develop a new baseline method identifying the critical components to solve the NMO tasks, including a novel representation for modeling structural constraints and a domain-agnostic pretraining strategy to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias. Our results surpass state-of-the-art physical properties and reveal previously unknown structural motifs, offering new insights for the nanotechnology community and demonstrating that ML can drive genuine scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark, which replaces standard drug-like property proxies with quantum simulations to test generative molecular design on nanotechnology-relevant tasks, revealing that simpler methods outperform advanced ML approaches and proposing a new baseline with novel representations and bias-free pretraining.
AI.motivation: Current generative molecular design benchmarks rely on proxy drug-like properties and pharmaceutical datasets, which limits transferability to structurally distinct domains like nanotechnology. The authors aim to bridge ML and quantum materials science to drive discovery toward real scientific targets.
AI.method: The NMO Benchmark replaces proxy oracles with quantum simulations, imposes physics-based hard structural constraints and rugged fitness landscapes, and introduces strict protocols prioritizing scientific utility. A new baseline method uses a novel representation for structural constraints and domain-agnostic pretraining to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias.
AI.result: Advanced molecular optimization methods underperform simpler approaches on NMO tasks. The proposed baseline surpasses state-of-the-art physical properties and discovers previously unknown structural motifs relevant to nanotechnology.
AI.conclusion: ML-driven generative models can achieve genuine scientific discovery in nanotechnology when benchmarked appropriately. The NMO Benchmark exposes limitations of current methods and provides a pathway toward more domain-relevant molecular design.

title: UniTriSplat: A Unified 3D Gaussian Splatting Framework with Uniform Spherical Rasterization for Universal Cameras
comment: 32 pages, 14 figures, 6 tables. Project page: https://yipengzhu0809.github.io/UniTriSplat/ . UniTriSplat was accepted to ECCV 2026
summary: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) frameworks rely on camera-specific rasterization, suffering from inconsistent solid-angle sampling and degraded performance across heterogeneous camera models (e.g., perspective, fisheye, omnidirectional). To address this limitation, we propose UniTriSplat, a unified 3DGS framework for universal cameras that reformulates Gaussian splatting on the unit sphere via HEALPix discretization. Leveraging the equal-area property of HEALPix, we construct a spherical sampling grid aligned with the angular resolution of input images. We derive the forward rendering and gradient propagation of Gaussians directly in the spherical radian domain, yielding uniform optimization behavior from narrow-FoV images to full 360-degree panoramas. To enhance perceptual reconstruction quality, we additionally introduce a HEALPix-aware SSIM loss that respects spherical neighborhood structure. Extensive experiments across diverse camera models demonstrate that UniTriSplat consistently improves cross-camera generalization while preserving geometric fidelity and rendering quality.

AI.tldr: UniTriSplat is a unified 3D Gaussian Splatting framework that uses HEALPix spherical discretization to handle diverse camera models (perspective, fisheye, panoramic) consistently, improving cross-camera generalization and rendering quality.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS methods rely on camera-specific rasterization, leading to inconsistent sampling and degraded performance across heterogeneous camera models (perspective, fisheye, omnidirectional).
AI.method: Reformulates Gaussian splatting on the unit sphere via HEALPix discretization, constructing equal-area spherical sampling grids aligned with input angular resolution, with forward rendering and gradient propagation in spherical radian domain, plus a HEALPix-aware SSIM loss.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in cross-camera generalization across diverse camera models, preserving geometric fidelity and rendering quality from narrow-FoV to 360-degree panoramas.
AI.conclusion: UniTriSplat provides a unified solution for 3DGS that works universally across camera types, overcoming the inconsistent sampling problem of prior camera-specific approaches.

title: Characterizing Optimizer-Dependent Training Dynamics Through Hessian Eigenvector Displacement and Localization
comment: Accepted as a poster at High-dimensional Learning Dynamics (HiLD), ICML 2026. OpenReview: https://openreview.net/forum?id=SabYcw5Nh6
summary: Hessian spectral properties are a standard tool in analysing neural-network training, with eigenvalues linked to sharpness, generalization, and optimization dynamics. Eigenvalues quantify curvature magnitude, while eigenvectors identify which parameters generate that curvature. In this work, we study how the leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during training and how they affect the learning trajectories. We track the training dynamics of multilayer perceptrons on a classification problem and measure eigenvector dynamics through two complementary statistics: (i) displacement over time, inspired by analyses of glassy systems, and (ii) localization via the inverse participation ratio. The metrics are compared against a random null model of the Hessian induced by the architecture. Our results reveal clear optimizer-dependent behaviour. SGD leads to progressively more stable leading curvature directions, while Adam exhibits substantially stronger reorganization of eigenvectors throughout training. We also observe a localization phenomenon under Adam, where a small subset of parameters contributes disproportionately to the leading curvature directions. These results suggest that Hessian eigenvector dynamics capture key differences in optimizer behaviour and the resulting training trajectories.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during neural network training, revealing that SGD leads to stable curvature directions while Adam causes ongoing eigenvector reorganization and parameter localization.
AI.motivation: To understand how leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during training and how they affect learning trajectories, capturing optimizer-specific behaviors.
AI.method: Tracked MLP training dynamics on classification tasks, measuring eigenvector dynamics via displacement (glassy system analogy) and inverse participation ratio (localization), compared against a random null model.
AI.result: SGD produces progressively stable leading curvature directions; Adam exhibits substantial eigenvector reorganization and a localization phenomenon where few parameters dominate curvature directions.
AI.conclusion: Hessian eigenvector dynamics capture key differences in optimizer behavior and training trajectories, with optimizer-dependent curvature evolution patterns.

title: Concept Removal Guidance: Evidence-Calibrated Negative Guidance for Safe Diffusion Sampling
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts that elicit disallowed content, motivating reliable inference-time controls. A popular approach is negative guidance, which subtracts a negative prompt direction with a fixed weight. However, it often forces a safety-fidelity trade-off, causing artifacts or prompt drift when over-applied and failing under attacks when under-applied. Dynamic variants reweight guidance using posterior-odds signals, which can be brittle for open-vocabulary compositional prompts, while lightweight similarity-based methods ignore the evolving image evidence along the denoising trajectory. We introduce Concept Removal Guidance (CRG), a training-free method that estimates unwanted-concept presence at each diffusion step from the model's noise predictions, and adaptively calibrates negative guidance via a closed-form constrained update enforcing a target presence threshold while minimally perturbing the conditional trajectory. Across red-teaming benchmarks, CRG reduces attack success rates while preserving benign fidelity, and extends to additional suppression targets such as artist style and violence without fine-tuning or external classifiers.

AI.tldr: CRG is a training-free, adaptive negative guidance method that estimates unwanted concept presence from noise predictions and calibrates suppression via constrained updates, reducing attack success rates without requiring fine-tuning or external classifiers.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts that elicit disallowed content, and existing inference-time controls like negative guidance impose a safety-fidelity trade-off, being either over-applied (artifacts/drift) or under-applied (failing under attacks).
AI.method: CRG estimates unwanted-concept presence at each diffusion step from the model's noise predictions, then adaptively calibrates negative guidance via a closed-form constrained update that enforces a target presence threshold while minimally perturbing the conditional trajectory.
AI.result: Across red-teaming benchmarks, CRG reduces attack success rates while preserving benign fidelity, and extends to additional suppression targets (e.g., artist style, violence) without fine-tuning or external classifiers.
AI.conclusion: CRG effectively mitigates the safety-fidelity trade-off in diffusion model safety by adaptively suppressing unwanted concepts at inference time, without requiring retraining or external models.

title: B3O: Scalable Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization
summary: Modern engineering workflows increasingly rely on massive parallel simulation, driving the need for scalable, large-batch Bayesian Optimization (BO). Existing batch BO methods, however, incur large computational cost or rely on approximations that erode batch diversity. We propose B3O (Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization), a framework that reframes batch generation as a pure sampling problem: drawing samples directly from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function avoids the bottlenecks of existing large-batch methods. Theoretically, we prove that queries sampled from this distribution incur only negligible additional regret. Empirically, B3O outperforms existing batch BO methods on standard synthetic benchmarks and adapts robustly across complex applied tasks, including multi-objective electrode design and mixed-variable race car configuration.

AI.tldr: B3O proposes a batch Bayesian Optimization method that generates batches by directly sampling from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function, achieving scalability while provably maintaining near-optimal regret.
AI.motivation: Existing batch BO methods are computationally expensive or rely on approximations that reduce batch diversity, limiting scalability for large-batch parallel simulation workflows.
AI.method: B3O reframes batch generation as a pure sampling problem, drawing queries directly from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function.
AI.result: Outperforms existing batch BO methods on synthetic benchmarks, and adapts robustly to complex real-world tasks including multi-objective electrode design and mixed-variable race car configuration.
AI.conclusion: Theoretically proven to incur only negligible additional regret, B3O provides a scalable and effective solution for large-batch Bayesian Optimization.

title: Clearer Sight, Fewer Lies: Oriented Pickup Preference Optimization for Multimodal Hallucination Mitigation
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are prone to hallucination as their generation preferences are insufficiently calibrated to visual evidence, causing them to fall back on linguistic priors, rather than faithful grounding. In this work, we start from an empirical observation: when query-relevant visual evidence is explicitly strengthened using the model's own attention, generation becomes more accurate, suggesting that many failures do not arise solely from missing perception, but from an insufficient tendency to trust the evidence the model has already attended to. Motivated by this finding, we propose Oriented Pickup Preference Optimization (\texttt{OPPO}), an evidence-aware alignment objective that learns preferences over the strength of visual evidence, rather than only response quality. Concretely, \texttt{OPPO} contrasts the same faithful response under stronger, anchored, weaker-evidence views, turning naive visual preference into ordered visual-evidence alignment. We further combine this objective with fine-grained span-level and token-level regularization to stabilize the training. Besides, we provide a theoretical analysis showing that ordered evidence margins induce a positive lower bound on local visual sensitivity. Extensive evaluations across hallucination and general-purpose benchmarks demonstrate that \texttt{OPPO} consistently outperforms baseline methods.

AI.tldr: OPPO is a new alignment method that reduces hallucination in MLLMs by learning preferences over visual evidence strength rather than just response quality, using ordered evidence views and fine-grained regularization.
AI.motivation: MLLMs hallucinate because their generation preferences are insufficiently calibrated to visual evidence, causing them to rely on linguistic priors instead of faithful visual grounding. The authors observe that strengthening query-relevant visual evidence via attention improves generation accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes Oriented Pickup Preference Optimization (OPPO), an evidence-aware alignment objective that: (1) contrasts the same faithful response under stronger, anchored, and weaker-evidence views; (2) combines with fine-grained span-level and token-level regularization; (3) provides theoretical analysis showing ordered evidence margins induce positive lower bound on local visual sensitivity.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms baseline methods across hallucination and general-purpose benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Many MLLM failures stem not from missing perception but from insufficient tendency to trust already-attended visual evidence. OPPO effectively addresses this via evidence-aware preference optimization.

title: Curvature-Guided Sheaf Diffusion for Unsupervised Community Detection on Heterophilic Graphs
summary: Detecting communities in heterophilic graphs -- where connected nodes often belong to different classes -- is hard for unsupervised methods: classical modularity and spectral methods are feature agnostic, while deep graph-clustering methods rely on contrastive or generative machinery that is opaque. We propose Curvature-Guided Sheaf Diffusion (CGSD), a fully unsupervised community-detection algorithm that uses the discrete Forman--Ricci curvature of each edge as its single topological signal, propagated through every stage of an end-to-end pipeline. CGSD makes three concrete contributions: (i)~a curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder that gates edge messages by $σ(κ_e)$ and is trained from three label-free structural losses (modularity, anti-collapse, curvature-weighted reconstruction); (ii)~a curvature-aware spectral clusterer (CSpec) that re-weights the $k$-NN affinity of the embedding by $σ(ακ_{e^*})$ before Ng--Jordan--Weiss; and (iii)~a unified label-free evaluation against nine truly-unsupervised baselines. On five heterophilic benchmarks (Cora, Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin, Chameleon), CGSD wins outright on Wisconsin and Chameleon and is competitive on the remaining three against nine unsupervised baselines. The gain over the strongest baseline is driven by the clusterer, not the encoder: on the same embedding, CSpec improves mean NMI from $0.091$ with $K$-Means to $0.107$ ($+15\%$, paired $t$-test $p=0.008$). The mechanism is interpretable: intra-community and inter-community curvature distributions are visibly separated. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/woodywff/cgsd.

AI.tldr: CGSD is an unsupervised community detection algorithm for heterophilic graphs that uses Forman-Ricci curvature as a topological signal to guide sheaf diffusion and spectral clustering, achieving strong results on heterophilic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised community detection methods fail on heterophilic graphs (where connected nodes often belong to different classes) because classical methods like modularity/spectral clustering are feature-agnostic and deep methods rely on opaque contrastive/generative approaches.
AI.method: CGSD proposes (i) a curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder that gates edge messages by σ(κ_e) trained with three label-free losses (modularity, anti-collapse, curvature-weighted reconstruction); (ii) a curvature-aware spectral clusterer (CSpec) that re-weights k-NN affinity by σ(ακ_{e^*}) before Ng-Jordan-Weiss spectral clustering.
AI.result: On five heterophilic benchmarks (Cora, Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin, Chameleon), CGSD wins on Wisconsin and Chameleon and is competitive on the rest. CSpec improves mean NMI from 0.091 (K-Means) to 0.107 (+15%, p=0.008) on the same embeddings. Curvature distributions of intra- vs inter-community edges are visibly separated.
AI.conclusion: Curvature is an effective topological signal for unsupervised community detection in heterophilic graphs, and the curvature-aware spectral clusterer (not the encoder) drives the performance gain.

title: Consistency as Inductive Bias: Learning Cross-View Invariance for Robust Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Inductive biases steer learning toward generalizable solutions by encoding task structure. In this work, we identify a crucial missing bias in MLLMs: cross-view consistency, \textit{i.e.}, semantically invariant views of the same instance should lead to the same answer. Standard reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) objectives do not impose this constraint, but instead assign pointwise rewards to each visual input. Even with data augmentation (DA), transformed views are typically rewarded independently, providing little signal once within-view rewards saturate. We propose \textbf{ConsistRoll}, a simple but effective method that injects cross-view consistency into RLVR training by reusing the group-sampling mechanism of GRPO. Specifically, ConsistRoll places original and semantically invariant transformed views in the same generation group, and assigns a joint reward only when paired completions are both correct and consistent. In this way, ConsistRoll turns consistency into an online credit-assignment signal, \textbf{without extra generation overhead and annotations}. Theoretically, we show that cross-view consistency is a valid inductive bias, and ConsistRoll introduces a cross-view correction term absent from DA, penalizing view dependence and alleviating advantage collapse. Comprehensive benchmarks across math, general-purpose, hallucination domains confirm that ConsistRoll achieves robust improvements in multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: ConsistRoll injects cross-view consistency into MLLM training via GRPO-style group sampling, pairing original and semantically invariant views, and assigning joint rewards only when both are correct and consistent, improving multimodal reasoning without extra overhead.
AI.motivation: MLLMs lack cross-view consistency; standard RLVR and data augmentation assign pointwise rewards per view without enforcing that semantically invariant views yield the same answer, limiting generalization.
AI.method: ConsistRoll reuses GRPO's group-sampling mechanism to pair original and augmented views in the same generation group, assigning a joint reward only when paired completions are both correct and consistent, turning consistency into an online credit-assignment signal.
AI.result: Comprehensive benchmarks across math, general-purpose, and hallucination domains show robust improvements in multimodal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Cross-view consistency is a valid inductive bias; ConsistRoll introduces a cross-view correction term absent in DA, penalizing view dependence and alleviating advantage collapse, achieving gains without extra generation cost or annotations.

title: KnowsTFM: Knowledge-Informed Fine-Tuning of Small Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models have advanced deep learning for tabular data by delivering strong default performance across many small and medium tasks.
